Permission removed successfully
# Log permission removal for audit trail from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone # Record the revocation event revoked_event = Skills.objects.create( name="Layer Permission Revoked", description="django-dependencies v5 access removed from dev account" ) print(f"Access revocation logged: {revoked_event.name}") print(f"Layer: django-dependencies version 5") print(f"Statement removed: share-with-dev-account") print(f"Timestamp: {timezone.now()}")
Example 2: Remove Organization-Wide Access

Revoke organization-wide access to a utilities layer when migrating to a new version.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove org-wide access from old layer version

aws lambda remove-layer-version-permission \
    --layer-name common-utilities \
    --version-number 3 \
    --statement-id org-wide-access \
    --region us-west-2

echo "Organization access revoked from v3"
Organization access revoked from v3
# Notify users about layer version migration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.core.mail import send_mail # Get developers affected by this change developers = User.objects.filter(is_staff=True, is_active=True) print(f"Notifying {developers.count()} developers about layer migration") print(f"Old version (v3) access revoked") print(f"Please migrate to common-utilities v4") for dev in developers[:5]: print(f" - {dev.username} ({dev.email})")
Example 3: Safely Remove Permission with Revision Check

Remove a permission statement only if the policy hasn't been modified by another process.

#!/bin/bash
# Get current revision before removing permission

LAYER_NAME="pillow-image-processor"
VERSION=8
STATEMENT_ID="partner-account-access"

# Get current revision
REVISION=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --query 'RevisionId' \
    --output text)

echo "Current policy revision: $REVISION"

# Remove only if revision matches
aws lambda remove-layer-version-permission \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --statement-id "$STATEMENT_ID" \
    --revision-id "$REVISION" \
    --region eu-west-1

echo "Permission safely removed"
Current policy revision: f9g8h7i6-2345-67ef-abcd-layer5432198 Permission safely removed
# Track policy changes for security audit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import json # Find job posts using image processing image_posts = JobPost.objects.exclude(image='').count() print(f"Image processing layer policy updated") print(f"Layer: pillow-image-processor v{VERSION}") print(f"Removed statement: {STATEMENT_ID}") print(f"Policy revision checked: {REVISION}") print(f"\nJob posts with images: {image_posts}") print(f"Partner access revoked from layer")
Example 4: Bulk Remove Multiple Permissions

Remove all external account permissions when decommissioning a shared layer.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove all external permissions from deprecated layer

LAYER_NAME="django-auth-layer-deprecated"
VERSION=2

# List of statement IDs to remove
STATEMENTS=("testing-account" "staging-account" "partner-account")

for stmt_id in "${STATEMENTS[@]}"; do
    echo "Removing permission: $stmt_id"
    aws lambda remove-layer-version-permission \
        --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
        --version-number $VERSION \
        --statement-id "$stmt_id" \
        --region ap-southeast-1
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "✓ Removed: $stmt_id"
    else
        echo "✗ Failed to remove: $stmt_id"
    fi
done

echo "\nAll permissions removed from deprecated layer"
Removing permission: testing-account ✓ Removed: testing-account Removing permission: staging-account ✓ Removed: staging-account Removing permission: partner-account ✓ Removed: partner-account All permissions removed from deprecated layer
# Log bulk permission removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.contrib.contenttypes.models import ContentType removed_statements = ["testing-account", "staging-account", "partner-account"] print(f"Bulk permission removal completed") print(f"Layer: django-auth-layer-deprecated v{VERSION}") print(f"\nRemoved statements:") for stmt in removed_statements: print(f" - {stmt}") print(f"\nTotal permissions revoked: {len(removed_statements)}") print(f"Layer is now private")
Example 5: Remove and Verify Permission Deletion

Remove a permission and verify it no longer exists in the layer policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove permission and verify

LAYER_NAME="django-core"
VERSION=12
STATEMENT_ID="partner-stack-django"

echo "Before removal:"
aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text | jq .

# Remove the permission
aws lambda remove-layer-version-permission \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --statement-id "$STATEMENT_ID"

echo "\nPermission removed"

echo "\nAfter removal:"
aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text 2>&1 | jq . || echo "No policy exists (all permissions removed)"
Before removal: { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "partner-stack-django", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::777888999000:root" }, "Action": "lambda:GetLayerVersion", "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:999888777666:layer:django-core:12" } ] } Permission removed After removal: No policy exists (all permissions removed)
# Verify and log layer security status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q # Check if any users are still depending on this layer django_users = User.objects.filter( author__skills__name__icontains='django' ).distinct() print(f"Django developers in system: {django_users.count()}") print(f"\nLayer security status:") print(f" Layer: {LAYER_NAME} v{VERSION}") print(f" Statement removed: {STATEMENT_ID}") print(f" Current policy: Empty (layer is now private)") print(f" Access: Restricted to owner account only") # Check job posts related to Django django_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( Q(title__icontains='django') | Q(skills__name__icontains='django') ).distinct().count() print(f"\nDjango job posts in system: {django_posts}")

get-layer-version-by-arn

Returns information about a layer version using its ARN. Useful when you have the full ARN but not the layer name or version number, such as when working with layers shared from other accounts or discovering layers used in Django Lambda functions.

Syntax
aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn <layer-version-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --arn (string, required): Full ARN of the layer version including the version number. Format: arn:aws:lambda:region:account-id:layer:layer-name:version
Return Value
  • Content (object): Information about the layer archive including download location, SHA256 hash, and size in bytes.
  • LayerArn (string): ARN of the layer without version number.
  • LayerVersionArn (string): Complete ARN including the version number.
  • Description (string): Layer description provided during creation.
  • CreatedDate (string): Timestamp when the layer version was created.
  • Version (integer): Layer version number.
  • CompatibleRuntimes (list): List of compatible Lambda runtime versions (e.g., python3.9, python3.11).
  • CompatibleArchitectures (list, optional): Supported architectures (x86_64, arm64).
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Django Dependencies Layer Details

Retrieve information about a Django dependencies layer using its full ARN from another account.

#!/bin/bash
# Get layer details from shared ARN

LAYER_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-dependencies:5"

aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$LAYER_ARN" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "Content": { "Location": "https://awslambda-us-east-1-layers.s3.amazonaws.com/snapshots/123456789012/django-dependencies-abc123def456...", "CodeSha256": "abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu901vwx234yz=", "CodeSize": 5242880 }, "LayerArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-dependencies", "LayerVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-dependencies:5", "Description": "Django 4.2 with Pillow, boto3, psycopg2 for JobPost app", "CreatedDate": "2024-01-15T10:30:00.000+0000", "Version": 5, "CompatibleRuntimes": ["python3.9", "python3.10", "python3.11"], "CompatibleArchitectures": ["x86_64"] }
# Catalog layer information for dependency tracking from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost layer_info = { 'name': 'django-dependencies', 'version': 5, 'size_mb': 5242880 / (1024 * 1024), 'runtimes': ['python3.9', 'python3.10', 'python3.11'] } print(f"Layer: {layer_info['name']}") print(f"Version: {layer_info['version']}") print(f"Size: {layer_info['size_mb']:.2f} MB") print(f"Compatible runtimes: {', '.join(layer_info['runtimes'])}") # Track skills related to these dependencies skills = Skills.objects.filter(name__in=['Django', 'Python', 'AWS Lambda']) print(f"\nRelated skills in system: {skills.count()}")
Example 2: Verify Layer Compatibility for Python Version

Check if a shared layer is compatible with your Lambda function's Python runtime.

#!/bin/bash
# Check layer compatibility with Python 3.11

LAYER_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-west-2:555666777888:layer:pillow-image-processor:8"
REQUIRED_RUNTIME="python3.11"

echo "Checking layer compatibility..."

LAYER_INFO=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$LAYER_ARN" \
    --query '{Runtimes:CompatibleRuntimes,Size:Content.CodeSize,Version:Version}' \
    --output json)

echo "$LAYER_INFO" | jq .

# Check if runtime is supported
if echo "$LAYER_INFO" | jq -e ".Runtimes[] | select(. == \"$REQUIRED_RUNTIME\")" > /dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Layer is compatible with $REQUIRED_RUNTIME"
else
    echo "✗ Layer is NOT compatible with $REQUIRED_RUNTIME"
    echo "Available runtimes:"
    echo "$LAYER_INFO" | jq -r '.Runtimes[]'
fi
Checking layer compatibility... { "Runtimes": [ "python3.9", "python3.10", "python3.11" ], "Size": 15728640, "Version": 8 } ✓ Layer is compatible with python3.11
# Track Python version compatibility in jobpost system from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q # Find job posts requiring Python skills python_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( Q(title__icontains='python') | Q(skills__name__icontains='python') ).distinct() print(f"Job posts requiring Python: {python_posts.count()}") print(f"\nLayer compatibility check:") print(f"Layer: pillow-image-processor v8") print(f"Required runtime: python3.11") print(f"Status: Compatible ✓") print(f"Size: 15 MB") # List Python experts experts = User.objects.filter(author__skills__name='Python').distinct()[:5] for expert in experts: print(f" - {expert.username}")
Example 3: Download and Inspect Layer Contents

Get layer download URL and verify contents for security audit.

#!/bin/bash
# Download layer for security inspection

LAYER_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:eu-west-1:999888777666:layer:django-auth-utilities:12"

echo "Getting layer download URL..."

DOWNLOAD_URL=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$LAYER_ARN" \
    --query 'Content.Location' \
    --output text)

CODE_SHA=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$LAYER_ARN" \
    --query 'Content.CodeSha256' \
    --output text)

echo "Download URL: $DOWNLOAD_URL"
echo "SHA256: $CODE_SHA"

# Download layer (URL valid for 10 minutes)
echo "\nDownloading layer..."
curl -o layer.zip "$DOWNLOAD_URL"

# Verify contents
echo "\nLayer contents:"
unzip -l layer.zip | head -20

echo "\n✓ Layer downloaded and inspected"
Getting layer download URL... Download URL: https://awslambda-eu-west-1-layers.s3.amazonaws.com/snapshots/999888777666/django-auth-utilities-xyz789... SHA256: xyz789abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu= Downloading layer... % Total % Received % Xferd Average Speed Time Time Time Current Dload Upload Total Spent Left Speed 100 2048k 100 2048k 0 0 5120k 0 --:--:-- --:--:-- --:--:-- 5120k Layer contents: Archive: layer.zip Length Date Time Name --------- ---------- ----- ---- 0 01-15-2024 10:30 python/ 0 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/ 0 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/python3.11/ 0 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/python3.11/site-packages/ 5432 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/python3.11/site-packages/django_auth_utils.py 12345 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/python3.11/site-packages/jwt_helper.py 8901 01-15-2024 10:30 python/lib/python3.11/site-packages/permissions.py ✓ Layer downloaded and inspected
# Log security audit of layer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import hashlib # Record audit event audit_log = Skills.objects.create( name="Layer Security Audit", description=f"django-auth-utilities v12 inspected on {timezone.now()}" ) print(f"Security audit logged: {audit_log.name}") print(f"Layer: django-auth-utilities v12") print(f"SHA256: xyz789abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu=") print(f"Status: Downloaded and inspected") print(f"\nKey files found:") print(" - django_auth_utils.py") print(" - jwt_helper.py") print(" - permissions.py") # Track users with security audit permissions admins = User.objects.filter(is_superuser=True) print(f"\nAdmins notified: {admins.count()}")
Example 4: Compare Layer Versions by ARN

Compare two layer versions to determine which to use in production.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare two layer versions

OLD_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-core:10"
NEW_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-core:11"

echo "Comparing layer versions...\n"

echo "=== OLD VERSION (v10) ==="
aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$OLD_ARN" \
    --query '{Version:Version,Size:Content.CodeSize,Created:CreatedDate,Runtimes:CompatibleRuntimes}' \
    --output table

echo "\n=== NEW VERSION (v11) ==="
aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
    --arn "$NEW_ARN" \
    --query '{Version:Version,Size:Content.CodeSize,Created:CreatedDate,Runtimes:CompatibleRuntimes}' \
    --output table

# Calculate size difference
OLD_SIZE=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn --arn "$OLD_ARN" --query 'Content.CodeSize' --output text)
NEW_SIZE=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn --arn "$NEW_ARN" --query 'Content.CodeSize' --output text)
DIFF=$((NEW_SIZE - OLD_SIZE))

echo "\nSize difference: $DIFF bytes"
Comparing layer versions... === OLD VERSION (v10) === --------------------------------------------------------- | GetLayerVersionByArn | +----------+------------------------------------------+ | Created | 2024-01-10T08:15:00.000+0000 | | Runtimes| python3.9 | | | python3.10 | | Size | 8388608 | | Version | 10 | +----------+------------------------------------------+ === NEW VERSION (v11) === --------------------------------------------------------- | GetLayerVersionByArn | +----------+------------------------------------------+ | Created | 2024-01-15T14:22:00.000+0000 | | Runtimes| python3.9 | | | python3.10 | | | python3.11 | | Size | 8912896 | | Version | 11 | +----------+------------------------------------------+ Size difference: 524288 bytes
# Track layer version migration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost old_version = {'version': 10, 'size': 8388608, 'runtimes': 2} new_version = {'version': 11, 'size': 8912896, 'runtimes': 3} size_diff = new_version['size'] - old_version['size'] print("Layer Version Comparison:") print(f"\nOld Version {old_version['version']}:") print(f" Size: {old_version['size'] / (1024*1024):.2f} MB") print(f" Runtimes: {old_version['runtimes']}") print(f"\nNew Version {new_version['version']}:") print(f" Size: {new_version['size'] / (1024*1024):.2f} MB") print(f" Runtimes: {new_version['runtimes']}") print(f" Added: Python 3.11 support") print(f"\nSize increase: {size_diff / 1024:.2f} KB") print(f"Recommendation: Upgrade to v11 for Python 3.11 support") # Check functions to migrate functions = JobPost.objects.all().count() print(f"\nFunctions to migrate: {functions}")
Example 5: Bulk Inspect Multiple Layers from Function

Get details for all layers used by a Lambda function for inventory purposes.

#!/bin/bash
# Inspect all layers used by a function

FUNCTION_NAME="jobpost-api-prod"

echo "Getting layers used by $FUNCTION_NAME...\n"

# Get layer ARNs from function
LAYER_ARNS=$(aws lambda get-function-configuration \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION_NAME" \
    --query 'Layers[].Arn' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$LAYER_ARNS" ]; then
    echo "No layers found"
    exit 0
fi

echo "Found layers, getting details...\n"

# Loop through each layer
for arn in $LAYER_ARNS; do
    echo "=== Layer: $arn ==="
    aws lambda get-layer-version-by-arn \
        --arn "$arn" \
        --query '{Description:Description,Size:Content.CodeSize,Runtimes:CompatibleRuntimes}' \
        --output table
    echo ""
done

echo "✓ Layer inventory complete"
Getting layers used by jobpost-api-prod... Found layers, getting details... === Layer: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:django-dependencies:5 === --------------------------------------------------------------- | GetLayerVersionByArn | +---------------+----------------------------------------------+ | Description | Django 4.2 with Pillow, boto3, psycopg2 | | Runtimes | python3.9 | | | python3.10 | | | python3.11 | | Size | 5242880 | +---------------+----------------------------------------------+ === Layer: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:layer:common-utilities:7 === --------------------------------------------------------------- | GetLayerVersionByArn | +---------------+----------------------------------------------+ | Description | Utility functions and helpers | | Runtimes | python3.9 | | | python3.10 | | | python3.11 | | Size | 1048576 | +---------------+----------------------------------------------+ ✓ Layer inventory complete
# Create comprehensive layer inventory from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count layers = [ {'name': 'django-dependencies', 'version': 5, 'size_mb': 5.0, 'type': 'framework'}, {'name': 'common-utilities', 'version': 7, 'size_mb': 1.0, 'type': 'utilities'} ] print(f"Layer inventory for function: jobpost-api-prod") print(f"Total layers: {len(layers)}\n") total_size = 0 for layer in layers: print(f"Layer: {layer['name']} v{layer['version']}") print(f" Type: {layer['type']}") print(f" Size: {layer['size_mb']} MB") total_size += layer['size_mb'] print(f"\nTotal layer size: {total_size} MB") # Track related job skills skills = Skills.objects.filter(name__in=['Django', 'Python', 'AWS Lambda']) print(f"\nRelated skills tracked: {skills.count()}") # Active job posts using these skills active_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( skills__in=skills, is_active=True ).distinct().count() print(f"Active job posts: {active_posts}")

get-layer-version-policy

Returns the resource-based policy for a layer version showing who has permission to use the layer. Essential for auditing layer sharing and managing cross-account access in Django deployments.

Syntax
aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name <layer-name> \
    --version-number <version>
Parameters Description
  • --layer-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda layer to retrieve policy for.
  • --version-number (integer, required): Layer version number. Each version has its own independent policy.
Return Value
  • Policy (string): JSON string containing the resource-based policy document with all permission statements.
  • RevisionId (string): Unique identifier for the current policy revision. Used for conditional updates.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: View Django Layer Sharing Policy

Retrieve and parse the policy to see which accounts can access your Django dependencies layer.

#!/bin/bash
# Get and parse layer policy

LAYER_NAME="django-dependencies"
VERSION=5

echo "Getting policy for $LAYER_NAME v$VERSION..."

aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --region us-east-1 \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text | jq .
Getting policy for django-dependencies v5... { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Id": "default", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "share-with-dev-account", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:root" }, "Action": "lambda:GetLayerVersion", "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:999888777666:layer:django-dependencies:5" }, { "Sid": "share-with-partner", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::555666777888:root" }, "Action": "lambda:GetLayerVersion", "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:999888777666:layer:django-dependencies:5" } ] }
# Audit layer access policies from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost import json shared_accounts = [ {'account_id': '123456789012', 'name': 'Development', 'statement': 'share-with-dev-account'}, {'account_id': '555666777888', 'name': 'Partner', 'statement': 'share-with-partner'} ] print(f"Layer Policy Audit: django-dependencies v{VERSION}") print(f"\nShared with {len(shared_accounts)} accounts:\n") for account in shared_accounts: print(f" - {account['name']} ({account['account_id']})") print(f" Statement ID: {account['statement']}") # Track internal teams teams = User.objects.filter(is_staff=True).count() print(f"\nInternal teams with access: {teams}")
Example 2: Extract Shared Account IDs

Parse the policy to extract all AWS account IDs that have access to the layer.

#!/bin/bash
# Extract all account IDs from layer policy

LAYER_NAME="pillow-image-processor"
VERSION=8

echo "Extracting shared account IDs from $LAYER_NAME v$VERSION\n"

POLICY=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text)

# Extract account IDs using jq
ACCOUNTS=$(echo "$POLICY" | jq -r '.Statement[].Principal.AWS | select(. != null)' | grep -o '[0-9]\{12\}')

echo "Accounts with access:"
echo "$ACCOUNTS" | while read account; do
    echo "  - $account"
done

COUNT=$(echo "$ACCOUNTS" | wc -l)
echo "\nTotal accounts: $COUNT"
Extracting shared account IDs from pillow-image-processor v8 Accounts with access: - 123456789012 - 555666777888 - 777888999000 Total accounts: 3
# Track layer access for compliance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost shared_account_ids = ['123456789012', '555666777888', '777888999000'] print(f"Layer Access Compliance Report") print(f"Layer: pillow-image-processor v{VERSION}") print(f"\nExternal accounts with access: {len(shared_account_ids)}") for idx, account_id in enumerate(shared_account_ids, 1): print(f" {idx}. {account_id}") # Find job posts using image processing image_posts = JobPost.objects.exclude(image='').count() print(f"\nJob posts with images: {image_posts}") print(f"Image processing layer is shared for collaboration")
Example 3: Check if Specific Account Has Access

Verify whether a particular AWS account has permission to use a layer before sharing resources.

#!/bin/bash
# Check if account has layer access

LAYER_NAME="common-utilities"
VERSION=7
CHECK_ACCOUNT="111222333444"

echo "Checking access for account $CHECK_ACCOUNT..."

POLICY=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text 2>&1)

if echo "$POLICY" | grep -q "ResourceNotFoundException"; then
    echo "✗ No policy found - layer is private"
    exit 0
fi

# Check if account is in policy
if echo "$POLICY" | jq -e ".Statement[].Principal.AWS | select(. | tostring | contains(\"$CHECK_ACCOUNT\"))" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "✓ Account $CHECK_ACCOUNT HAS access to $LAYER_NAME v$VERSION"
    
    # Get statement ID
    SID=$(echo "$POLICY" | jq -r ".Statement[] | select(.Principal.AWS | tostring | contains(\"$CHECK_ACCOUNT\")) | .Sid")
    echo "Statement ID: $SID"
else
    echo "✗ Account $CHECK_ACCOUNT does NOT have access to $LAYER_NAME v$VERSION"
fi
Checking access for account 111222333444... ✓ Account 111222333444 HAS access to common-utilities v7 Statement ID: testing-environment-access
# Log access verification check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone check_result = { 'layer': 'common-utilities', 'version': 7, 'account': '111222333444', 'has_access': True, 'statement_id': 'testing-environment-access', 'checked_at': timezone.now() } print(f"Access Verification Report") print(f"\nLayer: {check_result['layer']} v{check_result['version']}") print(f"Account: {check_result['account']}") print(f"Status: {'✓ Has Access' if check_result['has_access'] else '✗ No Access'}") print(f"Statement: {check_result['statement_id']}") print(f"Verified: {check_result['checked_at']}") # Track verification events author_count = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nTotal authors in system: {author_count}")
Example 4: Compare Policies Across Layer Versions

Check policy differences between old and new layer versions to track permission changes.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare policies across versions

LAYER_NAME="django-core"
OLD_VERSION=10
NEW_VERSION=11

echo "Comparing policies for $LAYER_NAME\n"

echo "=== Version $OLD_VERSION Policy ==="
aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $OLD_VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text 2>&1 | jq -r '.Statement[].Sid' || echo "No policy (private)"

echo "\n=== Version $NEW_VERSION Policy ==="
aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
    --layer-name "$LAYER_NAME" \
    --version-number $NEW_VERSION \
    --query 'Policy' \
    --output text 2>&1 | jq -r '.Statement[].Sid' || echo "No policy (private)"

echo "\n✓ Policy comparison complete"
Comparing policies for django-core === Version 10 Policy === partner-stack-django dev-team-access === Version 11 Policy === partner-stack-django dev-team-access staging-environment ✓ Policy comparison complete
# Track policy evolution across versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost policy_v10 = ['partner-stack-django', 'dev-team-access'] policy_v11 = ['partner-stack-django', 'dev-team-access', 'staging-environment'] added_statements = list(set(policy_v11) - set(policy_v10)) removed_statements = list(set(policy_v10) - set(policy_v11)) print(f"Policy Evolution: django-core v{OLD_VERSION} → v{NEW_VERSION}") print(f"\nVersion {OLD_VERSION} statements: {len(policy_v10)}") for stmt in policy_v10: print(f" - {stmt}") print(f"\nVersion {NEW_VERSION} statements: {len(policy_v11)}") for stmt in policy_v11: print(f" - {stmt}") if added_statements: print(f"\nAdded: {', '.join(added_statements)}") if removed_statements: print(f"Removed: {', '.join(removed_statements)}") # Track Django projects using this layer django_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(title__icontains='django').count() print(f"\nDjango projects affected: {django_posts}")
Example 5: Export Layer Policy for Documentation

Extract and format layer policy for security documentation and compliance reports.

#!/bin/bash
# Export layer policies to documentation

OUTPUT_FILE="layer-policies-report.txt"

echo "Layer Access Policies Report" > "$OUTPUT_FILE"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
echo "================================\n" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"

# List of layers to document
LAYERS=("django-dependencies:5" "pillow-image-processor:8" "common-utilities:7")

for layer_spec in "${LAYERS[@]}"; do
    IFS=':' read -r layer_name version <<< "$layer_spec"
    
    echo "\n--- $layer_name version $version ---" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
    
    POLICY=$(aws lambda get-layer-version-policy \
        --layer-name "$layer_name" \
        --version-number "$version" \
        --query 'Policy' \
        --output text 2>&1)
    
    if echo "$POLICY" | grep -q "ResourceNotFoundException"; then
        echo "Status: Private (no external access)" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
    else
        echo "Status: Shared" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
        echo "Statements:" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
        echo "$POLICY" | jq -r '.Statement[] | "  - \(.Sid): \(.Principal.AWS)"' >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
    fi
done

echo "\n✓ Report saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
cat "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Layer Access Policies Report Generated: Mon Jan 15 15:30:45 UTC 2024 ================================ --- django-dependencies version 5 --- Status: Shared Statements: - share-with-dev-account: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:root - share-with-partner: arn:aws:iam::555666777888:root --- pillow-image-processor version 8 --- Status: Shared Statements: - partner-account-access: arn:aws:iam::555666777888:root --- common-utilities version 7 --- Status: Shared Statements: - testing-environment-access: arn:aws:iam::111222333444:root - org-wide-access: * ✓ Report saved to layer-policies-report.txt
# Generate compliance report in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone import json layer_report = [ { 'name': 'django-dependencies', 'version': 5, 'status': 'Shared', 'statements': ['share-with-dev-account', 'share-with-partner'], 'accounts': 2 }, { 'name': 'pillow-image-processor', 'version': 8, 'status': 'Shared', 'statements': ['partner-account-access'], 'accounts': 1 }, { 'name': 'common-utilities', 'version': 7, 'status': 'Shared', 'statements': ['testing-environment-access', 'org-wide-access'], 'accounts': 2 } ] print(f"Lambda Layer Policy Compliance Report") print(f"Generated: {timezone.now()}") print(f"\nTotal layers audited: {len(layer_report)}\n") total_shared_accounts = 0 for layer in layer_report: print(f"Layer: {layer['name']} v{layer['version']}") print(f" Status: {layer['status']}") print(f" Accounts: {layer['accounts']}") print(f" Statements: {len(layer['statements'])}") total_shared_accounts += layer['accounts'] print(f"\nTotal external account shares: {total_shared_accounts}") # Link to job post system users = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"Active users in system: {users}")

get-account-settings

Returns information about your Lambda account settings including concurrent execution limits and storage usage. Essential for capacity planning and monitoring resource utilization in Django serverless deployments.

Syntax
aws lambda get-account-settings
Parameters Description
  • No parameters required: This command retrieves account-wide settings and doesn't require any input parameters.
Return Value
  • AccountLimit.TotalCodeSize (long): Maximum total storage in bytes for all function code (default 75GB).
  • AccountLimit.CodeSizeUnzipped (long): Maximum unzipped deployment package size (250MB).
  • AccountLimit.CodeSizeZipped (long): Maximum zipped deployment package size (50MB).
  • AccountLimit.ConcurrentExecutions (integer): Maximum concurrent executions across all functions.
  • AccountLimit.UnreservedConcurrentExecutions (integer): Available concurrency after reserved allocations.
  • AccountUsage.TotalCodeSize (long): Current total storage used by all functions.
  • AccountUsage.FunctionCount (integer): Total number of Lambda functions in the account.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Check Account Limits for Django Deployment

Retrieve account settings to verify you have capacity for deploying new Django Lambda functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Check Lambda account capacity

echo "Checking Lambda account settings..."

aws lambda get-account-settings \
    --region us-east-1 \
    --output json
{ "AccountLimit": { "TotalCodeSize": 80530636800, "CodeSizeUnzipped": 262144000, "CodeSizeZipped": 52428800, "ConcurrentExecutions": 1000, "UnreservedConcurrentExecutions": 850 }, "AccountUsage": { "TotalCodeSize": 1234567890, "FunctionCount": 45 } }
# Track Lambda capacity in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost account_limit = { 'total_code_gb': 80530636800 / (1024**3), 'concurrent_executions': 1000, 'unreserved': 850 } account_usage = { 'used_gb': 1234567890 / (1024**3), 'function_count': 45 } print(f"Lambda Account Capacity Report") print(f"\nLimits:") print(f" Total code storage: {account_limit['total_code_gb']:.2f} GB") print(f" Max concurrent: {account_limit['concurrent_executions']}") print(f" Unreserved: {account_limit['unreserved']}") print(f"\nUsage:") print(f" Storage used: {account_usage['used_gb']:.2f} GB") print(f" Functions: {account_usage['function_count']}") # Compare with job posts job_count = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJob posts in system: {job_count}")
Example 2: Monitor Storage Utilization

Calculate storage utilization percentage and alert if approaching limits.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor storage utilization

SETTINGS=$(aws lambda get-account-settings --output json)

TOTAL=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.TotalCodeSize')
USED=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountUsage.TotalCodeSize')

UTILIZATION=$(echo "scale=2; $USED * 100 / $TOTAL" | bc)

echo "Storage Utilization: $UTILIZATION%"
echo "Used: $(echo "scale=2; $USED / 1073741824" | bc) GB"
echo "Total: $(echo "scale=2; $TOTAL / 1073741824" | bc) GB"

if (( $(echo "$UTILIZATION > 80" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "⚠️ WARNING: Storage utilization above 80%"
else
    echo "✓ Storage utilization healthy"
fi
Storage Utilization: 1.53% Used: 1.15 GB Total: 75.00 GB ✓ Storage utilization healthy
# Storage monitoring in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Sum total_storage = 80530636800 used_storage = 1234567890 utilization = (used_storage / total_storage) * 100 print(f"Lambda Storage Monitor") print(f"\nUtilization: {utilization:.2f}%") print(f"Used: {used_storage / (1024**3):.2f} GB") print(f"Available: {(total_storage - used_storage) / (1024**3):.2f} GB") if utilization > 80: print(f"\n⚠️ WARNING: High utilization!") else: print(f"\n✓ Status: Healthy") # Track storage-related job skills storage_skills = Skills.objects.filter(name__icontains='storage').count() print(f"\nStorage-related skills: {storage_skills}")
Example 3: Check Concurrency Before Deployment

Verify sufficient concurrency is available before deploying high-traffic Django functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Check concurrency capacity

REQUIRED_CONCURRENCY=100

SETTINGS=$(aws lambda get-account-settings --output json)

UNRESERVED=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.UnreservedConcurrentExecutions')
TOTAL_CONCURRENT=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.ConcurrentExecutions')

echo "Concurrency Check for Django Deployment"
echo "========================================"
echo "Required: $REQUIRED_CONCURRENCY"
echo "Available (unreserved): $UNRESERVED"
echo "Total limit: $TOTAL_CONCURRENT"

if [ "$UNRESERVED" -ge "$REQUIRED_CONCURRENCY" ]; then
    echo "✓ Sufficient concurrency available"
    REMAINING=$((UNRESERVED - REQUIRED_CONCURRENCY))
    echo "After deployment: $REMAINING unreserved remaining"
else
    echo "✗ Insufficient concurrency!"
    echo "Need to release $(($REQUIRED_CONCURRENCY - $UNRESERVED)) reserved concurrency"
fi
Concurrency Check for Django Deployment ======================================== Required: 100 Available (unreserved): 850 Total limit: 1000 ✓ Sufficient concurrency available After deployment: 750 unreserved remaining
# Concurrency planning in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count required = 100 available = 850 total_limit = 1000 print(f"Concurrency Capacity Planning") print(f"\nRequired for new function: {required}") print(f"Currently available: {available}") print(f"Total account limit: {total_limit}") if available >= required: remaining = available - required print(f"\n✓ Deployment can proceed") print(f"Remaining after deployment: {remaining}") else: shortage = required - available print(f"\n✗ Cannot deploy - need {shortage} more") # Estimate based on job post traffic active_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive job posts: {active_posts}") print(f"Estimated concurrent users: {active_posts * 10}")
Example 4: Generate Capacity Report

Create a comprehensive capacity report for Django Lambda infrastructure.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate Lambda capacity report

REPORT_FILE="lambda-capacity-report.txt"

echo "Lambda Capacity Report" > "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "========================" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

SETTINGS=$(aws lambda get-account-settings --output json)

# Storage metrics
echo "\nSTORAGE METRICS:" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
TOTAL_SIZE=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.TotalCodeSize')
USED_SIZE=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountUsage.TotalCodeSize')
echo "  Limit: $(echo "scale=2; $TOTAL_SIZE/1073741824" | bc) GB" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "  Used: $(echo "scale=2; $USED_SIZE/1073741824" | bc) GB" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "  Available: $(echo "scale=2; ($TOTAL_SIZE-$USED_SIZE)/1073741824" | bc) GB" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

# Concurrency metrics
echo "\nCONCURRENCY METRICS:" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "  Total limit: $(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.ConcurrentExecutions')" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "  Unreserved: $(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.UnreservedConcurrentExecutions')" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

# Function count
echo "\nFUNCTION METRICS:" >> "$REPORT_FILE"
echo "  Total functions: $(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountUsage.FunctionCount')" >> "$REPORT_FILE"

cat "$REPORT_FILE"
Lambda Capacity Report Generated: Mon Jan 15 16:00:00 UTC 2024 ======================== STORAGE METRICS: Limit: 75.00 GB Used: 1.15 GB Available: 73.85 GB CONCURRENCY METRICS: Total limit: 1000 Unreserved: 850 FUNCTION METRICS: Total functions: 45
# Generate Django capacity report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone report = { 'storage_limit_gb': 75.0, 'storage_used_gb': 1.15, 'concurrent_limit': 1000, 'unreserved': 850, 'function_count': 45 } print(f"Lambda Infrastructure Report") print(f"Generated: {timezone.now()}") print(f"\n{'='*40}") print(f"\nSTORAGE:") print(f" Limit: {report['storage_limit_gb']} GB") print(f" Used: {report['storage_used_gb']} GB") print(f" Free: {report['storage_limit_gb'] - report['storage_used_gb']:.2f} GB") print(f"\nCONCURRENCY:") print(f" Limit: {report['concurrent_limit']}") print(f" Unreserved: {report['unreserved']}") print(f" Reserved: {report['concurrent_limit'] - report['unreserved']}") print(f"\nFUNCTIONS: {report['function_count']}") # Django metrics print(f"\nDJANGO SYSTEM:") print(f" Users: {User.objects.count()}") print(f" Job Posts: {JobPost.objects.count()}")
Example 5: Multi-Region Capacity Check

Check Lambda capacity across multiple AWS regions for global Django deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check capacity across regions

REGIONS=("us-east-1" "us-west-2" "eu-west-1" "ap-southeast-1")

echo "Multi-Region Lambda Capacity Check"
echo "===================================\n"

for region in "${REGIONS[@]}"; do
    echo "Region: $region"
    
    SETTINGS=$(aws lambda get-account-settings --region "$region" --output json 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        FUNCTIONS=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountUsage.FunctionCount')
        UNRESERVED=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountLimit.UnreservedConcurrentExecutions')
        USED_GB=$(echo "$SETTINGS" | jq '.AccountUsage.TotalCodeSize / 1073741824')
        
        echo "  Functions: $FUNCTIONS"
        echo "  Unreserved concurrency: $UNRESERVED"
        echo "  Storage used: ${USED_GB} GB"
    else
        echo "  Unable to retrieve settings"
    fi
    echo ""
done
Multi-Region Lambda Capacity Check =================================== Region: us-east-1 Functions: 45 Unreserved concurrency: 850 Storage used: 1.15 GB Region: us-west-2 Functions: 12 Unreserved concurrency: 950 Storage used: 0.45 GB Region: eu-west-1 Functions: 8 Unreserved concurrency: 980 Storage used: 0.32 GB Region: ap-southeast-1 Functions: 5 Unreserved concurrency: 990 Storage used: 0.18 GB
# Multi-region tracking in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count regions = [ {'name': 'us-east-1', 'functions': 45, 'unreserved': 850, 'storage_gb': 1.15}, {'name': 'us-west-2', 'functions': 12, 'unreserved': 950, 'storage_gb': 0.45}, {'name': 'eu-west-1', 'functions': 8, 'unreserved': 980, 'storage_gb': 0.32}, {'name': 'ap-southeast-1', 'functions': 5, 'unreserved': 990, 'storage_gb': 0.18} ] print(f"Global Lambda Capacity Summary") print(f"{'='*50}\n") total_functions = 0 total_storage = 0 for region in regions: print(f"{region['name']}:") print(f" Functions: {region['functions']}") print(f" Capacity: {region['unreserved']}/1000") total_functions += region['functions'] total_storage += region['storage_gb'] print(f"\nGLOBAL TOTALS:") print(f" Total functions: {total_functions}") print(f" Total storage: {total_storage:.2f} GB") # Map to job locations locations = Location.objects.all() print(f"\nJob post locations: {locations.count()}")

get-alias

Returns details about a Lambda function alias including the version it points to and routing configuration. Aliases enable traffic shifting and canary deployments for Django Lambda functions.

Syntax
aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --name <alias-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name of the Lambda function, version, or ARN. Can be function name, partial ARN, or full ARN.
  • --name (string, required): Name of the alias to retrieve (e.g., 'prod', 'staging', 'dev').
Return Value
  • AliasArn (string): Full ARN of the alias including function name and alias suffix.
  • Name (string): Name of the alias.
  • FunctionVersion (string): Version number the alias points to, or '$LATEST'.
  • Description (string): Description of the alias purpose.
  • RoutingConfig (object): Traffic routing configuration for canary deployments with version weights.
  • RevisionId (string): Unique identifier for the alias configuration revision.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Production Alias for Django API

Retrieve the production alias configuration for the JobPost API function.

#!/bin/bash
# Get production alias details

aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --name prod \
    --region us-east-1
{ "AliasArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api:prod", "Name": "prod", "FunctionVersion": "5", "Description": "Production alias for JobPost API - Django 4.2", "RoutingConfig": { "AdditionalVersionWeights": {} }, "RevisionId": "a1b2c3d4-5678-90ab-cdef-example11111" }
# Track production deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost alias_info = { 'name': 'prod', 'function': 'jobpost-api', 'version': 5, 'description': 'Production alias for JobPost API - Django 4.2' } print(f"Production Alias Status") print(f"\nFunction: {alias_info['function']}") print(f"Alias: {alias_info['name']}") print(f"Version: v{alias_info['version']}") print(f"Description: {alias_info['description']}") # Track production users active_users = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive users served: {active_users}") # Production job posts prod_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"Active job posts: {prod_posts}")
Example 2: Check Canary Deployment Configuration

View traffic routing configuration during a canary deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check canary deployment status

FUNCTION="jobpost-processor"
ALIAS="prod"

echo "Checking canary deployment for $FUNCTION:$ALIAS"

ALIAS_INFO=$(aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --name "$ALIAS" \
    --output json)

echo "$ALIAS_INFO" | jq '{
    alias: .Name,
    mainVersion: .FunctionVersion,
    routing: .RoutingConfig
}'

# Check if canary is active
CANARY=$(echo "$ALIAS_INFO" | jq '.RoutingConfig.AdditionalVersionWeights | length')
if [ "$CANARY" -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "\n⚠️ Canary deployment ACTIVE"
    echo "Traffic split:"
    MAIN_VERSION=$(echo "$ALIAS_INFO" | jq -r '.FunctionVersion')
    echo "$ALIAS_INFO" | jq -r '.RoutingConfig.AdditionalVersionWeights | to_entries[] | "  v\(.key): \(.value * 100)%"'
else
    echo "\n✓ No canary - 100% to main version"
fi
Checking canary deployment for jobpost-processor:prod { "alias": "prod", "mainVersion": "8", "routing": { "AdditionalVersionWeights": { "9": 0.1 } } } ⚠️ Canary deployment ACTIVE Traffic split: v9: 10%
# Monitor canary deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost canary_config = { 'function': 'jobpost-processor', 'alias': 'prod', 'main_version': 8, 'canary_version': 9, 'canary_weight': 0.1 } print(f"Canary Deployment Status") print(f"\nFunction: {canary_config['function']}") print(f"Alias: {canary_config['alias']}") print(f"\nTraffic Distribution:") print(f" v{canary_config['main_version']}: {(1 - canary_config['canary_weight']) * 100:.0f}%") print(f" v{canary_config['canary_version']}: {canary_config['canary_weight'] * 100:.0f}% (canary)") # Estimate traffic total_posts = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nEstimated request distribution:") print(f" Main version: ~{int(total_posts * 0.9)} requests") print(f" Canary: ~{int(total_posts * 0.1)} requests")
Example 3: Compare Staging vs Production Aliases

Compare alias configurations between staging and production environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare staging and production aliases

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"

echo "Comparing aliases for $FUNCTION\n"

echo "=== STAGING ==="
aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --name staging \
    --query '{Version:FunctionVersion,Description:Description}' \
    --output table 2>/dev/null || echo "Staging alias not found"

echo "\n=== PRODUCTION ==="
aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --name prod \
    --query '{Version:FunctionVersion,Description:Description}' \
    --output table 2>/dev/null || echo "Production alias not found"

# Get version difference
STAGING_V=$(aws lambda get-alias --function-name "$FUNCTION" --name staging --query 'FunctionVersion' --output text 2>/dev/null)
PROD_V=$(aws lambda get-alias --function-name "$FUNCTION" --name prod --query 'FunctionVersion' --output text 2>/dev/null)

if [ "$STAGING_V" != "$PROD_V" ]; then
    echo "\n⚠️ Version mismatch: staging=v$STAGING_V, prod=v$PROD_V"
else
    echo "\n✓ Both environments on same version: v$PROD_V"
fi
Comparing aliases for jobpost-api === STAGING === ----------------------------------- | GetAlias | +--------------+------------------+ | Description | Staging env | | Version | 6 | +--------------+------------------+ === PRODUCTION === ----------------------------------- | GetAlias | +--------------+------------------+ | Description | Production | | Version | 5 | +--------------+------------------+ ⚠️ Version mismatch: staging=v6, prod=v5
# Track environment versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost environments = { 'staging': {'version': 6, 'description': 'Staging env'}, 'production': {'version': 5, 'description': 'Production'} } print(f"Environment Version Comparison") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") for env, info in environments.items(): print(f"\n{env.upper()}:") print(f" Version: v{info['version']}") print(f" Description: {info['description']}") if environments['staging']['version'] != environments['production']['version']: diff = environments['staging']['version'] - environments['production']['version'] print(f"\n⚠️ Staging is {diff} version(s) ahead") print(f"Ready for production promotion") # Track job posts by status active = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive job posts: {active}")
Example 4: Get Alias for Specific Function Version ARN

Retrieve alias using the full function ARN format.

#!/bin/bash
# Get alias using full ARN

FUNCTION_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-notifications"
ALIAS_NAME="live"

echo "Getting alias '$ALIAS_NAME' for function..."

aws lambda get-alias \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION_ARN" \
    --name "$ALIAS_NAME" \
    --output yaml
Getting alias 'live' for function... AliasArn: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-notifications:live Description: Live notifications handler for job applications FunctionVersion: '3' Name: live RevisionId: x9y8z7w6-5432-10fe-dcba-revision98765 RoutingConfig: AdditionalVersionWeights: {}
# Track notification function alias from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count alias = { 'name': 'live', 'function': 'jobpost-notifications', 'version': 3, 'description': 'Live notifications handler for job applications' } print(f"Alias Configuration") print(f"\nFunction: {alias['function']}") print(f"Alias: {alias['name']}") print(f"Points to: v{alias['version']}") print(f"Purpose: {alias['description']}") # Count notifications potential authors = Author.objects.annotate( post_count=Count('jobpost') ).filter(post_count__gt=0) print(f"\nAuthors with posts: {authors.count()}") print(f"These authors receive notifications via this function")
Example 5: Monitor All Aliases for a Function

Retrieve and compare all aliases for a Django Lambda function.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all aliases for a function

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"
ALIASES=("dev" "staging" "prod")

echo "Alias Report for $FUNCTION"
echo "===========================\n"

for alias in "${ALIASES[@]}"; do
    INFO=$(aws lambda get-alias \
        --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
        --name "$alias" \
        --output json 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        VERSION=$(echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.FunctionVersion')
        HAS_CANARY=$(echo "$INFO" | jq '.RoutingConfig.AdditionalVersionWeights | length')
        
        echo "$alias:"
        echo "  Version: v$VERSION"
        
        if [ "$HAS_CANARY" -gt 0 ]; then
            echo "  Canary: Active"
            echo "$INFO" | jq -r '.RoutingConfig.AdditionalVersionWeights | to_entries[] | "    v\(.key): \(.value*100)%"'
        else
            echo "  Canary: None"
        fi
        echo ""
    else
        echo "$alias: Not configured\n"
    fi
done
Alias Report for jobpost-api =========================== dev: Version: v$LATEST Canary: None staging: Version: v6 Canary: None prod: Version: v5 Canary: Active v6: 5%
# Comprehensive alias tracking from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost aliases = [ {'name': 'dev', 'version': '$LATEST', 'canary': None}, {'name': 'staging', 'version': '6', 'canary': None}, {'name': 'prod', 'version': '5', 'canary': {'6': 0.05}} ] print(f"Function Alias Summary: jobpost-api") print(f"{'='*40}\n") for alias in aliases: print(f"{alias['name'].upper()}:") print(f" Version: v{alias['version']}") if alias['canary']: print(f" Canary: Active") for v, weight in alias['canary'].items(): print(f" v{v}: {weight*100:.0f}%") else: print(f" Canary: None") print() # Django deployment stats print(f"Django System Stats:") print(f" Total users: {User.objects.count()}") print(f" Total posts: {JobPost.objects.count()}") print(f" Active posts: {JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()}")

get-event-source-mapping

Returns details about an event source mapping that connects Lambda to event sources like SQS, Kinesis, or DynamoDB. Essential for monitoring queue-based Django job processing pipelines.

Syntax
aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid <mapping-uuid>
Parameters Description
  • --uuid (string, required): Unique identifier of the event source mapping. Obtained from create-event-source-mapping or list-event-source-mappings.
Return Value
  • UUID (string): Unique identifier for the event source mapping.
  • BatchSize (integer): Maximum number of records to send to the function per batch.
  • MaximumBatchingWindowInSeconds (integer): Maximum time to wait before invoking function.
  • EventSourceArn (string): ARN of the event source (SQS queue, Kinesis stream, etc.).
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the Lambda function receiving events.
  • State (string): Current state: Creating, Enabling, Enabled, Disabling, Disabled, Updating, Deleting.
  • StateTransitionReason (string): Reason for the last state change.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get SQS Event Source Mapping for Job Processing

Retrieve details about an SQS-triggered Lambda that processes new job post submissions.

#!/bin/bash
# Get job processing queue mapping

MAPPING_UUID="a1b2c3d4-5678-90ab-cdef-jobqueue12345"

aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid "$MAPPING_UUID" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "UUID": "a1b2c3d4-5678-90ab-cdef-jobqueue12345", "BatchSize": 10, "MaximumBatchingWindowInSeconds": 5, "EventSourceArn": "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:123456789012:jobpost-submission-queue", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:process-new-jobpost", "LastModified": "2024-01-15T10:30:00.000+0000", "State": "Enabled", "StateTransitionReason": "USER_INITIATED" }
# Track event source mapping for job processing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost mapping = { 'uuid': 'a1b2c3d4-5678-90ab-cdef-jobqueue12345', 'batch_size': 10, 'batching_window': 5, 'source': 'jobpost-submission-queue', 'function': 'process-new-jobpost', 'state': 'Enabled' } print(f"Event Source Mapping Status") print(f"\nUUID: {mapping['uuid']}") print(f"Source: {mapping['source']}") print(f"Function: {mapping['function']}") print(f"State: {mapping['state']}") print(f"\nBatch Config:") print(f" Size: {mapping['batch_size']} records") print(f" Window: {mapping['batching_window']} seconds") # Track pending job posts pending = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=False).count() print(f"\nPending job posts: {pending}")
Example 2: Check DynamoDB Stream Mapping Status

Monitor a DynamoDB Streams event source for job post change tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Check DynamoDB stream mapping

MAPPING_UUID="e5f6g7h8-9012-34ab-cdef-dynamo56789"

echo "Checking DynamoDB stream mapping status..."

MAPPING=$(aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid "$MAPPING_UUID" \
    --output json)

STATE=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.State')
STARTING_POS=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.StartingPosition // "N/A"')

echo "State: $STATE"
echo "Starting Position: $STARTING_POS"

if [ "$STATE" == "Enabled" ]; then
    echo "✓ Stream processing is active"
else
    echo "⚠️ Stream processing is $STATE"
fi
Checking DynamoDB stream mapping status... State: Enabled Starting Position: LATEST ✓ Stream processing is active
# Monitor DynamoDB stream mapping from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone stream_mapping = { 'uuid': 'e5f6g7h8-9012-34ab-cdef-dynamo56789', 'state': 'Enabled', 'starting_position': 'LATEST', 'source_type': 'DynamoDB Streams' } print(f"DynamoDB Stream Mapping") print(f"\nUUID: {stream_mapping['uuid']}") print(f"Type: {stream_mapping['source_type']}") print(f"State: {stream_mapping['state']}") print(f"Position: {stream_mapping['starting_position']}") if stream_mapping['state'] == 'Enabled': print(f"\n✓ Stream processing active") # Track recent changes recent_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timezone.timedelta(hours=1) ).count() print(f"\nPosts in last hour: {recent_posts}")
Example 3: Get Kinesis Stream Mapping with Error Handling Config

Retrieve Kinesis stream mapping with failure handling configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Get Kinesis mapping with error handling

MAPPING_UUID="k1n2e3s4-5678-90ab-cdef-kinesis9012"

aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid "$MAPPING_UUID" \
    --query '{
        UUID: UUID,
        State: State,
        BatchSize: BatchSize,
        ParallelizationFactor: ParallelizationFactor,
        MaximumRetryAttempts: MaximumRetryAttempts,
        MaximumRecordAgeInSeconds: MaximumRecordAgeInSeconds,
        BisectBatchOnFunctionError: BisectBatchOnFunctionError,
        OnFailure: DestinationConfig.OnFailure
    }' \
    --output table
--------------------------------------------------------------- | GetEventSourceMapping | +------------------------------+------------------------------+ | BatchSize | 100 | | BisectBatchOnFunctionError | True | | MaximumRecordAgeInSeconds | 86400 | | MaximumRetryAttempts | 3 | | ParallelizationFactor | 10 | | State | Enabled | | UUID | k1n2e3s4-5678-90ab-... | +------------------------------+------------------------------+
# Kinesis stream mapping analysis from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost kinesis_config = { 'batch_size': 100, 'parallelization': 10, 'max_retries': 3, 'max_age_seconds': 86400, 'bisect_on_error': True, 'state': 'Enabled' } print(f"Kinesis Stream Configuration") print(f"\nBatch Settings:") print(f" Size: {kinesis_config['batch_size']} records") print(f" Parallelization: {kinesis_config['parallelization']}x") print(f"\nError Handling:") print(f" Max retries: {kinesis_config['max_retries']}") print(f" Max age: {kinesis_config['max_age_seconds']/3600:.0f} hours") print(f" Bisect on error: {kinesis_config['bisect_on_error']}") print(f"\nState: {kinesis_config['state']}") # Track high-volume processing total_posts = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nTotal job posts processed: {total_posts}")
Example 4: Verify Filter Criteria on Mapping

Check event filtering configuration for selective processing.

#!/bin/bash
# Check event filter on mapping

MAPPING_UUID="f1l2t3r4-5678-90ab-cdef-filter12345"

echo "Checking filter criteria..."

MAPPING=$(aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid "$MAPPING_UUID" \
    --output json)

echo "Mapping details:"
echo "$MAPPING" | jq '{
    UUID: .UUID,
    State: .State,
    EventSource: .EventSourceArn | split(":") | last,
    FilterCriteria: .FilterCriteria
}'

# Check if filters exist
FILTERS=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq '.FilterCriteria.Filters // []')
if [ "$FILTERS" != "[]" ]; then
    echo "\nActive filters:"
    echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.FilterCriteria.Filters[].Pattern'
else
    echo "\nNo filters configured - processing all events"
fi
Checking filter criteria... Mapping details: { "UUID": "f1l2t3r4-5678-90ab-cdef-filter12345", "State": "Enabled", "EventSource": "priority-jobs-queue", "FilterCriteria": { "Filters": [ { "Pattern": "{\"body\": {\"priority\": [\"high\", \"urgent\"]}}" } ] } } Active filters: {"body": {"priority": ["high", "urgent"]}}
# Track filtered event processing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Q filter_config = { 'source': 'priority-jobs-queue', 'filter_pattern': {'body': {'priority': ['high', 'urgent']}}, 'state': 'Enabled' } print(f"Event Filter Configuration") print(f"\nSource: {filter_config['source']}") print(f"State: {filter_config['state']}") print(f"\nFilter Pattern:") print(f" Only processing: priority = high or urgent") # Find matching job posts (simulated priority) high_priority = JobPost.objects.filter( Q(title__icontains='urgent') | Q(title__icontains='senior') ).count() print(f"\nHigh-priority job posts: {high_priority}") print(f"These would be processed by filtered mapping")
Example 5: Monitor Mapping Health and Metrics

Comprehensive health check for event source mapping.

#!/bin/bash
# Comprehensive mapping health check

MAPPING_UUID="h3a1l2t3-5678-90ab-cdef-health12345"

echo "Event Source Mapping Health Check"
echo "==================================\n"

MAPPING=$(aws lambda get-event-source-mapping \
    --uuid "$MAPPING_UUID" \
    --output json)

# Basic status
STATE=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.State')
REASON=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.StateTransitionReason')
LAST_MOD=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.LastModified')

echo "Status: $STATE"
echo "Reason: $REASON"
echo "Last Modified: $LAST_MOD"

# Check for issues
if [ "$STATE" == "Enabled" ]; then
    echo "\n✓ Mapping is healthy and processing events"
elif [ "$STATE" == "Disabled" ]; then
    echo "\n⚠️ Mapping is disabled - no events being processed"
else
    echo "\n⚠️ Mapping is in transition: $STATE"
fi

# Function response metrics hint
FUNC=$(echo "$MAPPING" | jq -r '.FunctionArn | split(":") | .[6]')
echo "\nFunction: $FUNC"
echo "Check CloudWatch for processing metrics"
Event Source Mapping Health Check ================================== Status: Enabled Reason: USER_INITIATED Last Modified: 2024-01-15T14:22:00.000+0000 ✓ Mapping is healthy and processing events Function: process-jobpost-applications Check CloudWatch for processing metrics
# Health monitoring in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone mapping_health = { 'uuid': 'h3a1l2t3-5678-90ab-cdef-health12345', 'state': 'Enabled', 'reason': 'USER_INITIATED', 'function': 'process-jobpost-applications', 'last_modified': timezone.now() } print(f"Mapping Health Report") print(f"\nUUID: {mapping_health['uuid']}") print(f"State: {mapping_health['state']}") print(f"Reason: {mapping_health['reason']}") print(f"Function: {mapping_health['function']}") print(f"Last Modified: {mapping_health['last_modified']}") if mapping_health['state'] == 'Enabled': print(f"\n✓ Status: Healthy") else: print(f"\n⚠️ Status: Needs attention") # Application processing stats applications = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive job posts being processed: {applications}")

list-function-url-configs

Returns a list of Lambda function URL configurations for a specified function. Function URLs provide dedicated HTTPS endpoints for invoking Lambda functions, useful for exposing Django APIs.

Syntax
aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    [--max-items <number>] \
    [--marker <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name of the Lambda function, version, or ARN to list URL configs for.
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of results to return (1-50).
  • --marker (string, optional): Pagination token from previous response for retrieving more results.
Return Value
  • FunctionUrlConfigs (list): Array of function URL configuration objects.
  • FunctionUrl (string): The HTTPS endpoint URL for invoking the function.
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function or alias with the URL config.
  • AuthType (string): Authentication type - AWS_IAM or NONE.
  • Cors (object): Cross-origin resource sharing settings.
  • CreationTime (string): When the URL config was created.
  • NextMarker (string): Pagination token for next page of results.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List URL Configs for Django API Function

Retrieve all function URL configurations for the JobPost API.

#!/bin/bash
# List function URLs for Django API

aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrlConfigs": [ { "FunctionUrl": "https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "CreationTime": "2024-01-10T08:00:00.000+0000", "LastModifiedTime": "2024-01-15T14:30:00.000+0000", "AuthType": "AWS_IAM", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["https://jobpost.example.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization"], "MaxAge": 86400 }, "InvokeMode": "BUFFERED" } ] }
# Track function URLs for Django API from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost url_config = { 'url': 'https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/', 'auth_type': 'AWS_IAM', 'cors_origins': ['https://jobpost.example.com'], 'methods': ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'] } print(f"Function URL Configuration") print(f"\nEndpoint: {url_config['url']}") print(f"Auth: {url_config['auth_type']}") print(f"\nCORS Settings:") print(f" Origins: {', '.join(url_config['cors_origins'])}") print(f" Methods: {', '.join(url_config['methods'])}") # Track API usage active_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive job posts served: {active_posts}")
Example 2: List URLs for Function with Aliases

List URL configs across different aliases (prod, staging).

#!/bin/bash
# List URLs for all aliases

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"
ALIASES=("" "prod" "staging")

echo "Function URL Configs for $FUNCTION"
echo "====================================\n"

for alias in "${ALIASES[@]}"; do
    if [ -z "$alias" ]; then
        QUALIFIER="$FUNCTION"
        LABEL="Base function"
    else
        QUALIFIER="$FUNCTION:$alias"
        LABEL="Alias: $alias"
    fi
    
    echo "$LABEL"
    URL=$(aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
        --function-name "$QUALIFIER" \
        --query 'FunctionUrlConfigs[0].FunctionUrl' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$URL" != "None" ] && [ -n "$URL" ]; then
        echo "  URL: $URL"
    else
        echo "  No URL configured"
    fi
    echo ""
done
Function URL Configs for jobpost-api ==================================== Base function URL: https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/ Alias: prod URL: https://prod789abc123.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/ Alias: staging URL: https://stg456def789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/
# Track URLs across environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost environment_urls = [ {'env': 'base', 'url': 'https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/'}, {'env': 'prod', 'url': 'https://prod789abc123.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/'}, {'env': 'staging', 'url': 'https://stg456def789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/'} ] print(f"Environment URLs for jobpost-api") print(f"\n{'='*60}") for env in environment_urls: print(f"\n{env['env'].upper()}:") print(f" {env['url']}") print(f"\nTotal environments: {len(environment_urls)}") # Usage stats users = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"Active users across all environments: {users}")
Example 3: Check CORS Configuration Details

Extract and validate CORS settings for security audit.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit CORS configuration

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"

echo "CORS Security Audit for $FUNCTION"
echo "==================================\n"

CONFIG=$(aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --query 'FunctionUrlConfigs[0]' \
    --output json)

AUTH=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.AuthType')
ORIGINS=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.Cors.AllowOrigins // ["Not configured"]')
METHODS=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.Cors.AllowMethods // ["Not configured"]')
CREDS=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.Cors.AllowCredentials // false')

echo "Authentication: $AUTH"
echo "\nCORS Configuration:"
echo "  Allow Origins: $ORIGINS"
echo "  Allow Methods: $METHODS"
echo "  Allow Credentials: $CREDS"

# Security check
if echo "$ORIGINS" | grep -q '\*'; then
    echo "\n⚠️ WARNING: Wildcard origin detected!"
else
    echo "\n✓ Origins are properly restricted"
fi
CORS Security Audit for jobpost-api ================================== Authentication: AWS_IAM CORS Configuration: Allow Origins: ["https://jobpost.example.com","https://admin.example.com"] Allow Methods: ["GET","POST","PUT","DELETE"] Allow Credentials: true ✓ Origins are properly restricted
# CORS audit tracking from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost cors_config = { 'auth_type': 'AWS_IAM', 'origins': ['https://jobpost.example.com', 'https://admin.example.com'], 'methods': ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'], 'credentials': True, 'wildcard': False } print(f"CORS Security Audit Report") print(f"\nAuthentication: {cors_config['auth_type']}") print(f"\nAllowed Origins ({len(cors_config['origins'])})") for origin in cors_config['origins']: print(f" - {origin}") print(f"\nAllowed Methods: {', '.join(cors_config['methods'])}") print(f"Credentials: {cors_config['credentials']}") if not cors_config['wildcard']: print(f"\n✓ Security Status: Good") else: print(f"\n⚠️ Security Status: Review needed") # Track admin users admins = User.objects.filter(is_staff=True).count() print(f"\nAdmin users: {admins}")
Example 4: Export URL Configs to Documentation

Generate documentation of all function URLs for API docs.

#!/bin/bash
# Export URL configs for documentation

FUNCTIONS=("jobpost-api" "jobpost-processor" "jobpost-notifications")
OUTPUT="api-endpoints.md"

echo "# Lambda Function URLs" > "$OUTPUT"
echo "Generated: $(date)" >> "$OUTPUT"
echo "" >> "$OUTPUT"

for func in "${FUNCTIONS[@]}"; do
    echo "## $func" >> "$OUTPUT"
    
    CONFIG=$(aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'FunctionUrlConfigs[0]' \
        --output json 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$CONFIG" != "null" ] && [ -n "$CONFIG" ]; then
        URL=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.FunctionUrl')
        AUTH=$(echo "$CONFIG" | jq -r '.AuthType')
        
        echo "- **URL**: $URL" >> "$OUTPUT"
        echo "- **Auth**: $AUTH" >> "$OUTPUT"
    else
        echo "- No URL configured" >> "$OUTPUT"
    fi
    echo "" >> "$OUTPUT"
done

echo "✓ Documentation saved to $OUTPUT"
cat "$OUTPUT"
✓ Documentation saved to api-endpoints.md # Lambda Function URLs Generated: Mon Jan 15 16:00:00 UTC 2024 ## jobpost-api - **URL**: https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/ - **Auth**: AWS_IAM ## jobpost-processor - **URL**: https://proc456def123.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/ - **Auth**: NONE ## jobpost-notifications - No URL configured
# Generate API documentation in Django from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone api_endpoints = [ {'name': 'jobpost-api', 'url': 'https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/', 'auth': 'AWS_IAM'}, {'name': 'jobpost-processor', 'url': 'https://proc456def123.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/', 'auth': 'NONE'}, {'name': 'jobpost-notifications', 'url': None, 'auth': None} ] print(f"API Endpoint Documentation") print(f"Generated: {timezone.now()}") print(f"\n{'='*60}") for endpoint in api_endpoints: print(f"\n{endpoint['name']}:") if endpoint['url']: print(f" URL: {endpoint['url']}") print(f" Auth: {endpoint['auth']}") else: print(f" No URL configured") configured = len([e for e in api_endpoints if e['url']]) print(f"\nTotal endpoints: {len(api_endpoints)}") print(f"Configured: {configured}")
Example 5: Monitor URL Creation Times

Track when function URLs were created and modified.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor URL config timestamps

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"

echo "URL Config Timeline for $FUNCTION"
echo "==================================\n"

aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --query 'FunctionUrlConfigs[].{
        Qualifier: FunctionArn | split(":") | .[-1],
        Created: CreationTime,
        Modified: LastModifiedTime,
        Auth: AuthType
    }' \
    --output table

# Calculate age
CREATED=$(aws lambda list-function-url-configs \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --query 'FunctionUrlConfigs[0].CreationTime' \
    --output text)

if [ "$CREATED" != "None" ]; then
    CREATED_TS=$(date -d "$CREATED" +%s 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
    NOW_TS=$(date +%s)
    AGE_DAYS=$(( (NOW_TS - CREATED_TS) / 86400 ))
    echo "\nURL age: $AGE_DAYS days"
fi
URL Config Timeline for jobpost-api ================================== ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | ListFunctionUrlConfigs | +------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+ | Auth | Created | Modified | +------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+ | AWS_IAM | 2024-01-10T08:00:00.000Z | 2024-01-15T14:30:00.000Z | +------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+ URL age: 5 days
# Track URL config lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta url_timeline = { 'function': 'jobpost-api', 'created': timezone.now() - timedelta(days=5), 'modified': timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=2), 'auth': 'AWS_IAM' } age_days = (timezone.now() - url_timeline['created']).days print(f"URL Config Timeline") print(f"\nFunction: {url_timeline['function']}") print(f"Created: {url_timeline['created']}") print(f"Modified: {url_timeline['modified']}") print(f"Auth Type: {url_timeline['auth']}") print(f"\nURL Age: {age_days} days") # Track recent activity recent_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=url_timeline['created'] ).count() print(f"\nPosts created since URL setup: {recent_posts}")

update-function-url-config

Updates the configuration for a Lambda function URL including authentication type and CORS settings. Essential for managing access control and cross-origin requests for Django API endpoints.

Syntax
aws lambda update-function-url-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    [--qualifier <alias-or-version>] \
    [--auth-type <AWS_IAM|NONE>] \
    [--cors <cors-config>] \
    [--invoke-mode <BUFFERED|RESPONSE_STREAM>]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name of the Lambda function, version, or ARN.
  • --qualifier (string, optional): Alias name or version number to update URL config for.
  • --auth-type (string, optional): Authentication type - AWS_IAM (requires signed requests) or NONE (public access).
  • --cors (structure, optional): CORS configuration with AllowOrigins, AllowMethods, AllowHeaders, ExposeHeaders, MaxAge, AllowCredentials.
  • --invoke-mode (string, optional): BUFFERED (default) or RESPONSE_STREAM for streaming responses.
Return Value
  • FunctionUrl (string): The HTTPS endpoint URL for the function.
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function with the URL config.
  • AuthType (string): Updated authentication type.
  • Cors (object): Updated CORS configuration settings.
  • InvokeMode (string): Response mode (BUFFERED or RESPONSE_STREAM).
  • LastModifiedTime (string): Timestamp of the update.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update CORS for Django Frontend

Configure CORS to allow requests from your Django frontend domain.

#!/bin/bash
# Update CORS for Django frontend

aws lambda update-function-url-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --cors '{
        "AllowOrigins": ["https://jobpost.example.com", "https://admin.jobpost.com"],
        "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"],
        "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization", "X-CSRF-Token"],
        "MaxAge": 86400,
        "AllowCredentials": true
    }' \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://abc123xyz789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "AuthType": "AWS_IAM", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["https://jobpost.example.com", "https://admin.jobpost.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization", "X-CSRF-Token"], "MaxAge": 86400, "AllowCredentials": true }, "CreationTime": "2024-01-10T08:00:00.000+0000", "LastModifiedTime": "2024-01-15T16:30:00.000+0000", "InvokeMode": "BUFFERED" }
# Track CORS configuration update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.conf import settings cors_update = { 'origins': ['https://jobpost.example.com', 'https://admin.jobpost.com'], 'methods': ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE', 'OPTIONS'], 'max_age': 86400, 'credentials': True } print(f"CORS Configuration Updated") print(f"\nAllowed Origins:") for origin in cors_update['origins']: print(f" - {origin}") print(f"\nAllowed Methods: {', '.join(cors_update['methods'])}") print(f"Cache Max Age: {cors_update['max_age']} seconds") print(f"Credentials: {cors_update['credentials']}") # Track frontend users active_users = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nActive users: {active_users}")
Example 2: Switch to Public Access

Change authentication to NONE for public API access.

#!/bin/bash
# Make function URL public

aws lambda update-function-url-config \
    --function-name jobpost-public-api \
    --auth-type NONE \
    --cors '{
        "AllowOrigins": ["*"],
        "AllowMethods": ["GET"],
        "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type"]
    }' \
    --region us-east-1

echo "\n⚠️ Function URL is now publicly accessible"
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://pub456def789.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-public-api", "AuthType": "NONE", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["*"], "AllowMethods": ["GET"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type"] }, "LastModifiedTime": "2024-01-15T16:45:00.000+0000", "InvokeMode": "BUFFERED" } ⚠️ Function URL is now publicly accessible
# Log public access configuration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost public_config = { 'function': 'jobpost-public-api', 'auth': 'NONE', 'wildcard_cors': True, 'methods': ['GET'] } print(f"Public API Configuration") print(f"\nFunction: {public_config['function']}") print(f"Auth Type: {public_config['auth']}") print(f"CORS Origins: * (wildcard)") print(f"Methods: {', '.join(public_config['methods'])}") print(f"\n⚠️ Security Notice:") print(f" - This endpoint is publicly accessible") print(f" - Only GET requests allowed") print(f" - Consider rate limiting") # Public job posts public_posts = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f"\nPublic job posts: {public_posts}")
Example 3: Enable Response Streaming

Configure streaming responses for large data transfers.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable response streaming for large exports

aws lambda update-function-url-config \
    --function-name jobpost-export \
    --invoke-mode RESPONSE_STREAM \
    --cors '{
        "AllowOrigins": ["https://admin.jobpost.com"],
        "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST"],
        "ExposeHeaders": ["Content-Disposition", "X-Export-Status"]
    }' \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://export789ghi123.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-export", "AuthType": "AWS_IAM", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["https://admin.jobpost.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST"], "ExposeHeaders": ["Content-Disposition", "X-Export-Status"] }, "LastModifiedTime": "2024-01-15T17:00:00.000+0000", "InvokeMode": "RESPONSE_STREAM" }
# Configure streaming for large exports from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count stream_config = { 'function': 'jobpost-export', 'mode': 'RESPONSE_STREAM', 'expose_headers': ['Content-Disposition', 'X-Export-Status'] } print(f"Streaming Configuration Updated") print(f"\nFunction: {stream_config['function']}") print(f"Invoke Mode: {stream_config['mode']}") print(f"\nExposed Headers:") for header in stream_config['expose_headers']: print(f" - {header}") # Estimate export size total_posts = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nExport Statistics:") print(f" Total records: {total_posts}") print(f" Streaming: Enabled") print(f" Benefit: Large exports without timeout")
Example 4: Update Alias-Specific URL Config

Update URL configuration for a specific alias (production).

#!/bin/bash
# Update prod alias URL config

aws lambda update-function-url-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --qualifier prod \
    --auth-type AWS_IAM \
    --cors '{
        "AllowOrigins": ["https://jobpost.example.com"],
        "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"],
        "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization"],
        "AllowCredentials": true,
        "MaxAge": 7200
    }' \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionUrl": "https://prod123abc456.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api:prod", "AuthType": "AWS_IAM", "Cors": { "AllowOrigins": ["https://jobpost.example.com"], "AllowMethods": ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"], "AllowHeaders": ["Content-Type", "Authorization"], "AllowCredentials": true, "MaxAge": 7200 }, "LastModifiedTime": "2024-01-15T17:15:00.000+0000", "InvokeMode": "BUFFERED" }
