aws lambda list-provisioned-concurrency-configs \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --output json > provisioned-report.json

echo "Report saved to provisioned-report.json"
Report saved to provisioned-report.json
# Export report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Report exported") print(f"\nFile: provisioned-report.json") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Monitor Utilization

Check available vs allocated instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor utilization

aws lambda list-provisioned-concurrency-configs \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'ProvisionedConcurrencyConfigs[].{Requested:RequestedProvisionedConcurrentExecutions,Available:AvailableProvisionedConcurrentExecutions}'
[ {"Requested": 100, "Available": 100}, {"Requested": 25, "Available": 25} ]
# Monitor utilization from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost util = [{'requested': 100, 'available': 100}, {'requested': 25, 'available': 25}] print(f"Utilization Report") for u in util: print(f"\nRequested: {u['requested']}, Available: {u['available']}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-provisioned-concurrency-config

Deletes provisioned concurrency configuration for a function alias or version. Remove warm instance allocations to reduce costs when not needed.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --qualifier <alias-or-version>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --qualifier (string, required): Alias name or version number.
Return Value
  • HTTP 204: No content returned on successful deletion.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Staging Config

Remove provisioned concurrency from staging.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete staging config

aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --qualifier staging \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Provisioned concurrency removed from staging"
Provisioned concurrency removed from staging
# Delete config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Provisioned concurrency deleted") print(f"\nAlias: staging") print(f"Status: Removed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Cost Reduction Cleanup

Remove all non-production provisioned configs.

#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup non-prod configs

for alias in dev staging test; do
    aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
        --function-name jobpost-api \
        --qualifier "$alias" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Deleted: $alias" || echo "No config: $alias"
done
Deleted: dev Deleted: staging No config: test
# Cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['dev', 'staging'] print(f"Cleanup Complete") for alias in deleted: print(f"\nDeleted: {alias}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Delete After Traffic Drop

Remove provisioned capacity after peak traffic.

#!/bin/bash
# Post-peak cleanup

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"
ALIAS="burst"

echo "Removing burst capacity..."
aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --qualifier "$ALIAS"

echo "Burst capacity removed - reverting to on-demand"
Removing burst capacity... Burst capacity removed - reverting to on-demand
# Post-peak cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Post-Peak Cleanup") print(f"\nBurst capacity removed") print(f"Mode: On-demand") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Scheduled Scale Down

Remove during off-hours via scheduled script.

#!/bin/bash
# Scheduled off-hours cleanup

HOUR=$(date +%H)

if [ "$HOUR" -ge 22 ] || [ "$HOUR" -lt 6 ]; then
    aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
        --function-name jobpost-api \
        --qualifier prod
    echo "Off-hours: Provisioned concurrency removed"
fi
Off-hours: Provisioned concurrency removed
# Scheduled cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scheduled Scale Down") print(f"\nTime: Off-hours") print(f"Action: Removed provisioned capacity") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Delete with Verification

Remove and verify deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification

FUNCTION="jobpost-api"
ALIAS="staging"

aws lambda delete-provisioned-concurrency-config \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --qualifier "$ALIAS"

# Verify deletion
if ! aws lambda get-provisioned-concurrency-config \
    --function-name "$FUNCTION" \
    --qualifier "$ALIAS" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Verified: Config deleted"
fi
Verified: Config deleted
# Verify deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete Verification") print(f"\nAlias: staging") print(f"Status: Verified deleted") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-code-signing-config

Creates a code signing configuration to ensure only trusted code runs in your Django Lambda functions. Enforce security by requiring signed deployments.

Syntax
aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=<arns> \
    [--description <description>] \
    [--code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=<action>]
Parameters Description
  • --allowed-publishers (structure, required): List of signing profile version ARNs allowed to sign code.
  • --description (string, optional): Description of the code signing configuration.
  • --code-signing-policies (structure, optional): Policy action on untrusted artifacts (Warn or Enforce).
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfig (object): Created code signing configuration details.
  • CodeSigningConfigId (string): Unique identifier for the config.
  • CodeSigningConfigArn (string): ARN of the code signing configuration.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Basic Config

Set up code signing with a signing profile.

#!/bin/bash
# Create code signing config

aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/JobPostProfile \
    --description "Django JobPost code signing" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-abc123def456", "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123def456", "Description": "Django JobPost code signing", "AllowedPublishers": { "SigningProfileVersionArns": [ "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/JobPostProfile" ] } } }
# Create config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code Signing Config Created") print(f"\nID: csc-abc123def456") print(f"Description: Django JobPost code signing") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Enforce Signing Policy

Block unsigned deployments.

#!/bin/bash
# Enforce signed code

aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/ProdProfile \
    --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Enforce \
    --description "Production enforcement"
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-prod789", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Enforce" } } }
# Enforce signing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Enforcement Enabled") print(f"\nPolicy: Block unsigned") print(f"Mode: Enforce") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Warning Only Mode

Allow unsigned with warnings for testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Warning mode for dev

aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/DevProfile \
    --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Warn \
    --description "Development warning mode"
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-dev456", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Warn" } } }
# Warning mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Warning Mode Enabled") print(f"\nPolicy: Warn only") print(f"Unsigned: Allowed with warning") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Multiple Signing Profiles

Allow multiple trusted signers.

#!/bin/bash
# Multiple profiles

aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers 'SigningProfileVersionArns=["arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamA","arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamB"]' \
    --description "Multi-team signing"
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "AllowedPublishers": { "SigningProfileVersionArns": [ "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamA", "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamB" ] } } }
# Multi-team signing from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost teams = ['TeamA', 'TeamB'] print(f"Multiple Signers Configured") for team in teams: print(f"\nAllowed: {team}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Save Config ARN

Create and capture ARN for function assignment.

#!/bin/bash
# Create and save ARN

CONFIG_ARN=$(aws lambda create-code-signing-config \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/JobPostProfile \
    --description "JobPost signing" \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfig.CodeSigningConfigArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Config ARN: $CONFIG_ARN"
echo "$CONFIG_ARN" > code-signing-config-arn.txt
Config ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123
# Save config ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost config_arn = "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123" print(f"Config ARN Saved") print(f"\nARN: {config_arn}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-code-signing-config

Returns details about a code signing configuration. Review allowed publishers and signing policies for your Django Lambda security setup.

Syntax
aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn <config-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --code-signing-config-arn (string, required): ARN of the code signing configuration to retrieve.
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfig (object): Full code signing configuration details.
  • AllowedPublishers (object): List of signing profile ARNs allowed.
  • CodeSigningPolicies (object): Enforcement policy settings.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Config Details

Retrieve full configuration information.

#!/bin/bash
# Get config details

aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-abc123", "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123", "Description": "Django JobPost code signing", "AllowedPublishers": { "SigningProfileVersionArns": [ "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/JobPostProfile" ] }, "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Enforce" } } }
# Get config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code Signing Config") print(f"\nID: csc-abc123") print(f"Policy: Enforce") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check Enforcement Policy

Verify if signing is enforced or warned.

#!/bin/bash
# Check enforcement

POLICY=$(aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfig.CodeSigningPolicies.UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment' \
    --output text)

echo "Policy: $POLICY"
Policy: Enforce
# Check policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost policy = "Enforce" print(f"Enforcement Policy") print(f"\nMode: {policy}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: List Allowed Signers

Get all allowed signing profile ARNs.

#!/bin/bash
# List signers

aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfig.AllowedPublishers.SigningProfileVersionArns[]' \
    --output table
-------------------------------------------------------------------- | SigningProfileVersionArns | +------------------------------------------------------------------+ | arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamA | | arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamB | +------------------------------------------------------------------+
# List signers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost signers = ['TeamA', 'TeamB'] print(f"Allowed Signers") for signer in signers: print(f"\n{signer}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Security Audit

Get config for security documentation.

#!/bin/bash
# Security audit

aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --output json > code-signing-audit.json

echo "Audit saved to code-signing-audit.json"
Audit saved to code-signing-audit.json
# Audit report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security Audit Exported") print(f"\nFile: code-signing-audit.json") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Compare Configs

Retrieve and compare multiple configs.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare configs

for env in prod staging dev; do
    ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-${env}"
    POLICY=$(aws lambda get-code-signing-config \
        --code-signing-config-arn "$ARN" \
        --query 'CodeSigningConfig.CodeSigningPolicies.UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
    echo "$env: $POLICY"
done
prod: Enforce staging: Warn dev: N/A
# Compare configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost configs = {'prod': 'Enforce', 'staging': 'Warn', 'dev': 'N/A'} print(f"Config Comparison") for env, policy in configs.items(): print(f"\n{env}: {policy}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-code-signing-config

Updates a code signing configuration with new allowed publishers or policies. Modify your Django Lambda security settings without recreating configs.

Syntax
aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn <config-arn> \
    [--description <description>] \
    [--allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=<arns>] \
    [--code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=<action>]
Parameters Description
  • --code-signing-config-arn (string, required): ARN of the code signing configuration to update.
  • --description (string, optional): Updated description for the configuration.
  • --allowed-publishers (structure, optional): Updated list of signing profile ARNs.
  • --code-signing-policies (structure, optional): Updated policy (Warn or Enforce).
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfig (object): Updated code signing configuration.
  • LastModified (string): Timestamp of the last modification.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update Description

Change the configuration description.

#!/bin/bash
# Update description

aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --description "Updated Django JobPost signing - v2" \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-abc123", "Description": "Updated Django JobPost signing - v2", "LastModified": "2024-01-16T14:20:00.000+0000" } }
# Update description from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Updated") print(f"\nDescription: Updated Django JobPost signing - v2") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Switch to Enforce Mode

Upgrade from warning to enforcement.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable enforcement

aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Enforce
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Enforce" } } }
# Enable enforcement from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Enforcement Enabled") print(f"\nPolicy: Enforce") print(f"Unsigned: Blocked") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Add Signing Profile

Add a new allowed signer.

#!/bin/bash
# Add new signer

aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --allowed-publishers 'SigningProfileVersionArns=["arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamA","arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamB","arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamC"]'
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "AllowedPublishers": { "SigningProfileVersionArns": [ "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamA", "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamB", "arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/TeamC" ] } } }
# Add signer from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost teams = ['TeamA', 'TeamB', 'TeamC'] print(f"Signers Updated") for team in teams: print(f"\nAllowed: {team}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Downgrade to Warning

Switch from enforcement to warning mode.

#!/bin/bash
# Downgrade to warn

aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Warn \
    --description "Temporary warning mode for testing"
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "Description": "Temporary warning mode for testing", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Warn" } } }
# Warning mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Warning Mode Enabled") print(f"\nPolicy: Warn") print(f"Purpose: Testing") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Full Update

Update all configuration aspects.

#!/bin/bash
# Full update

aws lambda update-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --description "Production signing config v3" \
    --allowed-publishers SigningProfileVersionArns=arn:aws:signer:us-east-1:123456789012:/signing-profiles/ProdProfile \
    --code-signing-policies UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment=Enforce

echo "Full update complete"
{ "CodeSigningConfig": { "Description": "Production signing config v3", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Enforce" } } } Full update complete
# Full update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Full Update Complete") print(f"\nDescription: Production signing config v3") print(f"Policy: Enforce") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-code-signing-config

Deletes a code signing configuration. Cannot delete if in use by any Django Lambda function. Detach from functions first.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn <config-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --code-signing-config-arn (string, required): ARN of the code signing configuration to delete.
Return Value
  • HTTP 204: No content returned on successful deletion.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Config

Remove an unused code signing configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete config

aws lambda delete-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Code signing config deleted"
Code signing config deleted
# Delete config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Code Signing Config Deleted") print(f"\nID: csc-abc123") print(f"Status: Removed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Cleanup Old Configs

Delete deprecated configurations.

#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup old configs

for config in csc-old1 csc-old2 csc-deprecated; do
    aws lambda delete-code-signing-config \
        --code-signing-config-arn "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:${config}" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Deleted: $config" || echo "Failed or in use: $config"
done
Deleted: csc-old1 Deleted: csc-old2 Failed or in use: csc-deprecated
# Cleanup configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost deleted = ['csc-old1', 'csc-old2'] print(f"Cleanup Complete") for config in deleted: print(f"\nDeleted: {config}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Delete After Detaching

Remove config from function then delete.

#!/bin/bash
# Detach and delete

CONFIG_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

# First detach from function
aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --code-signing-config-arn "" 2>/dev/null || true

# Then delete
aws lambda delete-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN"

echo "Config detached and deleted"
Config detached and deleted
# Detach and delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Removed") print(f"\nStep 1: Detached from function") print(f"Step 2: Config deleted") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Safe Delete with Check

Check if in use before deleting.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe delete

CONFIG_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

# Check if in use
IN_USE=$(aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN" \
    --query 'length(FunctionArns)' --output text)

if [ "$IN_USE" -eq 0 ]; then
    aws lambda delete-code-signing-config --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN"
    echo "Deleted successfully"
else
    echo "Cannot delete - in use by $IN_USE functions"
fi
Deleted successfully
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Delete Check") print(f"\nIn use: 0 functions") print(f"Status: Deleted successfully") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Delete with Verification

Delete and confirm removal.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete with verification

CONFIG_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

aws lambda delete-code-signing-config --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN"

# Verify deletion
if ! aws lambda get-code-signing-config --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Verified: Config no longer exists"
fi
Verified: Config no longer exists
# Verify deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deletion Verified") print(f"\nConfig: csc-abc123") print(f"Status: No longer exists") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-code-signing-configs

Lists all code signing configurations in the account. Review all security configurations available for your Django Lambda functions.

Syntax
aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    [--max-items <number>] \
    [--marker <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of configurations to return.
  • --marker (string, optional): Pagination token from previous response.
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfigs (array): List of code signing configurations.
  • NextMarker (string): Token for next page if more results exist.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Configs

Get all code signing configurations.

#!/bin/bash
# List all configs

aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfigs": [ { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-prod123", "Description": "Production signing", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Enforce" } }, { "CodeSigningConfigId": "csc-dev456", "Description": "Development signing", "CodeSigningPolicies": { "UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment": "Warn" } } ] }
# List configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost configs = [('csc-prod123', 'Enforce'), ('csc-dev456', 'Warn')] print(f"Code Signing Configs") for id, policy in configs: print(f"\n{id}: {policy}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Count Configs

Get total number of configurations.

#!/bin/bash
# Count configs

COUNT=$(aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    --query 'length(CodeSigningConfigs)' \
    --output text)

echo "Total configs: $COUNT"
Total configs: 5
# Count configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost total = 5 print(f"Total Configs: {total}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: List Enforcement Configs

Find configs with enforcement enabled.

#!/bin/bash
# Find enforcing configs

aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfigs[?CodeSigningPolicies.UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment==`Enforce`].{ID:CodeSigningConfigId,Desc:Description}' \
    --output table
------------------------------------------- | ListCodeSigningConfigs | +--------------+--------------------------+ | ID | Desc | +--------------+--------------------------+ | csc-prod123 | Production signing | | csc-api789 | API signing | +--------------+--------------------------+
# Enforcing configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost enforcing = [('csc-prod123', 'Production'), ('csc-api789', 'API')] print(f"Enforcing Configs") for id, desc in enforcing: print(f"\n{id}: {desc}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Export Config List

Save all configs to JSON file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export configs

aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    --output json > code-signing-configs.json

echo "Configs exported to code-signing-configs.json"
Configs exported to code-signing-configs.json
# Export configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Configs Exported") print(f"\nFile: code-signing-configs.json") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Paginated Listing

Handle large config lists with pagination.

#!/bin/bash
# Paginated listing

MARKER=""
while true; do
    RESULT=$(aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
        --max-items 10 \
        ${MARKER:+--marker "$MARKER"})
    
    echo "$RESULT" | jq '.CodeSigningConfigs[].CodeSigningConfigId'
    
    MARKER=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.NextMarker // empty')
    [ -z "$MARKER" ] && break
done
"csc-prod123" "csc-dev456" "csc-api789"
# Paginated list from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost ids = ['csc-prod123', 'csc-dev456', 'csc-api789'] print(f"All Config IDs") for id in ids: print(f"\n{id}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-function-code-signing-config

Returns the code signing configuration for a function. Check which signing config is attached to your Django Lambda function.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name <function-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfigArn (string): ARN of the attached code signing config.
  • FunctionName (string): Name of the function.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Function Config

Retrieve signing config for a function.

#!/bin/bash
# Get function config

aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123", "FunctionName": "jobpost-api" }
# Get function config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function Code Signing") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Config: csc-abc123") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check If Secured

Verify function has signing config.

#!/bin/bash
# Check if secured

if aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Function is code-signing protected"
else
    echo "WARNING: No code signing config"
fi
{ "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123" } Function is code-signing protected
# Check security from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security Check") print(f"\nCode Signing: Enabled") print(f"Status: Protected") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Config ARN Only

Extract just the config ARN.

#!/bin/bash
# Get ARN only

CONFIG_ARN=$(aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfigArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Config ARN: $CONFIG_ARN"
Config ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123
# Get ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arn = "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123" print(f"Config ARN") print(f"\n{arn}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Audit Multiple Functions

Check signing config for all functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit all functions

for func in jobpost-api jobpost-worker jobpost-scheduler; do
    CONFIG=$(aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'CodeSigningConfigArn' \
        --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "None")
    echo "$func: $CONFIG"
done
jobpost-api: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 jobpost-worker: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 jobpost-scheduler: None
# Audit functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost funcs = {'api': 'csc-abc123', 'worker': 'csc-abc123', 'scheduler': 'None'} print(f"Function Audit") for func, config in funcs.items(): print(f"\n{func}: {config}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Security Report

Generate security compliance report.

#!/bin/bash
# Security report

FUNC="jobpost-api"

CONFIG=$(aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$CONFIG" ]; then
    echo "$FUNC: COMPLIANT - Code signing enabled"
    echo "$CONFIG" | jq '.'
else
    echo "$FUNC: NON-COMPLIANT - No code signing"
fi
jobpost-api: COMPLIANT - Code signing enabled { "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123", "FunctionName": "jobpost-api" }
# Security report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security Report") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Status: COMPLIANT") print(f"Code Signing: Enabled") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

put-function-code-signing-config

Associates a code signing configuration with a function. Attach signing requirements to your Django Lambda for deployment security.

Syntax
aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --code-signing-config-arn <config-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --code-signing-config-arn (string, required): ARN of the code signing configuration to attach.
Return Value
  • CodeSigningConfigArn (string): ARN of the attached code signing config.
  • FunctionName (string): Name of the function.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Attach Config to Function

Associate signing config with function.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach config

aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123", "FunctionName": "jobpost-api" }
# Attach config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Attached") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Config: csc-abc123") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Secure All Functions

Apply config to multiple functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Secure all functions

CONFIG_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

for func in jobpost-api jobpost-worker jobpost-scheduler; do
    aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN"
    echo "Secured: $func"
done
Secured: jobpost-api Secured: jobpost-worker Secured: jobpost-scheduler
# Secure all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost funcs = ['jobpost-api', 'jobpost-worker', 'jobpost-scheduler'] print(f"All Functions Secured") for func in funcs: print(f"\nSecured: {func}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Update Config

Change to a different signing config.

#!/bin/bash
# Update to new config

aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-new456

echo "Config updated to csc-new456"
{ "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-new456", "FunctionName": "jobpost-api" } Config updated to csc-new456
# Update config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Updated") print(f"\nOld: csc-abc123") print(f"New: csc-new456") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Deploy with Signing

Attach config then deploy signed code.

#!/bin/bash
# Attach and deploy

FUNC="jobpost-api"
CONFIG="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

# Attach config
aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG"

# Deploy signed code
aws lambda update-function-code \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --s3-bucket deploy-bucket \
    --s3-key signed-code.zip

echo "Signed deployment complete"
{ "CodeSigningConfigArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123" } Signed deployment complete
# Deploy signed from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Signed Deployment") print(f"\nConfig attached") print(f"Code deployed") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Environment-Based Config

Apply different configs per environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Environment configs

ENV="$1"
FUNC="jobpost-api-${ENV}"

case $ENV in
    prod) CONFIG="csc-prod-enforce" ;;
    staging) CONFIG="csc-staging-warn" ;;
    *) CONFIG="csc-dev" ;;
esac

aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --code-signing-config-arn "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:${CONFIG}"

echo "Applied $CONFIG to $FUNC"
Applied csc-prod-enforce to jobpost-api-prod
# Env config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost configs = {'prod': 'csc-prod-enforce', 'staging': 'csc-staging-warn'} print(f"Environment Configs") for env, cfg in configs.items(): print(f"\n{env}: {cfg}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-function-code-signing-config

Removes the code signing configuration from a function. Detach signing requirements from your Django Lambda function.

Syntax
aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name <function-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
Return Value
  • HTTP 204: No content returned on successful removal.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Remove Config

Detach code signing from function.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove config

aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Code signing config removed"
Code signing config removed
# Remove config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Removed") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Status: Unsigned allowed") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Remove for Testing

Temporarily disable for development.

#!/bin/bash
# Disable for testing

FUNC="jobpost-api-dev"

aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC"

echo "Signing disabled on $FUNC for testing"
Signing disabled on jobpost-api-dev for testing
# Testing mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Testing Mode") print(f"\nSigning: Disabled") print(f"Purpose: Development testing") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Batch Remove

Remove signing from multiple dev functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch remove

for func in dev-api dev-worker dev-scheduler; do
    aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
        --function-name "$func" 2>/dev/null && \
        echo "Removed: $func" || echo "No config: $func"
done
Removed: dev-api Removed: dev-worker No config: dev-scheduler
# Batch remove from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost removed = ['dev-api', 'dev-worker'] print(f"Batch Removal") for func in removed: print(f"\nRemoved: {func}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Remove and Replace

Remove old config before attaching new.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove and replace

FUNC="jobpost-api"

# Remove old
aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC"

# Attach new
aws lambda put-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-new

echo "Config replaced"
Config replaced
# Replace config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Config Replaced") print(f"\nOld config removed") print(f"New config attached") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Verify Removal

Remove and confirm detachment.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify removal

FUNC="jobpost-api"

aws lambda delete-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC"

# Verify
if ! aws lambda get-function-code-signing-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Verified: No code signing config attached"
fi
Verified: No code signing config attached
# Verify removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Removal Verified") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Signing: None attached") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-functions-by-code-signing-config

Lists functions that use a specific code signing configuration. Find all Django Lambda functions sharing a signing config.

Syntax
aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn <config-arn> \
    [--max-items <number>] \
    [--marker <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --code-signing-config-arn (string, required): ARN of the code signing configuration.
  • --max-items (integer, optional): Maximum number of function ARNs to return.
  • --marker (string, optional): Pagination token from previous response.
Return Value
  • FunctionArns (array): List of function ARNs using this signing config.
  • NextMarker (string): Token for next page if more results exist.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List Functions Using Config

Find all functions with a specific signing config.

#!/bin/bash
# List functions

aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionArns": [ "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-processor", "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-worker" ] }
# List functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost functions = ['jobpost-api', 'jobpost-processor', 'jobpost-worker'] print(f"Functions Using Config") for func in functions: print(f"\n{func}") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Count Functions

Count how many functions use a config.

#!/bin/bash
# Count functions

COUNT=$(aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --query 'length(FunctionArns)' \
    --output text)

echo "Functions using config: $COUNT"
Functions using config: 3
# Count functions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost count = 3 print(f"Functions Using Config: {count}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check Before Delete

Verify config is not in use before deleting.

#!/bin/bash
# Check usage

CONFIG_ARN="arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123"

FUNCS=$(aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn "$CONFIG_ARN" \
    --query 'FunctionArns')

if [ "$FUNCS" = "[]" ]; then
    echo "Config not in use - safe to delete"
else
    echo "Config in use by: $FUNCS"
fi
Config in use by: ["arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api"]
# Check usage from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Usage Check") print(f"\nIn use by: jobpost-api") print(f"Safe to delete: No") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Export Function List

Save function ARNs to file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export list

aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
    --code-signing-config-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123 \
    --query 'FunctionArns[]' \
    --output text > signed-functions.txt

echo "Exported to signed-functions.txt"
Exported to signed-functions.txt
# Export list from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function List Exported") print(f"\nFile: signed-functions.txt") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Audit All Configs

List usage for all signing configs.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit all configs

for config in $(aws lambda list-code-signing-configs \
    --query 'CodeSigningConfigs[].CodeSigningConfigArn' --output text); do
    
    COUNT=$(aws lambda list-functions-by-code-signing-config \
        --code-signing-config-arn "$config" \
        --query 'length(FunctionArns)' --output text)
    
    echo "$config: $COUNT functions"
done
arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-abc123: 3 functions arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:code-signing-config:csc-def456: 1 functions
# Audit configs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost configs = {'csc-abc123': 3, 'csc-def456': 1} print(f"Config Usage Audit") for cfg, count in configs.items(): print(f"\n{cfg}: {count} functions") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-runtime-management-config

Returns the runtime management configuration for a function. Check automatic update settings for your Django Lambda runtime.

Syntax
aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    [--qualifier <alias-or-version>]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --qualifier (string, optional): Alias name or version number.
Return Value
  • UpdateRuntimeOn (string): When runtime updates occur (Auto, Manual, FunctionUpdate).
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function.
  • RuntimeVersionArn (string): ARN of the current runtime version.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Config

Check runtime management settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Get config

aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1
{ "UpdateRuntimeOn": "Auto", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "RuntimeVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11" }
# Get config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Runtime Management") print(f"\nUpdate Mode: Auto") print(f"Runtime: python3.11") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check Update Mode

Get just the update mode setting.

#!/bin/bash
# Check mode

MODE=$(aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'UpdateRuntimeOn' \
    --output text)

echo "Update mode: $MODE"
Update mode: Auto
# Check mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost mode = "Auto" print(f"Update Mode: {mode}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Runtime Version

Retrieve current runtime version ARN.

#!/bin/bash
# Get version

aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'RuntimeVersionArn' \
    --output text
arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v23
# Get version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost version = "python3.11.v23" print(f"Runtime Version") print(f"\n{version}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Audit All Functions

Check settings for all functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit all

for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    MODE=$(aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'UpdateRuntimeOn' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
    echo "$func: $MODE"
done
jobpost-api: Auto jobpost-worker: Manual jobpost-scheduler: FunctionUpdate
# Audit all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost modes = {'api': 'Auto', 'worker': 'Manual', 'scheduler': 'FunctionUpdate'} print(f"Runtime Mode Audit") for func, mode in modes.items(): print(f"\n{func}: {mode}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Version Comparison

Compare versions across aliases.

#!/bin/bash
# Compare versions

for alias in prod staging dev; do
    VERSION=$(aws lambda get-runtime-management-config \
        --function-name jobpost-api \
        --qualifier "$alias" \
        --query 'RuntimeVersionArn' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
    echo "$alias: $VERSION"
done
prod: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22 staging: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v23 dev: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v23
# Compare versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost versions = {'prod': 'v22', 'staging': 'v23', 'dev': 'v23'} print(f"Version Comparison") for alias, ver in versions.items(): print(f"\n{alias}: {ver}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

put-runtime-management-config

Configures runtime management settings for automatic or manual runtime updates. Control when your Django Lambda runtime receives security patches.

Syntax
aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --update-runtime-on <Auto|Manual|FunctionUpdate> \
    [--qualifier <alias-or-version>] \
    [--runtime-version-arn <runtime-arn>]
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --update-runtime-on (string, required): When to update (Auto, Manual, FunctionUpdate).
  • --qualifier (string, optional): Alias name or version number.
  • --runtime-version-arn (string, optional): Specific runtime version ARN for Manual mode.
Return Value
  • UpdateRuntimeOn (string): Configured update mode.
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function.
  • RuntimeVersionArn (string): ARN of the runtime version.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Enable Auto Updates

Let AWS manage runtime updates automatically.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable auto updates

aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --update-runtime-on Auto \
    --region us-east-1
{ "UpdateRuntimeOn": "Auto", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "RuntimeVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11" }
# Auto updates from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Runtime Management Set") print(f"\nMode: Auto") print(f"Updates: Automatic") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Manual Mode

Pin to specific runtime version.

#!/bin/bash
# Manual mode

aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --update-runtime-on Manual \
    --runtime-version-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22
{ "UpdateRuntimeOn": "Manual", "RuntimeVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22" }
# Manual mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Manual Mode Enabled") print(f"\nPinned to: python3.11.v22") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Function Update Mode

Update runtime only on function updates.

#!/bin/bash
# Function update mode

aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --update-runtime-on FunctionUpdate
{ "UpdateRuntimeOn": "FunctionUpdate", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api" }
# Function update mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"FunctionUpdate Mode") print(f"\nRuntime updates on: Function deploys only") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Production Stability

Pin production to tested version.

#!/bin/bash
# Pin production

aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --qualifier prod \
    --update-runtime-on Manual \
    --runtime-version-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22

echo "Production pinned to stable runtime"
{ "UpdateRuntimeOn": "Manual", "RuntimeVersionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22" } Production pinned to stable runtime
# Pin production from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production Pinned") print(f"\nAlias: prod") print(f"Version: python3.11.v22") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Dev Auto, Prod Manual

Different strategies per environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Env strategies

# Dev - auto updates
aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --qualifier dev \
    --update-runtime-on Auto

# Prod - manual
aws lambda put-runtime-management-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --qualifier prod \
    --update-runtime-on Manual \
    --runtime-version-arn arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1::runtime:python3.11.v22

echo "Environment strategies applied"
Environment strategies applied
# Env strategies from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost strategies = {'dev': 'Auto', 'prod': 'Manual'} print(f"Environment Strategies") for env, mode in strategies.items(): print(f"\n{env}: {mode}") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-function-recursion-config

Returns the recursion configuration for a function to prevent infinite loops. Check if your Django Lambda terminates or allows recursive calls.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name <function-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
Return Value
  • RecursiveLoop (string): Current setting (Allow or Terminate).
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Check Recursion Setting

Get current recursion configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Check recursion

aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1
{ "RecursiveLoop": "Terminate" }
# Check recursion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recursion Config") print(f"\nMode: Terminate") print(f"Infinite loops: Prevented") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Simple Status Check

Get just the recursion status.

#!/bin/bash
# Status check

STATUS=$(aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'RecursiveLoop' \
    --output text)

echo "Recursion mode: $STATUS"
Recursion mode: Terminate
# Status check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost status = "Terminate" print(f"Recursion Mode: {status}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Security Audit

Verify recursion protection is enabled.

#!/bin/bash
# Security audit

MODE=$(aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'RecursiveLoop' --output text)

if [ "$MODE" = "Terminate" ]; then
    echo "SECURE: Recursion protection enabled"
else
    echo "WARNING: Recursion loops allowed"
fi
SECURE: Recursion protection enabled
# Security audit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security Audit") print(f"\nRecursion: Terminate") print(f"Status: SECURE") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Audit All Functions

Check recursion settings across functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit all functions

for func in jobpost-api jobpost-worker jobpost-scheduler; do
    MODE=$(aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'RecursiveLoop' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
    echo "$func: $MODE"
done
jobpost-api: Terminate jobpost-worker: Terminate jobpost-scheduler: Allow
# Audit all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost modes = {'api': 'Terminate', 'worker': 'Terminate', 'scheduler': 'Allow'} print(f"Recursion Audit") for func, mode in modes.items(): print(f"\n{func}: {mode}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Compliance Report

Generate recursion compliance report.

#!/bin/bash
# Compliance report

echo "Recursion Compliance Report"
echo "============================"

for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    MODE=$(aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'RecursiveLoop' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "Unknown")
    
    if [ "$MODE" = "Terminate" ]; then
        echo "$func: COMPLIANT"
    else
        echo "$func: NON-COMPLIANT ($MODE)"
    fi
done
Recursion Compliance Report ============================ jobpost-api: COMPLIANT jobpost-worker: COMPLIANT
# Compliance report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost compliant = ['jobpost-api', 'jobpost-worker'] print(f"Compliance Report") for func in compliant: print(f"\n{func}: COMPLIANT") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

put-function-recursion-config

Configures recursion detection to terminate or allow recursive invocations. Protect your Django Lambda from infinite loop costs.

Syntax
aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --recursive-loop <Allow|Terminate>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --recursive-loop (string, required): Allow or Terminate recursive invocations.
Return Value
  • RecursiveLoop (string): Configured recursion mode.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Enable Termination

Terminate recursive loops automatically.

#!/bin/bash
# Enable termination

aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --recursive-loop Terminate \
    --region us-east-1
{ "RecursiveLoop": "Terminate" }
# Enable termination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recursion Protection Enabled") print(f"\nMode: Terminate") print(f"Infinite loops: Blocked") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Allow Recursion

Enable for legitimate recursive processing.

#!/bin/bash
# Allow recursion

aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name jobpost-tree-processor \
    --recursive-loop Allow

echo "Recursion allowed for tree processing"
{ "RecursiveLoop": "Allow" } Recursion allowed for tree processing
# Allow recursion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recursion Allowed") print(f"\nFunction: tree-processor") print(f"Use case: Hierarchical data") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Secure All Functions

Enable protection across all functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Secure all functions

for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --recursive-loop Terminate
    echo "Secured: $func"
done
Secured: jobpost-api Secured: jobpost-worker Secured: jobpost-scheduler
# Secure all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost funcs = ['jobpost-api', 'jobpost-worker', 'jobpost-scheduler'] print(f"All Functions Secured") for func in funcs: print(f"\nSecured: {func}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Toggle for Testing

Temporarily allow for testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Toggle for testing

FUNC="jobpost-api"

# Allow for testing
aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --recursive-loop Allow

echo "Testing with recursion allowed..."
sleep 60

# Restore protection
aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --recursive-loop Terminate

echo "Protection restored"
Testing with recursion allowed... Protection restored
# Toggle test from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Testing Toggle") print(f"\n1. Allow for testing") print(f"2. Restore protection") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Compliance Enforcement

Enforce recursion protection policy.

#!/bin/bash
# Compliance enforcement

echo "Enforcing recursion protection policy..."

for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    CURRENT=$(aws lambda get-function-recursion-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'RecursiveLoop' --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$CURRENT" != "Terminate" ]; then
        aws lambda put-function-recursion-config \
            --function-name "$func" \
            --recursive-loop Terminate
        echo "Fixed: $func"
    fi
done

echo "Compliance enforced"
Enforcing recursion protection policy... Fixed: jobpost-scheduler Compliance enforced
# Compliance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Compliance Enforcement") print(f"\nFixed: jobpost-scheduler") print(f"Status: All compliant") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-function-scaling-config

Returns the scaling configuration for a function including max concurrency. Check concurrency limits for your Django Lambda function.

Syntax
aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name <function-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
Return Value
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function.
  • MaximumConcurrency (integer): Maximum concurrent executions allowed.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Scaling Config

Retrieve current scaling settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Get scaling config

aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "MaximumConcurrency": 500 }
# Get config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling Config") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Max Concurrency: 500") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Max Concurrency

Extract just the concurrency limit.

#!/bin/bash
# Get max concurrency

MAX=$(aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --query 'MaximumConcurrency' \
    --output text)

echo "Max concurrency: $MAX"
Max concurrency: 500
# Max concurrency from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost max_conc = 500 print(f"Max Concurrency: {max_conc}") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Capacity Planning

Check capacity across all functions.

#!/bin/bash
# Capacity check

TOTAL=0
for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    MAX=$(aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'MaximumConcurrency' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
    echo "$func: $MAX"
    TOTAL=$((TOTAL + MAX))
done

echo "Total capacity: $TOTAL"
jobpost-api: 500 jobpost-worker: 200 Total capacity: 700
# Capacity from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost capacity = {'api': 500, 'worker': 200} print(f"Capacity Report") for func, cap in capacity.items(): print(f"\n{func}: {cap}") print(f"\nTotal: 700") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Monitor Scaling

Check if near capacity limits.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor scaling

FUNC="jobpost-api"

MAX=$(aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --query 'MaximumConcurrency' --output text)

if [ "$MAX" -lt 100 ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Low concurrency limit ($MAX)"
else
    echo "Concurrency OK: $MAX"
fi
Concurrency OK: 500
# Monitor from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling Monitor") print(f"\nMax: 500") print(f"Status: OK") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Scaling Report

Generate scaling configuration report.

#!/bin/bash
# Export report

echo "Function,MaxConcurrency" > scaling-report.csv

for func in $(aws lambda list-functions --query 'Functions[].FunctionName' --output text); do
    MAX=$(aws lambda get-function-scaling-config \
        --function-name "$func" \
        --query 'MaximumConcurrency' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "Unlimited")
    echo "$func,$MAX" >> scaling-report.csv
done

echo "Report saved to scaling-report.csv"
Report saved to scaling-report.csv
# Export report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling Report Exported") print(f"\nFile: scaling-report.csv") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

put-function-scaling-config

Configures scaling settings for a function including maximum concurrency limit. Control how many concurrent Django Lambda instances can run.

Syntax
aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name <function-name> \
    --maximum-concurrency <number>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --maximum-concurrency (integer, required): Maximum number of concurrent executions.
Return Value
  • FunctionArn (string): ARN of the function.
  • MaximumConcurrency (integer): Configured maximum concurrency.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Set Concurrency Limit

Configure maximum concurrent executions.

#!/bin/bash
# Set concurrency

aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --maximum-concurrency 500 \
    --region us-east-1
{ "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:jobpost-api", "MaximumConcurrency": 500 }
# Set limit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling Config Set") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Max Concurrency: 500") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Cost Control

Limit concurrency to control costs.

#!/bin/bash
# Cost control limit

aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-worker \
    --maximum-concurrency 100

echo "Concurrency limited to 100 for cost control"
{ "MaximumConcurrency": 100 } Concurrency limited to 100 for cost control
# Cost control from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cost Control Enabled") print(f"\nLimit: 100 concurrent") print(f"Purpose: Budget protection") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Environment Limits

Different limits per environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Environment limits

aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api-prod \
    --maximum-concurrency 1000

aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api-staging \
    --maximum-concurrency 50

echo "Env limits configured"
Env limits configured
# Env limits from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost limits = {'prod': 1000, 'staging': 50} print(f"Environment Limits") for env, limit in limits.items(): print(f"\n{env}: {limit}") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Scale Up for Peak

Increase limit for high traffic periods.

#!/bin/bash
# Scale up for peak

FUNC="jobpost-api"

echo "Scaling up for peak traffic..."
aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --maximum-concurrency 2000

echo "Peak scaling enabled: 2000 max concurrency"
Scaling up for peak traffic... Peak scaling enabled: 2000 max concurrency
# Peak scaling from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Peak Traffic Scaling") print(f"\nMax: 2000") print(f"Status: Ready for peak") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Downstream Protection

Limit to protect downstream services.

#!/bin/bash
# Protect downstream

# Limit based on database connection pool
DB_POOL_SIZE=100

aws lambda put-function-scaling-config \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --maximum-concurrency $DB_POOL_SIZE

echo "Concurrency matched to DB pool: $DB_POOL_SIZE"
Concurrency matched to DB pool: 100
# Downstream protection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Downstream Protection") print(f"\nDB Pool: 100") print(f"Lambda Max: 100") print(f"Protection: Enabled") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

wait

Wait commands to poll Lambda until a resource reaches a desired state. Essential for CI/CD pipelines deploying Django Lambda functions.

Syntax
# Wait for function to be active
aws lambda wait function-active \
    --function-name <function-name>

# Wait for function to be updated
aws lambda wait function-updated \
    --function-name <function-name>

# Wait for function to exist
aws lambda wait function-exists \
    --function-name <function-name>

# Wait for published version to be active
aws lambda wait published-version-active \
    --function-name <function-name>
Parameters Description
  • --function-name (string, required): Name or ARN of the Lambda function.
  • --qualifier (string, optional): Alias or version for some waiters.
Return Value
  • function-active: Waits until function state is Active.
  • function-exists: Waits until function exists.
  • function-updated: Waits until LastUpdateStatus is Successful.
  • published-version-active: Waits until published version is Active.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Wait for Active

Wait until function is ready to invoke.

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for active

echo "Waiting for function to be active..."
aws lambda wait function-active \
    --function-name jobpost-api \
    --region us-east-1

echo "Function is now active!"
Waiting for function to be active... Function is now active!
# Wait active from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Wait Complete") print(f"\nFunction: jobpost-api") print(f"Status: Active") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Deploy Pipeline

Wait after update before invoking.

#!/bin/bash
# Deploy pipeline

FUNC="jobpost-api"

# Update code
aws lambda update-function-code \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --zip-file fileb://function.zip

# Wait for update to complete
aws lambda wait function-updated \
    --function-name "$FUNC"

# Now safe to invoke
aws lambda invoke --function-name "$FUNC" out.json
echo "Deployment complete"
Deployment complete
# Deploy pipeline from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deploy Pipeline") print(f"\n1. Code updated") print(f"2. Waited for completion") print(f"3. Invoked successfully") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Wait for Creation

Wait until new function exists.

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for creation

FUNC="jobpost-new-api"

# Create function
aws lambda create-function \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --runtime python3.11 \
    --handler handler.main \
    --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/lambda-role \
    --zip-file fileb://function.zip

# Wait until it exists and is ready
aws lambda wait function-exists --function-name "$FUNC"
aws lambda wait function-active --function-name "$FUNC"

echo "Function created and ready"
Function created and ready
# Wait creation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Function Creation") print(f"\nCreated: jobpost-new-api") print(f"Status: Ready") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Version Publishing

Wait for published version to be active.

#!/bin/bash
# Version publishing

FUNC="jobpost-api"

# Publish version
VERSION=$(aws lambda publish-version \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --query 'Version' --output text)

# Wait for version to be active
aws lambda wait published-version-active \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --qualifier "$VERSION"

echo "Version $VERSION is active"
Version 5 is active
# Version publish from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Published") print(f"\nVersion: 5") print(f"Status: Active") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: CI/CD Integration

Full deployment with waits.

#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD deployment

FUNC="jobpost-api"

echo "Starting deployment..."

# Update code
aws lambda update-function-code \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --s3-bucket deploy-bucket \
    --s3-key function.zip

echo "Waiting for code update..."
aws lambda wait function-updated --function-name "$FUNC"

# Update config
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
    --function-name "$FUNC" \
    --memory-size 512

echo "Waiting for config update..."
aws lambda wait function-updated --function-name "$FUNC"

echo "Deployment complete!"
Starting deployment... Waiting for code update... Waiting for config update... Deployment complete!
# CI/CD deploy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CI/CD Deployment") print(f"\n1. Code updated") print(f"2. Config updated") print(f"3. Complete") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-capacity-provider

Creates a capacity provider for Lambda functions to manage compute resources. Organize compute capacity for your Django Lambda workloads.

Syntax
aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
    --name <provider-name> \
    --compute-platform <platform> \
    [--tags <tags>]
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name for the capacity provider.
  • --compute-platform (string, required): Compute platform type (Lambda).
  • --tags (map, optional): Resource tags for organization.
Return Value
  • CapacityProvider (object): Created capacity provider details.
  • Name (string): Name of the provider.
  • Arn (string): ARN of the capacity provider.
  • Status (string): Current status (ACTIVE).
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Basic Provider

Create a Lambda capacity provider.

#!/bin/bash
# Create provider

aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
    --name jobpost-capacity \
    --compute-platform Lambda \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CapacityProvider": { "Name": "jobpost-capacity", "Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:capacity-provider/jobpost-capacity", "Status": "ACTIVE" } }
# Create provider from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Capacity Provider Created") print(f"\nName: jobpost-capacity") print(f"Status: ACTIVE") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create with Tags

Add tags for organization.

#!/bin/bash
# Create with tags

aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
    --name jobpost-prod-capacity \
    --compute-platform Lambda \
    --tags Environment=production,Project=jobpost
{ "CapacityProvider": { "Name": "jobpost-prod-capacity", "Status": "ACTIVE" } }
# Tagged provider from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Provider Created with Tags") print(f"\nEnvironment: production") print(f"Project: jobpost") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Multiple Providers

Create providers for different environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Multiple providers

for env in prod staging dev; do
    aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
        --name "jobpost-${env}-capacity" \
        --compute-platform Lambda \
        --tags Environment=$env
    echo "Created: jobpost-${env}-capacity"
done
Created: jobpost-prod-capacity Created: jobpost-staging-capacity Created: jobpost-dev-capacity
# Multiple providers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost envs = ['prod', 'staging', 'dev'] print(f"Providers Created") for env in envs: print(f"\njobpost-{env}-capacity") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Save ARN

Create and capture the ARN.

#!/bin/bash
# Save ARN

ARN=$(aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
    --name jobpost-capacity \
    --compute-platform Lambda \
    --query 'CapacityProvider.Arn' \
    --output text)

echo "Provider ARN: $ARN"
echo "$ARN" > capacity-provider-arn.txt
Provider ARN: arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:capacity-provider/jobpost-capacity
# Save ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost arn = "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:capacity-provider/jobpost-capacity" print(f"ARN Saved") print(f"\n{arn}") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Infrastructure as Code

Create provider in deployment script.

#!/bin/bash
# IaC deployment

PROVIDER="jobpost-capacity"

# Check if exists
if ! aws lambda get-capacity-provider --name "$PROVIDER" 2>/dev/null; then
    aws lambda create-capacity-provider \
        --name "$PROVIDER" \
        --compute-platform Lambda \
        --tags ManagedBy=IaC
    echo "Created new provider"
else
    echo "Provider already exists"
fi
Created new provider
# IaC deploy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"IaC Deployment") print(f"\nProvider: jobpost-capacity") print(f"Status: Created") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

get-capacity-provider

Returns details about a Lambda capacity provider. Review compute resource configuration for your Django Lambda setup.

Syntax
aws lambda get-capacity-provider \
    --name <provider-name>
Parameters Description
  • --name (string, required): Name of the capacity provider to retrieve.
Return Value
  • CapacityProvider (object): Capacity provider details.
  • Name (string): Provider name.
  • Arn (string): Provider ARN.
  • Status (string): Current status.
  • ComputePlatform (string): Platform type.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Provider Details

Retrieve capacity provider information.

#!/bin/bash
# Get provider

aws lambda get-capacity-provider \
    --name jobpost-capacity \
    --region us-east-1
{ "CapacityProvider": { "Name": "jobpost-capacity", "Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:capacity-provider/jobpost-capacity", "Status": "ACTIVE", "ComputePlatform": "Lambda" } }
# Get provider from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Capacity Provider") print(f"\nName: jobpost-capacity") print(f"Status: ACTIVE") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")

Verify provider is active.

#!/bin/bash

STATUS=$(aws lambda get-capacity-provider \
    --name jobpost-capacity \

    --output text)

echo "Provider status: $STATUS"
Provider status: ACTIVE

#!/bin/bash
