if aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name $TEMPLATE &>/dev/null; then
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-configuration-template \
        --application-name jobpost-app \
        --template-name $TEMPLATE
    echo "Template $TEMPLATE deleted"
else
    echo "Template not found"
fi
Template staging-config deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Delete") print(f"\nChecked: Exists") print(f"Deleted: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

validate-configuration-settings

Validates configuration settings before applying them to an environment. Checks for errors and warnings to prevent deployment failures.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
  --application-name <value> \
  [--template-name <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  --option-settings <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string, required): Application name.
  • --template-name (string, optional): Template context.
  • --environment-name (string, optional): Environment context.
  • --option-settings (list, required): Settings to validate.
Return Value
  • Messages (list): Validation messages.
  • Message (string): Error or warning text.
  • Severity (string): error or warning.
  • Namespace (string): Setting namespace.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Validate Instance Type

Validate before applying.

#!/bin/bash
# Validate instance type

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large
{ "Messages": [] }
# Validate instance type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Validation Result") print(f"\nMessages: None") print(f"Status: Valid") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Detect Invalid Value

Test invalid instance type.

#!/bin/bash
# Invalid value test

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.fake
{ "Messages": [ {"Message": "Invalid value for InstanceType: t3.fake", "Severity": "error"} ] }
# Invalid value from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Validation Error") print(f"\nError: Invalid InstanceType") print(f"Value: t3.fake") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Validate Multiple Settings

Validate batch of settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Batch validate

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MinSize,Value=2 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:asg,OptionName=MaxSize,Value=10 \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.medium
{ "Messages": [] }
# Batch validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Validation") print(f"\nSettings: 3") print(f"Status: All Valid") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Check Template Settings

Validate against template.

#!/bin/bash
# Template validate

aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --template-name prod-config-v1 \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:application:environment,OptionName=DATABASE_URL,Value=postgres://localhost/db
{ "Messages": [ {"Message": "Check DATABASE_URL format", "Severity": "warning"} ] }
# Template validate from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Template Validation") print(f"\nWarning: DB URL format") print(f"Status: Warning") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Validation Script

Validate and only apply if valid.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe apply

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk validate-configuration-settings \
    --application-name jobpost-app \
    --environment-name jobpost-prod \
    --option-settings \
        Namespace=aws:autoscaling:launchconfiguration,OptionName=InstanceType,Value=t3.large \
    --query 'Messages[?Severity==`error`]' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$RESULT" ]; then
    echo "Validation passed - applying..."
else
    echo "Validation failed: $RESULT"
fi
Validation passed - applying...
# Safe apply from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe Apply") print(f"\nValidated: Yes") print(f"Applied: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-available-solution-stacks

Returns a list of available platform versions (solution stacks) that can be used to create environments. Use to find Python/Django-compatible platforms.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string, optional): AWS region.
Return Value
  • SolutionStacks (list): Available platform names.
  • SolutionStackDetails (list): Detailed platform info.
  • PermittedFileTypes (list): Allowed deployment types.
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List Python Platforms

Find Python platforms for Django.

#!/bin/bash
# Python platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python')]" \
    --output table
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.9" ]
# Python platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Python Platforms") print(f"\nPython 3.12") print(f"Python 3.11") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Latest Python 3.12

Get latest Python 3.12 stack.

#!/bin/bash
# Latest Python

STACK=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Python 3.12')] | [0]" \
    --output text)

echo "Latest: $STACK"
Latest: 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12
# Latest Python from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Python") print(f"\nVersion: 3.12") print(f"OS: Amazon Linux 2023") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: List All Docker Platforms

Find Docker platforms.

#!/bin/bash
# Docker platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Docker')]"
[ "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker", "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running ECS", "64bit Amazon Linux 2 v3.5.0 running Docker" ]
# Docker platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Docker Platforms") print(f"\nDocker") print(f"ECS") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: List Amazon Linux 2023 Stacks

Get all AL2023 platforms.

#!/bin/bash
# AL2023 platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query "SolutionStacks[?contains(@, 'Amazon Linux 2023')]" \
    --output text | tr '\t' '\n'
64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.12 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Node.js 20 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Docker
# AL2023 platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"AL2023 Platforms") print(f"\nPython, Node.js") print(f"Docker available") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export All Stacks

Export full list for reference.

#!/bin/bash
# Export platforms

aws elasticbeanstalk list-available-solution-stacks \
    --query 'SolutionStacks' \
    > available-platforms.json

echo "Platforms exported"
Platforms exported
# Export platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platforms Exported") print(f"\nFile: available-platforms.json") print(f"Count: 45") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

check-dns-availability

Checks if a CNAME prefix is available for use when creating a new Elastic Beanstalk environment. The CNAME creates a subdomain under elasticbeanstalk.com.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
  --cname-prefix <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --cname-prefix (string) [Required]: Desired CNAME prefix (subdomain) to check for availability
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region for the DNS availability check
Return Value
  • Available (boolean): Whether the CNAME prefix is available for use
  • FullyQualifiedCNAME (string): Full domain name if available (prefix.region.elasticbeanstalk.com)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Check DNS Availability for Production

Check if anihpj-prod CNAME is available for Django deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check production CNAME availability

PREFIX="anihpj-prod"

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --output json)

AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
CNAME=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
    echo "CNAME available: $CNAME"
else
    echo "CNAME not available"
fi
CNAME available: anihpj-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Check production DNS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production DNS Check") print(f"\nPrefix: anihpj-prod") print(f"Available: True") print(f"CNAME: anihpj-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check Unavailable DNS Name

Handle case when CNAME is already taken.

#!/bin/bash
# Check common name availability

PREFIX="production"

AVAILABLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX" \
    --query 'Available' \
    --output text)

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "False" ]; then
    echo "Name '$PREFIX' is already taken"
    echo "Try a more unique prefix"
fi
Name 'production' is already taken Try a more unique prefix
# Check unavailable DNS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Check Result") print(f"\nPrefix: production") print(f"Available: False") print(f"Status: Already taken") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Find Available DNS Variation

Loop through variations to find available CNAME.

#!/bin/bash
# Find available CNAME variation

BASE="anihpj-django"
SUFFIXES=("" "-app" "-web" "-api" "-prod")

for SUFFIX in "${SUFFIXES[@]}"; do
    PREFIX="${BASE}${SUFFIX}"
    RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
        --cname-prefix "$PREFIX")
    
    AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
    
    if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
        CNAME=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')
        echo "Found available: $PREFIX"
        echo "URL: http://$CNAME"
        break
    else
        echo "Taken: $PREFIX"
    fi
done
Taken: anihpj-django Found available: anihpj-django-app URL: http://anihpj-django-app.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com
# Find available variation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Variation Search") print(f"\nBase: anihpj-django") print(f"Found: anihpj-django-app") print(f"Available: True") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Check Multiple Environment CNAMEs

Validate all environment names before deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Check all environment CNAMEs

ENVIRONMENTS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-prod")
ALL_AVAILABLE=true

echo "Checking environment CNAMEs..."
for ENV in "${ENVIRONMENTS[@]}"; do
    AVAILABLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
        --cname-prefix "$ENV" \
        --query 'Available' \
        --output text)
    
    if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "True" ]; then
        echo "$ENV: Available"
    else
        echo "$ENV: Not available"
        ALL_AVAILABLE=false
    fi
done

if [ "$ALL_AVAILABLE" = true ]; then
    echo "All environment CNAMEs are available!"
fi
Checking environment CNAMEs... anihpj-dev: Available anihpj-staging: Available anihpj-prod: Available All environment CNAMEs are available!
# Check multiple environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Multi-Environment Check") print(f"\nanihpj-dev: Available") print(f"anihpj-staging: Available") print(f"anihpj-prod: Available") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export DNS Check to Configuration

Save available CNAME to environment configuration file.

#!/bin/bash
# Export DNS check result to config

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_TYPE="prod"

PREFIX="${APP_NAME}-${ENV_TYPE}"

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk check-dns-availability \
    --cname-prefix "$PREFIX")

AVAILABLE=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.Available')
FQDN=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.FullyQualifiedCNAME')

if [ "$AVAILABLE" = "true" ]; then
    echo "CNAME_PREFIX=$PREFIX" > env.config
    echo "FQDN=$FQDN" >> env.config
    echo "DNS_AVAILABLE=true" >> env.config
    echo "Configuration saved to env.config"
    cat env.config
else
    echo "CNAME not available, cannot proceed"
    exit 1
fi
Configuration saved to env.config CNAME_PREFIX=anihpj-jobpost-prod FQDN=anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com DNS_AVAILABLE=true
# Export DNS config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Configuration Exported") print(f"\nPrefix: anihpj-jobpost-prod") print(f"FQDN: anihpj-jobpost-prod.us-east-1.elasticbeanstalk.com") print(f"Config: env.config") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-storage-location

Creates an S3 bucket for Elastic Beanstalk to store application versions, logs, and other artifacts. Called automatically when needed, but can be invoked manually.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region for the storage bucket creation
Return Value
  • S3Bucket (string): Name of the S3 bucket created or already existing (elasticbeanstalk-region-account-id format)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Storage Bucket for Django App

Initialize Elastic Beanstalk storage bucket for application deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Create Elastic Beanstalk storage bucket

echo "Creating EB storage location..."

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --output json)

BUCKET=$(echo $RESULT | jq -r '.S3Bucket')

echo "Storage bucket: $BUCKET"
echo "Ready for Django application uploads"
Creating EB storage location... Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 Ready for Django application uploads
# Create storage bucket from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EB Storage Created") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"Region: us-east-1") print(f"Status: Ready") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create Storage in Specific Region

Create storage bucket in EU region for compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Create storage in EU region

REGION="eu-west-1"

BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --region "$REGION" \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "EU Storage bucket: $BUCKET"
echo "Region: $REGION"
EU Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-eu-west-1-123456789012 Region: eu-west-1
# Create EU storage from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EU Storage Created") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-eu-west-1-123456789012") print(f"Region: eu-west-1") print(f"GDPR Compliant: Yes") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get or Create Storage and List Contents

Ensure storage exists and view existing artifacts.

#!/bin/bash
# Get or create storage and list contents

BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "Using bucket: $BUCKET"
echo ""
echo "Current contents:"
aws s3 ls "s3://$BUCKET/" --human-readable

echo ""
echo "Application versions:"
aws s3 ls "s3://$BUCKET/resources/" --recursive 2>/dev/null || echo "No versions yet"
Using bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 Current contents: PRE resources/ PRE environments/ Application versions: 2024-01-15 10:30:00 5.2 MiB resources/anihpj-v1.zip
# List storage contents from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Storage Contents") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"Folders: resources, environments") print(f"Versions: 1") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Upload Django App to Storage

Create storage and upload application bundle.

#!/bin/bash
# Get bucket and upload Django application

APP_NAME="anihpj-django"
VERSION="v1.0.0"

# Get or create storage bucket
BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

echo "Storage bucket: $BUCKET"

# Create application bundle
zip -r "${APP_NAME}-${VERSION}.zip" . -x "*.git*" -x "__pycache__/*"

# Upload to EB storage
aws s3 cp "${APP_NAME}-${VERSION}.zip" \
    "s3://${BUCKET}/${APP_NAME}/versions/${VERSION}.zip"

echo "Django app uploaded: s3://${BUCKET}/${APP_NAME}/versions/${VERSION}.zip"
Storage bucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012 adding: application.py (deflated 65%) adding: requirements.txt (deflated 45%) upload: anihpj-django-v1.0.0.zip to s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/anihpj-django/versions/v1.0.0.zip Django app uploaded: s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/anihpj-django/versions/v1.0.0.zip
# Upload Django app from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django App Uploaded") print(f"\nApp: anihpj-django") print(f"Version: v1.0.0") print(f"Location: s3://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012/") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Bucket Name for CI/CD Pipeline

Save bucket name for use in GitHub Actions deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Export bucket name for CI/CD pipeline

# Get or create storage bucket
BUCKET=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-storage-location \
    --query 'S3Bucket' \
    --output text)

# Save to file for local use
echo "$BUCKET" > .eb-bucket

# Export for GitHub Actions
if [ -n "$GITHUB_ENV" ]; then
    echo "EB_BUCKET=$BUCKET" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "Exported EB_BUCKET to GitHub Actions"
fi

# Export for GitLab CI
if [ -n "$CI_PROJECT_DIR" ]; then
    echo "export EB_BUCKET=$BUCKET" >> .env
    echo "Exported EB_BUCKET for GitLab CI"
fi

echo "Bucket name saved: $BUCKET"
Exported EB_BUCKET to GitHub Actions Bucket name saved: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012
# Export bucket for CI/CD from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CI/CD Configuration") print(f"\nBucket: elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012") print(f"GitHub Env: EB_BUCKET set") print(f"Local File: .eb-bucket created") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

request-environment-info

Initiates compilation of environment logs or bundle info. The request is asynchronous - use retrieve-environment-info to get results.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
  --info-type <value> \
  [--environment-id <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --info-type (string) [Required]: Type of information to request - tail (last 100 lines) or bundle (full logs zipped)
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: Environment ID to request info from
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Environment name to request info from
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the environment
Return Value
  • No return value - request is initiated asynchronously
  • Use retrieve-environment-info after ~30 seconds to get the results
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Request Tail Logs for Django Environment

Request the last 100 lines of logs for debugging Django errors.

#!/bin/bash
# Request tail logs for Django environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Requesting tail logs for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type tail

echo "Log request initiated successfully"
echo "Wait 30 seconds, then use retrieve-environment-info to get logs"
Requesting tail logs for anihpj-prod... Log request initiated successfully Wait 30 seconds, then use retrieve-environment-info to get logs
# Request tail logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Log Request Initiated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Type: tail (last 100 lines)") print(f"Status: Pending") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Request Full Log Bundle

Request complete log bundle with all application logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Request full log bundle for comprehensive debugging

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Requesting full log bundle for $ENV_NAME..."
echo "This may take 1-2 minutes to compile"

aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type bundle

echo "Bundle request submitted"
echo "Contains: Apache logs, Django logs, deployment logs"
Requesting full log bundle for anihpj-prod... This may take 1-2 minutes to compile Bundle request submitted Contains: Apache logs, Django logs, deployment logs
# Request full bundle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bundle Request Initiated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Type: bundle (full logs)") print(f"Includes: Apache, Django, deployment") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Request Logs by Environment ID

Request logs using environment ID for precise targeting.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs using environment ID

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Get environment ID first
ENV_ID=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentId' \
    --output text)

echo "Environment ID: $ENV_ID"

# Request logs by ID
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-id "$ENV_ID" \
    --info-type tail

echo "Log request submitted for environment $ENV_ID"
Environment ID: e-abcd1234ef Log request submitted for environment e-abcd1234ef
# Request by environment ID from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Request by ID") print(f"\nEnvironment ID: e-abcd1234ef") print(f"Type: tail") print(f"Status: Submitted") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Request Logs and Wait for Retrieval

Complete workflow to request and retrieve logs.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs and wait for availability

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
INFO_TYPE="tail"

echo "Step 1: Requesting $INFO_TYPE logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE"

echo "Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds for compilation..."
sleep 30

echo "Step 3: Retrieving logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE" \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[0].Message' \
    --output text
Step 1: Requesting tail logs... Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds for compilation... Step 3: Retrieving logs... https://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012.s3.amazonaws.com/resources/environments/logs/tail/...
# Request and wait workflow from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Log Retrieval Workflow") print(f"\nStep 1: Request initiated") print(f"Step 2: Waited 30 seconds") print(f"Step 3: Logs retrieved") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Request Logs for Multiple Environments

Request logs from all Django environments for comparison.

#!/bin/bash
# Request logs from multiple environments

ENVIRONMENTS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-prod")
INFO_TYPE="tail"

echo "Requesting $INFO_TYPE logs from all environments..."

for ENV in "${ENVIRONMENTS[@]}"; do
    echo "Requesting logs for $ENV..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
        --environment-name "$ENV" \
        --info-type "$INFO_TYPE"
done

echo ""
echo "All requests submitted"
echo "Wait 30 seconds, then retrieve each environment's logs"
Requesting tail logs from all environments... Requesting logs for anihpj-dev... Requesting logs for anihpj-staging... Requesting logs for anihpj-prod... All requests submitted Wait 30 seconds, then retrieve each environment's logs
# Request multi-environment logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Multi-Environment Log Request") print(f"\nanihpj-dev: Requested") print(f"anihpj-staging: Requested") print(f"anihpj-prod: Requested") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

retrieve-environment-info

Retrieves environment logs or bundle previously requested with request-environment-info. Returns S3 URLs to download the information.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
  --info-type <value> \
  [--environment-id <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --info-type (string) [Required]: Type of information to retrieve - tail (last 100 lines) or bundle (full logs)
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: Environment ID to retrieve info from
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Environment name to retrieve info from
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the environment
Return Value
  • EnvironmentInfo (array): List of log information objects
  • InfoType (string): Type of info returned (tail or bundle)
  • Ec2InstanceId (string): ID of the instance the logs are from
  • SampleTimestamp (timestamp): When the logs were captured
  • Message (string): S3 presigned URL to download the logs
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Retrieve Tail Logs for Django Environment

Get the tail logs after requesting them.

#!/bin/bash
# Retrieve tail logs for Django environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Retrieving tail logs for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type tail \
    --output json
Retrieving tail logs for anihpj-prod... { "EnvironmentInfo": [ { "InfoType": "tail", "Ec2InstanceId": "i-0abc123def456", "SampleTimestamp": "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z", "Message": "https://elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-123456789012.s3.amazonaws.com/resources/environments/logs/tail/..." } ] }
# Retrieve tail logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tail Logs Retrieved") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Instance: i-0abc123def456") print(f"Type: tail") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Download Logs to Local File

Retrieve and download all log files locally.

#!/bin/bash
# Download logs to local file

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
OUTPUT_FILE="django-logs.txt"

echo "Retrieving log URLs..."
URLS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type tail \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[].Message' \
    --output text)

echo "Downloading logs..."
rm -f "$OUTPUT_FILE"
for URL in $URLS; do
    echo "Downloading from instance log..."
    curl -s "$URL" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
    echo "" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
done

echo "Logs saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
wc -l "$OUTPUT_FILE"
Retrieving log URLs... Downloading logs... Downloading from instance log... Logs saved to django-logs.txt 2543 django-logs.txt
# Download logs locally from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Logs Downloaded") print(f"\nFile: django-logs.txt") print(f"Lines: 2543") print(f"Instances: 1") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Download Full Log Bundle

Retrieve and download complete log bundle ZIP.

#!/bin/bash
# Download full log bundle

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
OUTPUT_FILE="full-logs.zip"

echo "Retrieving bundle URL..."
BUNDLE_URL=$(aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type bundle \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[0].Message' \
    --output text)

if [ -n "$BUNDLE_URL" ] && [ "$BUNDLE_URL" != "None" ]; then
    echo "Downloading bundle..."
    wget -q -O "$OUTPUT_FILE" "$BUNDLE_URL"
    echo "Bundle downloaded: $OUTPUT_FILE"
    ls -lh "$OUTPUT_FILE"
else
    echo "No bundle available. Request first with request-environment-info."
fi
Retrieving bundle URL... Downloading bundle... Bundle downloaded: full-logs.zip -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 15M Jan 15 10:30 full-logs.zip
# Download log bundle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bundle Downloaded") print(f"\nFile: full-logs.zip") print(f"Size: 15MB") print(f"Contents: Apache, Django, deployment logs") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Logs Per Instance

Retrieve logs organized by EC2 instance.

#!/bin/bash
# Get logs organized by instance

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Logs by instance for $ENV_NAME:"
echo ""

aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type tail \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[].{Instance:Ec2InstanceId,Timestamp:SampleTimestamp,URL:Message}' \
    --output table
Logs by instance for anihpj-prod: --------------------------------------------------------------------- | EnvironmentInfo | +----------------+------------------------+---------------------------+ | Instance | Timestamp | URL | +----------------+------------------------+---------------------------+ | i-0abc123def | 2024-01-15T10:00:00Z | https://s3.amazonaws... | | i-0def456abc | 2024-01-15T10:00:05Z | https://s3.amazonaws... | +----------------+------------------------+---------------------------+
# Logs per instance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Logs Per Instance") print(f"\ni-0abc123def: Available") print(f"i-0def456abc: Available") print(f"Total: 2 instances") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Complete Log Retrieval Workflow

Full workflow from request to download and search.

#!/bin/bash
# Complete log retrieval workflow

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
INFO_TYPE="tail"
OUTPUT_FILE="all-logs.txt"

echo "Step 1: Requesting logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk request-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE"

echo "Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds..."
sleep 30

echo "Step 3: Retrieving and downloading logs..."
aws elasticbeanstalk retrieve-environment-info \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --info-type "$INFO_TYPE" \
    --query 'EnvironmentInfo[].Message' \
    --output text | xargs -I{} curl -s "{}" > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

echo "Step 4: Searching for Django errors..."
grep -i "error\|exception\|traceback" "$OUTPUT_FILE" | head -20

echo ""
echo "All logs saved to $OUTPUT_FILE"
Step 1: Requesting logs... Step 2: Waiting 30 seconds... Step 3: Retrieving and downloading logs... Step 4: Searching for Django errors... Django error: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'jobpost' Traceback (most recent call last): All logs saved to all-logs.txt
# Complete log workflow from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Log Workflow Complete") print(f"\nFile: all-logs.txt") print(f"Errors found: Yes") print(f"Action: Check Django imports") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

abort-environment-update

Cancels an in-progress environment configuration update or deployment. Use to rollback when a deployment is failing or taking too long.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
  [--environment-id <value>] \
  [--environment-name <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: ID of the environment to abort update on
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Name of the environment to abort update on
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the environment
Return Value
  • No return value on success - the update is aborted silently
  • Environment returns to previous stable state
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Abort Failing Django Deployment

Cancel a stuck or failing deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Abort a stuck deployment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Aborting deployment for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Deployment aborted successfully"
echo "Environment will return to previous stable state"
Aborting deployment for anihpj-prod... Deployment aborted successfully Environment will return to previous stable state
# Abort deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deployment Aborted") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Status: Returning to stable") print(f"Action: Rollback complete") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Emergency Production Rollback

Abort and verify environment returns to ready state.

#!/bin/bash
# Emergency abort - production issues detected

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "EMERGENCY: Aborting production update!"

aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Waiting for environment to stabilize..."
sleep 10

# Check status
STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
    --output text)

echo "Environment status: $STATUS"
EMERGENCY: Aborting production update! Waiting for environment to stabilize... Environment status: Ready
# Emergency rollback from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Emergency Rollback") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Status: Ready") print(f"Rollback: Successful") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Abort Update by Environment ID

Use environment ID for precise targeting.

#!/bin/bash
# Abort using environment ID

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Get environment ID
ENV_ID=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentId' \
    --output text)

echo "Environment ID: $ENV_ID"
echo "Aborting update..."

aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
    --environment-id "$ENV_ID"

echo "Update aborted for $ENV_ID"
Environment ID: e-abcd1234ef Aborting update... Update aborted for e-abcd1234ef
# Abort by ID from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Abort by Environment ID") print(f"\nEnvironment ID: e-abcd1234ef") print(f"Status: Aborted") print(f"Result: Success") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Conditional Abort If Updating

Check status before attempting abort.

#!/bin/bash
# Only abort if actually updating

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Check current status
STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
    --output text)

echo "Current status: $STATUS"

if [ "$STATUS" = "Updating" ]; then
    echo "Update in progress - aborting..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
        --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"
    echo "Update aborted successfully"
else
    echo "No update in progress - nothing to abort"
fi
Current status: Updating Update in progress - aborting... Update aborted successfully
# Conditional abort from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Conditional Abort") print(f"\nStatus checked: Updating") print(f"Action: Abort executed") print(f"Result: Success") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: CI/CD Timeout Abort Protection

Abort deployment if it exceeds timeout in CI/CD pipeline.

#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD timeout abort protection

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
TIMEOUT=600  # 10 minutes

echo "Monitoring deployment with ${TIMEOUT}s timeout..."
START=$(date +%s)

while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
        --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
        --query 'Environments[0].Status' \
        --output text)
    
    if [ "$STATUS" = "Ready" ]; then
        echo "Deployment completed successfully!"
        break
    fi
    
    ELAPSED=$(($(date +%s) - START))
    
    if [ $ELAPSED -gt $TIMEOUT ]; then
        echo "TIMEOUT: Deployment exceeded ${TIMEOUT}s"
        echo "Aborting deployment..."
        aws elasticbeanstalk abort-environment-update \
            --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"
        echo "Deployment aborted - check logs for issues"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Status: $STATUS (${ELAPSED}s elapsed)"
    sleep 30
done
Monitoring deployment with 600s timeout... Status: Updating (30s elapsed) Status: Updating (60s elapsed) Deployment completed successfully!
# CI/CD timeout protection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"CI/CD Timeout Protection") print(f"\nTimeout: 600 seconds") print(f"Status: Completed") print(f"Duration: 60 seconds") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-tags-for-resource

Returns tags associated with an Elastic Beanstalk resource (application, environment, etc.). Use for cost allocation and resource organization.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-arn <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string) [Required]: ARN of the Elastic Beanstalk resource to list tags for
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the resource
Return Value
  • ResourceArn (string): ARN of the queried resource
  • ResourceTags (array): List of key-value tag pairs
  • Key (string): Tag key name
  • Value (string): Tag value
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List Environment Tags

Get all tags for a Django production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Get tags for production environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Get environment ARN
ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Environment ARN: $ENV_ARN"
echo ""
echo "Tags:"

aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --output json
Environment ARN: arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:environment/anihpj-jobpost/anihpj-prod Tags: { "ResourceArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:environment/anihpj-jobpost/anihpj-prod", "ResourceTags": [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"}, {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "CostCenter", "Value": "engineering"} ] }
# List environment tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Tags") print(f"\nEnvironment: production") print(f"Project: anihpj") print(f"CostCenter: engineering") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: List Application Tags

Get tags for the Elastic Beanstalk application.

#!/bin/bash
# Get tags for application

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

# Get application ARN
APP_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --application-names "$APP_NAME" \
    --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Application: $APP_NAME"
echo ""

aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$APP_ARN" \
    --query 'ResourceTags'
Application: anihpj-jobpost [ {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "Team", "Value": "backend"}, {"Key": "Framework", "Value": "Django"} ]
# List application tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Tags") print(f"\nProject: anihpj") print(f"Team: backend") print(f"Framework: Django") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Extract Specific Tag Value

Get a specific tag value for cost allocation.

#!/bin/bash
# Get CostCenter tag value

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

# Extract specific tag
COST_CENTER=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --query "ResourceTags[?Key=='CostCenter'].Value" \
    --output text)

PROJECT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --query "ResourceTags[?Key=='Project'].Value" \
    --output text)

echo "Cost Center: $COST_CENTER"
echo "Project: $PROJECT"
Cost Center: engineering Project: anihpj
# Extract specific tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tag Extraction") print(f"\nCost Center: engineering") print(f"Project: anihpj") print(f"Use: Billing allocation") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Display Tags as Table

Format tags in a readable table format.

#!/bin/bash
# Display tags in table format

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Tags for $ENV_NAME:"
echo ""

aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --query 'ResourceTags[].{Key:Key,Value:Value}' \
    --output table
Tags for anihpj-prod: ------------------------------- | ResourceTags | +-------------+---------------+ | Key | Value | +-------------+---------------+ | Environment | production | | Project | anihpj | | CostCenter | engineering | | Team | backend | +-------------+---------------+
# Tags as table from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags Table") print(f"\nEnvironment: production") print(f"Project: anihpj") print(f"CostCenter: engineering") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Export Tags for Backup

Export all environment tags to JSON file for backup.

#!/bin/bash
# Export all environment tags for backup

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
BACKUP_FILE="env-tags-backup.json"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Exporting tags for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    > "$BACKUP_FILE"

echo "Tags exported to $BACKUP_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Contents:"
cat "$BACKUP_FILE" | jq '.ResourceTags'
Exporting tags for anihpj-prod... Tags exported to env-tags-backup.json Contents: [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"}, {"Key": "Project", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "CostCenter", "Value": "engineering"} ]
# Export tags backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags Backup") print(f"\nFile: env-tags-backup.json") print(f"Tags: 3 exported") print(f"Format: JSON") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-tags-for-resource

Adds, updates, or removes tags from an Elastic Beanstalk resource. Use for cost tracking, organization, and compliance labeling.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-arn <value> \
  [--tags-to-add <value>] \
  [--tags-to-remove <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-arn (string) [Required]: ARN of the Elastic Beanstalk resource to update tags on
  • --tags-to-add (list) [Optional]: Tags to add or update in Key=string,Value=string format
  • --tags-to-remove (list) [Optional]: Tag key names to remove from the resource
  • --region (string) [Optional]: AWS region of the resource
Return Value
  • No return value on success - tags are updated silently
  • Use list-tags-for-resource to verify changes
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Add Environment Tags for Cost Allocation

Add cost allocation and project tags to Django environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Add cost allocation tags to environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

# Get environment ARN
ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Adding tags to $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --tags-to-add \
        Key=Environment,Value=production \
        Key=Project,Value=anihpj \
        Key=CostCenter,Value=engineering

echo "Tags added successfully"
Adding tags to anihpj-prod... Tags added successfully
# Add environment tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags Added") print(f"\nEnvironment: production") print(f"Project: anihpj") print(f"CostCenter: engineering") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Update Existing Tag Value

Change the value of an existing tag.

#!/bin/bash
# Update cost center tag value

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Updating CostCenter tag..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --tags-to-add Key=CostCenter,Value=platform-team

echo "CostCenter updated to: platform-team"
Updating CostCenter tag... CostCenter updated to: platform-team
# Update tag value from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tag Updated") print(f"\nCostCenter: platform-team") print(f"Previous: engineering") print(f"Status: Updated") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Remove Deprecated Tags

Remove old or deprecated tags from environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove deprecated tags

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Removing deprecated tags..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --tags-to-remove OldTag DeprecatedLabel TempTag

echo "Deprecated tags removed"
Removing deprecated tags... Deprecated tags removed
# Remove deprecated tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags Removed") print(f"\nOldTag: Removed") print(f"DeprecatedLabel: Removed") print(f"TempTag: Removed") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Add and Remove Tags Simultaneously

Replace team assignment tags in single operation.

#!/bin/bash
# Replace team tag - add new and remove old

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

ENV_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].EnvironmentArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Updating team assignment..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$ENV_ARN" \
    --tags-to-add Key=Team,Value=devops Key=Owner,Value=devops-lead \
    --tags-to-remove OldTeam LegacyOwner

echo "Team assignment updated"
Updating team assignment... Team assignment updated
# Add and remove tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tag Replacement") print(f"\nAdded: Team=devops, Owner=devops-lead") print(f"Removed: OldTeam, LegacyOwner") print(f"Status: Complete") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Tag Application with Full Metadata

Apply comprehensive tags to Elastic Beanstalk application.

#!/bin/bash
# Tag application with full metadata

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

# Get application ARN
APP_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --application-names "$APP_NAME" \
    --query 'Applications[0].ApplicationArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Tagging application: $APP_NAME"

aws elasticbeanstalk update-tags-for-resource \
    --resource-arn "$APP_ARN" \
    --tags-to-add \
        Key=Application,Value=anihpj-jobpost \
        Key=Framework,Value=Django \
        Key=Owner,Value=backend-team \
        Key=Repository,Value=github.com/anihpj/jobpost \
        Key=Documentation,Value=docs.anihpj.com

echo "Application tagged with full metadata"
Tagging application: anihpj-jobpost Application tagged with full metadata
# Tag application from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Tagged") print(f"\nFramework: Django") print(f"Owner: backend-team") print(f"Tags: 5 applied") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

apply-environment-managed-action

Applies a scheduled managed action immediately. Managed actions are platform updates, patches, or maintenance operations that Elastic Beanstalk schedules for your environment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
  --environment-name <environment-name> \
  --action-id <action-id> \
  [--environment-id <environment-id>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Name of the environment to apply the action to
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: ID of the environment (alternative to name)
  • --action-id (string) [Required]: The action ID of the scheduled managed action to execute
Return Value
  • ActionId (string): ID of the applied action
  • ActionDescription (string): Description of what the action does
  • ActionType (string): Type of action - InstanceRefresh, PlatformUpdate, or Unknown
  • Status (string): Current status - Scheduled, Pending, Running, or Unknown
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Apply Pending Platform Update

Apply a scheduled platform update immediately for Django environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Apply a scheduled platform update immediately

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
ACTION_ID="action-12345abcde"

echo "Applying platform update to $ENV_NAME..."

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --action-id "$ACTION_ID" \
    --output json)

echo "$RESULT" | jq '.'
echo "Managed action applied successfully"
Applying platform update to anihpj-prod... { "ActionId": "action-12345abcde", "ActionDescription": "Platform update to Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Running" } Managed action applied successfully
# Apply platform update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Update Applied") print(f"\nAction ID: action-12345abcde") print(f"Type: PlatformUpdate") print(f"Status: Running") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Apply Instance Refresh Action

Apply instance refresh to update EC2 instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Apply instance refresh action

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"
ACTION_ID="refresh-67890fghij"

echo "Starting instance refresh for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --action-id "$ACTION_ID"

echo "Instance refresh initiated"
Starting instance refresh for anihpj-staging... { "ActionId": "refresh-67890fghij", "ActionDescription": "Refresh EC2 instances with latest AMI", "ActionType": "InstanceRefresh", "Status": "Running" } Instance refresh initiated
# Instance refresh from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Instance Refresh Started") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-staging") print(f"Type: InstanceRefresh") print(f"Status: Running") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Apply Action by Environment ID

Apply managed action using environment ID.

#!/bin/bash
# Apply action using environment ID

ENV_ID="e-abcd1234ef"
ACTION_ID="action-maint-99999"

echo "Applying maintenance action by environment ID..."

aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
    --environment-id "$ENV_ID" \
    --action-id "$ACTION_ID"

echo "Maintenance action applied"
Applying maintenance action by environment ID... { "ActionId": "action-maint-99999", "ActionDescription": "Security patch update", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Pending" } Maintenance action applied
# Apply by environment ID from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Maintenance Action Applied") print(f"\nEnvironment ID: e-abcd1234ef") print(f"Action: Security patch") print(f"Status: Pending") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Apply Action with Status Monitoring

Apply action and monitor its progress.

#!/bin/bash
# Apply action and monitor status

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
ACTION_ID="action-update-12345"

echo "Applying managed action..."
aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --action-id "$ACTION_ID"

echo ""
echo "Checking action status..."
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --status Executing \
    --query 'ManagedActions[].{ID:ActionId,Status:Status,Type:ActionType}' \
    --output table

echo "Action applied and monitoring started"
Applying managed action... { "ActionId": "action-update-12345", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Running" } Checking action status... ----------------------------------------- | ManagedActions | +-------------------+----------+--------+ | ID | Status | Type | +-------------------+----------+--------+ | action-update-12345| Running | PlatformUpdate | +-------------------+----------+--------+ Action applied and monitoring started
# Apply and monitor from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Action Applied with Monitoring") print(f"\nAction ID: action-update-12345") print(f"Status: Running") print(f"Monitoring: Active") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Apply All Pending Managed Actions

Apply all scheduled actions in batch.

#!/bin/bash
# Apply all pending managed actions for an environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Fetching scheduled actions for $ENV_NAME..."

# Get all pending actions
ACTIONS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --status Scheduled \
    --query 'ManagedActions[].ActionId' \
    --output text)

if [ -z "$ACTIONS" ]; then
    echo "No pending actions found"
    exit 0
fi

echo "Found actions: $ACTIONS"
echo ""

for ACTION_ID in $ACTIONS; do
    echo "Applying action: $ACTION_ID"
    aws elasticbeanstalk apply-environment-managed-action \
        --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
        --action-id "$ACTION_ID"
    echo ""
done

echo "All pending actions applied"
Fetching scheduled actions for anihpj-prod... Found actions: action-12345abcde action-67890fghij Applying action: action-12345abcde {"ActionId": "action-12345abcde", "Status": "Running"} Applying action: action-67890fghij {"ActionId": "action-67890fghij", "Status": "Running"} All pending actions applied
# Batch apply actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Actions Applied") print(f"\nActions: 2 applied") print(f"Status: All running") print(f"Environment: anihpj-prod") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

associate-environment-operations-role

Adds an operations role to an environment. The operations role allows Elastic Beanstalk to perform enhanced health monitoring and managed platform updates on your behalf.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
  --environment-name <environment-name> \
  --operations-role <iam-role-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string) [Required]: Name of the environment to associate the operations role with
  • --operations-role (string) [Required]: ARN of the IAM role for Elastic Beanstalk operations
Return Value
  • No return value on success (HTTP 200)
  • Role is silently associated with the environment
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Associate Operations Role with Production

Add operations role to Django production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate operations role with production environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-operations-role"

echo "Associating operations role with $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --operations-role "$ROLE_ARN"

echo "Operations role associated successfully"
echo "Enhanced monitoring and managed updates enabled"
Associating operations role with anihpj-prod... Operations role associated successfully Enhanced monitoring and managed updates enabled
# Associate operations role from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Operations Role Associated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Role: eb-operations-role") print(f"Features: Enhanced monitoring, managed updates") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Associate Service-Linked Role

Use AWS service-linked role for enhanced monitoring.

#!/bin/bash
# Use service-linked role for enhanced monitoring

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"
SERVICE_ROLE="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role"

echo "Enabling enhanced monitoring for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --operations-role "$SERVICE_ROLE"

echo "Service role associated for enhanced health monitoring"
Enabling enhanced monitoring for anihpj-staging... Service role associated for enhanced health monitoring
# Service-linked role from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service Role Associated") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-staging") print(f"Role: aws-elasticbeanstalk-service-role") print(f"Status: Enhanced monitoring enabled") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Associate Custom Operations Role

Associate a custom IAM role with specific permissions.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate custom operations role with specific permissions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
CUSTOM_ROLE="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-eb-operations"

echo "Associating custom operations role..."
echo "Role: $CUSTOM_ROLE"

aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --operations-role "$CUSTOM_ROLE"

echo "Custom operations role configured successfully"
Associating custom operations role... Role: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-eb-operations Custom operations role configured successfully
# Custom operations role from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Custom Role Configured") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Role: anihpj-eb-operations") print(f"Permissions: Custom") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Associate Role to All Environments

Batch associate operations role to all application environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate operations role to all environments

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-operations-role"

echo "Fetching environments for $APP_NAME..."

# Get all ready environments
ENVS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[?Status==`Ready`].EnvironmentName' \
    --output text)

echo "Found environments: $ENVS"
echo ""

for ENV in $ENVS; do
    echo "Associating role with $ENV..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
        --environment-name "$ENV" \
        --operations-role "$ROLE_ARN"
    echo "Role associated with $ENV"
done

echo ""
echo "All environments configured"
Fetching environments for anihpj-jobpost... Found environments: anihpj-prod anihpj-staging anihpj-dev Associating role with anihpj-prod... Role associated with anihpj-prod Associating role with anihpj-staging... Role associated with anihpj-staging Associating role with anihpj-dev... Role associated with anihpj-dev All environments configured
# Batch role association from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Role Association") print(f"\nanihpj-prod: Configured") print(f"anihpj-staging: Configured") print(f"anihpj-dev: Configured") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Associate and Verify Role

Associate role and verify the configuration.

#!/bin/bash
# Associate and verify operations role

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
ROLE_ARN="arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-operations-role"

echo "Associating operations role..."

aws elasticbeanstalk associate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --operations-role "$ROLE_ARN"

echo ""
echo "Verifying role association..."

# Verify by describing environment
CONFIGURED_ROLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].OperationsRole' \
    --output text)

echo "Configured role: $CONFIGURED_ROLE"

if [ "$CONFIGURED_ROLE" = "$ROLE_ARN" ]; then
    echo "Role association verified successfully"
else
    echo "Warning: Role mismatch"
fi
Associating operations role... Verifying role association... Configured role: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-operations-role Role association verified successfully
# Verify role association from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Role Verification") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Expected: eb-operations-role") print(f"Actual: eb-operations-role") print(f"Status: Verified") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

compose-environments

Creates and launches an environment for each specified environment group (tier). This command creates linked environments that can communicate with each other as part of a single application deployment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
  --application-name <application-name> \
  [--group-name <group-name>] \
  [--version-labels <label1> <label2> ...]
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string) [Required]: Name of the application containing the versions to deploy
  • --group-name (string) [Optional]: Name of the group to which environments belong
  • --version-labels (list) [Optional]: List of version labels to use for each environment group
Return Value
  • Environments (array): List of created environments
  • EnvironmentName (string): Name of each environment
  • EnvironmentId (string): ID of each environment
  • ApplicationName (string): Application the environment belongs to
  • VersionLabel (string): Version deployed to the environment
  • Status (string): Current status - Launching, Updating, Ready, Terminating, or Terminated
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Compose Multi-Tier Django Environments

Create linked frontend and backend environments for Django application.

#!/bin/bash
# Create linked frontend and backend environments

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
GROUP="production"

echo "Composing multi-tier environments..."

aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --group-name "$GROUP" \
    --version-labels v1.0.0-frontend v1.0.0-backend

echo "Multi-tier environments composed successfully"
Composing multi-tier environments... { "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-frontend-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-abc123def", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "VersionLabel": "v1.0.0-frontend", "Status": "Launching" }, { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-backend-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-xyz789ghi", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "VersionLabel": "v1.0.0-backend", "Status": "Launching" } ] } Multi-tier environments composed successfully
# Multi-tier deployment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Multi-Tier Deployment") print(f"\nFrontend: anihpj-frontend-prod") print(f"Backend: anihpj-backend-prod") print(f"Status: Launching") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Compose Staging Environment Group

Create environments for staging group.

#!/bin/bash
# Create environments for staging group

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Composing staging environment group..."

aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --group-name staging

echo "Staging environment group composed"
Composing staging environment group... { "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-staging-web", "EnvironmentId": "e-stg123abc", "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "Status": "Launching" } ] } Staging environment group composed
# Staging group from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging Environment Created") print(f"\nGroup: staging") print(f"Environment: anihpj-staging-web") print(f"Status: Launching") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Compose Web and Worker Tiers

Create web and worker tier environments for Django.

#!/bin/bash
# Create web and worker tier environments

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating web and worker tier environments..."

aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --group-name production \
    --version-labels anihpj-web-v2.0 anihpj-worker-v2.0

echo "Web and worker environments created"
Creating web and worker tier environments... { "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-web-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-web123456", "Status": "Launching" }, { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-worker-prod", "EnvironmentId": "e-wrk789012", "Status": "Launching" } ] } Web and worker environments created
# Web and worker tiers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Web and Worker Deployment") print(f"\nWeb Tier: anihpj-web-prod") print(f"Worker Tier: anihpj-worker-prod") print(f"Purpose: API + Background tasks") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Compose Development Environment

Create development environment group for testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Create development environment group

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Creating development environment..."

aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --group-name development \
    --version-labels dev-latest

echo "Development environment composed"
Creating development environment... { "Environments": [ { "EnvironmentName": "anihpj-dev-all", "EnvironmentId": "e-dev567890", "VersionLabel": "dev-latest", "Status": "Launching" } ] } Development environment composed
# Development environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Development Environment") print(f"\nName: anihpj-dev-all") print(f"Version: dev-latest") print(f"Group: development") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Compose and Monitor Environment Status

Create environments and wait for them to be ready.

#!/bin/bash
# Compose and wait for environments to be ready

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
GROUP="production"

echo "Composing environments..."

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk compose-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --group-name "$GROUP" \
    --version-labels v3.0.0-api v3.0.0-web \
    --output json)

echo "Environments launching..."
echo ""

# Extract environment names and wait
ENV_NAMES=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.Environments[].EnvironmentName')

for ENV in $ENV_NAMES; do
    echo "Waiting for $ENV..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-exists --environment-names "$ENV"
    echo "$ENV is ready"
done

echo ""
echo "All composed environments are ready"
Composing environments... Environments launching... Waiting for anihpj-api-prod... anihpj-api-prod is ready Waiting for anihpj-web-prod... anihpj-web-prod is ready All composed environments are ready
# Compose and monitor from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Compose and Monitor") print(f"\nanihpj-api-prod: Ready") print(f"anihpj-web-prod: Ready") print(f"Total: 2 environments") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-platform-version

Creates a new version of a custom platform. Custom platforms allow you to create your own Elastic Beanstalk platform with custom AMIs and configurations for specialized Django deployment needs.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
  --platform-name <platform-name> \
  --platform-version <version-string> \
  --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=<bucket>,S3Key=<key> \
  [--environment-name <environment-name>] \
  [--option-settings <settings>] \
  [--tags <tags>]
Parameters Description
  • --platform-name (string) [Required]: Name of the custom platform
  • --platform-version (string) [Required]: Version string for the platform
  • --platform-definition-bundle (structure) [Required]: S3 location of the platform definition bundle
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Name of the builder environment
  • --option-settings (list) [Optional]: Configuration options for the platform
  • --tags (list) [Optional]: Tags for the platform version
Return Value
  • PlatformSummary (object): Summary of the created platform
  • PlatformArn (string): ARN of the platform
  • PlatformOwner (string): Owner of the platform (self for custom)
  • PlatformStatus (string): Creating, Failed, Ready, Deleting, or Deleted
  • SupportedTierList (array): WebServer/Standard, Worker/SQS/HTTP
  • Builder (object): ARN of the builder environment
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Custom Django Platform

Create a custom platform for Django application deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# Create a custom Django platform version

PLATFORM_NAME="anihpj-django-platform"
VERSION="1.0.0"
BUCKET="anihpj-platforms"

echo "Creating custom platform $PLATFORM_NAME v$VERSION..."

aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
    --platform-name "$PLATFORM_NAME" \
    --platform-version "$VERSION" \
    --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=$BUCKET,S3Key=django-platform-v1.zip

echo "Custom platform version created"
Creating custom platform anihpj-django-platform v1.0.0... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/1.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "self", "PlatformStatus": "Creating", "PlatformCategory": "custom" }, "Builder": { "ARN": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:environment/builder-env" } } Custom platform version created
# Custom platform creation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Custom Platform Creation") print(f"\nName: anihpj-django-platform") print(f"Version: 1.0.0") print(f"Status: Creating") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create Platform with Tags

Create platform with organization tags for tracking.

#!/bin/bash
# Create platform with organization tags

echo "Creating tagged platform..."

aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
    --platform-name anihpj-django-optimized \
    --platform-version 2.0.0 \
    --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-platforms,S3Key=optimized-v2.zip \
    --tags Key=Project,Value=anihpj Key=Environment,Value=production

echo "Tagged platform version created"
Creating tagged platform... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-optimized/2.0.0", "PlatformStatus": "Creating", "OperatingSystemName": "Amazon Linux 2023" } } Tagged platform version created
# Tagged platform from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tagged Platform Creation") print(f"\nName: anihpj-django-optimized") print(f"Version: 2.0.0") print(f"Tags: Project=anihpj, Environment=production") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Create Platform with Option Settings

Create platform with custom configuration options.

#!/bin/bash
# Create platform with custom settings

echo "Creating platform with options..."

aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
    --platform-name anihpj-python311-platform \
    --platform-version 1.0.0 \
    --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-platforms,S3Key=python311.zip \
    --option-settings Namespace=aws:elasticbeanstalk:environment,OptionName=EnvironmentType,Value=SingleInstance

echo "Platform with custom options created"
Creating platform with options... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-python311-platform/1.0.0", "PlatformStatus": "Creating", "SupportedTierList": ["WebServer/Standard", "Worker/SQS/HTTP"] } } Platform with custom options created
# Platform with options from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform with Options") print(f"\nName: anihpj-python311-platform") print(f"Python: 3.11") print(f"Type: SingleInstance") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Create Major Version Upgrade

Create new major version of existing platform.

#!/bin/bash
# Create new major version of platform

PLATFORM="anihpj-django-platform"

echo "Creating major version upgrade..."

aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
    --platform-name "$PLATFORM" \
    --platform-version 2.0.0 \
    --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-platforms,S3Key=django-v2.zip \
    --tags Key=MajorVersion,Value=2 Key=BreakingChanges,Value=true

echo "Major platform version 2.0.0 created"
Creating major version upgrade... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/2.0.0", "PlatformStatus": "Creating" } } Major platform version 2.0.0 created
# Major version upgrade from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Major Version Upgrade") print(f"\nPlatform: anihpj-django-platform") print(f"Version: 2.0.0") print(f"Django: 5.x support") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Create and Wait for Platform Ready

Create platform and wait for it to become ready.

#!/bin/bash
# Create platform and wait for it to be ready

PLATFORM_NAME="anihpj-django-platform"
VERSION="3.0.0"

echo "Creating platform version..."

RESULT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk create-platform-version \
    --platform-name "$PLATFORM_NAME" \
    --platform-version "$VERSION" \
    --platform-definition-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-platforms,S3Key=v3.zip \
    --output json)

PLATFORM_ARN=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.PlatformSummary.PlatformArn')
echo "Platform ARN: $PLATFORM_ARN"

# Wait for platform to be ready
while true; do
    STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
        --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN" \
        --query 'PlatformDescription.PlatformStatus' --output text)
    
    if [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ]; then
        echo "Platform $VERSION is ready"
        break
    fi
    echo "Status: $STATUS - waiting..."
    sleep 30
done
Creating platform version... Platform ARN: arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/3.0.0 Status: Creating - waiting... Status: Creating - waiting... Platform 3.0.0 is ready
# Create and wait from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Create and Wait") print(f"\nPlatform: anihpj-django-platform") print(f"Version: 3.0.0") print(f"Status: Ready") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-environment-configuration

Deletes the draft configuration associated with the running environment. This resets environment configuration settings to their default values.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
  --application-name <application-name> \
  --environment-name <environment-name>
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string) [Required]: Name of the application to delete configuration from
  • --environment-name (string) [Required]: Name of the environment to delete configuration from
Return Value
  • None: No output on success (HTTP 200)
  • Draft configuration is deleted and reset to defaults
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Environment Draft Configuration

Delete draft configuration from production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete draft configuration from environment

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Deleting draft configuration..."

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Environment configuration deleted"
Deleting draft configuration... Environment configuration deleted
# Configuration deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Configuration Deletion") print(f"\nApp: anihpj-jobpost") print(f"Env: anihpj-prod") print(f"Status: Deleted") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Reset Staging Environment Config

Reset staging configuration to default values.

#!/bin/bash
# Reset staging configuration to defaults

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"

echo "Resetting staging configuration..."

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Staging configuration reset to defaults"
Resetting staging configuration... Staging configuration reset to defaults
# Staging reset from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Staging Configuration Reset") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-staging") print(f"Action: Reset to defaults") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Clean Configuration Before Deployment

Clear old configuration before deploying new version.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean config before deploying new version

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_NAME="anihpj-dev"

echo "Clearing old configuration..."

# Delete old configuration
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Old configuration cleared"
echo "Ready for fresh deployment"
Clearing old configuration... Old configuration cleared Ready for fresh deployment
# Pre-deployment cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Pre-Deployment Cleanup") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-dev") print(f"Config: Cleared") print(f"Ready: True") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Delete Config Across All Environments

Delete configuration from all environments in application.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete configuration from all environments

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"

echo "Finding all environments..."

ENVS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[].EnvironmentName' --output text)

for ENV in $ENVS; do
    echo "Deleting config from $ENV..."
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
        --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
        --environment-name "$ENV"
    echo "Configuration deleted from $ENV"
done

echo ""
echo "All configurations cleared"
Finding all environments... Deleting config from anihpj-prod... Configuration deleted from anihpj-prod Deleting config from anihpj-staging... Configuration deleted from anihpj-staging Deleting config from anihpj-dev... Configuration deleted from anihpj-dev All configurations cleared
