# Batch config deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Configuration Deletion") print(f"\nEnvironments: 3") print(f"- anihpj-prod") print(f"- anihpj-staging") print(f"- anihpj-dev") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Delete and Verify Configuration Reset

Delete configuration and verify the reset occurred.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete config and verify reset

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Deleting draft configuration..."

# Delete draft configuration
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-environment-configuration \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Configuration deleted"
echo ""

# Verify by getting current settings
DATE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-configuration-settings \
    --application-name "$APP_NAME" \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'ConfigurationSettings[0].DateUpdated' --output text)

echo "Last updated: $DATE"
echo "Configuration reset verified"
Deleting draft configuration... Configuration deleted Last updated: 2024-01-15T12:00:00Z Configuration reset verified
# Verify reset from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Configuration Reset Verification") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-prod") print(f"Action: Reset") print(f"Verified: True") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

delete-platform-version

Deletes a custom platform version. You can only delete platform versions that are not in use by any environment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version \
  --platform-arn <platform-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --platform-arn (string) [Required]: ARN of the platform version to delete
Return Value
  • PlatformSummary (object): Summary of the deleted platform
  • PlatformArn (string): ARN of the deleted platform
  • PlatformOwner (string): Owner of the platform (self for custom)
  • PlatformStatus (string): Status changes to Deleting
  • PlatformCategory (string): Category of the platform
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Platform Version by ARN

Delete a custom platform version using its ARN.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete a custom platform version

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/1.0.0"

echo "Deleting platform version..."

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"

echo "Platform version deleted"
Deleting platform version... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/1.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "self", "PlatformStatus": "Deleting" } } Platform version deleted
# Platform deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Deletion") print(f"\nName: anihpj-django-platform") print(f"Version: 1.0.0") print(f"Status: Deleting") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Delete Old Platform Version

Delete an outdated platform version.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete outdated platform version

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/0.9.0"

echo "Deleting old platform v0.9.0..."

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"

echo "Old platform v0.9.0 deleted"
Deleting old platform v0.9.0... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/0.9.0", "PlatformStatus": "Deleting" } } Old platform v0.9.0 deleted
# Old platform cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old Platform Cleanup") print(f"\nVersion: 0.9.0") print(f"Status: Deleted") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Delete Multiple Old Versions

Delete all platform versions older than v2.0.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete all platform versions older than v2.0

PLATFORM_NAME="anihpj-django-platform"
ACCOUNT_ID="123456789012"
REGION="us-east-1"

OLD_VERSIONS=("0.9.0" "1.0.0" "1.1.0" "1.2.0")

echo "Deleting old platform versions..."

for VERSION in "${OLD_VERSIONS[@]}"; do
    PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:$REGION:$ACCOUNT_ID:platform/$PLATFORM_NAME/$VERSION"
    aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"
    echo "Deleted version $VERSION"
done

echo ""
echo "All old versions deleted"
Deleting old platform versions... Deleted version 0.9.0 Deleted version 1.0.0 Deleted version 1.1.0 Deleted version 1.2.0 All old versions deleted
# Batch version deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Version Deletion") print(f"\nVersions deleted: 4") print(f"- 0.9.0, 1.0.0, 1.1.0, 1.2.0") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Delete Failed Platform Build

Clean up a failed platform build.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete a failed platform version

FAILED_PLATFORM="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-failed-build/1.0.0"

echo "Deleting failed platform build..."

aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$FAILED_PLATFORM"

echo "Failed platform build deleted"
Deleting failed platform build... { "PlatformSummary": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-failed-build/1.0.0", "PlatformStatus": "Deleting" } } Failed platform build deleted
# Failed build cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Failed Build Cleanup") print(f"\nPlatform: anihpj-failed-build") print(f"Status: Cleaned up") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Delete Unused Custom Platforms

Find and delete platforms not in use by any environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Find and delete unused custom platforms

echo "Finding unused platforms..."

PLATFORMS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --filters Type=PlatformOwner,Values=self \
    --query 'PlatformSummaryList[?PlatformStatus==`Ready`].PlatformArn' --output text)

for PLATFORM_ARN in $PLATFORMS; do
    # Check if platform is in use
    IN_USE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
        --query "Environments[?PlatformArn=='$PLATFORM_ARN'].EnvironmentName" --output text)
    
    if [ -z "$IN_USE" ]; then
        aws elasticbeanstalk delete-platform-version --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"
        echo "Deleted unused: $PLATFORM_ARN"
    else
        echo "In use by: $IN_USE"
    fi
done

echo ""
echo "Cleanup complete"
Finding unused platforms... Deleted unused: arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/old-platform/1.0.0 In use by: anihpj-prod Deleted unused: arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/test-platform/1.0.0 Cleanup complete
# Unused platforms cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Unused Platforms Cleanup") print(f"\nDeleted: 2 platforms") print(f"Skipped: 1 (in use)") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-account-attributes

Returns attributes related to AWS Elastic Beanstalk in your account, including the number of applications, environments, and other resource quotas.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes
Parameters Description
  • None: This command takes no parameters
Return Value
  • ResourceQuotas (object): Container for all quota information
  • ApplicationQuota.Maximum (integer): Maximum number of applications (default 75)
  • ApplicationVersionQuota.Maximum (integer): Maximum versions (default 1000)
  • EnvironmentQuota.Maximum (integer): Maximum environments (default 200)
  • ConfigurationTemplateQuota.Maximum (integer): Maximum config templates (default 2000)
  • CustomPlatformQuota.Maximum (integer): Maximum custom platforms (default 50)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Account Quotas

Retrieve Elastic Beanstalk account quotas.

#!/bin/bash
# Get Elastic Beanstalk account quotas

echo "Getting account attributes..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes

echo ""
echo "Account attributes retrieved"
Getting account attributes... { "ResourceQuotas": { "ApplicationQuota": { "Maximum": 75 }, "ApplicationVersionQuota": { "Maximum": 1000 }, "EnvironmentQuota": { "Maximum": 200 }, "ConfigurationTemplateQuota": { "Maximum": 2000 }, "CustomPlatformQuota": { "Maximum": 50 } } } Account attributes retrieved
# Account quotas from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Account Quotas") print(f"\nApplications: 75") print(f"Versions: 1000") print(f"Environments: 200") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Check Application Quota

Check remaining application quota capacity.

#!/bin/bash
# Check remaining application quota

echo "Checking application quota..."

APP_QUOTA=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes \
    --query 'ResourceQuotas.ApplicationQuota.Maximum' --output text)

CURRENT_APPS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --query 'Applications | length(@)')

echo "Application Quota: $APP_QUOTA"
echo "Current Applications: $CURRENT_APPS"
echo "Remaining: $((APP_QUOTA - CURRENT_APPS))"
Checking application quota... Application Quota: 75 Current Applications: 5 Remaining: 70
# Application quota from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Application Quota Check") print(f"\nQuota: 75") print(f"Used: 5") print(f"Remaining: 70") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check Environment Quota

Check environment quota availability.

#!/bin/bash
# Check environment quota availability

echo "Checking environment quota..."

ENV_QUOTA=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes \
    --query 'ResourceQuotas.EnvironmentQuota.Maximum' --output text)

CURRENT_ENVS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --query 'Environments | length(@)')

echo "Environment Quota: $ENV_QUOTA"
echo "Current Environments: $CURRENT_ENVS"
PERCENT=$((CURRENT_ENVS * 100 / ENV_QUOTA))
echo "Usage: $PERCENT%"
Checking environment quota... Environment Quota: 200 Current Environments: 12 Usage: 6%
# Environment quota from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment Quota Check") print(f"\nQuota: 200") print(f"Used: 12") print(f"Usage: 6%") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Monitor All Quotas

Display all Elastic Beanstalk resource quotas.

#!/bin/bash
# Monitor all EB resource quotas

echo "=== Elastic Beanstalk Resource Quotas ==="
echo ""

QUOTAS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes --output json)

echo "Applications: $(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.ApplicationQuota.Maximum')"
echo "Versions: $(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.ApplicationVersionQuota.Maximum')"
echo "Environments: $(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.EnvironmentQuota.Maximum')"
echo "Config Templates: $(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.ConfigurationTemplateQuota.Maximum')"
echo "Custom Platforms: $(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.CustomPlatformQuota.Maximum')"
=== Elastic Beanstalk Resource Quotas === Applications: 75 Versions: 1000 Environments: 200 Config Templates: 2000 Custom Platforms: 50
# All quotas from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All Quotas") print(f"\nApplications: 75") print(f"Versions: 1000") print(f"Environments: 200") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Quota Alert Script

Alert when approaching quota limits.

#!/bin/bash
# Alert when approaching quota limits

THRESHOLD=80

echo "Checking quota usage..."

QUOTAS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-account-attributes --output json)
APP_QUOTA=$(echo "$QUOTAS" | jq '.ResourceQuotas.ApplicationQuota.Maximum')

CURRENT_APPS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-applications \
    --query 'Applications | length(@)')

USAGE=$((CURRENT_APPS * 100 / APP_QUOTA))

if [ $USAGE -ge $THRESHOLD ]; then
    echo "WARNING: Application quota at ${USAGE}% ($CURRENT_APPS/$APP_QUOTA)"
else
    echo "Application quota OK: ${USAGE}% ($CURRENT_APPS/$APP_QUOTA)"
fi
Checking quota usage... Application quota OK: 6% (5/75)
# Quota alert from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Quota Alert Check") print(f"\nUsage: 6%") print(f"Threshold: 80%") print(f"Status: OK") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-environment-managed-action-history

Lists an environment's completed and failed managed actions, including platform updates and instance refreshes that Elastic Beanstalk has executed.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
  [--environment-id <environment-id>] \
  [--environment-name <environment-name>] \
  [--next-token <token>] \
  [--max-items <integer>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: Environment ID to filter results
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Environment name to filter results
  • --next-token (string) [Optional]: Token for pagination
  • --max-items (integer) [Optional]: Maximum number of items to return
Return Value
  • ManagedActionHistoryItems (array): List of completed/failed actions
  • ActionId (string): Unique identifier for the action
  • ActionType (string): InstanceRefresh, PlatformUpdate, or Unknown
  • Status (string): Completed, Failed, or Unknown
  • FailureType (string): Type of failure if action failed
  • ExecutedTime/FinishedTime (timestamp): When action ran
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Action History for Environment

Retrieve managed action history for production environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Get managed action history for production environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Getting action history for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Action history retrieved"
Getting action history for anihpj-prod... { "ManagedActionHistoryItems": [ { "ActionId": "action-12345abcde", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "ActionDescription": "Platform update to Python 3.11", "Status": "Completed", "ExecutedTime": "2024-01-10T08:00:00Z", "FinishedTime": "2024-01-10T08:45:00Z" }, { "ActionId": "action-67890fghij", "ActionType": "InstanceRefresh", "ActionDescription": "Refresh EC2 instances with latest AMI", "Status": "Completed", "ExecutedTime": "2024-01-05T02:00:00Z", "FinishedTime": "2024-01-05T02:30:00Z" } ] } Action history retrieved
# Action history from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Action History") print(f"\nPlatformUpdate: Completed") print(f"InstanceRefresh: Completed") print(f"Total: 2 actions") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Failed Actions

Retrieve only failed managed actions.

#!/bin/bash
# Get failed managed actions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"

echo "Getting failed actions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'ManagedActionHistoryItems[?Status==`Failed`]'

echo "Failed actions retrieved"
Getting failed actions... [ { "ActionId": "action-failed-123", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "ActionDescription": "Platform update to Python 3.12", "FailureType": "RollbackSuccessful", "Status": "Failed", "FailureDescription": "Platform update failed - disk space issue", "ExecutedTime": "2024-01-08T10:00:00Z", "FinishedTime": "2024-01-08T10:25:00Z" } ] Failed actions retrieved
# Failed actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Failed Actions") print(f"\nAction: PlatformUpdate") print(f"Failure: RollbackSuccessful") print(f"Issue: Disk space") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Recent Completed Actions

Get last 5 completed managed actions.

#!/bin/bash
# Get last 5 completed managed actions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Getting recent completed actions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --max-items 5 \
    --query 'ManagedActionHistoryItems[?Status==`Completed`]'

echo "Recent completed actions retrieved"
Getting recent completed actions... [ { "ActionId": "action-11111", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Completed", "FinishedTime": "2024-01-10T08:45:00Z" }, { "ActionId": "action-22222", "ActionType": "InstanceRefresh", "Status": "Completed", "FinishedTime": "2024-01-05T02:30:00Z" } ] Recent completed actions retrieved
# Recent completions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Recent Completed Actions") print(f"\nPlatformUpdate: 2024-01-10") print(f"InstanceRefresh: 2024-01-05") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Audit Platform Updates

Audit all platform update actions for compliance.

#!/bin/bash
# Audit all platform update actions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Auditing platform updates..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'ManagedActionHistoryItems[?ActionType==`PlatformUpdate`].{
        ID:ActionId,
        Description:ActionDescription,
        Status:Status,
        Finished:FinishedTime
    }'

echo "Platform update audit complete"
Auditing platform updates... [ { "ID": "action-12345", "Description": "Platform update to Python 3.11", "Status": "Completed", "Finished": "2024-01-10T08:45:00Z" }, { "ID": "action-98765", "Description": "Platform update to Amazon Linux 2023", "Status": "Completed", "Finished": "2023-12-15T04:30:00Z" } ] Platform update audit complete
# Platform audit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Update Audit") print(f"\nPython 3.11: Completed") print(f"Amazon Linux 2023: Completed") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Generate Maintenance Report

Generate maintenance report for all environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate maintenance report for all environments

ENVS=("anihpj-prod" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-dev")

echo "=== Managed Action History Report ==="

for ENV in "${ENVS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "--- $ENV ---"
    COMPLETED=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
        --environment-name "$ENV" \
        --query 'ManagedActionHistoryItems[?Status==`Completed`] | length(@)')
    FAILED=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-action-history \
        --environment-name "$ENV" \
        --query 'ManagedActionHistoryItems[?Status==`Failed`] | length(@)')
    echo "Completed: $COMPLETED"
    echo "Failed: $FAILED"
done
=== Managed Action History Report === --- anihpj-prod --- Completed: 12 Failed: 0 --- anihpj-staging --- Completed: 8 Failed: 1 --- anihpj-dev --- Completed: 15 Failed: 2
# Maintenance report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Maintenance Report") print(f"\nanihpj-prod: 12 completed, 0 failed") print(f"anihpj-staging: 8 completed, 1 failed") print(f"anihpj-dev: 15 completed, 2 failed") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-environment-managed-actions

Lists an environment's upcoming and in-progress managed actions. Managed actions are platform updates and maintenance operations that Elastic Beanstalk schedules for your environment.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
  [--environment-name <environment-name>] \
  [--environment-id <environment-id>] \
  [--status <Scheduled|Pending|Running|Unknown>]
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string) [Optional]: Name of the environment
  • --environment-id (string) [Optional]: ID of the environment
  • --status (string) [Optional]: Filter by action status: Scheduled, Pending, Running, Unknown
Return Value
  • ManagedActions (array): List of upcoming/in-progress actions
  • ActionId (string): Unique identifier for the action
  • ActionDescription (string): Description of the action
  • ActionType (string): InstanceRefresh, PlatformUpdate, or Unknown
  • Status (string): Scheduled, Pending, Running, or Unknown
  • WindowStartTime (timestamp): When action is scheduled to run
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Managed Actions

List all upcoming managed actions for production.

#!/bin/bash
# List all upcoming managed actions for production

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Getting managed actions for $ENV_NAME..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Managed actions retrieved"
Getting managed actions for anihpj-prod... { "ManagedActions": [ { "ActionId": "action-sched-12345", "ActionDescription": "Platform update to Python 3.12", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Scheduled", "WindowStartTime": "2024-01-20T02:00:00Z" }, { "ActionId": "action-sched-67890", "ActionDescription": "Instance refresh for security patches", "ActionType": "InstanceRefresh", "Status": "Scheduled", "WindowStartTime": "2024-01-25T03:00:00Z" } ] } Managed actions retrieved
# Managed actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Managed Actions") print(f"\nPlatformUpdate: 2024-01-20") print(f"InstanceRefresh: 2024-01-25") print(f"Total: 2 scheduled") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Scheduled Actions Only

Get only scheduled (upcoming) actions.

#!/bin/bash
# Get only scheduled (upcoming) actions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Getting scheduled actions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --status Scheduled

echo "Scheduled actions listed"
Getting scheduled actions... { "ManagedActions": [ { "ActionId": "action-sched-12345", "ActionDescription": "Platform update to Python 3.12", "ActionType": "PlatformUpdate", "Status": "Scheduled", "WindowStartTime": "2024-01-20T02:00:00Z" } ] } Scheduled actions listed
# Scheduled actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scheduled Actions") print(f"\nPlatformUpdate: Python 3.12") print(f"Scheduled: 2024-01-20") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check Running Actions

Check for currently running maintenance actions.

#!/bin/bash
# Check for currently running maintenance actions

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"

echo "Checking for running actions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --status Running

echo "Running actions checked"
Checking for running actions... { "ManagedActions": [] } Running actions checked
# Running actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Running Actions Check") print(f"\nStatus: No running actions") print(f"Environment: Available") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Pending Actions

Get actions pending execution.

#!/bin/bash
# Get actions pending execution

ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"

echo "Getting pending actions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME" \
    --status Pending \
    --query 'ManagedActions[].{ID:ActionId,Type:ActionType,Window:WindowStartTime}'

echo "Pending actions retrieved"
Getting pending actions... [ { "ID": "action-pending-99999", "Type": "InstanceRefresh", "Window": "2024-01-15T02:00:00Z" } ] Pending actions retrieved
# Pending actions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Pending Actions") print(f"\nInstanceRefresh: Pending") print(f"Window: 2024-01-15") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Monitor All Environments Actions

Check managed actions for all environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Check managed actions for all environments

ENVS=("anihpj-prod" "anihpj-staging" "anihpj-dev")

echo "=== Upcoming Managed Actions ==="

for ENV in "${ENVS[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "--- $ENV ---"
    aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-managed-actions \
        --environment-name "$ENV" \
        --query 'ManagedActions[].{
            Type:ActionType,
            Description:ActionDescription,
            Scheduled:WindowStartTime
        }' --output table
done
=== Upcoming Managed Actions === --- anihpj-prod --- ------------------------------------------------------ | ManagedActions | +----------------+------------------+------------------+ | Type | Description | Scheduled | +----------------+------------------+------------------+ | PlatformUpdate| Python 3.12 upgrade| 2024-01-20T02:00Z | +----------------+------------------+------------------+ --- anihpj-staging --- (No actions scheduled) --- anihpj-dev --- (No actions scheduled)
# All environments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Managed Actions Report") print(f"\nanihpj-prod: 1 action") print(f"anihpj-staging: 0 actions") print(f"anihpj-dev: 0 actions") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-platform-version

Describes a platform version including its supported solution stacks, configuration options, programming languages, and supported AWS regions.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
  --platform-arn <platform-arn>
Parameters Description
  • --platform-arn (string) [Required]: ARN of the platform version to describe
Return Value
  • PlatformDescription (object): Detailed platform information
  • PlatformArn (string): ARN of the platform
  • PlatformName (string): Name of the platform
  • PlatformVersion (string): Version number
  • PlatformStatus (string): Creating, Failed, Ready, Deleting, or Deleted
  • ProgrammingLanguages (array): Supported languages and versions
  • SupportedTierList (array): WebServer/Standard, Worker/SQS/HTTP
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Describe Python Platform

Get details of Python 3.11 platform.

#!/bin/bash
# Get details of Python 3.11 platform

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0"

echo "Getting platform details..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"

echo "Platform details retrieved"
Getting platform details... { "PlatformDescription": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "AWSElasticBeanstalk", "PlatformName": "Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "PlatformVersion": "4.0.0", "SolutionStackName": "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "OperatingSystemName": "Amazon Linux 2023", "ProgrammingLanguages": [ {"Name": "Python", "Version": "3.11"} ], "SupportedTierList": ["WebServer/Standard", "Worker/SQS/HTTP"] } } Platform details retrieved
# Platform details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Details") print(f"\nName: Python 3.11 on Amazon Linux 2023") print(f"Version: 4.0.0") print(f"Status: Ready") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Custom Platform Details

Describe custom platform version.

#!/bin/bash
# Describe custom platform version

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/2.0.0"

echo "Getting custom platform details..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN"

echo "Custom platform details retrieved"
Getting custom platform details... { "PlatformDescription": { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/2.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "self", "PlatformName": "anihpj-django-platform", "PlatformVersion": "2.0.0", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "DateCreated": "2024-01-01T10:00:00Z", "OperatingSystemName": "Amazon Linux 2023", "ProgrammingLanguages": [ {"Name": "Python", "Version": "3.11"} ], "Frameworks": [ {"Name": "Django", "Version": "4.2"} ] } } Custom platform details retrieved
# Custom platform from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Custom Platform") print(f"\nName: anihpj-django-platform") print(f"Version: 2.0.0") print(f"Django: 4.2") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Check Platform Status

Check if platform version is ready.

#!/bin/bash
# Check if platform version is ready

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/2.0.0"

echo "Checking platform status..."

STATUS=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN" \
    --query 'PlatformDescription.PlatformStatus' --output text)

echo "Platform Status: $STATUS"

if [ "$STATUS" == "Ready" ]; then
    echo "Platform is ready for deployments"
fi
Checking platform status... Platform Status: Ready Platform is ready for deployments
# Platform status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Status Check") print(f"\nStatus: Ready") print(f"Deployable: True") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Supported Tiers

Check what tiers the platform supports.

#!/bin/bash
# Check what tiers the platform supports

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0"

echo "Getting supported tiers..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN" \
    --query 'PlatformDescription.SupportedTierList'

echo "Supported tiers retrieved"
Getting supported tiers... [ "WebServer/Standard", "Worker/SQS/HTTP" ] Supported tiers retrieved
# Supported tiers from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Supported Tiers") print(f"\n- WebServer/Standard") print(f"- Worker/SQS/HTTP") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Get Programming Languages

Get programming languages supported by platform.

#!/bin/bash
# Get programming languages supported by platform

PLATFORM_ARN="arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0"

echo "Getting language support..."

aws elasticbeanstalk describe-platform-version \
    --platform-arn "$PLATFORM_ARN" \
    --query 'PlatformDescription.{
        Languages:ProgrammingLanguages,
        Frameworks:Frameworks,
        OS:OperatingSystemName
    }'

echo "Language support retrieved"
Getting language support... { "Languages": [ {"Name": "Python", "Version": "3.11"} ], "Frameworks": [], "OS": "Amazon Linux 2023" } Language support retrieved
# Language support from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Language Support") print(f"\nPython: 3.11") print(f"OS: Amazon Linux 2023") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

disassociate-environment-operations-role

Removes the association between an environment and an operations role. After disassociation, the environment will not have managed platform updates capability.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
  --environment-name <environment-name>
Parameters Description
  • --environment-name (string) [Required]: Name of the environment to disassociate from operations role
Return Value
  • None: No output on success (HTTP 200)
  • Environment loses managed platform updates capability
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Disassociate Operations Role

Remove operations role from environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove operations role from environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-dev"

echo "Disassociating operations role..."

aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Operations role disassociated from $ENV_NAME"
Disassociating operations role... Operations role disassociated from anihpj-dev
# Role disassociation from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Role Disassociation") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-dev") print(f"Status: Disassociated") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Disassociate Before Termination

Clean up role before terminating environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up role before terminating environment

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging-old"

echo "Cleaning up before termination..."

aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Role disassociated, ready for termination"
Cleaning up before termination... Role disassociated, ready for termination
# Pre-termination cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Pre-Termination Cleanup") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-staging-old") print(f"Ready for termination: True") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Disable Managed Updates

Disable managed updates by removing role.

#!/bin/bash
# Disable managed updates by removing role

ENV_NAME="anihpj-manual-updates"

echo "Disabling managed updates..."

aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

echo "Managed updates disabled"
echo "Environment will require manual platform updates"
Disabling managed updates... Managed updates disabled Environment will require manual platform updates
# Disable auto updates from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Managed Updates Disabled") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-manual-updates") print(f"Update mode: Manual") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Disassociate from Multiple Environments

Remove operations role from development environments.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove operations role from development environments

DEV_ENVS=("anihpj-dev" "anihpj-test" "anihpj-qa")

echo "Removing operations role from dev environments..."

for ENV in "${DEV_ENVS[@]}"; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
        --environment-name "$ENV"
    echo "Role removed from $ENV"
done

echo ""
echo "All dev environments set to manual updates"
Removing operations role from dev environments... Role removed from anihpj-dev Role removed from anihpj-test Role removed from anihpj-qa All dev environments set to manual updates
# Batch role removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Batch Role Removal") print(f"\nEnvironments: 3") print(f"- anihpj-dev") print(f"- anihpj-test") print(f"- anihpj-qa") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Verify Role Disassociation

Disassociate and verify removal.

#!/bin/bash
# Disassociate and verify removal

ENV_NAME="anihpj-staging"

echo "Disassociating operations role..."

aws elasticbeanstalk disassociate-environment-operations-role \
    --environment-name "$ENV_NAME"

# Verify by checking environment details
OPS_ROLE=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environments \
    --environment-names "$ENV_NAME" \
    --query 'Environments[0].OperationsRole' --output text)

if [ "$OPS_ROLE" == "None" ] || [ -z "$OPS_ROLE" ]; then
    echo "Operations role successfully removed"
else
    echo "Role still attached: $OPS_ROLE"
fi
Disassociating operations role... Operations role successfully removed
# Verify removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Verify Role Removal") print(f"\nEnvironment: anihpj-staging") print(f"Role: None") print(f"Verified: True") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-platform-branches

Lists the platform branches available for your account in the current AWS region. Platform branches represent major platform lines like Python 3.9, Python 3.11, etc.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches \
  [--filters <filters>] \
  [--max-records <integer>] \
  [--next-token <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --filters (list) [Optional]: Filters for platform branch criteria
  • --max-records (integer) [Optional]: Maximum number of records to return
  • --next-token (string) [Optional]: Token for pagination
Return Value
  • PlatformBranchSummaryList (array): List of platform branches
  • PlatformName (string): Platform name (e.g., Python)
  • BranchName (string): Branch name with details
  • LifecycleState (string): Beta, Supported, Deprecated, or Retired
  • BranchOrder (integer): Order of preference (1 = recommended)
  • SupportedTierList (array): WebServer/Standard, Worker/SQS/HTTP
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Platform Branches

List all available platform branches.

#!/bin/bash
# List all available platform branches

echo "Listing all platform branches..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches

echo "Platform branches listed"
Listing all platform branches... { "PlatformBranchSummaryList": [ { "PlatformName": "Python", "BranchName": "Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "LifecycleState": "Supported", "BranchOrder": 1, "SupportedTierList": ["WebServer/Standard", "Worker/SQS/HTTP"] }, { "PlatformName": "Python", "BranchName": "Python 3.9 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2", "LifecycleState": "Supported", "BranchOrder": 2, "SupportedTierList": ["WebServer/Standard", "Worker/SQS/HTTP"] } ] } Platform branches listed
# Platform branches from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Branches") print(f"\nPython 3.11: Supported") print(f"Python 3.9: Supported") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Filter Python Branches

List only Python platform branches.

#!/bin/bash
# List only Python platform branches

echo "Getting Python branches..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches \
    --filters Attribute=PlatformName,Values=Python,Operator=eq

echo "Python branches listed"
Getting Python branches... { "PlatformBranchSummaryList": [ { "PlatformName": "Python", "BranchName": "Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "LifecycleState": "Supported", "BranchOrder": 1 }, { "PlatformName": "Python", "BranchName": "Python 3.9 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2", "LifecycleState": "Supported", "BranchOrder": 2 }, { "PlatformName": "Python", "BranchName": "Python 3.8 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2", "LifecycleState": "Deprecated", "BranchOrder": 3 } ] } Python branches listed
# Python branches from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Python Branches") print(f"\nSupported: 3.11, 3.9") print(f"Deprecated: 3.8") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Get Supported Branches Only

List only supported (not deprecated) branches.

#!/bin/bash
# List only supported (not deprecated) branches

echo "Getting supported branches..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches \
    --filters Attribute=LifecycleState,Values=Supported,Operator=eq \
    --query 'PlatformBranchSummaryList[].BranchName'

echo "Supported branches listed"
Getting supported branches... [ "Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "Python 3.9 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2", "Python 3.8 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2", "Node.js 18 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023", "Docker running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023" ] Supported branches listed
# Supported branches from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Supported Branches") print(f"\nTotal: 5 branches") print(f"Python versions: 3") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: List Web Server Tier Branches

List branches that support web server tier.

#!/bin/bash
# List branches that support web server tier

echo "Getting web server branches..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches \
    --filters Attribute=TierType,Values=WebServer/Standard,Operator=contains \
    --query 'PlatformBranchSummaryList[?LifecycleState==`Supported`].{
        Branch:BranchName,
        Order:BranchOrder
    }' --output table

echo "Web server branches listed"
Getting web server branches... ---------------------------------------------------------- | PlatformBranchSummaryList | +-------------------------------------------+------------+ | Branch | Order | +-------------------------------------------+------------+ | Python 3.11 running on Amazon Linux 2023 | 1 | | Python 3.9 running on Amazon Linux 2 | 2 | | Docker running on Amazon Linux 2023 | 1 | +-------------------------------------------+------------+ Web server branches listed
# Web server branches from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Web Server Branches") print(f"\nPython 3.11: Order 1") print(f"Python 3.9: Order 2") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Find Latest Python Branch

Find the recommended Python branch.

#!/bin/bash
# Find the recommended Python branch (lowest order number)

echo "Finding latest Python branch..."

LATEST=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-branches \
    --filters \
        Attribute=PlatformName,Values=Python,Operator=eq \
        Attribute=LifecycleState,Values=Supported,Operator=eq \
    --query 'PlatformBranchSummaryList | sort_by(@, &BranchOrder) | [0].BranchName' \
    --output text)

echo "Recommended Python branch: $LATEST"
echo "Use this for new Django deployments"
Finding latest Python branch... Recommended Python branch: Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023 Use this for new Django deployments
# Latest Python from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Python Branch") print(f"\nRecommended: Python 3.11") print(f"OS: Amazon Linux 2023") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

list-platform-versions

Lists the platform versions available for your account. You can filter by owner (AWS vs self for custom platforms), platform name, and status.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
  [--filters <filters>] \
  [--max-records <integer>] \
  [--next-token <token>]
Parameters Description
  • --filters (list) [Optional]: Filters: Type=PlatformName|PlatformOwner|PlatformStatus, Values=[values], Operator=eq|begins_with|contains
  • --max-records (integer) [Optional]: Maximum number of records to return
  • --next-token (string) [Optional]: Token for pagination
Return Value
  • PlatformSummaryList (array): List of platform summaries
  • PlatformArn (string): Platform ARN with full version info
  • PlatformOwner (string): AWSElasticBeanstalk or self
  • PlatformStatus (string): Creating, Failed, Ready, Deleting, or Deleted
  • PlatformVersion (string): Version number (e.g., 4.0.0)
  • PlatformBranchName (string): Branch name for the platform
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Platform Versions

List all available platform versions with limit.

#!/bin/bash
# List all available platform versions

echo "Listing platform versions..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --max-records 10

echo "Platform versions listed"
Listing platform versions... { "PlatformSummaryList": [ { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "AWSElasticBeanstalk", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "OperatingSystemName": "Amazon Linux 2023", "PlatformVersion": "4.0.0", "PlatformBranchName": "Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023" }, { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.9 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2/3.5.0", "PlatformOwner": "AWSElasticBeanstalk", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "OperatingSystemName": "Amazon Linux 2", "PlatformVersion": "3.5.0" } ] } Platform versions listed
# Platform versions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Platform Versions") print(f"\nPython 3.11: v4.0.0") print(f"Python 3.9: v3.5.0") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: List Custom Platforms

List only custom (self-owned) platforms.

#!/bin/bash
# List only custom (self-owned) platforms

echo "Getting custom platforms..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --filters Type=PlatformOwner,Values=self

echo "Custom platforms listed"
Getting custom platforms... { "PlatformSummaryList": [ { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/2.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "self", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "PlatformVersion": "2.0.0" }, { "PlatformArn": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1:123456789012:platform/anihpj-django-platform/1.0.0", "PlatformOwner": "self", "PlatformStatus": "Ready", "PlatformVersion": "1.0.0" } ] } Custom platforms listed
# Custom platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Custom Platforms") print(f"\nVersion 2.0.0: Ready") print(f"Version 1.0.0: Ready") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Filter Python Platforms

List only Python platform versions.

#!/bin/bash
# List Python platform versions

echo "Filtering Python platforms..."

aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --filters Type=PlatformName,Values=Python,Operator=begins_with \
    --query 'PlatformSummaryList[].{
        ARN:PlatformArn,
        Status:PlatformStatus,
        Version:PlatformVersion
    }'

echo "Python platforms filtered"
Filtering Python platforms... [ { "ARN": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0", "Status": "Ready", "Version": "4.0.0" }, { "ARN": "arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.9 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2/3.5.0", "Status": "Ready", "Version": "3.5.0" } ] Python platforms filtered
# Python platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Python Platforms") print(f"\n3.11: Ready (v4.0.0)") print(f"3.9: Ready (v3.5.0)") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Ready Platforms Only

Count platforms that are ready for deployment.

#!/bin/bash
# List only ready (usable) platforms

echo "Counting ready platforms..."

COUNT=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --filters Type=PlatformStatus,Values=Ready \
    --query 'PlatformSummaryList | length(@)' \
    --output text)

echo "$COUNT platforms are ready for deployment"
Counting ready platforms... 42 platforms are ready for deployment
# Ready platforms from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Ready Platforms") print(f"\nTotal: 42 platforms") print(f"All ready for use") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Find Latest Python Platform Version

Find the latest Python platform for Django.

#!/bin/bash
# Find the latest Python platform for Django

echo "Finding latest Python platform..."

LATEST_ARN=$(aws elasticbeanstalk list-platform-versions \
    --filters \
        Type=PlatformOwner,Values=AWSElasticBeanstalk \
        Type=PlatformStatus,Values=Ready \
    --query 'PlatformSummaryList[?contains(PlatformArn, `Python 3.11`)] | [0].PlatformArn' \
    --output text)

echo "Latest Python Platform ARN:"
echo "$LATEST_ARN"
echo "Use this for Django deployments"
Finding latest Python platform... Latest Python Platform ARN: arn:aws:elasticbeanstalk:us-east-1::platform/Python 3.11 running on 64bit Amazon Linux 2023/4.0.0 Use this for Django deployments
# Latest Python from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Latest Python Platform") print(f"\nVersion: 3.11") print(f"Platform: 4.0.0") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

update-application-resource-lifecycle

Modifies lifecycle settings for an application. This controls automatic deletion of old application versions to manage storage costs and application version limits.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
  --application-name <application-name> \
  --resource-lifecycle-config <config>
Parameters Description
  • --application-name (string) [Required]: Name of the application to update lifecycle settings
  • --resource-lifecycle-config (structure) [Required]: Lifecycle configuration with ServiceRole and VersionLifecycleConfig
Return Value
  • ApplicationName (string): Name of the application
  • ResourceLifecycleConfig (object): Current lifecycle configuration
  • ServiceRole (string): IAM role for lifecycle operations
  • MaxCountRule (object): Rule limiting version count
  • MaxAgeRule (object): Rule limiting version age
  • DeleteSourceFromS3 (boolean): Whether to delete source from S3
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Set Max Version Count

Limit application versions to 100.

#!/bin/bash
# Limit application versions to 100

echo "Setting max version count..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service-role",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxCount": 100,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": false
            }
        }
    }'

echo "Lifecycle configured to keep 100 versions"
Setting max version count... { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service-role", "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": { "Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 100, "DeleteSourceFromS3": false } } } } Lifecycle configured to keep 100 versions
# Max version count from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Version Count Lifecycle") print(f"\nMax Count: 100") print(f"Delete from S3: No") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Set Max Age Rule

Delete versions older than 90 days.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete versions older than 90 days

echo "Setting max age rule..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service-role",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxAgeRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxAgeInDays": 90,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'

echo "Versions older than 90 days will be deleted"
Setting max age rule... { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service-role", "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxAgeRule": { "Enabled": true, "MaxAgeInDays": 90, "DeleteSourceFromS3": true } } } } Versions older than 90 days will be deleted
# Max age rule from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Age-Based Lifecycle") print(f"\nMax Age: 90 days") print(f"Delete from S3: Yes") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Combined Count and Age Rules

Set both max count and max age rules.

#!/bin/bash
# Set both max count and max age rules

echo "Setting combined rules..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-service-role",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxCount": 50,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": false
            },
            "MaxAgeRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxAgeInDays": 180,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'

echo "Combined lifecycle rules configured"
Setting combined rules... { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 50}, "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxAgeInDays": 180} } } } Combined lifecycle rules configured
# Combined rules from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Combined Lifecycle") print(f"\nMax Count: 50") print(f"Max Age: 180 days") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Disable Lifecycle Rules

Disable automatic version cleanup.

#!/bin/bash
# Disable automatic version cleanup

echo "Disabling lifecycle rules..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": false
            },
            "MaxAgeRule": {
                "Enabled": false
            }
        }
    }'

echo "Lifecycle rules disabled - manual cleanup required"
Disabling lifecycle rules... { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": false}, "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": false} } } } Lifecycle rules disabled - manual cleanup required
# Disable lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle Disabled") print(f"\nManual cleanup required") print(f"No auto-deletion") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Production Lifecycle Settings

Configure production-grade lifecycle settings.

#!/bin/bash
# Configure production-grade lifecycle settings

echo "Setting production lifecycle..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-application-resource-lifecycle \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --resource-lifecycle-config '{
        "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-lifecycle-role",
        "VersionLifecycleConfig": {
            "MaxCountRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxCount": 200,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": false
            },
            "MaxAgeRule": {
                "Enabled": true,
                "MaxAgeInDays": 365,
                "DeleteSourceFromS3": true
            }
        }
    }'

echo "Production lifecycle: 200 versions or 1 year retention"
Setting production lifecycle... { "ApplicationName": "anihpj-jobpost", "ResourceLifecycleConfig": { "ServiceRole": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/eb-lifecycle-role", "VersionLifecycleConfig": { "MaxCountRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxCount": 200, "DeleteSourceFromS3": false}, "MaxAgeRule": {"Enabled": true, "MaxAgeInDays": 365, "DeleteSourceFromS3": true} } } } Production lifecycle: 200 versions or 1 year retention
# Production lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production Lifecycle") print(f"\nMax Versions: 200") print(f"Max Age: 365 days") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

wait

Wait until a particular condition is satisfied for Elastic Beanstalk resources. This is useful in scripts to pause execution until an environment reaches a specific state.

Syntax
aws elasticbeanstalk wait <wait-condition> \
  [--environment-names <name1> <name2> ...] \
  [--environment-ids <id1> <id2> ...] \
  [--application-name <application-name>]

# Available wait conditions:
# - environment-exists
# - environment-terminated
# - environment-updated
Parameters Description
  • wait-condition (string) [Required]: environment-exists, environment-terminated, or environment-updated
  • --environment-names (list) [Optional]: Names of environments to wait for
  • --environment-ids (list) [Optional]: IDs of environments to wait for
  • --application-name (string) [Optional]: Application name to filter environments
Return Value
  • Exit Code 0: Condition was satisfied successfully
  • Exit Code 255: Waiter failed (timeout or max attempts reached)
  • No Output: Command completes silently on success
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Wait for Environment to Exist

Wait for environment to be created and ready.

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for environment to be created and ready

echo "Creating environment..."

aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
    --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
    --environment-name anihpj-prod \
    --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11"

echo "Waiting for environment to be ready..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-exists \
    --environment-names anihpj-prod

echo "Environment anihpj-prod is ready!"
Creating environment... Waiting for environment to be ready... Environment anihpj-prod is ready!
# Wait for environment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Wait for Environment") print(f"\nStatus: Ready") print(f"Name: anihpj-prod") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Wait for Environment Termination

Terminate and wait for cleanup.

#!/bin/bash
# Terminate and wait for cleanup

echo "Terminating environment..."

aws elasticbeanstalk terminate-environment \
    --environment-name anihpj-old-staging

echo "Waiting for environment termination..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-terminated \
    --environment-names anihpj-old-staging

echo "Environment terminated and cleaned up"
Terminating environment... Waiting for environment termination... Environment terminated and cleaned up
# Wait for termination from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Wait for Termination") print(f"\nEnvironment: Terminated") print(f"Cleanup: Complete") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Wait for Environment Update

Deploy new version and wait for completion.

#!/bin/bash
# Deploy new version and wait

echo "Deploying version v2.0.0..."

aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name anihpj-prod \
    --version-label v2.0.0

echo "Waiting for deployment..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-updated \
    --environment-names anihpj-prod

echo "Deployment complete!"
Deploying version v2.0.0... Waiting for deployment... Deployment complete!
# Wait for update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Wait for Update") print(f"\nVersion: v2.0.0") print(f"Status: Deployed") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Wait for Multiple Environments

Create multiple environments and wait for all.

#!/bin/bash
# Create multiple environments and wait

ENVS=("anihpj-web" "anihpj-worker")

echo "Creating environments..."
for ENV in "${ENVS[@]}"; do
    aws elasticbeanstalk create-environment \
        --application-name anihpj-jobpost \
        --environment-name $ENV \
        --solution-stack-name "64bit Amazon Linux 2023 v4.0.0 running Python 3.11" &
done

echo "Waiting for all environments..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-exists \
    --environment-names "${ENVS[@]}"

echo "All environments ready"
Creating environments... Waiting for all environments... All environments ready
# Wait for multiple from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Wait for Multiple") print(f"\nWeb: Ready") print(f"Worker: Ready") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: CI/CD Pipeline with Wait

Complete deployment pipeline with waits.

#!/bin/bash
# Complete deployment pipeline with waits

APP_NAME="anihpj-jobpost"
ENV_NAME="anihpj-prod"
VERSION="v$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"

echo "Creating version $VERSION..."
aws elasticbeanstalk create-application-version \
    --application-name $APP_NAME \
    --version-label $VERSION \
    --source-bundle S3Bucket=anihpj-deploys,S3Key=app.zip

echo "Deploying to $ENV_NAME..."
aws elasticbeanstalk update-environment \
    --environment-name $ENV_NAME \
    --version-label $VERSION

echo "Waiting for deployment..."
aws elasticbeanstalk wait environment-updated \
    --environment-names $ENV_NAME

HEALTH=$(aws elasticbeanstalk describe-environment-health \
    --environment-name $ENV_NAME \
    --attribute-names HealthStatus --query 'HealthStatus' --output text)

echo "Deployment successful - Health: $HEALTH"
Creating version v20240115-143022... Deploying to anihpj-prod... Waiting for deployment... Deployment successful - Health: Ok
# CI/CD pipeline from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime print(f"CI/CD Pipeline") print(f"\nVersion: v{datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d')}") print(f"Health: Ok") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

AWS Fargate

Serverless container orchestration for deploying Docker containers without managing EC2 instances

📋 Fargate Overview

AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that lets you run Docker containers without provisioning or managing EC2 instances. Fargate integrates with Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) to automatically manage infrastructure scaling, patching, and security. Deploy your anihpj Django application in Docker containers with Fargate handling all the EC2 management, allowing your team to focus on deploying JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data services.

Core Fargate Operations for Django

create-cluster - Create ECS cluster for Fargate
create-service - Deploy containerized Django app
register-task-definition - Define Docker container specs
run-task - Run one-off tasks/jobs
describe-services - Get service status
update-service - Update deployment config
list-tasks - List running containers
describe-tasks - Get container details

create-cluster

Creates a new ECS cluster to run Fargate containers. A cluster is a logical grouping of resources where your Django application containers will run.

Syntax
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name  \
  [--tags ] \
  [--settings ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster-name (string): [Required] Name of the cluster
  • --tags (list): Key-value tags for the cluster
  • --settings (list): Cluster settings (containerInsights enabled/disabled)
Return Value
  • Cluster (Object): Created cluster details
  • ClusterArn (String): ARN of the cluster
Example 1: Create Basic Fargate Cluster
#!/bin/bash
# Create basic ECS cluster for Fargate
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name anihpj-fargate-cluster
{ "Cluster": { "ClusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-fargate-cluster", "ClusterName": "anihpj-fargate-cluster", "Status": "ACTIVE", "RegisteredContainerInstancesCount": 0, "RunningCount": 0, "PendingCount": 0 } }
# Fargate cluster from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Fargate cluster created")
Example 2: Create Cluster with Container Insights
#!/bin/bash
# Create cluster with monitoring enabled
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled \
  --tags key=Environment,value=production key=Project,value=anihpj
{ "Cluster": { "ClusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-prod-cluster", "ClusterName": "anihpj-prod-cluster", "Settings": [ {"Name": "containerInsights", "Value": "enabled"} ] } }
# Prod cluster from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production cluster with monitoring")
Example 3: Create Dev Cluster
#!/bin/bash
# Create development cluster
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name anihpj-dev-cluster \
  --tags key=Environment,value=development
{ "Cluster": { "ClusterName": "anihpj-dev-cluster", "Status": "ACTIVE" } }
# Dev cluster from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Development cluster ready")
Example 4: Create Multi-Region Clusters
#!/bin/bash
# Create clusters in multiple regions
for region in us-east-1 us-west-2 eu-west-1; do
  aws ecs create-cluster \
    --cluster-name anihpj-cluster-$region \
    --region $region \
    --tags key=Region,value=$region
  echo "Created cluster in $region"
done
Created cluster in us-east-1 Created cluster in us-west-2 Created cluster in eu-west-1
# Multi-region from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 regional clusters created")
Example 5: Export Cluster ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Create cluster and save ARN for CI/CD
CLUSTER_ARN=$(aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --query 'Cluster.ClusterArn' --output text)

echo "FARGATE_CLUSTER=$CLUSTER_ARN" >> .env
echo "✓ Cluster ARN saved to .env"
✓ Cluster ARN saved to .env
# Save ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cluster ARN exported")

describe-clusters

Returns information about existing ECS clusters. Use to check cluster status, resource counts, and monitoring settings.

Syntax
aws ecs describe-clusters \
  [--clusters ] \
  [--include ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --clusters (list): Cluster names or ARNs to describe
  • --include (list): Include ATTACHMENTS, SETTINGS, or STATISTICS
Return Value
  • Clusters (Array): List of cluster details
  • Failures (Array): Any clusters that couldn't be found
Example 1: Describe All Clusters
#!/bin/bash
# Get all clusters
aws ecs describe-clusters
{ "Clusters": [ { "ClusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-fargate-cluster", "ClusterName": "anihpj-fargate-cluster", "Status": "ACTIVE", "RegisteredContainerInstancesCount": 0, "RunningCount": 2, "PendingCount": 0 } ] }
# List clusters from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Clusters listed")
Example 2: Describe Specific Cluster
#!/bin/bash
# Get details for production cluster
aws ecs describe-clusters \
  --clusters anihpj-prod-cluster
{ "Clusters": [ { "ClusterName": "anihpj-prod-cluster", "Status": "ACTIVE", "RunningCount": 5, "Settings": [ {"Name": "containerInsights", "Value": "enabled"} ] } ] }
# Specific cluster from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod cluster details")
Example 3: Describe Multiple Clusters
#!/bin/bash
# Check status of dev and prod clusters
aws ecs describe-clusters \
  --clusters anihpj-dev-cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --query 'Clusters[].{Name:ClusterName,Status:Status,Running:RunningCount}' \
  --output table
---------------------------------------------------------- | Clusters | +-----------------------+----------+----------+----------+ | Name | Status | Running | +-----------------------+----------+----------+----------+ | anihpj-dev-cluster | ACTIVE | 1 | | anihpj-prod-cluster | ACTIVE | 5 | +-----------------------+----------+----------+----------+
# Multi describe from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev and prod cluster status")
Example 4: Get Cluster with Statistics
#!/bin/bash
# Detailed cluster metrics
aws ecs describe-clusters \
  --clusters anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --include STATISTICS
{ "Clusters": [ { "ClusterName": "anihpj-prod-cluster", "Statistics": [ {"Name": "RegisteredInstances", "Value": "0"}, {"Name": "RunningTasks", "Value": "5"}, {"Name": "PendingTasks", "Value": "0"} ] } ] }
# Statistics from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cluster statistics")
Example 5: Export Cluster Info
#!/bin/bash
# Save cluster details to JSON
aws ecs describe-clusters \
  --clusters anihpj-prod-cluster \
  > cluster-info.json

echo "✓ Cluster info saved"
✓ Cluster info saved
# Export info from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cluster info exported")

delete-cluster

Deletes an empty ECS cluster. All running tasks must be stopped and services deleted before deletion.

Syntax
aws ecs delete-cluster \
  --cluster  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): [Required] Cluster name or ARN
Return Value
  • Cluster (Object): Deleted cluster details
Example 1: Delete Dev Cluster
#!/bin/bash
# Delete development cluster
aws ecs delete-cluster \
  --cluster anihpj-dev-cluster

echo "✓ Cluster deleted"
✓ Cluster deleted
# Delete cluster from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev cluster deleted")
Example 2: Delete with Cleanup
#!/bin/bash
# Cleanup cluster and services first
CLUSTER="anihpj-test-cluster"

# Delete all services
aws ecs list-services --cluster $CLUSTER --query 'serviceArns' --output text | \
while read svc; do
  aws ecs delete-service --cluster $CLUSTER --service $svc --force
done

# Delete cluster
aws ecs delete-cluster --cluster $CLUSTER
echo "✓ Cluster and services deleted"
✓ Cluster and services deleted
# Cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Full cleanup done")
Example 3: Delete Cluster by ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Delete using full ARN
CLUSTER_ARN="arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-old-cluster"

aws ecs delete-cluster --cluster $CLUSTER_ARN
{ "Cluster": { "ClusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-old-cluster", "Status": "DELETE_IN_PROGRESS" } }
# By ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cluster deletion in progress")
Example 4: Check Before Delete
#!/bin/bash
# Verify cluster is empty before deleting
CLUSTER="anihpj-empty-cluster"

RUNNING=$(aws ecs describe-clusters --clusters $CLUSTER \
  --query 'Clusters[0].RunningCount' --output text)

if [ "$RUNNING" -eq 0 ]; then
  aws ecs delete-cluster --cluster $CLUSTER
  echo "✓ Empty cluster deleted"
else
  echo "Cannot delete: $RUNNING tasks still running"
fi
✓ Empty cluster deleted
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe deletion verified")
Example 5: Batch Delete Clusters
#!/bin/bash
# Delete multiple old clusters
for cluster in anihpj-old-1 anihpj-old-2 anihpj-old-3; do
  aws ecs delete-cluster --cluster $cluster 2>/dev/null && \
  echo "Deleted: $cluster"
done
Deleted: anihpj-old-1 Deleted: anihpj-old-2 Deleted: anihpj-old-3
# Batch delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 clusters deleted")

create-service

Creates a new service that runs and maintains your Django application on Fargate. A service manages a specified number of task replicas running simultaneously.

Syntax
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster  \
  --service-name  \
  --task-definition  \
  --desired-count  \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  [--network-configuration ] \
  [--load-balancers ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): [Required] Cluster name or ARN
  • --service-name (string): [Required] Service name
  • --task-definition (string): [Required] Task definition name:revision
  • --desired-count (integer): Number of tasks to run (default: 1)
  • --launch-type (string): Set to FARGATE for serverless
  • --network-configuration (structure): VPC/subnet/security group config
Return Value
  • Service (Object): Created service details
  • ServiceArn (String): ARN of the service
Example 1: Create Basic Fargate Service
#!/bin/bash
# Create simple Fargate service
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-django-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:1 \
  --desired-count 2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-12345678],securityGroups=[sg-87654321],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}"
{ "Service": { "ServiceArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-fargate-cluster/anihpj-django-service", "ServiceName": "anihpj-django-service", "Status": "ACTIVE", "DesiredCount": 2, "RunningCount": 0 } }
# Create service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django service created with 2 replicas")
Example 2: Create Service with Load Balancer
#!/bin/bash
# Create service with ALB
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-prod-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --desired-count 3 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --load-balancers targetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-tg/1234567890123456,containerName=django,containerPort=8000 \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-12345678,subnet-87654321],securityGroups=[sg-prod-sg],assignPublicIp=DISABLED}"
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-prod-service", "DesiredCount": 3, "LoadBalancers": [ {"TargetGroupArn": "...", "ContainerName": "django", "ContainerPort": 8000} ], "Status": "ACTIVE" } }
# LB service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production service with LB created")
Example 3: Create Service with Auto-Scaling
#!/bin/bash
# Create service (then attach auto-scaling separately)
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-api-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django-api:1 \
  --desired-count 2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-12345678],securityGroups=[sg-87654321]}"

echo "✓ Service created - auto-scaling ready"
✓ Service created - auto-scaling ready
# Scalable service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scalable Django service")
Example 4: Create Dev Service (Single Task)
#!/bin/bash
# Create minimal dev service
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-dev-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-dev-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django-dev:1 \
  --desired-count 1 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-dev],securityGroups=[sg-dev]}"
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-dev-service", "DesiredCount": 1, "Status": "ACTIVE" } }
# Dev service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev environment service")
Example 5: Create Service with Environment Variables
#!/bin/bash
# Create service (env vars in task definition)
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-prod-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:latest \
  --desired-count 2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-prod],securityGroups=[sg-prod]}"

echo "✓ Service deployed with task definition env vars"
✓ Service deployed with task definition env vars
# Service with env from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service with env vars deployed")

describe-services

Returns detailed information about ECS services. Use to check service status, task counts, and deployment information.

Syntax
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster  \
  --services  \
  [--include ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): [Required] Cluster name or ARN
  • --services (list): [Required] Service names or ARNs
  • --include (list): Include TAGS
Return Value
  • Services (Array): List of service details
  • Status (String): Service status (ACTIVE, INACTIVE, DRAINING)
Example 1: Describe Single Service
#!/bin/bash
# Get service status
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service
{ "Services": [ { "ServiceArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-fargate-cluster/anihpj-django-service", "ServiceName": "anihpj-django-service", "Status": "ACTIVE", "DesiredCount": 2, "RunningCount": 2 } ] }
# Service status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service running: 2/2 tasks")
Example 2: Describe Multiple Services
#!/bin/bash
# Check all services in cluster
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --services anihpj-prod-service anihpj-api-service \
  --query 'Services[].{Name:ServiceName,Status:Status,Running:RunningCount,Desired:DesiredCount}' \
  --output table
----------------------------------------------- | Services | +-------------------+--------+-------+-------+ | Name | Status | Running | Desired| +-------------------+--------+-------+-------+ | anihpj-prod-svc | ACTIVE | 3 | 3 | | anihpj-api-svc | ACTIVE | 2 | 2 | +-------------------+--------+-------+-------+
# Multi service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All services running normally")
Example 3: Get Service with Full Details
#!/bin/bash
# Get comprehensive service info
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service \
  --include TAGS
{ "Services": [ { "ServiceName": "anihpj-django-service", "Status": "ACTIVE", "Deployments": [ {"Status": "PRIMARY", "TaskDefinition": "anihpj-django:1", "RunningCount": 2} ], "Tags": [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "production"} ] } ] }
# Full details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service details with tags")
Example 4: Check Service Deployments
#!/bin/bash
# Monitor deployment rollout
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --services anihpj-prod-service \
  --query 'Services[0].Deployments' \
  --output json
[ { "Status": "PRIMARY", "TaskDefinition": "anihpj-django:2", "DesiredCount": 3, "RunningCount": 3, "PendingCount": 0 } ]
# Deployments from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All tasks deployed successfully")
Example 5: Export Service Info to JSON
#!/bin/bash
# Backup service configuration
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service \
  > service-backup.json

echo "✓ Service info exported"
✓ Service info exported
# Export service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service config backed up")

update-service

Updates an ECS service configuration. Use to change task counts, deploy new versions, update load balancer config, or modify deployment strategy.

Syntax
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster  \
  --service  \
  [--task-definition ] \
  [--desired-count ] \
  [--deployment-configuration ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): [Required] Cluster name or ARN
  • --service (string): [Required] Service name or ARN
  • --task-definition (string): New task definition version
  • --desired-count (integer): New task count
  • --deployment-configuration (structure): Rolling update settings
Return Value
  • Service (Object): Updated service details
Example 1: Deploy New Version
#!/bin/bash
# Update to new Django version
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-prod-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-prod-service", "Status": "ACTIVE", "Deployments": [ {"Status": "PRIMARY", "TaskDefinition": "anihpj-django:2", "RunningCount": 2}, {"Status": "ACTIVE", "TaskDefinition": "anihpj-django:1", "RunningCount": 1} ] } }
# Deploy new version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rolling deployment started")
Example 2: Scale Up Service
#!/bin/bash
# Increase task count for high traffic
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-prod-service \
  --desired-count 5
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-prod-service", "DesiredCount": 5, "RunningCount": 3 } }
# Scale up from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaling to 5 tasks")
Example 3: Scale Down Service
#!/bin/bash
# Reduce task count to save costs
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-prod-service \
  --desired-count 2

echo "✓ Scaled down to 2 tasks"
✓ Scaled down to 2 tasks
# Scale down from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cost optimization: 2 tasks")
Example 4: Update with Rolling Deployment
#!/bin/bash
# Deploy with controlled rollout
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-prod-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:3 \
  --deployment-configuration "maximumPercent=200,minimumHealthyPercent=100"
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-prod-service", "DeploymentConfiguration": { "MaximumPercent": 200, "MinimumHealthyPercent": 100 } } }
