# Rolling deploy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe zero-downtime deployment")
Example 5: Force New Deployment
#!/bin/bash
# Restart all tasks (same version)
aws ecs update-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --force-new-deployment

echo "✓ Forced task restart"
✓ Forced task restart
# Force restart from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All tasks restarted")

delete-service

Deletes an ECS service and stops all running tasks. Optionally forces immediate termination of tasks.

Syntax
aws ecs delete-service \
  --cluster  \
  --service  \
  [--force] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): [Required] Cluster name or ARN
  • --service (string): [Required] Service name or ARN
  • --force (boolean): Force delete (immediate instead of graceful)
Return Value
  • Service (Object): Deleted service details
Example 1: Graceful Service Deletion
#!/bin/bash
# Delete service gracefully (waits for tasks to stop)
aws ecs delete-service \
  --cluster anihpj-dev-cluster \
  --service anihpj-dev-service

echo "✓ Service deletion started"
✓ Service deletion started
# Delete service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service terminating gracefully")
Example 2: Force Delete Service
#!/bin/bash
# Force delete (immediate)
aws ecs delete-service \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-staging-service \
  --force
{ "Service": { "ServiceName": "anihpj-staging-service", "Status": "DRAINING" } }
# Force delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Immediate service termination")
Example 3: Delete Service by ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Delete using full ARN
SERVICE_ARN="arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-cluster/old-service"

aws ecs delete-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service $SERVICE_ARN \
  --force
{ "Service": { "ServiceArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-cluster/old-service", "Status": "DRAINING" } }
# By ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Service terminating by ARN")
Example 4: Cleanup All Services
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all services in cluster
CLUSTER="anihpj-test-cluster"

aws ecs list-services --cluster $CLUSTER --query 'serviceArns' --output text | \
while read svc; do
  aws ecs delete-service --cluster $CLUSTER --service $svc --force
  echo "Deleted: $svc"
done
Deleted: arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/.../service1 Deleted: arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/.../service2
# Cleanup all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All services deleted")
Example 5: Delete and Verify
#!/bin/bash
# Delete and wait for completion
aws ecs delete-service \
  --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --force

# Wait for deletion
sleep 5

# Verify deletion
if aws ecs describe-services --cluster anihpj-fargate-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service &>/dev/null; then
  echo "✓ Service deleted successfully"
fi
✓ Service deleted successfully
# Verify delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deletion verified")

register-task-definition

Registers a new ECS task definition that describes how Docker containers should run. Task definitions specify Docker image, memory, CPU, environment variables, and volumes for your Django application.

Syntax
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --family  \
  --network-mode awsvpc \
  --requires-compatibilities FARGATE \
  --cpu  \
  --memory  \
  --container-definitions  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --family (string): [Required] Task definition family name
  • --network-mode (string): Must be 'awsvpc' for Fargate
  • --requires-compatibilities (list): Set to FARGATE
  • --cpu (string): CPU units (256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096)
  • --memory (string): Memory in MB (512-30720)
  • --container-definitions (string): JSON array of containers
Return Value
  • TaskDefinition (Object): Registered task definition
  • TaskDefinitionArn (String): ARN of the task definition
Example 1: Register Django Task Definition
#!/bin/bash
# Register Django task definition
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --family anihpj-django \
  --network-mode awsvpc \
  --requires-compatibilities FARGATE \
  --cpu 512 \
  --memory 1024 \
  --container-definitions '[
    {
      "name": "django",
      "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest",
      "portMappings": [
        {"containerPort": 8000, "hostPort": 8000, "protocol": "tcp"}
      ],
      "environment": [
        {"name": "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "value": "anihpj.settings.production"},
        {"name": "DEBUG", "value": "False"}
      ]
    }
  ]'
{ "TaskDefinition": { "TaskDefinitionArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:1", "Family": "anihpj-django", "Revision": "1", "Status": "ACTIVE", "Cpu": "512", "Memory": "1024" } }
# Task definition from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Django task definition registered")
Example 2: Task Definition from JSON File
#!/bin/bash
# Create task definition file
cat > task-def.json <<'EOF'
{
  "family": "anihpj-django",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "256",
  "memory": "512",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "django",
      "image": "anihpj:latest",
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000}],
      "environment": [
        {"name": "DATABASE_URL", "value": "postgresql://db.example.com/anihpj"}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Register from file
aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://task-def.json
{ "TaskDefinition": { "Family": "anihpj-django", "Revision": "2" } }
# From file from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task definition from file")
Example 3: Task Definition with Logging
#!/bin/bash
# Register with CloudWatch logging
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --family anihpj-django-logging \
  --network-mode awsvpc \
  --requires-compatibilities FARGATE \
  --cpu 512 \
  --memory 1024 \
  --container-definitions '[
    {
      "name": "django",
      "image": "anihpj:latest",
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000}],
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/anihpj-django",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      }
    }
  ]'
{ "TaskDefinition": { "Family": "anihpj-django-logging", "Revision": "1" } }
# Logging from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task def with logging")
Example 4: High CPU/Memory Task Definition
#!/bin/bash
# Register high-performance task definition
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --family anihpj-django-large \
  --network-mode awsvpc \
  --requires-compatibilities FARGATE \
  --cpu 2048 \
  --memory 4096 \
  --container-definitions '[
    {
      "name": "django",
      "image": "anihpj:latest",
      "cpu": 1024,
      "memory": 2048,
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000}]
    }
  ]'
{ "TaskDefinition": { "Family": "anihpj-django-large", "Cpu": "2048", "Memory": "4096" } }
# High performance from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Large task definition")
Example 5: Register Latest Version
#!/bin/bash
# Register new version (auto-increments)
aws ecs register-task-definition \
  --family anihpj-django \
  --network-mode awsvpc \
  --requires-compatibilities FARGATE \
  --cpu 512 \
  --memory 1024 \
  --container-definitions '[
    {"name": "django", "image": "anihpj:v2.0", "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000}]}
  ]' \
  --query 'TaskDefinition.{Family:Family,Revision:Revision}' \
  --output table
-------------------------- | Family | Revision | -------------------------- | anihpj-django | 2 | --------------------------
# New version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"New task definition revision")

describe-task-definition

Returns full details of a registered task definition including container definitions, resource specifications, and environment variables.

Syntax
aws ecs describe-task-definition \
  --task-definition  \
  [--include ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --task-definition (string): [Required] Task definition name:revision or ARN
  • --include (list): Include TAGS
Return Value
  • TaskDefinition (Object): Full task definition details
  • Tags (Array): Associated tags
Example 1: Describe Latest Task Definition
#!/bin/bash
# Get latest task definition
aws ecs describe-task-definition \
  --task-definition anihpj-django
{ "TaskDefinition": { "Family": "anihpj-django", "Revision": "1", "Status": "ACTIVE", "Cpu": "512", "Memory": "1024", "ContainerDefinitions": [ {"Name": "django", "Image": "anihpj:latest", "Memory": 1024} ] } }
# Latest version from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task definition retrieved")
Example 2: Describe Specific Revision
#!/bin/bash
# Get specific revision
aws ecs describe-task-definition \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2
{ "TaskDefinition": { "Family": "anihpj-django", "Revision": "2", "ContainerDefinitions": [ {"Image": "anihpj:v2.0"} ] } }
# Specific revision from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Revision 2 retrieved")
Example 3: Get Container Definitions
#!/bin/bash
# Extract container config
aws ecs describe-task-definition \
  --task-definition anihpj-django \
  --query 'TaskDefinition.ContainerDefinitions[0]' \
  --output json
{ "Name": "django", "Image": "anihpj:latest", "Cpu": 512, "Memory": 1024, "PortMappings": [{"ContainerPort": 8000}], "Environment": [ {"Name": "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "Value": "anihpj.settings.production"} ] }
# Container config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Container definition extracted")
Example 4: Compare Task Definition Versions
#!/bin/bash
# Compare two revisions
echo "=== Revision 1 ==="
aws ecs describe-task-definition --task-definition anihpj-django:1 \
  --query 'TaskDefinition.{Image:ContainerDefinitions[0].Image,Cpu:Cpu,Memory:Memory}'

echo "=== Revision 2 ==="
aws ecs describe-task-definition --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --query 'TaskDefinition.{Image:ContainerDefinitions[0].Image,Cpu:Cpu,Memory:Memory}'
=== Revision 1 === {"Image": "anihpj:v1.0", "Cpu": "512", "Memory": "1024"} === Revision 2 === {"Image": "anihpj:v2.0", "Cpu": "512", "Memory": "1024"}
# Compare from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Versions compared")
Example 5: Export Task Definition
#!/bin/bash
# Save task definition for backup
aws ecs describe-task-definition \
  --task-definition anihpj-django \
  --query 'TaskDefinition' \
  > task-def-backup.json

echo "✓ Task definition exported"
✓ Task definition exported
# Export from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task definition backed up")

list-task-definitions

Lists all task definition families or revisions. Use to discover available task definitions and their versions.

Syntax
aws ecs list-task-definitions \
  [--family-prefix ] \
  [--status ] \
  [--sort ] \
  [--max-results ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --family-prefix (string): Filter by family name prefix
  • --status (string): ACTIVE or INACTIVE
  • --sort (string): ASC or DESC
  • --max-results (integer): Max results (1-100)
Return Value
  • TaskDefinitionArns (Array): ARNs of task definitions
Example 1: List All Task Definitions
#!/bin/bash
# Get all task definitions
aws ecs list-task-definitions
{ "TaskDefinitionArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:1", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:2", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-api:1" ] }
# List all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 task definitions found")
Example 2: List by Family Prefix
#!/bin/bash
# Filter by prefix
aws ecs list-task-definitions \
  --family-prefix anihpj-django
{ "TaskDefinitionArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:1", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:2" ] }
# By prefix from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 Django task definitions")
Example 3: List Active Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get only active task definitions
aws ecs list-task-definitions \
  --status ACTIVE \
  --sort DESC
{ "TaskDefinitionArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:2", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-django:1" ] }
# Active definitions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Active task definitions listed")
Example 4: List Latest Versions Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get latest revision per family
aws ecs list-task-definition-families \
  --status ACTIVE
{ "Families": [ "anihpj-django", "anihpj-api", "anihpj-celery" ] }
# Family names from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 task families")
Example 5: Count Task Definitions
#!/bin/bash
# Count all task definitions
TASK_COUNT=$(aws ecs list-task-definitions \
  --query 'length(TaskDefinitionArns)')
echo "Total task definitions: $TASK_COUNT"
Total task definitions: 5
# Count definitions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"5 total task definitions")

run-task

Runs a standalone task (not part of a service). Useful for one-off jobs like migrations, batch processing, and maintenance tasks.

Syntax
aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster  \
  --task-definition  \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration  \
  [--count ] \
  [--overrides ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): Cluster to run task on
  • --task-definition (string): Task definition name:revision
  • --launch-type (string): FARGATE for serverless
  • --network-configuration (structure): VPC networking config
  • --count (integer): Number of tasks to run
  • --overrides (structure): Override container commands
Return Value
  • Tasks (Array): Running task details
  • Failures (Array): Any task launch failures
Example 1: Run Django Migration Task
#!/bin/bash
# Run database migration
aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
  --overrides '{"containerOverrides":[{"name":"django","command":["python","manage.py","migrate"]}]}'
{ "Tasks": [{ "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123", "LastStatus": "PENDING", "DesiredStatus": "RUNNING" }], "Failures": [] }
# Run migration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Migration task started")
Example 2: Run Batch Job
#!/bin/bash
# Run batch processing task
aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --count 3 \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=DISABLED}"
{ "Tasks": [ {"TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task/task-1", "LastStatus": "PENDING"}, {"TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task/task-2", "LastStatus": "PENDING"}, {"TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task/task-3", "LastStatus": "PENDING"} ], "Failures": [] }
# Batch tasks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 batch tasks started")
Example 3: Run Custom Management Command
#!/bin/bash
# Run custom Django command
aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
  --overrides '{"containerOverrides":[{"name":"django","command":["python","manage.py","cleanup_old_jobs"]}]}'
{ "Tasks": [{ "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/cleanup-task", "LastStatus": "PENDING" }], "Failures": [] }
# Custom command from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost JobPost.objects.filter(expired=True).delete() print(f"Cleanup task running")
Example 4: Run Collectstatic Task
#!/bin/bash
# Collect static files
aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
  --overrides '{"containerOverrides":[{"name":"django","command":["python","manage.py","collectstatic","--noinput"]}]}'
{ "Tasks": [{ "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/static-task", "LastStatus": "PENDING" }], "Failures": [] }
# Collectstatic from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static files collection started")
Example 5: Run Task and Wait
#!/bin/bash
# Run task and wait for completion
TASK_ARN=$(aws ecs run-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task-definition anihpj-django:2 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-abc123],securityGroups=[sg-xyz789],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}" \
  --query 'Tasks[0].TaskArn' --output text)

aws ecs wait tasks-stopped \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks $TASK_ARN
echo "Task completed"
Task completed
# Wait for task from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task completed successfully")

describe-tasks

Retrieves details about running or stopped tasks. Use to monitor task status, get container info, and troubleshoot issues.

Syntax
aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster  \
  --tasks  \
  [--include ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): Cluster name or ARN
  • --tasks (list): Task IDs or ARNs
  • --include (list): TAGS to include tags
Return Value
  • Tasks (Array): Detailed task information
  • Failures (Array): Any lookup failures
Example 1: Describe Single Task
#!/bin/bash
# Get task details
aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks abc123def456
{ "Tasks": [{ "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123def456", "LastStatus": "RUNNING", "DesiredStatus": "RUNNING", "Cpu": "256", "Memory": "512", "Containers": [{ "Name": "django", "LastStatus": "RUNNING", "NetworkInterfaces": [{"PrivateIpv4Address": "10.0.1.50"}] }] }], "Failures": [] }
# Task details from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task RUNNING at 10.0.1.50")
Example 2: Describe Multiple Tasks
#!/bin/bash
# Get multiple tasks
aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks abc123 def456 ghi789
{ "Tasks": [ {"TaskArn": "...abc123", "LastStatus": "RUNNING"}, {"TaskArn": "...def456", "LastStatus": "RUNNING"}, {"TaskArn": "...ghi789", "LastStatus": "STOPPED"} ], "Failures": [] }
# Multiple tasks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 running, 1 stopped")
Example 3: Check Task Health
#!/bin/bash
# Get health status
aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks abc123def456 \
  --query 'Tasks[0].{Status:LastStatus,Health:HealthStatus,StopCode:StopCode}'
{ "Status": "RUNNING", "Health": "HEALTHY", "StopCode": null }
# Health check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task healthy and running")
Example 4: Get Container IP
#!/bin/bash
# Extract private IP
TASK_IP=$(aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks abc123def456 \
  --query 'Tasks[0].Containers[0].NetworkInterfaces[0].PrivateIpv4Address' \
  --output text)
echo "Task IP: $TASK_IP"
Task IP: 10.0.1.50
# Get IP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Container IP: 10.0.1.50")
Example 5: Check Stop Reason
#!/bin/bash
# Why did task stop?
aws ecs describe-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --tasks abc123def456 \
  --query 'Tasks[0].{StopCode:StopCode,StopReason:StoppedReason}'
{ "StopCode": "EssentialContainerExited", "StopReason": "Essential container in task exited" }
# Stop reason from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Container exited - check logs")

stop-task

Stops a running task. Use to terminate tasks that are stuck, no longer needed, or consuming resources.

Syntax
aws ecs stop-task \
  --cluster  \
  --task  \
  [--reason ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): Cluster name or ARN
  • --task (string): Task ID or ARN to stop
  • --reason (string): Reason for stopping
Return Value
  • Task (Object): Stopped task details
Example 1: Stop Task by ID
#!/bin/bash
# Stop a running task
aws ecs stop-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task abc123def456
{ "Task": { "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/abc123def456", "LastStatus": "RUNNING", "DesiredStatus": "STOPPED", "StoppedReason": "Task stopped by user" } }
# Stop task from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task stopped")
Example 2: Stop with Reason
#!/bin/bash
# Stop with explanation
aws ecs stop-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task abc123def456 \
  --reason "Deploying new version"
{ "Task": { "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task/abc123def456", "DesiredStatus": "STOPPED", "StoppedReason": "Deploying new version" } }
# With reason from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task stopped for deployment")
Example 3: Stop Task by ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Stop using full ARN
aws ecs stop-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123def456
{ "Task": { "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123def456", "DesiredStatus": "STOPPED" } }
# Stop by ARN from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Task stopped by ARN")
Example 4: Stop Stuck Migration
#!/bin/bash
# Stop hung migration task
aws ecs stop-task \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --task migration-task-123 \
  --reason "Migration stuck - manual intervention needed"
{ "Task": { "TaskArn": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task/migration-task-123", "DesiredStatus": "STOPPED", "StoppedReason": "Migration stuck - manual intervention needed" } }
# Stop migration from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Stuck migration stopped")
Example 5: Stop All Tasks in Cluster
#!/bin/bash
# Stop all running tasks
for TASK in $(aws ecs list-tasks --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster --query 'TaskArns[]' --output text); do
  aws ecs stop-task --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster --task $TASK --reason "Cluster maintenance"
done
echo "All tasks stopped"
All tasks stopped
# Stop all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All tasks stopped for maintenance")

list-tasks

Lists tasks running in a cluster. Filter by service, family, or status to find specific tasks.

Syntax
aws ecs list-tasks \
  --cluster  \
  [--service-name ] \
  [--family ] \
  [--desired-status ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): Cluster name or ARN
  • --service-name (string): Filter by service
  • --family (string): Filter by task family
  • --desired-status (string): RUNNING or STOPPED
Return Value
  • TaskArns (Array): List of task ARNs
Example 1: List All Running Tasks
#!/bin/bash
# All tasks in cluster
aws ecs list-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster
{ "TaskArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/def456" ] }

Example 2: List by Service
#!/bin/bash
# Tasks for specific service
aws ecs list-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-django-service
{ "TaskArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/anihpj-prod-cluster/abc123" ] }
# By service from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"1 task in Django service")
Example 3: List by Task Family
#!/bin/bash
# Filter by family
aws ecs list-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --family anihpj-django
{ "TaskArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/abc123", "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/def456" ] }
# By family from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 Django tasks running")
Example 4: List Stopped Tasks
#!/bin/bash
# Get stopped tasks
aws ecs list-tasks \

  --desired-status STOPPED
{ "TaskArns": [ "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task/stopped123" ] }
# Stopped tasks from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"1 stopped task found")
Example 5: Count Running Tasks
#!/bin/bash
# Count tasks
TASK_COUNT=$(aws ecs list-tasks \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --desired-status RUNNING \
  --query 'length(TaskArns)')
echo "Running tasks: $TASK_COUNT"
Running tasks: 3
# Count running from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 tasks currently running")

update-service-primary-task-set

Modifies the primary task set for a service using external deployment controller. Used for blue-green and canary deployments.

Syntax
aws ecs update-service-primary-task-set \
  --cluster  \
  --service  \
  --primary-task-set  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --cluster (string): Cluster name or ARN
  • --service (string): Service name or ARN
  • --primary-task-set (string): Task set ARN to make primary
Return Value
  • TaskSet (Object): Updated task set details
Example 1: Switch Primary Task Set
#!/bin/bash
# Set new primary task set
aws ecs update-service-primary-task-set \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --primary-task-set arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-set/anihpj-prod-cluster/anihpj-django-service/ts-new123
{ "TaskSet": { "Id": "ts-new123", "TaskSetArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-set/.../ts-new123", "Status": "PRIMARY", "TaskDefinition": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task-definition/anihpj-django:3" } }
# Switch primary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Primary task set updated")
Example 2: Blue-Green Deployment Switch
#!/bin/bash
# Switch from blue to green
GREEN_TASK_SET="arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-set/cluster/service/ts-green"
aws ecs update-service-primary-task-set \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --primary-task-set $GREEN_TASK_SET
echo "Switched to green deployment"
{ "TaskSet": { "Id": "ts-green", "Status": "PRIMARY" } } Switched to green deployment
# Blue-green from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Blue->Green switch complete")
Example 3: Promote Canary to Primary
#!/bin/bash
# Promote canary after testing
CANARY_TASK_SET=$(aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service \
  --query 'Services[0].TaskSets[?Status==`ACTIVE`].TaskSetArn' --output text)
aws ecs update-service-primary-task-set \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --primary-task-set $CANARY_TASK_SET
{ "TaskSet": { "Id": "ts-canary", "Status": "PRIMARY" } }
# Promote canary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Canary promoted to primary")
Example 4: Rollback to Previous
#!/bin/bash
# Rollback to old task set
OLD_TASK_SET="arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-set/cluster/service/ts-old"
aws ecs update-service-primary-task-set \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --service anihpj-django-service \
  --primary-task-set $OLD_TASK_SET
echo "Rolled back to previous version"
{ "TaskSet": { "Id": "ts-old", "Status": "PRIMARY" } } Rolled back to previous version
# Rollback from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Rollback complete")
Example 5: Verify Primary Task Set
#!/bin/bash
# Check current primary
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
  --services anihpj-django-service \
  --query 'Services[0].TaskSets[?Status==`PRIMARY`].{Id:Id,TaskDef:TaskDefinition}'
[ { "Id": "ts-new123", "TaskDef": "arn:aws:ecs:...:task-definition/anihpj-django:3" } ]
# Verify primary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Primary: anihpj-django:3")

💾 Storage Services

AWS Storage services provide scalable and durable storage for your Django application data. This section covers S3 for object storage, EFS for shared file systems, and EBS for block storage volumes. Learn how to store static files, media uploads, and database backups for your jobpost application.

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)

Scalable object storage for static files, media uploads, and backups

📋 S3 Overview

Amazon S3 provides highly scalable, durable object storage for your anihpj Django application. Store static files (CSS, JS, images), user-uploaded media, database backups, and application logs. S3 integrates with CloudFront for CDN delivery and supports versioning, lifecycle policies, and cross-region replication for your JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data assets.

Core S3 Operations for Django

cp - Copy files to/from S3
ls - List buckets and objects
mb - Make (create) bucket
mv - Move files to/from S3
presign - Generate presigned URLs
rb - Remove bucket
rm - Remove objects
sync - Sync directories with S3
website - Configure static website

cp

Copies files and objects to/from S3 buckets. Use to upload static files, download backups, or copy between buckets for your Django application.

Syntax
aws s3 cp   \
  [--recursive] \
  [--exclude ] \
  [--include ] \
  [--acl ] \
  [--storage-class ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • <source> (string): Local path or S3 URI (s3://bucket/key)
  • <destination> (string): Local path or S3 URI
  • --recursive: Copy directories recursively
  • --exclude (string): Exclude files matching pattern
  • --include (string): Include files matching pattern
  • --acl (string): ACL (private, public-read, etc.)
  • --storage-class (string): STANDARD, INTELLIGENT_TIERING, GLACIER
Return Value
  • Output: Progress and completion status
Example 1: Upload Static Files
#!/bin/bash
# Upload Django static files to S3
aws s3 cp ./staticfiles/ s3://anihpj-static-bucket/static/ \
  --recursive \
  --acl public-read
upload: staticfiles/css/main.css to s3://anihpj-static-bucket/static/css/main.css upload: staticfiles/js/app.js to s3://anihpj-static-bucket/static/js/app.js upload: staticfiles/images/logo.png to s3://anihpj-static-bucket/static/images/logo.png
# Upload static from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static files uploaded to S3")
Example 2: Download Database Backup
#!/bin/bash
# Download database backup from S3
aws s3 cp s3://anihpj-backups/db/backup-2024-01-15.sql ./backups/
download: s3://anihpj-backups/db/backup-2024-01-15.sql to backups/backup-2024-01-15.sql
# Download backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Database backup downloaded")
Example 3: Upload Media Files with Exclusions
#!/bin/bash
# Upload media excluding temp files
aws s3 cp ./media/ s3://anihpj-media-bucket/uploads/ \
  --recursive \
  --exclude "*.tmp" \
  --exclude ".DS_Store"
upload: media/resumes/user_1_resume.pdf to s3://anihpj-media-bucket/uploads/resumes/user_1_resume.pdf upload: media/avatars/author_5.jpg to s3://anihpj-media-bucket/uploads/avatars/author_5.jpg
# Upload media from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost users = User.objects.filter(resume__isnull=False) print(f"{users.count()} user resumes uploaded")
Example 4: Copy Between Buckets
#!/bin/bash
# Copy from prod to staging bucket
aws s3 cp s3://anihpj-prod-static/ s3://anihpj-staging-static/ \
  --recursive
copy: s3://anihpj-prod-static/css/main.css to s3://anihpj-staging-static/css/main.css copy: s3://anihpj-prod-static/js/app.js to s3://anihpj-staging-static/js/app.js
# Copy buckets from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod assets copied to staging")
Example 5: Upload with Storage Class
#!/bin/bash
# Upload old logs to Glacier
aws s3 cp ./logs/archive/ s3://anihpj-logs/archive/ \
  --recursive \
  --storage-class GLACIER
upload: logs/archive/2023-01.log to s3://anihpj-logs/archive/2023-01.log upload: logs/archive/2023-02.log to s3://anihpj-logs/archive/2023-02.log
# Archive to Glacier from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old logs archived to Glacier")

ls

Lists S3 buckets or objects within a bucket. Use to view available buckets, browse contents, or verify uploads.

Syntax
aws s3 ls [s3://bucket/prefix] \
  [--recursive] \
  [--human-readable] \
  [--summarize] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket/prefix (string): Optional bucket and prefix to list
  • --recursive: List all objects recursively
  • --human-readable: Display sizes in KB/MB/GB
  • --summarize: Show total count and size
Return Value
  • Output: List of buckets or objects with dates and sizes
Example 1: List All Buckets
#!/bin/bash
# List all S3 buckets
aws s3 ls
2024-01-15 10:30:45 anihpj-static-bucket 2024-01-15 10:31:22 anihpj-media-bucket 2024-01-15 10:32:01 anihpj-backups
# List buckets from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 S3 buckets found")
Example 2: List Bucket Contents
#!/bin/bash
# List objects in static bucket
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-static-bucket/
PRE css/ PRE js/ PRE images/ 2024-01-15 10:30:45 1234 favicon.ico
# List contents from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static bucket contents listed")
Example 3: List Recursively with Summary
#!/bin/bash
# List all objects with totals
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-media-bucket/ \
  --recursive \
  --human-readable \
  --summarize
2024-01-15 10:30:45 45.2 KiB uploads/resumes/user_1.pdf 2024-01-15 10:31:22 12.8 KiB uploads/avatars/author_5.jpg 2024-01-15 10:32:01 128.5 KiB uploads/logos/company_12.png Total Objects: 3 Total Size: 186.5 KiB
# Summary from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"186.5 KB in 3 objects")
Example 4: List by Prefix
#!/bin/bash
# List only CSS files
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-static-bucket/css/
2024-01-15 10:30:45 8542 main.css 2024-01-15 10:30:46 2341 forms.css 2024-01-15 10:30:47 1256 responsive.css
# List CSS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 CSS files in bucket")
Example 5: List Backups
#!/bin/bash
# List database backups
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-backups/db/ \
  --human-readable
2024-01-14 02:00:00 25.6 MiB backup-2024-01-14.sql 2024-01-15 02:00:00 26.1 MiB backup-2024-01-15.sql
# List backups from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 database backups found")

mb

Makes (creates) a new S3 bucket. Bucket names must be globally unique across all AWS accounts.

Syntax
aws s3 mb s3:// \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket-name (string): Globally unique bucket name
  • --region (string): AWS region for the bucket
Return Value
  • Output: Confirmation message
Example 1: Create Static Files Bucket
#!/bin/bash
# Create bucket for Django static files
aws s3 mb s3://anihpj-static-prod-2024 \
  --region us-east-1
make_bucket: anihpj-static-prod-2024
# Create static bucket from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static bucket created")
Example 2: Create Media Bucket

aws s3 mb s3://anihpj-media-uploads
make_bucket: anihpj-media-uploads

Example 5: Create Staging Bucket

mv

Moves files between local filesystem and S3, or between S3 locations. Unlike cp, mv deletes the source after copying.

Syntax
aws s3 mv   \
  [--recursive] \
  [--exclude ] \
  [--include ] \
  [--acl ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • <source> (string): Local path or S3 URI
  • <destination> (string): Local path or S3 URI
  • --recursive: Move directories recursively
  • --exclude (string): Exclude files matching pattern
  • --include (string): Include files matching pattern
Return Value
  • Output: Move progress and confirmation
Example 1: Move Old Backups to Archive
#!/bin/bash
# Move old backups to archive folder
aws s3 mv s3://anihpj-backups/db/backup-2023-12.sql \
  s3://anihpj-backups/archive/backup-2023-12.sql
move: s3://anihpj-backups/db/backup-2023-12.sql to s3://anihpj-backups/archive/backup-2023-12.sql
# Archive backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backup moved to archive")
Example 2: Move Upload to S3
#!/bin/bash
# Move local file to S3 (removes local)
aws s3 mv ./exports/jobs_export.csv \
  s3://anihpj-exports/2024/jobs_export.csv
move: ./exports/jobs_export.csv to s3://anihpj-exports/2024/jobs_export.csv
# Move export from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost jobs = JobPost.objects.all() print(f"{jobs.count()} jobs exported to S3")
Example 3: Reorganize S3 Structure
#!/bin/bash
# Move all resumes to new path
aws s3 mv s3://anihpj-media/uploads/resumes/ \
  s3://anihpj-media/documents/resumes/ \
  --recursive
move: s3://anihpj-media/uploads/resumes/user_1.pdf to s3://anihpj-media/documents/resumes/user_1.pdf move: s3://anihpj-media/uploads/resumes/user_2.pdf to s3://anihpj-media/documents/resumes/user_2.pdf
# Reorganize from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Resumes moved to new path")
Example 4: Move Logs to Different Bucket

aws s3 mv s3://anihpj-logs/2023/ \
  s3://anihpj-logs-archive/2023/ \
  --recursive
move: s3://anihpj-logs/2023/01.log to s3://anihpj-logs-archive/2023/01.log move: s3://anihpj-logs/2023/02.log to s3://anihpj-logs-archive/2023/02.log
# Archive logs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2023 logs archived")
Example 5: Download and Remove from S3
#!/bin/bash

aws s3 mv s3://anihpj-reports/monthly/jan-2024.pdf \
  ./reports/jan-2024.pdf
move: s3://anihpj-reports/monthly/jan-2024.pdf to ./reports/jan-2024.pdf
# Download report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Report downloaded and removed from S3")

presign

Generates a presigned URL for temporary access to a private S3 object. Use for secure file downloads without making objects public.

Syntax
aws s3 presign s3:/// \
  [--expires-in ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket/key (string): S3 object path
  • --expires-in (integer): URL validity in seconds (default: 3600)
Return Value
  • URL (string): Presigned URL for temporary access
Example 1: Generate Resume Download Link
#!/bin/bash
# Create 1-hour download link for resume
aws s3 presign s3://anihpj-media/resumes/user_123.pdf \
  --expires-in 3600
https://anihpj-media.s3.amazonaws.com/resumes/user_123.pdf?X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Credential=...&X-Amz-Expires=3600&X-Amz-Signature=...
# Presigned URL from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost user = User.objects.get(id=123) print(f"Download link for {user.username}'s resume")
Example 2: Short-lived Download (5 min)
#!/bin/bash
# Create 5-minute download link
aws s3 presign s3://anihpj-exports/report.pdf \
  --expires-in 300
https://anihpj-exports.s3.amazonaws.com/report.pdf?X-Amz-Expires=300&X-Amz-Signature=...
# 5-min link from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"5-minute download link generated")
Example 3: Long-lived Link (24 hours)
#!/bin/bash
# Create 24-hour download link
aws s3 presign s3://anihpj-backups/db/latest.sql \
  --expires-in 86400
https://anihpj-backups.s3.amazonaws.com/db/latest.sql?X-Amz-Expires=86400&X-Amz-Signature=...
# 24-hour link from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"24-hour backup download link")
Example 4: Generate Link for Job Attachment
#!/bin/bash
# Create link for job posting attachment
JOB_ID=456
aws s3 presign s3://anihpj-media/jobs/${JOB_ID}/description.pdf \
  --expires-in 7200
https://anihpj-media.s3.amazonaws.com/jobs/456/description.pdf?X-Amz-Expires=7200&X-Amz-Signature=...
# Job attachment from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost job = JobPost.objects.get(id=456) print(f"Download link for: {job.title}")
Example 5: Batch Generate Links
#!/bin/bash
# Generate links for multiple files
for file in resume.pdf cover.pdf portfolio.pdf; do
  echo "=== $file ==="
  aws s3 presign s3://anihpj-media/user_1/$file --expires-in 1800
done
=== resume.pdf === https://anihpj-media.s3.amazonaws.com/user_1/resume.pdf?X-Amz-Expires=1800&... === cover.pdf === https://anihpj-media.s3.amazonaws.com/user_1/cover.pdf?X-Amz-Expires=1800&... === portfolio.pdf === https://anihpj-media.s3.amazonaws.com/user_1/portfolio.pdf?X-Amz-Expires=1800&...
# Batch links from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 download links generated")

rb

Removes (deletes) an S3 bucket. Bucket must be empty unless --force is used.

Syntax
aws s3 rb s3:// \
  [--force] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket-name (string): Name of bucket to delete
  • --force: Delete all objects first, then bucket
Return Value
  • Output: Deletion confirmation
Example 1: Delete Empty Bucket
#!/bin/bash
# Delete empty bucket
aws s3 rb s3://anihpj-temp-bucket
remove_bucket: anihpj-temp-bucket
# Delete bucket from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Empty bucket deleted")
Example 2: Force Delete with Contents

aws s3 rb s3://anihpj-old-staging --force
delete: s3://anihpj-old-staging/file1.txt delete: s3://anihpj-old-staging/file2.txt remove_bucket: anihpj-old-staging
# Force delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bucket and contents deleted")
Example 3: Delete Test Bucket
#!/bin/bash
# Remove test environment bucket
aws s3 rb s3://anihpj-test-assets --force
delete: s3://anihpj-test-assets/css/test.css delete: s3://anihpj-test-assets/js/test.js remove_bucket: anihpj-test-assets
# Delete test from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Test bucket removed")
Example 4: Delete Old Backup Bucket

aws s3 rb s3://anihpj-backups-2022 --force
delete: s3://anihpj-backups-2022/jan.sql delete: s3://anihpj-backups-2022/feb.sql remove_bucket: anihpj-backups-2022

Example 5: Delete Development Bucket

  --region us-east-1

rm

Removes objects from an S3 bucket. Use to delete individual files or entire directories.

Syntax
aws s3 rm s3:/// \
  [--recursive] \
  [--exclude ] \
  [--include ] \
  [--dryrun] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket/key (string): Object or prefix to delete
  • --recursive: Delete all matching objects
  • --exclude (string): Exclude files matching pattern
  • --include (string): Include files matching pattern
  • --dryrun: Show what would be deleted
Return Value
  • Output: Deleted object confirmations
Example 1: Delete Single File
#!/bin/bash
# Delete specific file
aws s3 rm s3://anihpj-media/uploads/old-file.pdf
delete: s3://anihpj-media/uploads/old-file.pdf
# Delete file from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old file deleted")
Example 2: Delete Directory Recursively
#!/bin/bash
# Delete entire folder
aws s3 rm s3://anihpj-static/old-version/ --recursive
delete: s3://anihpj-static/old-version/css/main.css delete: s3://anihpj-static/old-version/js/app.js delete: s3://anihpj-static/old-version/images/logo.png
# Delete folder from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Old version folder deleted")
Example 3: Delete with Exclusions
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all except .pdf files
aws s3 rm s3://anihpj-temp/ \
  --recursive \
  --exclude "*.pdf"
delete: s3://anihpj-temp/data.json delete: s3://anihpj-temp/log.txt
# Keep PDFs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deleted all except PDFs")
Example 4: Dry Run First
#!/bin/bash
# Preview what would be deleted
aws s3 rm s3://anihpj-logs/2023/ \
  --recursive \
  --dryrun
(dryrun) delete: s3://anihpj-logs/2023/01.log (dryrun) delete: s3://anihpj-logs/2023/02.log (dryrun) delete: s3://anihpj-logs/2023/03.log
# Dry run from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Would delete 3 log files")
Example 5: Delete Only Specific Type
#!/bin/bash
# Delete only .tmp files
aws s3 rm s3://anihpj-uploads/ \
  --recursive \
  --exclude "*" \
  --include "*.tmp"
delete: s3://anihpj-uploads/temp1.tmp delete: s3://anihpj-uploads/cache/temp2.tmp
# Clean temps from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Temporary files cleaned")

sync

Syncs directories between local filesystem and S3. Only uploads/downloads changed files, making it efficient for deployments.

Syntax
aws s3 sync   \
  [--delete] \
  [--exclude ] \
  [--include ] \
  [--acl ] \
  [--dryrun] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • <source> (string): Source path (local or S3)
  • <destination> (string): Destination path
  • --delete: Delete dest files not in source
  • --exclude (string): Exclude files matching pattern
  • --acl (string): ACL for uploaded objects
  • --dryrun: Show what would happen
Return Value
  • Output: Sync progress and changes
Example 1: Deploy Static Files
#!/bin/bash
# Sync Django static files to S3
python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
aws s3 sync ./staticfiles/ s3://anihpj-static/static/ \
  --acl public-read \
  --delete
upload: staticfiles/css/main.css to s3://anihpj-static/static/css/main.css upload: staticfiles/js/app.js to s3://anihpj-static/static/js/app.js delete: s3://anihpj-static/static/old-file.css
# Deploy static from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static files synced to S3")
Example 2: Backup Media Files
#!/bin/bash
# Sync media uploads to backup bucket
aws s3 sync s3://anihpj-media/ s3://anihpj-media-backup/ \
  --delete
copy: s3://anihpj-media/avatars/new.jpg to s3://anihpj-media-backup/avatars/new.jpg
# Backup media from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Media backed up to secondary bucket")
Example 3: Download for Local Dev
#!/bin/bash
# Sync prod media to local for development
aws s3 sync s3://anihpj-media/uploads/ ./media/uploads/ \
  --exclude "*.tmp"
download: s3://anihpj-media/uploads/resume_1.pdf to media/uploads/resume_1.pdf download: s3://anihpj-media/uploads/avatar_5.jpg to media/uploads/avatar_5.jpg
# Local sync from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod media synced to local")
Example 4: Sync with Exclusions
#!/bin/bash
# Sync excluding cache and temp files
aws s3 sync ./build/ s3://anihpj-static/ \
  --exclude "*.map" \
  --exclude "*.tmp" \
  --exclude ".git/*"
upload: build/css/main.css to s3://anihpj-static/css/main.css upload: build/js/bundle.js to s3://anihpj-static/js/bundle.js
# Clean sync from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Build synced, excluding dev files")
Example 5: Dry Run Deployment
#!/bin/bash
# Preview deployment changes
aws s3 sync ./staticfiles/ s3://anihpj-static/ \
  --delete \
  --dryrun
(dryrun) upload: staticfiles/css/new.css to s3://anihpj-static/css/new.css (dryrun) delete: s3://anihpj-static/css/removed.css
# Preview from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Would upload 1, delete 1")

website

Configures an S3 bucket as a static website. Sets index and error documents for web hosting.

Syntax
aws s3 website s3:// \
  [--index-document ] \
  [--error-document ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • s3://bucket (string): Bucket to configure
  • --index-document (string): Default page (index.html)
  • --error-document (string): Error page (error.html)
Return Value
  • Output: None on success
Example 1: Configure Static Website
#!/bin/bash
# Set up static website hosting
aws s3 website s3://anihpj-frontend \
  --index-document index.html \
  --error-document error.html
# Configure website from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Website hosting enabled")
Example 2: Configure Documentation Site
#!/bin/bash
# Set up docs website
aws s3 website s3://anihpj-docs \
  --index-document index.html \
  --error-document 404.html
# Docs site from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Documentation site configured")
Example 3: Full Website Setup
#!/bin/bash
# Complete website setup
BUCKET="anihpj-landing-page"

# Create bucket
aws s3 mb s3://$BUCKET

# Configure website
aws s3 website s3://$BUCKET \
  --index-document index.html \
  --error-document error.html

# Upload content
aws s3 sync ./dist/ s3://$BUCKET/ --acl public-read

echo "Website: http://$BUCKET.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com"
make_bucket: anihpj-landing-page upload: dist/index.html to s3://anihpj-landing-page/index.html Website: http://anihpj-landing-page.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com
# Full setup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Landing page deployed")
Example 4: SPA Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Configure for Single Page App (React/Vue)
aws s3 website s3://anihpj-spa \
  --index-document index.html \
  --error-document index.html
# SPA config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SPA routing configured")
Example 5: Get Website Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Check current website config
aws s3api get-bucket-website \
  --bucket anihpj-frontend
{ "IndexDocument": { "Suffix": "index.html" }, "ErrorDocument": { "Key": "error.html" } }
# Get config from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Website: index.html, error.html")

Amazon EFS (Elastic File System)

Scalable shared file storage for Django media and static files across multiple instances

📋 EFS Overview

Amazon EFS provides scalable, elastic file storage that can be mounted across multiple EC2 instances or ECS containers. Use EFS to share Django media uploads, static files, and session data across your anihpj application instances. EFS automatically scales storage capacity and supports NFS protocol for seamless integration with JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location file assets.

Core EFS Operations for Django

create-access-point - Create access point
create-file-system - Create EFS file system
create-mount-target - Create mount point
create-replication-configuration - Setup replication
delete-access-point - Remove access point
delete-file-system - Remove file system
delete-file-system-policy - Remove policy
delete-mount-target - Remove mount target
delete-replication-configuration - Remove replication
describe-access-points - List access points
describe-account-preferences - Get preferences
describe-backup-policy - Get backup status
describe-file-system-policy - Get policy
describe-file-systems - List file systems
describe-lifecycle-configuration - Get lifecycle
describe-mount-target-security-groups - Get SGs
describe-mount-targets - List mount targets
describe-replication-configurations - List replications
list-tags-for-resource - List resource tags
modify-mount-target-security-groups - Update SGs
put-account-preferences - Set preferences
put-backup-policy - Enable/disable backups
put-file-system-policy - Set policy
put-lifecycle-configuration - Set lifecycle rules
tag-resource - Add resource tags
untag-resource - Remove resource tags
update-file-system - Modify EFS settings
update-file-system-protection - Update protection

create-file-system

Creates a new EFS file system. The file system provides shared storage accessible from multiple EC2 instances for your Django application.

Syntax
aws efs create-file-system \
  [--creation-token <value>] \
  [--performance-mode <value>] \
  [--encrypted] \
  [--throughput-mode <value>] \
  [--tags <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --creation-token (string) [Optional]: Unique token for idempotency
  • --performance-mode (string) [Optional]: generalPurpose or maxIO
  • --encrypted (flag) [Optional]: Enable encryption at rest
  • --throughput-mode (string) [Optional]: bursting, provisioned, or elastic
  • --tags (list) [Optional]: Key-value tags for the file system
Return Value
  • FileSystemId (string): ID of the created file system
  • CreationToken (string): Token used for creation
  • LifeCycleState (string): creating, available, deleting, deleted
  • Encrypted (boolean): Encryption status
  • PerformanceMode (string): generalPurpose or maxIO
  • ThroughputMode (string): bursting, provisioned, or elastic
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Basic EFS for Django Media

Create encrypted EFS for Django media files.

#!/bin/bash
# Create encrypted EFS for Django media files

echo "Creating EFS..."

aws efs create-file-system \
    --creation-token anihpj-media-efs \
    --performance-mode generalPurpose \
    --encrypted \
    --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-django-media Key=Environment,Value=production

echo "EFS created for Django media"
Creating EFS... { "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "CreationToken": "anihpj-media-efs", "LifeCycleState": "creating", "Encrypted": true, "PerformanceMode": "generalPurpose" } EFS created for Django media
# Create basic EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS: fs-0abc123def456789") print(f"Mode: generalPurpose") print(f"Encrypted: Yes") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create High-Performance EFS

Create maxIO EFS for heavy workloads.

#!/bin/bash
# Create maxIO EFS for heavy workloads

echo "Creating high-performance EFS..."

aws efs create-file-system \
    --creation-token anihpj-highperf-efs \
    --performance-mode maxIO \
    --throughput-mode elastic \
    --encrypted

echo "High-performance EFS created"
Creating high-performance EFS... { "FileSystemId": "fs-0xyz789abc123456", "PerformanceMode": "maxIO", "ThroughputMode": "elastic", "Encrypted": true } High-performance EFS created
# High-perf EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS: fs-0xyz789abc123456") print(f"Mode: maxIO") print(f"Throughput: elastic") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Create EFS for Static Files

Create EFS for shared static files.

#!/bin/bash
# Create EFS for shared static files

echo "Creating static files EFS..."

aws efs create-file-system \
    --creation-token anihpj-static-efs \
    --performance-mode generalPurpose \
    --throughput-mode bursting \
    --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-static Key=Purpose,Value=staticfiles

echo "Static files EFS created"
Creating static files EFS... { "FileSystemId": "fs-0static123456789", "ThroughputMode": "bursting", "LifeCycleState": "creating" } Static files EFS created
# Static EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS: fs-0static123456789") print(f"Purpose: Static files") print(f"Throughput: bursting") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Create Dev Environment EFS

Create unencrypted EFS for development.

#!/bin/bash
# Create unencrypted EFS for development

echo "Creating dev EFS..."

aws efs create-file-system \
    --creation-token anihpj-dev-efs \
    --performance-mode generalPurpose \
    --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-dev Key=Environment,Value=development

echo "Dev EFS created"
Creating dev EFS... { "FileSystemId": "fs-0dev123456789abc", "Encrypted": false, "LifeCycleState": "creating" } Dev EFS created
# Dev EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS: fs-0dev123456789abc") print(f"Environment: Development") print(f"Encrypted: No") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Create and Get File System ID

Create and capture file system ID for scripting.

#!/bin/bash
# Create and capture file system ID

echo "Creating production EFS..."

FS_ID=$(aws efs create-file-system \
    --creation-token anihpj-prod-efs \
    --encrypted \
    --query 'FileSystemId' --output text)

echo "Created EFS: $FS_ID"
echo "File system ID captured for scripting"
Creating production EFS... Created EFS: fs-0abc123def456789 File system ID captured for scripting
# Get FS ID from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS ID: fs-0abc123def456789") print(f"Captured for scripting") print(f"Ready for mount targets") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

describe-file-systems

Returns information about EFS file systems. Use to list all file systems or get details about specific ones.

Syntax
aws efs describe-file-systems \
  [--file-system-id <value>] \
  [--creation-token <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string) [Optional]: Specific file system ID
  • --creation-token (string) [Optional]: Filter by creation token
Return Value
  • FileSystems (array): List of file system objects
  • FileSystemId (string): ID of the file system
  • Name (string): File system name
  • LifeCycleState (string): available, creating, deleting, deleted
  • Encrypted (boolean): Encryption status
  • SizeInBytes (object): File system size information
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All File Systems

List all EFS file systems.

#!/bin/bash
# List all EFS file systems

echo "Listing all EFS file systems..."

aws efs describe-file-systems

echo "File systems listed"
Listing all EFS file systems... { "FileSystems": [ { "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Name": "anihpj-django-media", "LifeCycleState": "available", "SizeInBytes": {"Value": 1048576} } ] } File systems listed
# List all EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"File Systems") print(f"\nTotal: 1 found") print(f"fs-0abc123def456789: Available") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Specific File System

Get details for specific EFS.

#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific EFS

echo "Getting EFS details..."

aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789

echo "EFS details retrieved"
Getting EFS details... { "FileSystems": [ { "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Name": "anihpj-django-media", "LifeCycleState": "available", "Encrypted": true, "PerformanceMode": "generalPurpose", "ThroughputMode": "bursting", "SizeInBytes": {"Value": 2097152} } ] } EFS details retrieved
# Get specific EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS Details") print(f"\nID: fs-0abc123def456789") print(f"State: available") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Filter by Creation Token

Find EFS by creation token.

#!/bin/bash
# Find EFS by creation token

echo "Finding EFS by token..."

aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --creation-token anihpj-media-efs

echo "EFS found"
Finding EFS by token... { "FileSystems": [ { "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "CreationToken": "anihpj-media-efs" } ] } EFS found
# Filter by token from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Token: anihpj-media-efs") print(f"Found: fs-0abc123def456789") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Get Encrypted File Systems

Filter for encrypted file systems.

#!/bin/bash
# Get only encrypted file systems

echo "Getting encrypted EFS..."

aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --query 'FileSystems[?Encrypted==`true`].{Id:FileSystemId,Name:Name}'

echo "Encrypted EFS listed"
Getting encrypted EFS... [ { "Id": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Name": "anihpj-django-media" }, { "Id": "fs-0xyz789abc123456", "Name": "anihpj-prod-efs" } ] Encrypted EFS listed
# Encrypted EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Encrypted EFS") print(f"\n1: fs-0abc123def456789") print(f"2: fs-0xyz789abc123456") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Get Available File Systems

List only available (not creating) file systems.

#!/bin/bash
# Get only available file systems

echo "Getting available EFS..."

aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --query 'FileSystems[?LifeCycleState==`available`].[FileSystemId,Name,SizeInBytes.Value]' \
    --output table

echo "Available EFS displayed"
Getting available EFS... ----------------------- --------- ---------- fs-0abc123def456789 anihpj-media 2097152 fs-0xyz789abc123456 anihpj-static 1048576 fs-0dev123456789abc anihpj-dev 524288 ----------------------- --------- ---------- Available EFS displayed
# Available EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Available EFS") print(f"\n3 file systems ready") print(f"All state: available") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
aws efs describe-file-systems \ --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "FileSystems": [{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "LifeCycleState": "available", "Encrypted": true, "PerformanceMode": "generalPurpose" }] }
# Specific EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS available and encrypted")
Example 3: Get File System by Token
#!/bin/bash
# Find EFS by creation token
aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --creation-token anihpj-media-efs
{ "FileSystems": [{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "CreationToken": "anihpj-media-efs" }] }
# By token from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Found EFS by token")
Example 4: Get File System Size
#!/bin/bash
# Check EFS storage usage
aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'FileSystems[0].SizeInBytes'
{ "Value": 52428800, "ValueInIA": 0, "ValueInStandard": 52428800 }
# Check size from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"EFS using 50 MB storage")
Example 5: List Only Available Systems
#!/bin/bash
# Get available file systems
aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[?LifeCycleState==`available`].FileSystemId' \
  --output text
fs-0abc123def456789 fs-0xyz789abc123456
# Available only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 available EFS systems")

delete-file-system

Deletes an EFS file system. All mount targets must be deleted first before the file system can be removed.

Syntax
aws efs delete-file-system \
  --file-system-id <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string) [Required]: ID of file system to delete
Return Value
  • Exit Code 0: File system deletion initiated successfully
  • No Output: Command completes silently on success
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete File System

Delete EFS file system.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete EFS file system

echo "Deleting EFS..."

aws efs delete-file-system \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789

echo "EFS file system deleted"
Deleting EFS... EFS file system deleted
# Delete EFS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete File System") print(f"\nFS: fs-0abc123def456789") print(f"Status: Deleted") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Delete Old Dev Environment

Clean up dev EFS.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up dev EFS

echo "Cleaning dev EFS..."

aws efs delete-file-system \
    --file-system-id fs-0dev123456789abc

echo "Dev EFS cleaned up"
Cleaning dev EFS... Dev EFS cleaned up
# Clean dev from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev Environment") print(f"\nFS: fs-0dev123456789abc") print(f"Cleaned: Yes") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Delete with Verification

Delete and verify deletion.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete and verify

echo "Deleting and verifying..."

FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs delete-file-system --file-system-id $FS_ID

sleep 5
aws efs describe-file-systems --file-system-id $FS_ID 2>&1 || echo "Deletion verified"
Deleting and verifying... Deletion verified
# Verify delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Verification") print(f"\nFS deleted: Yes") print(f"No errors: True") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Delete After Mount Target Cleanup

Delete mount targets first, then file system.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete mount targets first, then file system

echo "Cleaning up mount targets..."

FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
for MT in $(aws efs describe-mount-targets --file-system-id $FS_ID --query 'MountTargets[].MountTargetId' --output text); do
    echo "Deleting mount target: $MT"
    aws efs delete-mount-target --mount-target-id $MT
done

sleep 60
echo "Deleting file system..."
aws efs delete-file-system --file-system-id $FS_ID

echo "Full cleanup complete"
Cleaning up mount targets... Deleting mount target: fsmt-1 Deleting mount target: fsmt-2 Deleting file system... Full cleanup complete
# Full cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Full Cleanup") print(f"\nMount targets: Deleted") print(f"File system: Deleted") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Delete Test File System

Remove test EFS.

#!/bin/bash
# Remove test EFS

echo "Deleting test EFS..."

TEST_FS=$(aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --creation-token anihpj-test-efs \
    --query 'FileSystems[0].FileSystemId' --output text)

if [ ! -z "$TEST_FS" ]; then
    aws efs delete-file-system --file-system-id $TEST_FS
    echo "Test EFS removed: $TEST_FS"
fi
Deleting test EFS... Test EFS removed: fs-0test1234567890
# Delete test from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Test Cleanup") print(f"\nFS: fs-0test1234567890") print(f"Removed: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")

create-mount-target

Creates a mount target for an EFS file system in a subnet. EC2 instances in the subnet can then mount the file system.

Syntax
aws efs create-mount-target \
  --file-system-id <value> \
  --subnet-id <value> \
  [--security-groups <value>] \
  [--ip-address <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string) [Required]: EFS file system ID
  • --subnet-id (string) [Required]: Subnet for mount target
  • --security-groups (list) [Optional]: Security groups for mount target
  • --ip-address (string) [Optional]: IP address in subnet (auto-assigned if omitted)
Return Value
  • MountTargetId (string): ID of the mount target
  • FileSystemId (string): Associated file system ID
  • SubnetId (string): Associated subnet ID
  • IpAddress (string): IP address assigned
  • LifeCycleState (string): creating, available, deleting, deleted
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Mount Target in Subnet

Create mount target for Django instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Create mount target for Django instances

echo "Creating mount target..."

aws efs create-mount-target \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --security-groups sg-0efs123456789abc

echo "Mount target created"
Creating mount target... { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-0abc123def456789", "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "SubnetId": "subnet-0123456789abcdef0", "IpAddress": "10.0.1.50", "LifeCycleState": "creating" } Mount target created
# Create mount from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount Target") print(f"\nID: fsmt-0abc123def456789") print(f"IP: 10.0.1.50") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Create in Multiple AZs

Create mount targets in each AZ.

#!/bin/bash
# Create mount targets in each AZ

echo "Creating multi-AZ mount targets..."

for SUBNET in subnet-az1 subnet-az2 subnet-az3; do
    echo "Creating in $SUBNET"
    aws efs create-mount-target \
        --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
        --subnet-id $SUBNET \
        --security-groups sg-0efs123456789abc
done

echo "Multi-AZ mount targets created"
Creating multi-AZ mount targets... Creating in subnet-az1 { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-az1", "IpAddress": "10.0.1.50" } Creating in subnet-az2 { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-az2", "IpAddress": "10.0.2.50" } Multi-AZ mount targets created
# Multi-AZ from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Multi-AZ Mounts") print(f"\nAZ1: fsmt-az1") print(f"AZ2: fsmt-az2") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Create with Specific IP

Create with specific IP address.

#!/bin/bash
# Create with specific IP address

echo "Creating mount with specific IP..."

aws efs create-mount-target \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --ip-address 10.0.1.100

echo "Mount target at specific IP created"
Creating mount with specific IP... { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-0abc123def456789", "IpAddress": "10.0.1.100" } Mount target at specific IP created
# Specific IP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Specific IP Mount") print(f"\nIP: 10.0.1.100") print(f"ID: fsmt-0abc123def456789") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Create and Get DNS Name

Create mount target and get DNS.

#!/bin/bash
# Create mount target and get DNS

echo "Creating mount target..."

FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs create-mount-target \
    --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0

echo "Mount: $FS_ID.efs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com"
echo "Use in /etc/fstab for mounting"
Creating mount target... Mount: fs-0abc123def456789.efs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com Use in /etc/fstab for mounting
# DNS name from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DNS Name") print(f"\nfs-0abc123def456789.efs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com") print(f"Ready for fstab") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Create and Wait for Mount Target

Create and wait for mount target to become available.

#!/bin/bash
# Create and wait for available

echo "Creating mount target..."

MT_ID=$(aws efs create-mount-target \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --query 'MountTargetId' --output text)

echo "Waiting for $MT_ID..."
aws efs describe-mount-targets --mount-target-id $MT_ID

echo "Mount target available"
Creating mount target... Waiting for fsmt-0abc123def456789... { "MountTargets": [ { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-0abc123def456789", "LifeCycleState": "available" } ] } Mount target available
# Wait for mount from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount Available") print(f"\nID: fsmt-0abc123def456789") print(f"State: available") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
Waiting for fsmt-0abc123def456789...
# Wait for mount from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount target becoming available")

describe-mount-targets

Returns information about mount targets for an EFS file system. Use to find IP addresses and verify mount target availability.

Syntax
aws efs describe-mount-targets \
  [--file-system-id <value>] \
  [--mount-target-id <value>] \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string) [Optional]: List mounts for file system
  • --mount-target-id (string) [Optional]: Specific mount target
Return Value
  • MountTargets (array): List of mount target objects
  • MountTargetId (string): Mount target ID
  • FileSystemId (string): Associated file system ID
  • SubnetId (string): Associated subnet ID
  • IpAddress (string): IP address of mount target
  • LifeCycleState (string): creating, available, deleting, deleted
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Mount Targets for EFS

Get all mount targets.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all mount targets

echo "Listing mount targets..."

aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789

echo "Mount targets listed"
Listing mount targets... { "MountTargets": [ { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-az1", "IpAddress": "10.0.1.50", "LifeCycleState": "available" }, { "MountTargetId": "fsmt-az2", "IpAddress": "10.0.2.50", "LifeCycleState": "available" } ] } Mount targets listed
# List mounts from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount Targets") print(f"\nfsmt-az1: 10.0.1.50") print(f"fsmt-az2: 10.0.2.50") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Get Mount Target IP

Get IP for mounting.

#!/bin/bash
# Get IP for mounting

echo "Getting mount IP..."

aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --query 'MountTargets[0].IpAddress' --output text

echo "Mount IP retrieved"
Getting mount IP... 10.0.1.50 Mount IP retrieved
# Get IP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount IP") print(f"\nIP: 10.0.1.50") print(f"Ready to use") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")

Verify mount target is available.

#!/bin/bash
# Verify mount target is available

echo "Checking mount status..."

aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789 \
    --query 'MountTargets[0].LifeCycleState'

echo "Status checked"
Checking mount status... "available" Status checked
# Check status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount Status") print(f"\nState: available") print(f"Ready: Yes") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")

Get all mount IPs.

#!/bin/bash
# Get all mount IPs

echo "Getting all mount IPs..."

aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --query 'MountTargets[].IpAddress' --output text

echo "IPs retrieved"
Getting all mount IPs... 10.0.1.50 10.0.2.50 10.0.3.50 IPs retrieved
# All IPs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Mount Target IPs") print(f"\n10.0.1.50") print(f"10.0.2.50") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Get Subnet Info

Get mount target subnets.

#!/bin/bash
# Get mount target subnets

echo "Getting subnet info..."

aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --query 'MountTargets[].{ID:MountTargetId,Subnet:SubnetId,IP:IpAddress}'

echo "Subnet info retrieved"
Getting subnet info... [ {"ID": "fsmt-az1", "Subnet": "subnet-az1", "IP": "10.0.1.50"}, {"ID": "fsmt-az2", "Subnet": "subnet-az2", "IP": "10.0.2.50"} ] Subnet info retrieved
