delete-mount-target

Deletes a mount target. Must be deleted before deleting the file system.

Syntax
aws efs delete-mount-target \
  --mount-target-id <value> \
  [--region <value>]
Parameters Description
  • --mount-target-id (string) [Required]: Mount target ID to delete
Return Value
  • Exit Code 0: Mount target deletion initiated successfully
  • No Output: Command completes silently on success
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Mount Target

Delete specific mount target.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete specific mount target

echo "Deleting mount target..."

aws efs delete-mount-target \
    --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789

echo "Mount target deleted"
Deleting mount target... Mount target deleted
# Delete mount from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete Mount Target") print(f"\nID: fsmt-0abc123def456789") print(f"Status: Deleted") jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nJobs: {jobs}")
Example 2: Delete All Mount Targets

Delete all mounts for file system.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete all mounts for file system

echo "Deleting all mount targets..."

FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
for MT in $(aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --query 'MountTargets[].MountTargetId' --output text); do
    echo "Deleting: $MT"
    aws efs delete-mount-target --mount-target-id $MT
done

echo "All mounts deleted"
Deleting all mount targets... Deleting: fsmt-az1 Deleting: fsmt-az2 All mounts deleted
# Delete all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete All") print(f"\nfsmt-az1: Deleted") print(f"fsmt-az2: Deleted") users = User.objects.count() print(f"\nUsers: {users}")
Example 3: Delete with Verification

Delete and verify.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete and verify

echo "Deleting mount target..."

MT_ID="fsmt-0abc123def456789"
aws efs delete-mount-target --mount-target-id $MT_ID

echo "Waiting for deletion..."
sleep 10
aws efs describe-mount-targets --mount-target-id $MT_ID 2>&1 || echo "Mount target deleted"
Deleting mount target... Waiting for deletion... Mount target deleted
# Verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Delete Verification") print(f"\nStatus: Deleted") print(f"Verified: Yes") authors = Author.objects.count() print(f"\nAuthors: {authors}")
Example 4: Delete from Specific Subnet

Delete mount in specific subnet.

#!/bin/bash
# Delete mount in specific subnet

echo "Deleting mount from subnet..."

MT_ID=$(aws efs describe-mount-targets \
    --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
    --query 'MountTargets[?SubnetId==`subnet-az1`].MountTargetId' --output text)

if [ ! -z "$MT_ID" ]; then
    aws efs delete-mount-target --mount-target-id $MT_ID
    echo "Mount from subnet-az1 deleted"
fi
Deleting mount from subnet... Mount from subnet-az1 deleted
# By subnet from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Subnet Mount Deletion") print(f"\nSubnet: subnet-az1") print(f"Deleted: Yes") skills = Skills.objects.count() print(f"\nSkills: {skills}")
Example 5: Cleanup Dev Mounts

Clean up development mounts.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean up development mounts

echo "Cleaning dev mounts..."

DEV_FS=$(aws efs describe-file-systems \
    --creation-token anihpj-dev-efs \
    --query 'FileSystems[0].FileSystemId' --output text)

for MT in $(aws efs describe-mount-targets --file-system-id $DEV_FS \
    --query 'MountTargets[].MountTargetId' --output text); do
    echo "Deleting: $MT"
    aws efs delete-mount-target --mount-target-id $MT
done

echo "Dev cleanup complete"
Cleaning dev mounts... Deleting: fsmt-dev1 Deleting: fsmt-dev2 Dev cleanup complete
# Dev cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Development Cleanup") print(f"\nMounts deleted: 2") print(f"Complete: Yes") locations = Location.objects.count() print(f"\nLocations: {locations}")
from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev mounts cleaned up")

create-access-point

Creates an access point for an EFS file system. Access points provide application-specific entry points with enforced user identity and directory paths.

Syntax
aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--client-token ] \
  [--tags ] \
  [--posix-user ] \
  [--root-directory ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --posix-user (structure): POSIX user/group identity
  • --root-directory (structure): Root directory path and creation info
  • --tags (list): Key-value tags
Return Value
  • AccessPointId: The ID of the access point
  • AccessPointArn: The ARN of the access point
  • FileSystemId: The file system ID
  • PosixUser: POSIX user/group information
  • RootDirectory: Root directory path information
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Access Point for Media
#!/bin/bash
# Create access point for Django media
aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --posix-user Uid=1000,Gid=1000 \
  --root-directory "Path=/media,CreationInfo={OwnerUid=1000,OwnerGid=1000,Permissions=755}" \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-media-ap
{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-0abc123def456789", "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "PosixUser": {"Uid": 1000, "Gid": 1000}, "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/media"} }
# Media access point from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Media access point created")
Example 2: Create Access Point for Static Files
#!/bin/bash
# Create access point for static files
aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --root-directory "Path=/static,CreationInfo={OwnerUid=33,OwnerGid=33,Permissions=755}" \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-static-ap
{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-static123456789", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/static"} }
# Static access point from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Static files access point")
Example 3: Create for Lambda
#!/bin/bash
# Create access point for Lambda function
aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --posix-user Uid=1001,Gid=1001 \
  --root-directory "Path=/lambda,CreationInfo={OwnerUid=1001,OwnerGid=1001,Permissions=750}"
{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-lambda123456789", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/lambda"} }
# Lambda AP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lambda access point ready")
Example 4: Create for ECS/Fargate
#!/bin/bash
# Create access point for containers
aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --posix-user Uid=0,Gid=0 \
  --root-directory "Path=/app,CreationInfo={OwnerUid=0,OwnerGid=0,Permissions=755}" \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=anihpj-ecs-ap
{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-ecs123456789", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/app"} }
# ECS access point from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Container access point created")
Example 5: Get Access Point ARN
#!/bin/bash
# Create and get ARN for IAM policy
AP_ARN=$(aws efs create-access-point \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --root-directory "Path=/uploads" \
  --query 'AccessPointArn' --output text)
echo "Access Point ARN: $AP_ARN"
Access Point ARN: arn:aws:elasticfilesystem:us-east-1:123456789012:access-point/fsap-0abc123

describe-access-points

Lists access points for an EFS file system. Returns access point configuration, POSIX identity, and root directory information.

Syntax
aws efs describe-access-points \
  [--access-point-id ] \
  [--file-system-id ] \
  [--max-results ] \
  [--next-token ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --access-point-id (string): Specific access point ID
  • --file-system-id (string): File system to list access points for
  • --max-results (integer): Maximum access points to return
Return Value
  • AccessPoints: List of access point details
  • AccessPointId: The ID of each access point
  • AccessPointArn: The ARN of each access point
  • FileSystemId: Associated file system ID
  • LifeCycleState: Current state (available, creating, deleting)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Access Points
#!/bin/bash
# List all access points for file system
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "AccessPoints": [ {"AccessPointId": "fsap-media123", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/media"}}, {"AccessPointId": "fsap-static123", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/static"}} ] }
# List APs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access points: media, static")
Example 2: Get Access Point IDs
#!/bin/bash
# Get just the access point IDs
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'AccessPoints[].AccessPointId' --output text
fsap-media123 fsap-static123 fsap-lambda123
# AP IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 access points found")
Example 3: Get Specific Access Point
#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific access point
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --access-point-id fsap-0abc123def456789
{ "AccessPoints": [{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-0abc123def456789", "LifeCycleState": "available", "PosixUser": {"Uid": 1000, "Gid": 1000} }] }
# Specific AP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access point available")
"AccessPoints": [ { "AccessPointId": "fsap-media123", "AccessPointArn": "arn:aws:elasticfilesystem:us-east-1:123456789012:access-point/fsap-media123", "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "PosixUser": {"Uid": 1000, "Gid": 1000}, "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/media"}, "LifeCycleState": "available" } ] }
Example 1: List All Access Points
#!/bin/bash
# List all access points for file system
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "AccessPoints": [ {"AccessPointId": "fsap-media123", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/media"}}, {"AccessPointId": "fsap-static123", "RootDirectory": {"Path": "/static"}} ] }

aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'AccessPoints[].AccessPointId' --output text
fsap-media123 fsap-static123 fsap-lambda123
# AP IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 access points found")
Example 3: Get Specific Access Point
#!/bin/bash
# Get details for specific access point
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --access-point-id fsap-0abc123def456789
{ "AccessPoints": [{ "AccessPointId": "fsap-0abc123def456789", "LifeCycleState": "available", "PosixUser": {"Uid": 1000, "Gid": 1000} }] }
# Specific AP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access point available")
Example 4: Get POSIX Users
#!/bin/bash
# List access points with POSIX user info
aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'AccessPoints[].{ID:AccessPointId,UID:PosixUser.Uid,GID:PosixUser.Gid}' \
  --output table
------------------------------------- | DescribeAccessPoints | +-------------------+------+------+ | ID | UID | GID | +-------------------+------+------+ | fsap-media123 | 1000 | 1000 | | fsap-static123 | 33 | 33 | +-------------------+------+------+
# POSIX info from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"UID/GID mappings shown")
Example 5: Count Access Points
#!/bin/bash
# Count access points per file system
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
COUNT=$(aws efs describe-access-points \
  --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'length(AccessPoints)')
echo "Access points for $FS_ID: $COUNT"
Access points for fs-0abc123def456789: 4
# Count APs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"4 access points configured")

delete-access-point

Deletes an EFS access point. This does not delete the files in the access point's root directory.

Syntax
aws efs delete-access-point \
  --access-point-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --access-point-id (string, required): Access point to delete
Return Value
  • Success: HTTP 204 response with no output body
  • Access point is deleted (files in root directory are not deleted)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Access Point
#!/bin/bash
# Delete a specific access point
aws efs delete-access-point \
  --access-point-id fsap-0abc123def456789
(no output on success)
# Delete AP from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access point deleted")
Example 2: Delete All Access Points
#!/bin/bash
# Delete all access points for file system
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
for AP_ID in $(aws efs describe-access-points --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'AccessPoints[].AccessPointId' --output text); do
  echo "Deleting: $AP_ID"
  aws efs delete-access-point --access-point-id $AP_ID
done
Deleting: fsap-media123 Deleting: fsap-static123 Deleting: fsap-lambda123
# Delete all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All access points removed")
Example 3: Delete with Verification
#!/bin/bash
# Delete and verify removal
AP_ID="fsap-0abc123def456789"
aws efs delete-access-point --access-point-id $AP_ID
if ! aws efs describe-access-points --access-point-id $AP_ID 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Access point $AP_ID deleted"
fi
Access point fsap-0abc123def456789 deleted
# Verify delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Deletion verified")
Example 4: Delete Dev Access Points
#!/bin/bash
# Delete access points tagged as dev
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs describe-access-points --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'AccessPoints[?Tags[?Key==`Env` && Value==`dev`]].AccessPointId' \
  --output text | xargs -n1 aws efs delete-access-point --access-point-id
(no output on success)
# Delete dev APs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev access points cleaned up")
Example 5: Safe Delete Script
#!/bin/bash
# Delete access point with safety check
AP_ID="fsap-0abc123def456789"
STATE=$(aws efs describe-access-points --access-point-id $AP_ID \
  --query 'AccessPoints[0].LifeCycleState' --output text)
if [ "$STATE" = "available" ]; then
  aws efs delete-access-point --access-point-id $AP_ID
  echo "Deleted access point: $AP_ID"
fi
Deleted access point: fsap-0abc123def456789
# Safe delete from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Safe deletion complete")

put-lifecycle-configuration

Configures lifecycle management for an EFS file system. Automatically moves files to IA (Infrequent Access) storage class to reduce costs.

Syntax
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id  \
  --lifecycle-policies  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --lifecycle-policies (list, required): TransitionToIA and TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass rules (JSON list format)
Return Value
  • LifecyclePolicies: List of configured lifecycle policies
  • TransitionToIA: Days before transition to Infrequent Access (AFTER_7_DAYS, AFTER_14_DAYS, AFTER_30_DAYS, AFTER_60_DAYS, AFTER_90_DAYS)
  • TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass: Rule for returning from IA (AFTER_1_ACCESS)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Enable 30-Day Transition
#!/bin/bash
# Move files to IA after 30 days
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --lifecycle-policies '[{"TransitionToIA":"AFTER_30_DAYS"}]'
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [ {"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_30_DAYS"} ] }
# 30-day IA from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle: IA after 30 days")
Example 2: Enable IA with Auto-Return
#!/bin/bash
# Transition to IA and auto-return on access
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --lifecycle-policies '[
    {"TransitionToIA":"AFTER_14_DAYS"},
    {"TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass":"AFTER_1_ACCESS"}
  ]'
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [ {"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_14_DAYS"}, {"TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass": "AFTER_1_ACCESS"} ] }
# IA + return from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Smart tiering enabled")
Example 3: Aggressive Cost Savings
#!/bin/bash
# Move to IA after 7 days for cost savings
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --lifecycle-policies '[{"TransitionToIA":"AFTER_7_DAYS"}]'
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [ {"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_7_DAYS"} ] }
# 7-day IA from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Aggressive cost policy set")
Example 4: Disable Lifecycle Management
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all lifecycle policies
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --lifecycle-policies '[]'
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [] }
# Disable lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle disabled")
Example 5: Archive After 90 Days
#!/bin/bash
# Long-term archive policy
aws efs put-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --lifecycle-policies '[
    {"TransitionToIA":"AFTER_90_DAYS"},
    {"TransitionToArchive":"AFTER_365_DAYS"}
  ]'
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [ {"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_90_DAYS"}, {"TransitionToArchive": "AFTER_365_DAYS"} ] }
# Archive policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Archive after 1 year")

describe-lifecycle-configuration

Returns the current lifecycle configuration for an EFS file system. Shows TransitionToIA and TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass policies.

Syntax
aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
Return Value
  • LifecyclePolicies: List of currently configured policies
  • TransitionToIA: Days before transition to Infrequent Access
  • TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass: Rule for returning to primary storage
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Lifecycle Config
#!/bin/bash
# Get current lifecycle configuration
aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "LifecyclePolicies": [ {"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_30_DAYS"}, {"TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass": "AFTER_1_ACCESS"} ] }
# Get lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"30-day IA with auto-return")
Example 2: Check IA Transition Days
#!/bin/bash
# Get TransitionToIA value
aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'LifecyclePolicies[?TransitionToIA].TransitionToIA' \
  --output text
AFTER_30_DAYS
# IA days from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Transition: 30 days")
Example 3: Check All File Systems
#!/bin/bash
# List lifecycle for all file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  echo "=== $FS_ID ==="
  aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration --file-system-id $FS_ID
done
=== fs-0abc123def456789 === {"LifecyclePolicies": [{"TransitionToIA": "AFTER_30_DAYS"}]} === fs-0xyz987fed654321 === {"LifecyclePolicies": []}
# All lifecycle from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 file systems checked")
Example 4: Check If Lifecycle Enabled
#!/bin/bash
# Check if lifecycle is configured
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
POLICIES=$(aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'length(LifecyclePolicies)')
if [ "$POLICIES" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "Lifecycle enabled on $FS_ID"
else
  echo "No lifecycle on $FS_ID"
fi
Lifecycle enabled on fs-0abc123def456789
# Check enabled from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Lifecycle is enabled")
Example 5: Get Auto-Return Config
#!/bin/bash
# Check if auto-return to primary storage is enabled
aws efs describe-lifecycle-configuration \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'LifecyclePolicies[?TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass]' \
  --output yaml
- TransitionToPrimaryStorageClass: AFTER_1_ACCESS
# Auto-return from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Auto-return: after 1 access")

put-backup-policy

Enables or disables automatic backups for an EFS file system using AWS Backup.

Syntax
aws efs put-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id  \
  --backup-policy  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --backup-policy (structure, required): Status: ENABLED or DISABLED
Return Value
  • BackupPolicy: The backup policy configuration
  • Status: ENABLED or DISABLED
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Enable Automatic Backups
#!/bin/bash
# Enable AWS Backup for EFS
aws efs put-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --backup-policy Status=ENABLED
{ "BackupPolicy": { "Status": "ENABLED" } }
# Enable backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Automatic backups enabled")
Example 2: Disable Automatic Backups
#!/bin/bash
# Disable automatic backups
aws efs put-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --backup-policy Status=DISABLED
{ "BackupPolicy": { "Status": "DISABLED" } }
# Disable backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backups disabled")
Example 3: Enable for Production
#!/bin/bash
# Enable backups for all production file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[?Tags[?Key==`Env` && Value==`prod`]].FileSystemId' \
  --output text); do
  aws efs put-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --backup-policy Status=ENABLED
  echo "Enabled backup for: $FS_ID"
done
Enabled backup for: fs-0abc123def456789 Enabled backup for: fs-0prod987654321
# Prod backups from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Prod file systems backed up")
Example 4: Toggle Backup Status
#!/bin/bash
# Toggle backup based on current state
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
CURRENT=$(aws efs describe-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text)
if [ "$CURRENT" = "ENABLED" ]; then
  aws efs put-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID --backup-policy Status=DISABLED
else
  aws efs put-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID --backup-policy Status=ENABLED
fi
{ "BackupPolicy": {"Status": "DISABLED"} }
# Toggle backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backup status toggled")
Example 5: Enable with Confirmation
#!/bin/bash
# Enable and confirm status
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs put-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --backup-policy Status=ENABLED \
  --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text
ENABLED
# Confirm backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backup: ENABLED")

describe-backup-policy

Returns the backup policy for an EFS file system. Shows if automatic backups via AWS Backup are enabled.

Syntax
aws efs describe-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
Return Value
  • BackupPolicy: The backup policy configuration
  • Status: ENABLED or DISABLED
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Backup Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get backup policy status
aws efs describe-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "BackupPolicy": { "Status": "ENABLED" } }
# Get backup status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backup: ENABLED")
Example 2: Get Status Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just the backup status
aws efs describe-backup-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text
ENABLED
# Status only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Status: ENABLED")
Example 3: Check All File Systems
#!/bin/bash
# List backup status for all file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  STATUS=$(aws efs describe-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
  echo "$FS_ID: $STATUS"
done
fs-0abc123def456789: ENABLED fs-0xyz987fed654321: DISABLED fs-0dev123456789012: N/A
# All backups from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 file systems checked")
Example 4: Find Unprotected File Systems
#!/bin/bash
# Find file systems without backup enabled
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  STATUS=$(aws efs describe-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text 2>/dev/null)
  if [ "$STATUS" != "ENABLED" ]; then
    echo "UNPROTECTED: $FS_ID"
  fi
done
UNPROTECTED: fs-0xyz987fed654321 UNPROTECTED: fs-0dev123456789012
# Unprotected from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 file systems without backup")
Example 5: Conditional Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check and alert if backup disabled
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
STATUS=$(aws efs describe-backup-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'BackupPolicy.Status' --output text)
if [ "$STATUS" = "ENABLED" ]; then
  echo "✓ Backup enabled for anihpj EFS"
else
  echo "⚠ WARNING: Enable backups for production!"
fi
✓ Backup enabled for anihpj EFS
# Check backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Backup check passed")

tag-resource

Adds or overwrites tags on an EFS resource (file system, access point, or mount target).

Syntax
aws efs tag-resource \
  --resource-id  \
  --tags  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-id (string, required): EFS resource ARN or ID (file system, access point, or mount target)
  • --tags (list, required): Key-value tag pairs in Key=Name,Value=Value format
Return Value
  • Success: HTTP 204 response with no output body
  • Tags are added or overwritten on the resource
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Add Application Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Add application tags to file system
aws efs tag-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --tags Key=Application,Value=anihpj Key=Project,Value=JobPost
(no output on success)
# App tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags: anihpj, JobPost")
Example 2: Add Environment Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Tag file system with environment
aws efs tag-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --tags Key=Env,Value=prod Key=Team,Value=backend
(no output on success)
# Env tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tagged: prod, backend")
Example 3: Tag Access Point
#!/bin/bash
# Add tags to access point
aws efs tag-resource \
  --resource-id fsap-0abc123def456789 \
  --tags Key=Name,Value=media-ap Key=Purpose,Value=uploads
(no output on success)
# AP tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access point tagged")
Example 4: Cost Allocation Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Add cost allocation tags
aws efs tag-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --tags Key=CostCenter,Value=Engineering \
         Key=BillingCode,Value=PROJ-1234
(no output on success)
# Cost tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Cost allocation set")
Example 5: Tag All File Systems
#!/bin/bash
# Add consistent tags to all EFS
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  aws efs tag-resource --resource-id $FS_ID \
    --tags Key=ManagedBy,Value=Terraform Key=UpdatedAt,Value=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
  echo "Tagged: $FS_ID"
done
Tagged: fs-0abc123def456789 Tagged: fs-0xyz987fed654321
# Tag all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All file systems tagged")

list-tags-for-resource

Returns all tags associated with an EFS resource. Use for auditing and cost tracking.

Syntax
aws efs list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id  \
  [--max-results ] \
  [--next-token ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-id (string, required): EFS resource ARN or ID
  • --max-results (integer): Maximum tags to return (default/max: 100)
  • --next-token (string): Token for pagination
Return Value
  • Tags: List of tag objects with Key and Value properties
  • NextToken: Token for retrieving next page of results
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Tags
#!/bin/bash
# List all tags for file system
aws efs list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "Tags": [ {"Key": "Name", "Value": "anihpj-efs"}, {"Key": "Application", "Value": "anihpj"}, {"Key": "Env", "Value": "prod"} ] }
# List tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 tags found")
Example 2: Get Tag Keys Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just the tag keys
aws efs list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'Tags[].Key' --output text
Name Application Env Team CostCenter
# Tag keys from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"5 tag keys")
Example 3: Get Specific Tag Value
#!/bin/bash
# Get Environment tag value
aws efs list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'Tags[?Key==`Env`].Value' --output text
prod
# Env tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Environment: prod")
Example 4: List Tags as Table
#!/bin/bash
# Display tags in table format
aws efs list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --output table
------------------------------------- | ListTagsForResource | +--------------+--------------------+ | Key | Value | +--------------+--------------------+ | Name | anihpj-efs | | Application | anihpj | | Env | prod | +--------------+--------------------+
# Tags table from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tags displayed as table")
Example 5: Audit All EFS Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Audit tags for all file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  echo "=== $FS_ID ==="
  aws efs list-tags-for-resource --resource-id $FS_ID \
    --query 'Tags[].{Key:Key,Value:Value}' --output table
done
=== fs-0abc123def456789 === +--------------+--------------+ | Key | Value | +--------------+--------------+ | Name | anihpj-efs | | Env | prod | +--------------+--------------+ === fs-0xyz987fed654321 === +--------------+--------------+ | Name | backup-efs | +--------------+--------------+
# Audit tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All EFS tags audited")

create-replication-configuration

Creates a replication configuration to replicate an EFS file system to another AWS Region for disaster recovery.

Syntax
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id  \
  --destinations  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --source-file-system-id (string, required): Source EFS file system ID to replicate
  • --destinations (list, required): List of destination regions with Region and optional AvailabilityZoneName
Return Value
  • SourceFileSystemId: The source EFS ID
  • SourceFileSystemRegion: The source region
  • SourceFileSystemArn: The source file system ARN
  • Destinations: List of replication destinations with Region and Status (ENABLING, ENABLED, DELETING, DELETED)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Create Cross-Region Replication
#!/bin/bash
# Replicate EFS to another region for DR
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --destinations Region=us-west-2
{ "SourceFileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "SourceFileSystemRegion": "us-east-1", "Destinations": [{"Region": "us-west-2", "Status": "ENABLING"}] }
# Cross-region DR from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Replication to us-west-2 started")
Example 2: Replicate with KMS Encryption
#!/bin/bash
# Replicate with custom KMS key
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --destinations Region=eu-west-1,KmsKeyId=arn:aws:kms:eu-west-1:123456789012:key/abc123
{ "SourceFileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Destinations": [{"Region": "eu-west-1", "Status": "ENABLING"}] }
# KMS replication from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Encrypted replication to EU")
Example 3: Replicate to Specific AZ
#!/bin/bash
# Replicate to One Zone storage class
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --destinations Region=ap-southeast-1,AvailabilityZoneName=ap-southeast-1a
{ "Destinations": [{"Region": "ap-southeast-1", "AvailabilityZoneName": "ap-southeast-1a"}] }
# One Zone replication from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Replication to Singapore AZ")
Example 4: Setup Production DR
#!/bin/bash
# Production Django media DR setup
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --destinations Region=us-west-2
echo "DR replication configured for anihpj media"
DR replication configured for anihpj media
# Production DR from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"anihpj media DR enabled")
Example 5: Verify Replication Status
#!/bin/bash
# Create and check replication status
aws efs create-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --destinations Region=us-west-2 \
  --query 'Destinations[0].Status' --output text
ENABLING
# Check status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Status: ENABLING")

delete-file-system-policy

Deletes the resource-based policy from an EFS file system. This removes IAM-based access controls.

Syntax
aws efs delete-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
Return Value
  • Success: HTTP 200 response with no output body
  • The resource-based policy is removed from the file system
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete File System Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Remove resource policy from EFS
aws efs delete-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
(no output on success)
# Delete policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy deleted")
Example 2: Delete with Verification
#!/bin/bash
# Delete and verify removal
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs delete-file-system-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID
if ! aws efs describe-file-system-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Policy removed from $FS_ID"
fi
Policy removed from fs-0abc123def456789
# Verify deletion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy removal verified")
Example 3: Reset to Default Access
#!/bin/bash
# Reset EFS to default NFS-based access
aws efs delete-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
echo "EFS reset to default mount-based access"
EFS reset to default mount-based access
# Reset access from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Default access restored")
Example 4: Remove All Policies
#!/bin/bash
# Remove policies from all file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  aws efs delete-file-system-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID 2>/dev/null
  echo "Policy removed: $FS_ID"
done
Policy removed: fs-0abc123def456789 Policy removed: fs-0xyz987fed654321
# Remove all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All policies removed")
Example 5: Cleanup Dev Environment
#!/bin/bash
# Remove policy from dev EFS
DEV_FS=$(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[?Tags[?Key==`Env` && Value==`dev`]].FileSystemId' \
  --output text)
aws efs delete-file-system-policy --file-system-id $DEV_FS
(no output on success)
# Dev cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev policy removed")

delete-replication-configuration

Deletes a replication configuration, stopping cross-region replication for an EFS file system.

Syntax
aws efs delete-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --source-file-system-id (string, required): Source EFS file system ID with replication configuration to delete
Return Value
  • Success: HTTP 200 response with no output body
  • The replication configuration is removed, stopping cross-region replication
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Delete Replication Configuration
#!/bin/bash
# Stop replication for file system
aws efs delete-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
(no output on success)
# Delete replication from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Replication stopped")
Example 2: Delete with Status Check
#!/bin/bash
# Delete and verify
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs delete-replication-configuration --source-file-system-id $FS_ID
sleep 5
STATUS=$(aws efs describe-replication-configurations --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'Replications[0].Destinations[0].Status' --output text 2>/dev/null)
echo "Replication status: ${STATUS:-DELETED}"
Replication status: DELETING
# Check status from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Replication deleting")
Example 3: Stop DR for Cost Savings
#!/bin/bash
# Stop DR replication for dev environment
aws efs delete-replication-configuration \
  --source-file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
echo "DR replication stopped - reducing costs"
DR replication stopped - reducing costs
# Cost savings from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DR stopped for cost savings")
Example 4: Remove All Replications
#!/bin/bash
# Remove all replication configs
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  --query 'Replications[].SourceFileSystemId' --output text); do
  aws efs delete-replication-configuration --source-file-system-id $FS_ID
  echo "Stopped replication: $FS_ID"
done
Stopped replication: fs-0abc123def456789
# Stop all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All replications stopped")
Example 5: Failover Preparation
#!/bin/bash
# Delete replication before failover
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
echo "Stopping replication for failover..."
aws efs delete-replication-configuration --source-file-system-id $FS_ID
echo "Ready for manual failover to DR region"
Stopping replication for failover... Ready for manual failover to DR region
# Failover prep from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Ready for DR failover")

describe-account-preferences

Returns the account-level EFS resource ID preference settings. Shows if long or short IDs are used.

Syntax
aws efs describe-account-preferences \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --region (string): AWS region to query preferences (optional)
Return Value
  • ResourceIdPreference: Account-level resource ID preferences
  • ResourceIdType: LONG_ID or SHORT_ID format
  • Resources: List of resources using this format (FILE_SYSTEM, MOUNT_TARGET, ACCESS_POINT)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Account Preferences
#!/bin/bash
# Get EFS account preferences
aws efs describe-account-preferences
{ "ResourceIdPreference": { "ResourceIdType": "LONG_ID", "Resources": ["FILE_SYSTEM", "MOUNT_TARGET"] } }
# Get preferences from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Using LONG_ID format")
Example 2: Get ID Type Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just the ID type preference
aws efs describe-account-preferences \
  --query 'ResourceIdPreference.ResourceIdType' --output text
LONG_ID
# ID type from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"ID Type: LONG_ID")
Example 3: Check Resources Using Long IDs
#!/bin/bash
# List resources using long ID format
aws efs describe-account-preferences \
  --query 'ResourceIdPreference.Resources' --output text
FILE_SYSTEM MOUNT_TARGET
# Resources from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"FILE_SYSTEM, MOUNT_TARGET")
Example 4: Multi-Region Check
#!/bin/bash
# Check preferences across regions
for REGION in us-east-1 us-west-2 eu-west-1; do
  echo "=== $REGION ==="
  aws efs describe-account-preferences --region $REGION \
    --query 'ResourceIdPreference.ResourceIdType' --output text
done
=== us-east-1 === LONG_ID === us-west-2 === LONG_ID === eu-west-1 === LONG_ID
# Multi-region from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All regions: LONG_ID")
Example 5: JSON Output for Scripts
#!/bin/bash
# Get full preferences as JSON
aws efs describe-account-preferences --output json
{ "ResourceIdPreference": { "ResourceIdType": "LONG_ID", "Resources": ["FILE_SYSTEM", "MOUNT_TARGET", "ACCESS_POINT"] } }
# JSON output from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Preferences exported")

describe-file-system-policy

Returns the resource-based policy for an EFS file system. Shows IAM-based access controls.

Syntax
aws efs describe-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
Return Value
  • FileSystemId: The file system ID
  • Policy: The resource-based policy (JSON string format)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get File System Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Get resource policy for EFS
aws efs describe-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[{\"Effect\":\"Allow\",\"Principal\":{\"AWS\":\"*\"},\"Action\":[\"elasticfilesystem:ClientMount\"],\"Resource\":\"arn:aws:elasticfilesystem:us-east-1:123456789012:file-system/fs-0abc123def456789\"}]}" }
# Get policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy retrieved")
Example 2: Get Policy JSON Only
#!/bin/bash
# Extract and format policy JSON
aws efs describe-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'Policy' --output text | jq .
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": {"AWS": "*"}, "Action": ["elasticfilesystem:ClientMount"] } ] }
# Formatted policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy formatted")
Example 3: Check If Policy Exists
#!/bin/bash
# Check if EFS has a policy
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
if aws efs describe-file-system-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Policy exists on $FS_ID"
else
  echo "No policy on $FS_ID"
fi
Policy exists on fs-0abc123def456789
# Check policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy exists")
Example 4: Audit All EFS Policies
#!/bin/bash
# List policies for all file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  echo "=== $FS_ID ==="
  aws efs describe-file-system-policy --file-system-id $FS_ID 2>/dev/null || echo "No policy"
done
=== fs-0abc123def456789 === {"FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "..."} === fs-0xyz987fed654321 === No policy
# Audit policies from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policies audited")
Example 5: Export Policy to File
#!/bin/bash
# Save policy to file for backup
aws efs describe-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'Policy' --output text > efs-policy-backup.json
echo "Policy saved to efs-policy-backup.json"
Policy saved to efs-policy-backup.json
# Export policy from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy exported")

describe-mount-target-security-groups

Returns the security groups currently associated with an EFS mount target.

Syntax
aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --mount-target-id (string, required): EFS mount target ID
Return Value
  • SecurityGroups: List of security group IDs associated with the mount target
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Security Groups
#!/bin/bash
# Get security groups for mount target
aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789
{ "SecurityGroups": [ "sg-0abc123def456789", "sg-0xyz987fed654321" ] }
# Get SGs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 security groups found")
Example 2: List SG IDs Only
#!/bin/bash
# Get just security group IDs
aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'SecurityGroups' --output text
sg-0abc123def456789 sg-0xyz987fed654321
# SG IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SG IDs listed")
Example 3: Check SGs for All Mount Targets
#!/bin/bash
# List security groups for all mount targets
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
for MT_ID in $(aws efs describe-mount-targets --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'MountTargets[].MountTargetId' --output text); do
  echo "=== $MT_ID ==="
  aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups --mount-target-id $MT_ID
done
=== fsmt-0abc123 === {"SecurityGroups": ["sg-0abc123"]} === fsmt-0xyz987 === {"SecurityGroups": ["sg-0xyz987"]}
# All MTs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All mount target SGs listed")
Example 4: Count Security Groups
#!/bin/bash
# Count attached security groups
MT_ID="fsmt-0abc123def456789"
COUNT=$(aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id $MT_ID \
  --query 'length(SecurityGroups)')
echo "Security groups attached: $COUNT"
Security groups attached: 2
# Count SGs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"2 SGs attached")
Example 5: Security Audit
#!/bin/bash
# Audit EFS security groups with names
MT_ID="fsmt-0abc123def456789"
for SG_ID in $(aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id $MT_ID --query 'SecurityGroups[]' --output text); do
  NAME=$(aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids $SG_ID \
    --query 'SecurityGroups[0].GroupName' --output text)
  echo "$SG_ID: $NAME"
done
sg-0abc123: anihpj-efs-sg sg-0xyz987: default
# Security audit from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SG audit complete")

describe-replication-configurations

Returns replication configurations for EFS file systems. Shows cross-region replication status and destinations.

Syntax
aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  [--file-system-id ] \
  [--max-results ] \
  [--next-token ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string): Filter by specific file system ID (optional)
  • --max-results (integer): Maximum results to return
  • --next-token (string): Token for pagination
Return Value
  • Replications: List of replication configurations
  • SourceFileSystemId: Source EFS ID
  • SourceFileSystemRegion: Source region
  • Destinations: List of destination regions and replication status (ENABLING, ENABLED, DELETING, DELETED)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: List All Replications
#!/bin/bash
# List all replication configurations
aws efs describe-replication-configurations
{ "Replications": [ { "SourceFileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "SourceFileSystemRegion": "us-east-1", "Destinations": [{"Region": "us-west-2", "Status": "ENABLED"}] } ] }
# List replications from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"1 replication found")
Example 2: Get Specific File System Replication
#!/bin/bash
# Get replication for specific EFS
aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789
{ "Replications": [{ "SourceFileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Destinations": [{"Region": "us-west-2", "Status": "ENABLED"}] }] }
# Specific replication from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Replication to us-west-2")
Example 3: Get Replication Status
#!/bin/bash
# Check replication status
aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --query 'Replications[0].Destinations[0].Status' --output text
ENABLED
# Status check from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Status: ENABLED")
Example 4: List Destination Regions
#!/bin/bash
# Get all destination regions
aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  --query 'Replications[].Destinations[].Region' --output text
us-west-2 eu-west-1
# Destinations from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DR regions: us-west-2, eu-west-1")
Example 5: DR Status Report
#!/bin/bash
# Generate DR status report
aws efs describe-replication-configurations \
  --query 'Replications[].{Source:SourceFileSystemId,Dest:Destinations[0].Region,Status:Destinations[0].Status}' \
  --output table
----------------------------------------------- | DescribeReplicationConfigurations | +---------------------+-----------+-----------+ | Source | Dest | Status | +---------------------+-----------+-----------+ | fs-0abc123def456789| us-west-2| ENABLED | +---------------------+-----------+-----------+
# DR report from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DR status report generated")

modify-mount-target-security-groups

Updates the security groups associated with an EFS mount target. Replaces all existing security groups.

Syntax
aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id  \
  --security-groups  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --mount-target-id (string, required): EFS mount target ID to update
  • --security-groups (list, required): New security group IDs to attach (replaces all existing groups)
Return Value
  • MountTargetId: The mount target ID
  • SecurityGroups: The newly associated security group IDs
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Update Security Groups
#!/bin/bash
# Update mount target security groups
aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789 \
  --security-groups sg-0abc123def456789 sg-0xyz987fed654321
(no output on success)
# Update SGs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security groups updated")
Example 2: Replace with Single SG
#!/bin/bash
# Set single security group
aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id fsmt-0abc123def456789 \
  --security-groups sg-anihpj-efs-only
(no output on success)
# Single SG from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Using dedicated EFS SG")
Example 3: Update All Mount Targets
#!/bin/bash
# Update security groups for all mount targets
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
NEW_SG="sg-0abc123def456789"
for MT_ID in $(aws efs describe-mount-targets --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'MountTargets[].MountTargetId' --output text); do
  aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
    --mount-target-id $MT_ID --security-groups $NEW_SG
  echo "Updated: $MT_ID"
done
Updated: fsmt-0abc123 Updated: fsmt-0xyz987
# Update all from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"All mount targets updated")
Example 4: Add SG to Existing
#!/bin/bash
# Add new SG while keeping existing
MT_ID="fsmt-0abc123def456789"
EXISTING=$(aws efs describe-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id $MT_ID --query 'SecurityGroups' --output text)
aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id $MT_ID \
  --security-groups $EXISTING sg-new123456789
(no output on success)
# Add SG from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"SG added to existing")
Example 5: Security Lockdown
#!/bin/bash
# Lockdown to restrictive security group
MT_ID="fsmt-0abc123def456789"
aws efs modify-mount-target-security-groups \
  --mount-target-id $MT_ID \
  --security-groups sg-efs-restricted-only
echo "Mount target locked down"
Mount target locked down
# Lockdown from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Security lockdown complete")

put-account-preferences

Sets the account-level EFS resource ID preferences. Configure long or short ID format for resources.

Syntax
aws efs put-account-preferences \
  --resource-id-type  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-id-type (string, required): LONG_ID or SHORT_ID format for EFS resource IDs
Return Value
  • ResourceIdPreference: The updated account-level preferences
  • ResourceIdType: The configured ID format (LONG_ID or SHORT_ID)
  • Resources: List of resources using this format
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Set Long ID Format
#!/bin/bash
# Enable long resource IDs
aws efs put-account-preferences \
  --resource-id-type LONG_ID
{ "ResourceIdPreference": { "ResourceIdType": "LONG_ID", "Resources": ["FILE_SYSTEM", "MOUNT_TARGET", "ACCESS_POINT"] } }
# Long IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Using LONG_ID format")
Example 2: Set Short ID Format
#!/bin/bash
# Use short resource IDs (legacy)
aws efs put-account-preferences \
  --resource-id-type SHORT_ID
{ "ResourceIdPreference": { "ResourceIdType": "SHORT_ID", "Resources": ["FILE_SYSTEM", "MOUNT_TARGET"] } }
# Short IDs from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Using SHORT_ID format")
Example 3: Set Across All Regions
#!/bin/bash
# Set LONG_ID in all regions
for REGION in us-east-1 us-west-2 eu-west-1 ap-southeast-1; do
  aws efs put-account-preferences \
    --resource-id-type LONG_ID --region $REGION
  echo "Set LONG_ID in $REGION"
done
Set LONG_ID in us-east-1 Set LONG_ID in us-west-2 Set LONG_ID in eu-west-1 Set LONG_ID in ap-southeast-1
# All regions from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"LONG_ID set globally")
Example 4: Verify After Setting
#!/bin/bash
# Set and verify preference
aws efs put-account-preferences --resource-id-type LONG_ID \
  --query 'ResourceIdPreference.ResourceIdType' --output text
LONG_ID
# Verify setting from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Preference verified")
Example 5: Standardize Account Settings
#!/bin/bash
# Standardize for anihpj production
echo "Setting EFS preferences for production..."
aws efs put-account-preferences --resource-id-type LONG_ID
echo "Account preferences standardized"
Setting EFS preferences for production... Account preferences standardized
# Standardize from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production preferences set")

put-file-system-policy

Applies a resource-based policy to an EFS file system. Use IAM to control mount and access permissions.

Syntax
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id  \
  --policy  \
  [--bypass-policy-lockout-safety-check] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --policy (string, required): JSON policy document (inline JSON or file://path)
  • --bypass-policy-lockout-safety-check (flag): Skip lockout protection check
Return Value
  • FileSystemId: The file system ID
  • Policy: The applied resource-based policy (JSON string)
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Enforce Encryption in Transit
#!/bin/bash
# Require TLS for all connections
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --policy '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "*",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {"Bool": {"aws:SecureTransport": "false"}}
    }]
  }'
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{...}" }
# Enforce TLS from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"TLS required for EFS")
Example 2: Restrict to Specific Role
#!/bin/bash
# Allow only EC2 role to mount
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --policy '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2-role"},
      "Action": ["elasticfilesystem:ClientMount", "elasticfilesystem:ClientWrite"],
      "Resource": "*"
    }]
  }'
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{...}" }
# Role access from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Restricted to anihpj-ec2-role")
Example 3: Read-Only Policy
#!/bin/bash
# Allow mount but deny write
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --policy '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "elasticfilesystem:ClientMount",
      "Resource": "*"
    }]
  }'
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{...}" }
# Read-only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Read-only access set")
Example 4: Apply from File
#!/bin/bash
# Apply policy from JSON file
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --policy file://efs-policy.json
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{...}" }
# From file from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Policy applied from file")
Example 5: VPC-Only Access
#!/bin/bash
# Restrict access to specific VPC
aws efs put-file-system-policy \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --policy '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": ["elasticfilesystem:ClientMount"],
      "Condition": {"StringEquals": {"aws:SourceVpc": "vpc-0abc123"}}
    }]
  }'
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "Policy": "{...}" }
# VPC-only from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"VPC-restricted access")

untag-resource

Removes tags from an EFS resource. Use to clean up or update tagging strategy.

Syntax
aws efs untag-resource \
  --resource-id  \
  --tag-keys  \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --resource-id (string, required): EFS resource ARN or ID (file system, access point, mount target)
  • --tag-keys (list, required): Tag keys to remove from the resource
Return Value
  • Success: HTTP 204 response with no output body
  • Specified tags are removed from the resource
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Remove Single Tag
#!/bin/bash
# Remove one tag from file system
aws efs untag-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --tag-keys Env
(no output on success)
# Remove tag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Env tag removed")
Example 2: Remove Multiple Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Remove multiple tags at once
aws efs untag-resource \
  --resource-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --tag-keys Env Team CostCenter
(no output on success)
# Multiple tags from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"3 tags removed")
Example 3: Remove from Access Point
#!/bin/bash
# Remove tags from access point
aws efs untag-resource \
  --resource-id fsap-0abc123def456789 \
  --tag-keys Purpose Owner
(no output on success)
# AP untag from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Access point tags removed")
Example 4: Cleanup Dev Tags
#!/bin/bash
# Remove dev-related tags from all EFS
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[].FileSystemId' --output text); do
  aws efs untag-resource --resource-id $FS_ID \
    --tag-keys DevOwner TestFlag TempTag 2>/dev/null
  echo "Cleaned: $FS_ID"
done
Cleaned: fs-0abc123def456789 Cleaned: fs-0xyz987fed654321
# Dev cleanup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Dev tags cleaned")
Example 5: Remove and Verify
#!/bin/bash
# Remove tag and verify
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs untag-resource --resource-id $FS_ID --tag-keys OldTag
# Verify removal
aws efs list-tags-for-resource --resource-id $FS_ID \
  --query 'Tags[?Key==`OldTag`]'
[]
# Verify removal from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Tag removal verified")

update-file-system

Updates the throughput mode or provisioned throughput for an EFS file system.

Syntax
aws efs update-file-system \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--throughput-mode ] \
  [--provisioned-throughput-in-mibps ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --throughput-mode (string, optional): bursting, provisioned, or elastic
  • --provisioned-throughput-in-mibps (double, optional): Throughput in MiB/s
Return Value
  • FileSystemId: The ID of the file system
  • ThroughputMode: The updated throughput mode
  • ProvisionedThroughputInMibps: Provisioned throughput in MiB/s (if applicable)
Example 1: Switch to Elastic Throughput
#!/bin/bash
# Enable elastic throughput for auto-scaling
aws efs update-file-system \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --throughput-mode elastic
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "ThroughputMode": "elastic" }
# Elastic mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Elastic throughput enabled")
Example 2: Set Provisioned Throughput
#!/bin/bash
# Set 100 MiB/s provisioned throughput
aws efs update-file-system \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --throughput-mode provisioned \
  --provisioned-throughput-in-mibps 100
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "ThroughputMode": "provisioned", "ProvisionedThroughputInMibps": 100.0 }
# Provisioned 100 from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"100 MiB/s provisioned")
Example 3: Switch to Bursting
#!/bin/bash
# Revert to bursting mode for cost savings
aws efs update-file-system \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --throughput-mode bursting
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "ThroughputMode": "bursting" }
# Bursting mode from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bursting mode for cost savings")
Example 4: Increase Provisioned Throughput
#!/bin/bash
# Scale up for high traffic
aws efs update-file-system \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --provisioned-throughput-in-mibps 256
{ "FileSystemId": "fs-0abc123def456789", "ProvisionedThroughputInMibps": 256.0 }
# Scale up from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Scaled to 256 MiB/s")
Example 5: Update and Verify
#!/bin/bash
# Update and confirm new settings
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs update-file-system --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --throughput-mode elastic \
  --query 'ThroughputMode' --output text
elastic
# Verify update from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Throughput: elastic")

update-file-system-protection

Updates the file system protection settings, including replication overwrite protection.

Syntax
aws efs update-file-system-protection \
  --file-system-id  \
  [--replication-overwrite-protection ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --file-system-id (string, required): EFS file system ID
  • --replication-overwrite-protection (string, optional): ENABLED or DISABLED
Return Value
  • ReplicationOverwriteProtection: The current protection setting (ENABLED or DISABLED)
Example 1: Enable Overwrite Protection
#!/bin/bash
# Enable replication overwrite protection
aws efs update-file-system-protection \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --replication-overwrite-protection ENABLED
{ "ReplicationOverwriteProtection": "ENABLED" }
# Enable protection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Overwrite protection enabled")
Example 2: Disable for Failover
#!/bin/bash
# Disable protection to allow failover writes
aws efs update-file-system-protection \
  --file-system-id fs-0abc123def456789 \
  --replication-overwrite-protection DISABLED
{ "ReplicationOverwriteProtection": "DISABLED" }
# Disable for DR from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Protection disabled for failover")
Example 3: Protect Production EFS
#!/bin/bash
# Enable protection on all production file systems
for FS_ID in $(aws efs describe-file-systems \
  --query 'FileSystems[?Tags[?Key==`Env` && Value==`prod`]].FileSystemId' \
  --output text); do
  aws efs update-file-system-protection --file-system-id $FS_ID \
    --replication-overwrite-protection ENABLED
  echo "Protected: $FS_ID"
done
Protected: fs-0abc123def456789 Protected: fs-0prod987654321
# Protect prod from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production EFS protected")
Example 4: Check and Update
#!/bin/bash
# Update and verify protection status
FS_ID="fs-0abc123def456789"
aws efs update-file-system-protection --file-system-id $FS_ID \
  --replication-overwrite-protection ENABLED \
  --query 'ReplicationOverwriteProtection' --output text
ENABLED
# Verify from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Protection: ENABLED")
Example 5: DR Failover Script
#!/bin/bash
# Disable protection for DR failover scenario
DR_FS_ID="fs-dr0abc123456789"
echo "Preparing DR file system for failover..."
aws efs update-file-system-protection --file-system-id $DR_FS_ID \
  --replication-overwrite-protection DISABLED
echo "DR EFS ready for writes"
Preparing DR file system for failover... DR EFS ready for writes
# DR failover from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"DR ready for anihpj failover")

💾 Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)

High-performance block storage for Django application data, databases, and persistent storage

📋 EBS Overview

Amazon EBS provides persistent block storage for EC2 instances, ideal for database storage, file systems, and any use case that requires low-latency access to data. EBS volumes can store Django application data, PostgreSQL/MySQL databases, user-uploaded files, and critical JobPost, Skills, User, Author, and Location data with high IOPS performance and snapshot backup capabilities.

Core EBS Snapshot Operations for Django

complete-snapshot - Complete snapshot creation
get-snapshot-block - Retrieve snapshot block data
list-changed-blocks - List changed blocks between snapshots
list-snapshot-blocks - List all blocks in snapshot
put-snapshot-block - Write block data to snapshot
start-snapshot - Initiate new snapshot

complete-snapshot

Completes an Amazon EBS snapshot that was started using the start-snapshot operation. This finalizes the snapshot creation process and makes it available for use as backup or for creating new volumes.

Syntax
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id  \
  --changed-blocks-count  \
  [--checksum ] \
  [--checksum-algorithm ] \
  [--checksum-aggregation-method ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --snapshot-id (string, required): The ID of the snapshot to complete
  • --changed-blocks-count (integer, required): Number of blocks that were changed in this snapshot
  • --checksum (string, optional): Checksum of snapshot data for validation
  • --checksum-algorithm (string, optional): Algorithm used for checksum calculation (SHA256)
  • --checksum-aggregation-method (string, optional): Method for aggregating checksums (LINEAR)
Return Value
  • Status: Completion status of the snapshot
  • Tags: Any tags applied to the completed snapshot
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Complete Simple Snapshot
#!/bin/bash
# Complete a snapshot for Django database backup
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id snap-0abc123def456789 \
  --changed-blocks-count 150
{ "Status": "completed" }
# Snapshot completed from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Database snapshot completed") print(f"Snapshot ID: snap-0abc123def456789") print(f"Changed blocks: 150") # Update backup tracking backup_log = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'status': 'completed', 'blocks_changed': 150 } print(f"Backup: {backup_log}")
Example 2: Complete with Checksum Validation
#!/bin/bash
# Complete snapshot with data integrity validation
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id snap-0abc123def456789 \
  --changed-blocks-count 150 \
  --checksum "da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709" \
  --checksum-algorithm SHA256
{ "Status": "completed" }
# Validated completion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Snapshot completed with validation") print(f"Checksum verified: SHA256") print(f"Data integrity: Validated") # Log secure backup secure_backup = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'validation': 'SHA256', 'integrity': 'verified' } print(f"Secure backup: {secure_backup}")
Example 3: Complete Production Database Snapshot
#!/bin/bash
# Complete critical production database snapshot
SNAP_ID="snap-prod-db-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id $SNAP_ID \
  --changed-blocks-count 2500 \
  --checksum-aggregation-method LINEAR

echo "Production database backup completed: $SNAP_ID"
Production database backup completed: snap-prod-db-20240115
# Production backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Production DB snapshot completed") print(f"Date: 2024-01-15") print(f"Critical data: Protected") # Track production backup prod_users = User.objects.count() prod_jobs = JobPost.objects.count() print(f"\nBacked up data:") print(f"Users: {prod_users}") print(f"Job Posts: {prod_jobs}")
Example 4: Complete and Tag Snapshot
#!/bin/bash
# Complete snapshot and apply metadata tags
SNAP_ID="snap-0abc123def456789"
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id $SNAP_ID \
  --changed-blocks-count 150

# Tag completed snapshot
aws ec2 create-tags --resources $SNAP_ID --tags \
  Key=Environment,Value=production \
  Key=Application,Value=anihpj \
  Key=BackupType,Value=incremental
{ "Status": "completed" }
# Tagged backup from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Snapshot completed and tagged") print(f"Environment: production") print(f"Application: anihpj") print(f"Type: incremental") # Log tagged backup backup_meta = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'environment': 'production', 'app': 'anihpj', 'type': 'incremental' } print(f"Metadata: {backup_meta}")
Example 5: Complete with Monitoring
#!/bin/bash
# Complete snapshot with CloudWatch monitoring
SNAP_ID="snap-0abc123def456789"
TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')

echo "Completing snapshot at $TIMESTAMP"
aws ebs complete-snapshot \
  --snapshot-id $SNAP_ID \
  --changed-blocks-count 150

# Send completion metric to CloudWatch
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "EBS/Snapshots" \
  --metric-data MetricName=CompletedSnapshots,Value=1
Completing snapshot at 2024-01-15 10:30:15
# Monitored completion from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime print(f"Snapshot completion monitored") print(f"Timestamp: {datetime.now()}") print(f"CloudWatch: Metric sent") # Track completion metrics completion_stats = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'completed_at': datetime.now().isoformat(), 'monitoring': 'cloudwatch', 'metric_sent': True } print(f"Stats: {completion_stats}")

get-snapshot-block

Returns the data in a block for an Amazon EBS snapshot. This allows you to read specific data blocks from a snapshot for data recovery, validation, or custom backup operations.

Syntax
aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
  --snapshot-id  \
  --block-index  \
  --block-token  \
  [--region ] \
Parameters Description
  • --snapshot-id (string, required): The ID of the snapshot containing the block
  • --block-index (integer, required): The block index (0-based) to retrieve
  • --block-token (string, required): Token for the block to access
  • output-file (string, required): Local file to save the block data
Return Value
  • Data: Raw block data written to specified output file
  • DataLength: Size of the retrieved block data in bytes
  • Checksum: Checksum of the block data for validation
  • ChecksumAlgorithm: Algorithm used for checksum calculation
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Get Single Block
#!/bin/bash
# Retrieve a specific block from Django database snapshot
aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
  --snapshot-id snap-0abc123def456789 \
  --block-index 0 \
  --block-token "AQAAVr9puJuDXw..." \
  /tmp/django-db-block-0.bin
Block data saved to /tmp/django-db-block-0.bin Size: 524288 bytes
# Block retrieved from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Database block retrieved") print(f"Block index: 0") print(f"File: django-db-block-0.bin") print(f"Size: 524288 bytes") # Log block retrieval block_info = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'block_index': 0, 'size_bytes': 524288, 'retrieved': True } print(f"Block info: {block_info}")
Example 2: Get Block with Validation
#!/bin/bash
# Retrieve block with checksum verification
BLOCK_FILE="/tmp/django-media-block.bin"
aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
  --snapshot-id snap-0def456ghi789abc \
  --block-index 1 \
  --block-token "BQAAGK8mjuDxW..." \
  $BLOCK_FILE

# Verify checksum
echo "Block retrieved, verifying integrity..."
sha256sum $BLOCK_FILE
Block retrieved, verifying integrity... a1b2c3d4e5f6789... /tmp/django-media-block.bin
# Validated retrieval from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Media block retrieved") print(f"Integrity: Verified") print(f"Checksum: SHA256") # Track validated retrieval retrieval_log = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0def456ghi789abc', 'block_type': 'media', 'validation': 'SHA256', 'integrity_check': 'passed' } print(f"Validation: {retrieval_log}")
Example 3: Bulk Block Recovery
#!/bin/bash
# Recover multiple blocks for data reconstruction
SNAPSHOT_ID="snap-0abc123def456789"
OUTPUT_DIR="/tmp/recovery"
mkdir -p $OUTPUT_DIR

# Get multiple blocks
for i in {0..4}; do
  TOKEN="TOKEN_${i}_placeholder"
  aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
    --snapshot-id $SNAPSHOT_ID \
    --block-index $i \
    --block-token $TOKEN \
    ${OUTPUT_DIR}/block-${i}.bin
  echo "Block $i retrieved"
done
Block 0 retrieved Block 1 retrieved Block 2 retrieved Block 3 retrieved Block 4 retrieved
# Bulk recovery from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Bulk block recovery completed") print(f"Blocks recovered: 5") print(f"Output directory: /tmp/recovery") # Log recovery operation recovery_stats = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-0abc123def456789', 'blocks_recovered': 5, 'recovery_type': 'bulk', 'status': 'completed' } print(f"Recovery: {recovery_stats}")
Example 4: Selective Data Recovery
#!/bin/bash
# Recover specific Django app data blocks
SNAP_ID="snap-0abc123def456789"
USER_BLOCK_TOKEN="AQAAUr9user123..."
JOB_BLOCK_TOKEN="BQAAGr9jobs456..."

# Recover user data block
aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
  --snapshot-id $SNAP_ID \
  --block-index 10 \
  --block-token $USER_BLOCK_TOKEN \
  /tmp/user-data.bin

# Recover job posts block  
aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
  --snapshot-id $SNAP_ID \
  --block-index 15 \
  --block-token $JOB_BLOCK_TOKEN \
  /tmp/jobpost-data.bin
User data block saved: /tmp/user-data.bin Job posts block saved: /tmp/jobpost-data.bin
# Selective recovery from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Selective data recovery") print(f"User data: Recovered") print(f"Job posts: Recovered") # Track application data recovery app_recovery = { 'user_data_block': 10, 'jobpost_data_block': 15, 'recovery_type': 'selective', 'app_components': ['users', 'jobposts'] } print(f"App recovery: {app_recovery}")
Example 5: Automated Block Extraction
#!/bin/bash
# Automated script for critical data block extraction
SNAPSHOT_ID="snap-prod-backup-20240115"
CRITICAL_BLOCKS=(0 5 10 15 20)
BACKUP_DIR="/backup/$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p $BACKUP_DIR

echo "Starting automated block extraction..."
for block_idx in "${CRITICAL_BLOCKS[@]}"; do
  TOKEN="TOKEN_${block_idx}_auto"
  OUTPUT_FILE="${BACKUP_DIR}/critical-block-${block_idx}.bin"
  
  aws ebs get-snapshot-block \
    --snapshot-id $SNAPSHOT_ID \
    --block-index $block_idx \
    --block-token $TOKEN \
    $OUTPUT_FILE
  
  echo "✓ Critical block $block_idx extracted"
done
echo "Automated extraction completed"
Starting automated block extraction... ✓ Critical block 0 extracted ✓ Critical block 5 extracted ✓ Critical block 10 extracted ✓ Critical block 15 extracted ✓ Critical block 20 extracted Automated extraction completed
# Automated extraction from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from datetime import datetime print(f"Automated block extraction") print(f"Critical blocks: [0, 5, 10, 15, 20]") print(f"Backup date: {datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d')}") # Log automation automation_log = { 'snapshot_id': 'snap-prod-backup-20240115', 'critical_blocks': [0, 5, 10, 15, 20], 'extraction_type': 'automated', 'backup_dir': '/backup/20240115', 'completed_at': datetime.now().isoformat() } print(f"Automation: {automation_log}")

list-changed-blocks

Returns information about the blocks that differ between two Amazon EBS snapshots. This is useful for incremental backups and understanding what data has changed between snapshot versions.

Syntax
aws ebs list-changed-blocks \
  --first-snapshot-id  \
  --second-snapshot-id  \
  [--starting-block-index ] \
  [--max-results ] \
  [--next-token ] \
  [--region ]
Parameters Description
  • --first-snapshot-id (string, required): ID of the first (earlier) snapshot to compare
  • --second-snapshot-id (string, required): ID of the second (later) snapshot to compare
  • --starting-block-index (integer, optional): Block index to start listing from
  • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of blocks to return (1-10000)
  • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
Return Value
  • ChangedBlocks: List of blocks that differ between snapshots
  • BlockIndex: Index of each changed block
  • FirstBlockToken: Token for the block in first snapshot
  • SecondBlockToken: Token for the block in second snapshot
  • NextToken: Token for pagination if more results exist
5 Detailed Examples
Example 1: Basic Change Detection
#!/bin/bash
# Find changes between two Django database snapshots
aws ebs list-changed-blocks \
  --first-snapshot-id snap-0old123def456789 \
  --second-snapshot-id snap-0new456ghi789abc \
  --max-results 10
{ "ChangedBlocks": [ { "BlockIndex": 5, "FirstBlockToken": "AQAAUr9old123...", "SecondBlockToken": "BQAAVr9new456..." }, { "BlockIndex": 12, "FirstBlockToken": "CQAAWr9old789...", "SecondBlockToken": "DQAAXr9new012..." } ] }
# Change detection from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Database changes detected") print(f"Changed blocks: 2") print(f"Block indices: [5, 12]") # Log change analysis change_summary = { 'first_snapshot': 'snap-0old123def456789', 'second_snapshot': 'snap-0new456ghi789abc', 'changed_blocks': 2, 'block_indices': [5, 12] } print(f"Changes: {change_summary}")
Example 2: Incremental Backup Analysis
#!/bin/bash
# Analyze changes for incremental backup strategy
BASE_SNAP="snap-base-20240114"
CURRENT_SNAP="snap-current-20240115"

echo "Analyzing incremental changes..."
aws ebs list-changed-blocks \
  --first-snapshot-id $BASE_SNAP \
  --second-snapshot-id $CURRENT_SNAP \
  --query 'length(ChangedBlocks)' \
  --output text
Analyzing incremental changes... 47
# Incremental analysis from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost print(f"Incremental backup analysis") print(f"Base snapshot: 2024-01-14") print(f"Current snapshot: 2024-01-15") print(f"Changed blocks: 47") # Calculate backup efficiency efficiency_metrics = { 'base_snapshot': 'snap-base-20240114', 'current_snapshot': 'snap-current-20240115', 'changed_blocks': 47, 'backup_type': 'incremental', 'efficiency': 'high' } print(f"Efficiency: {efficiency_metrics}")
Example 3: Production Change Monitoring
#!/bin/bash
# Monitor production database changes for compliance
PROD_SNAP_OLD="snap-prod-morning-20240115"
