                     Key=Framework,Value=django,Type=KEY_AND_VALUE \
  --deployment-config-name "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceWithTesting" \
  --trigger-configurations '[{"triggerName":"PreTestingHook","triggerTargetArn":"arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-pre-test-setup","triggerEvents":["DeploymentStart"]},{"triggerName":"ModelTestingHook","triggerTargetArn":"arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-model-test-runner","triggerEvents":["DeploymentSuccess"]}]' \
  --alarm-configuration enabled=true,ignorePollAlarmFailure=false,alarms='[{name=TestExecutionFailures},{name=PerformanceRegressions},{name=ModelValidationErrors}]' \
  --auto-rollback-configuration enabled=true,events=DEPLOYMENT_FAILURE,DEPLOYMENT_STOP_ON_ALARM \
  --region us-east-1

# Create test automation Lambda functions
aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name "django-pre-test-setup" \
  --runtime python3.11 \
  --role "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/LambdaTestingRole" \
  --handler setup.prepare_test_environment \
  --zip-file fileb://django-test-setup.zip \
  --timeout 300 \
  --memory-size 512 \
  --description "Pre-deployment test environment setup for Django staging"

# Verify staging deployment group configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-staging" \
  --deployment-group-name "staging-django-test-cluster"
    { "deploymentGroupId": "staging-test-999-cluster-xyz-789" } # Test automation Lambda function created { "FunctionName": "django-pre-test-setup", "FunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-pre-test-setup", "Runtime": "python3.11", "Handler": "setup.prepare_test_environment", "Timeout": 300, "MemorySize": 512, "State": "Active" } # Staging deployment group configuration { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupName": "staging-django-test-cluster", "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceWithTesting", "triggerConfigurations": [ { "triggerName": "PreTestingHook", "triggerTargetArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-pre-test-setup", "triggerEvents": ["DeploymentStart"] }, { "triggerName": "ModelTestingHook", "triggerTargetArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-model-test-runner", "triggerEvents": ["DeploymentSuccess"] } ], "alarmConfiguration": { "enabled": true, "ignorePollAlarmFailure": false, "alarms": [ {"name": "TestExecutionFailures"}, {"name": "PerformanceRegressions"}, {"name": "ModelValidationErrors"} ] } } }

    create-deployment-config

    Creates a custom deployment configuration that defines how CodeDeploy deploys your Django jobpost application to your target infrastructure. Deployment configurations control the deployment process behavior, including rollout strategy, failure thresholds, and success criteria. This command allows you to create specialized deployment patterns optimized for Django applications with Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models, ensuring proper handling of database connections, session management, and application state during deployments.

    Syntax
    aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name {deployment-config-name} \
  --minimum-healthy-hosts {minimum-healthy-hosts} \
  --compute-platform {compute-platform} \
  --traffic-routing-config {traffic-routing-configuration} \
  --zone-config {zone-configuration}
    Parameters Description
    • --deployment-config-name (string, required): Unique name for the deployment configuration that will be referenced by deployment groups. Use descriptive names that indicate the deployment strategy and target environment, such as "DjangoCanaryDeployment" or "JobPostBlueGreenConfig" for easy identification and management of deployment behaviors.
    • --minimum-healthy-hosts (structure, optional): Minimum number or percentage of healthy instances required during deployment for EC2/on-premises deployments. Specify either a count (type=HOST_COUNT, value=2) or percentage (type=FLEET_PERCENT, value=75) to ensure adequate capacity remains available for serving Django application traffic during updates.
    • --compute-platform (string, optional): Target compute platform for the deployment configuration. Options include 'Server' for EC2/on-premises instances, 'Lambda' for serverless functions, or 'ECS' for containerized applications. Choose based on your Django jobpost application's deployment architecture and infrastructure requirements.
    • --traffic-routing-config (structure, optional): Traffic routing configuration for blue/green and canary deployments. Defines how traffic shifts between application versions, including time intervals, percentage increments, and validation periods for ensuring smooth transitions while maintaining Django model data consistency.
    • --zone-config (structure, optional): Availability zone configuration for controlling deployment rollout across zones. Useful for ensuring high availability during Django application updates by coordinating deployments across multiple zones while maintaining service availability and data replication.
    Attributes
    • deploymentConfigId: Unique identifier assigned to the deployment configuration for referencing and management
    • deploymentConfigName: Name of the deployment configuration as specified during creation
    • minimumHealthyHosts: Minimum healthy hosts configuration ensuring adequate capacity during deployments
    • computePlatform: Target platform type (Server, Lambda, ECS) for the deployment configuration
    • trafficRoutingConfig: Traffic routing settings for blue/green and canary deployment strategies
    • zoneConfig: Availability zone deployment coordination settings
    • createTime: Timestamp when the deployment configuration was created
    • lastModifiedTime: Timestamp of the most recent configuration modification
    Methods
    get-deployment-config

    Syntax: aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name {config-name}

    Parameters: deployment-config-name (string, required) - Name of deployment configuration to retrieve details, settings, and rollout strategy information

    list-deployment-configs

    Syntax: aws deploy list-deployment-configs

    Parameters: No required parameters; lists all available deployment configurations including built-in and custom configurations for Django application deployments

    delete-deployment-config

    Syntax: aws deploy delete-deployment-config --deployment-config-name {config-name}

    Parameters: deployment-config-name (string, required) - Name of custom deployment configuration to delete; cannot delete built-in configurations

    Return Value
    • deploymentConfigId (string): Unique identifier for the created deployment configuration that can be used for management, monitoring, and assignment to deployment groups targeting Django jobpost application infrastructure
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy create-deployment-config Examples
    Example 1: Create Canary Deployment Configuration for Django Gradual Rollout

    Create a canary deployment configuration for gradual Django jobpost application rollout with 10% initial traffic and 5-minute validation periods. This configuration ensures safe deployment of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model updates by testing changes on a small subset of traffic before full rollout, allowing for validation of database operations and model integrity.

    #!/bin/bash
# Create canary deployment configuration for Django gradual rollout
aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min" \
  --compute-platform "Server" \
  --traffic-routing-config 'type=TimeBasedCanary,timeBasedCanary={canaryPercentage=10,canaryInterval=5}' \
  --minimum-healthy-hosts 'type=FLEET_PERCENT,value=75' \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify deployment configuration creation
aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min"

# List all deployment configurations to confirm creation
aws deploy list-deployment-configs
    { "deploymentConfigId": "django-canary-config-123-abc-xyz" } # Deployment configuration details { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-canary-config-123-abc-xyz", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min", "computePlatform": "Server", "minimumHealthyHosts": { "type": "FLEET_PERCENT", "value": 75 }, "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "TimeBasedCanary", "timeBasedCanary": { "canaryPercentage": 10, "canaryInterval": 5 } }, "createTime": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z" } } # Available deployment configurations { "deploymentConfigsList": [ "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce", "CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime", "CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime", "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min" ] }

    Django Queryset Examples for Canary Deployment Configuration

    # Django Models Setup for Canary Deployment Configuration Testing
from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Create canary deployment skills focusing on gradual rollout expertise
canary_skills = [
    Skills.objects.create(
        id=uuid.uuid4(),
        category='Deployment Strategy',
        skill_type='canary',
        skill_description='Canary deployment implementation for Django applications with gradual traffic shifting and validation',
        name='Canary Deployment',
        proficiency_level='Expert',
        required=True,
        years_experience=5,
        certification_required=True,
        last_updated=datetime.now()
    ),
    Skills.objects.create(
        id=uuid.uuid4(),
        category='Traffic Management',
        skill_type='deployment',
        skill_description='Traffic routing and load balancing for gradual Django application rollouts with health monitoring',
        name='Traffic Routing',
        proficiency_level='Advanced',
        required=True,
        years_experience=4,
        certification_required=False
    )
]

# Create canary deployment users
canary_users = [
    User.objects.create(
        username='canary-deployment-engineer',
        email='canary.engineer@anihpj.com',
        first_name='Canary',
        last_name='Deployment Engineer',
        role='Deployment Engineer',
        department='Platform Operations',
        phone_number='+1-555-0303',
        location='Deployment Center',
        hire_date=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=1400),
        is_active=True,
        experience_years=6,
        certification_level='AWS Certified DevOps Engineer'
    )
]

# Create canary deployment authors
canary_authors = [
    Author.objects.create(
        user=canary_users[0],
        bio='Deployment Engineer specializing in canary releases and gradual rollout strategies for Django applications',
        profile_image='canary-engineer.jpg',
        website='https://canary-deployments.anihpj.com',
        social_media_links={'linkedin': 'linkedin.com/in/canary-engineer', 'github': 'github.com/canary-deploys'},
        expertise_areas=['Canary Deployment', 'Traffic Routing', 'Deployment Validation'],
        years_of_experience=6,
        published_posts_count=0,
        follower_count=180,
        following_count=120,
        rating=4.8,
        is_verified=True,
        specialization='Canary Deployment Engineering'
    )
]

# Create canary deployment locations
canary_locations = [
    Location.objects.create(
        id=uuid.uuid4(),
        country='United States',
        state='Texas',
        city='Austin',
        address='Deployment Operations Center',
        postal_code='73301',
        region='us-south-1',
        timezone='America/Chicago',
        is_remote=False,
        cost_of_living_index=95.2,
        job_market_rating='Very Good',
        population=965000,
        major_industries=['Technology', 'Deployment Engineering', 'Operations'],
        climate='Subtropical',
        coordinates={'lat': 30.2672, 'lng': -97.7431}
    )
]

# Create canary deployment job posts
canary_jobposts = [
    JobPost.objects.create(
        id=uuid.uuid4(),
        author=canary_authors[0],
        location=canary_locations[0],
        title='Deployment Engineer - Canary Release Specialist',
        description='Lead canary deployment strategies for Django jobpost applications with gradual traffic shifting and automated validation.',
        requirements='5+ years canary deployment experience, Django applications, Traffic routing, CodeDeploy automation',
        salary_range='$125,000 - $160,000',
        employment_type='Full-time',
        experience_level='Senior',
        is_active=True,
        posted_date=datetime.now(),
        job_category='Deployment Engineering'
    )
]

# Add skills to job posts (Many-to-Many relationship)
for job in canary_jobposts:
    job.required_skills.add(*canary_skills)

print("Canary deployment configuration created successfully for Django gradual rollouts")
    Example 2: Create Blue-Green Deployment Configuration for Zero-Downtime Django Updates

    Create a blue-green deployment configuration for zero-downtime Django jobpost application updates with full traffic cutover after validation. This configuration ensures seamless transitions between application versions while maintaining full availability of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model operations, with instant rollback capability if issues are detected.

    #!/bin/bash
# Create blue-green deployment configuration for zero-downtime Django updates
aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoBlueGreenZeroDowntime" \
  --compute-platform "Server" \
  --traffic-routing-config 'type=AllAtOnceBlueGreen,allAtOnceBlueGreen={}' \
  --minimum-healthy-hosts 'type=FLEET_PERCENT,value=100' \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify blue-green deployment configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name "DjangoBlueGreenZeroDowntime"
    { "deploymentConfigId": "django-bluegreen-config-456-def-abc" } # Blue-green deployment configuration details { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-bluegreen-config-456-def-abc", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoBlueGreenZeroDowntime", "computePlatform": "Server", "minimumHealthyHosts": { "type": "FLEET_PERCENT", "value": 100 }, "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "AllAtOnceBlueGreen", "allAtOnceBlueGreen": {} }, "createTime": "2026-01-15T11:00:00Z" } }
    Example 3: Create Linear Traffic Shift Configuration for Controlled Django Rollout

    Create a linear traffic shifting deployment configuration for controlled Django jobpost application rollout with 20% traffic increments every 10 minutes. This configuration provides balanced risk management for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments, allowing gradual traffic increase with extended validation periods between shifts.

    #!/bin/bash
# Create linear traffic shift deployment configuration for controlled Django rollout  
aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoLinear20Percent10Min" \
  --compute-platform "Server" \
  --traffic-routing-config 'type=TimeBasedLinear,timeBasedLinear={linearPercentage=20,linearInterval=10}' \
  --minimum-healthy-hosts 'type=HOST_COUNT,value=1' \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify linear deployment configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name "DjangoLinear20Percent10Min"
    { "deploymentConfigId": "django-linear-config-789-ghi-def" } # Linear deployment configuration details { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-linear-config-789-ghi-def", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoLinear20Percent10Min", "computePlatform": "Server", "minimumHealthyHosts": { "type": "HOST_COUNT", "value": 1 }, "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "TimeBasedLinear", "timeBasedLinear": { "linearPercentage": 20, "linearInterval": 10 } }, "createTime": "2026-01-15T11:15:00Z" } }
    Example 4: Create Lambda Deployment Configuration for Serverless Django Functions

    Create a deployment configuration optimized for AWS Lambda functions supporting Django jobpost application serverless components. This configuration handles Lambda-specific deployment patterns for serverless Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost processing functions with alias-based traffic routing and automatic rollback on error thresholds.

    #!/bin/bash
# Create Lambda deployment configuration for serverless Django functions
aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoLambdaCanaryAll" \
  --compute-platform "Lambda" \
  --traffic-routing-config 'type=TimeBasedCanary,timeBasedCanary={canaryPercentage=10,canaryInterval=2}' \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify Lambda deployment configuration  
aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name "DjangoLambdaCanaryAll"

# List Lambda-specific configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs | grep Lambda
    { "deploymentConfigId": "django-lambda-config-999-jkl-ghi" } # Lambda deployment configuration details { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-lambda-config-999-jkl-ghi", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoLambdaCanaryAll", "computePlatform": "Lambda", "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "TimeBasedCanary", "timeBasedCanary": { "canaryPercentage": 10, "canaryInterval": 2 } }, "createTime": "2026-01-15T11:30:00Z" } } # Lambda deployment configurations "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent10Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent15Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery1Minute" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery2Minutes" "DjangoLambdaCanaryAll"
    Example 5: Create ECS Deployment Configuration for Containerized Django Applications

    Create a deployment configuration for Amazon ECS containerized Django jobpost applications with rolling updates and health check integration. This configuration manages container-based deployments of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services with service discovery, load balancer integration, and automatic rollback on container health failures.

    #!/bin/bash
# Create ECS deployment configuration for containerized Django applications
aws deploy create-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoECSRollingUpdate" \
  --compute-platform "ECS" \
  --traffic-routing-config 'type=TimeBasedLinear,timeBasedLinear={linearPercentage=25,linearInterval=5}' \
  --region us-east-1

# Verify ECS deployment configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-config --deployment-config-name "DjangoECSRollingUpdate"

# List ECS-specific configurations  
aws deploy list-deployment-configs | grep ECS
    { "deploymentConfigId": "django-ecs-config-555-mno-jkl" } # ECS deployment configuration details { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-ecs-config-555-mno-jkl", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoECSRollingUpdate", "computePlatform": "ECS", "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "TimeBasedLinear", "timeBasedLinear": { "linearPercentage": 25, "linearInterval": 5 } }, "createTime": "2026-01-15T11:45:00Z" } } # ECS deployment configurations "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery3Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent15Minutes" "DjangoECSRollingUpdate"

    delete-application

    Deletes a CodeDeploy application and all associated deployment groups, configurations, and revisions for your Django jobpost application. This command permanently removes the application container that manages Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments. Use with caution as this action cannot be undone and will remove all deployment history and configurations.

    Syntax
    aws deploy delete-application \
  --application-name {application-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --application-name (string, required): Name of the CodeDeploy application to delete. All deployment groups and configurations within this application will be permanently removed.
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deletion.

    delete-deployment-config

    Deletes a custom deployment configuration from CodeDeploy. This command removes deployment configurations that define rollout strategies for your Django jobpost application deployments. Cannot delete predefined system configurations. Ensure no deployment groups are using this configuration before deletion.

    Syntax
    aws deploy delete-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name {deployment-config-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --deployment-config-name (string, required): Name of the custom deployment configuration to delete. Cannot delete system-provided configurations.
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deletion.

    delete-deployment-group

    Deletes a deployment group from a CodeDeploy application. This command removes the logical grouping that defines target infrastructure for Django jobpost application deployments, including auto scaling groups, load balancers, and deployment configurations for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model services.

    Syntax
    aws deploy delete-deployment-group \
  --application-name {application-name} \
  --deployment-group-name {deployment-group-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --application-name (string, required): Name of the CodeDeploy application containing the deployment group
    • --deployment-group-name (string, required): Name of the deployment group to delete
    Return Value
    • hooksNotCleanedUp: Information about lifecycle event hooks that weren't cleaned up

    delete-git-hub-account-token

    Deletes a GitHub account connection token from CodeDeploy. This command removes the authentication token used to access GitHub repositories containing Django jobpost application source code, preventing future deployments from pulling code from linked GitHub repositories for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model applications.

    Syntax
    aws deploy delete-git-hub-account-token \
  --token-name {token-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --token-name (string, required): Name of the GitHub account token to delete from CodeDeploy
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deletion.

    delete-resources-by-external-id

    Deletes CodeDeploy resources based on external identifiers. This command removes resources associated with external systems or third-party integrations for Django jobpost applications, helping maintain clean resource management when external systems are decommissioned or reconfigured.

    Syntax
    aws deploy delete-resources-by-external-id \
  --external-id {external-id} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --external-id (string, required): External identifier of the resources to delete
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deletion.

    get-application

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific CodeDeploy application containing Django jobpost deployment configurations. This command provides comprehensive application metadata, deployment history, and configuration details for managing Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments. Use this command to inspect application settings, verify deployment group associations, and gather information needed for troubleshooting and monitoring Django application deployment processes.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name {application-name}
    Parameters Description
    • --application-name (string, required): Name of the CodeDeploy application to retrieve information about. This application serves as the container for all Django jobpost deployment groups and manages the lifecycle of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments across your infrastructure environments.
    Attributes
    • applicationId: Unique identifier assigned to the application for tracking and management operations
    • applicationName: Name of the application as specified during creation
    • createTime: Timestamp when the application was created
    • linkedToGitHub: Boolean indicating if application is linked to GitHub repository
    • gitHubAccountName: GitHub account name if linked to GitHub integration
    • computePlatform: Target compute platform (Server, Lambda, ECS) for application deployments
    Methods
    list-applications

    Syntax: aws deploy list-applications

    Parameters: No required parameters; lists all CodeDeploy applications in your account for Django deployment management

    Return Value
    • applicationInfo (object): Comprehensive application information including metadata, configuration settings, and deployment group associations for Django jobpost application management and monitoring
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy get-application Examples
    Example 1: Get Basic Django Application Information for Development Environment

    Retrieve comprehensive information about a Django jobpost application in the development environment, including deployment groups, application metadata, and configuration settings. This example shows how to inspect application details for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployment management and troubleshooting.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get Django application information for development environment
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-dev" \
  --region us-east-1

# Get additional application statistics
aws deploy list-deployment-groups --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-dev"
    { "applicationInfo": { "applicationId": "12345678-dev-app-abcd-ef1234567890", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-dev", "createTime": "2026-01-10T09:30:00Z", "linkedToGitHub": false, "computePlatform": "Server" } } # Deployment groups in application { "deploymentGroups": [ "dev-django-servers", "dev-worker-instances" ] }

    Django Queryset Examples for Development Application Information

    # Django Models Setup for Development Application Information Retrieval
from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Create development application monitoring skills
dev_monitoring_skills = [
    Skills.objects.create(
        id=uuid.uuid4(),
        category='Application Monitoring',
        skill_type='development',
        skill_description='CodeDeploy application monitoring and information retrieval for Django development environments',
        name='Application Monitoring',
        proficiency_level='Advanced',
        required=True,
        years_experience=3,
        certification_required=False,
        last_updated=datetime.now()
    )
]

# Create development monitoring users
dev_users = [
    User.objects.create(
        username='dev-monitor-lead',
        email='dev.monitor@anihpj.com',
        first_name='Development',
        last_name='Monitor Lead',
        role='Development Operations',
        department='Engineering',
        phone_number='+1-555-0404',
        location='Development Center',
        hire_date=datetime.now() - timedelta(days=800),
        is_active=True,
        experience_years=4,
        certification_level='AWS Certified Developer'
    )
]

print("Development application monitoring setup completed")
    Example 2: Get Production Application Information with Deployment History

    Retrieve detailed information about a production Django jobpost application including comprehensive deployment history, linked GitHub repositories, and deployment group configurations. This example demonstrates inspection of production application settings for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models with complete deployment tracking and monitoring capabilities.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get production Django application information with deployment history
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-production" \
  --region us-east-1

# Get comprehensive deployment history
aws deploy list-deployments \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-production" \
  --include-only-statuses Succeeded Failed \
  --max-items 10
    { "applicationInfo": { "applicationId": "87654321-prod-app-xyz9-gh9876543210", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-production", "createTime": "2026-01-05T14:20:00Z", "linkedToGitHub": true, "gitHubAccountName": "anihpj-production", "computePlatform": "Server" } }
    Example 3: Get Lambda Application Information for Serverless Django Components

    Retrieve information about a Lambda-based CodeDeploy application managing serverless Django jobpost components. This example shows how to inspect Lambda application configurations for serverless Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost processing functions with alias management and version tracking.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get Lambda application information for serverless Django components
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-lambda" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "applicationInfo": { "applicationId": "lambda-456-serverless-abc-def789", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-lambda", "createTime": "2026-01-12T10:15:00Z", "linkedToGitHub": false, "computePlatform": "Lambda" } }
    Example 4: Get ECS Application Information for Containerized Django Services

    Retrieve information about an ECS-based CodeDeploy application managing containerized Django jobpost services. This example demonstrates inspection of container application settings for dockerized Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost microservices with service discovery and load balancer integration.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get ECS application information for containerized Django services  
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-ecs" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "applicationInfo": { "applicationId": "ecs-789-container-ghi-jkl123", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-ecs", "createTime": "2026-01-08T16:45:00Z", "linkedToGitHub": true, "gitHubAccountName": "anihpj-containers", "computePlatform": "ECS" } }
    Example 5: Get Multi-Region Application Information with Cross-Region Deployment Tracking

    Retrieve comprehensive information about a multi-region Django jobpost application with cross-region deployment coordination. This example shows how to inspect application configurations spanning multiple AWS regions for disaster recovery and global deployment of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get multi-region application information with cross-region tracking
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-global" \
  --region us-east-1

# Check application in secondary region
aws deploy get-application \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-global" \
  --region eu-west-1
    { "applicationInfo": { "applicationId": "global-999-multiregion-mno-pqr456", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-global", "createTime": "2026-01-03T12:00:00Z", "linkedToGitHub": true, "gitHubAccountName": "anihpj-global", "computePlatform": "Server" } }

    deregister

    Deregisters an on-premises instance from CodeDeploy, removing it from deployment targets for your Django jobpost application. This command unregisters instances that were previously configured to receive deployments, cleaning up the registration without affecting the actual server hardware or stopping services for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model applications.

    Syntax
    aws deploy deregister \
  --instance-name {instance-name} \
  --delete-iam-user \
  --no-delete-iam-user \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --instance-name (string, required): Name of the on-premises instance to deregister
    • --delete-iam-user | --no-delete-iam-user (boolean): Whether to delete the IAM user associated with the instance
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deregistration.

    deregister-on-premises-instance

    Deregisters an on-premises instance from CodeDeploy deployments. This command removes an on-premises server from the pool of available deployment targets for Django jobpost applications, effectively stopping it from receiving new deployments for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model services while preserving existing configurations.

    Syntax
    aws deploy deregister-on-premises-instance \
  --instance-name {instance-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --instance-name (string, required): Name of the on-premises instance to deregister from CodeDeploy
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deregistration.

    get-application-revision

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific application revision for your Django jobpost application. This command provides comprehensive revision metadata including source location, commit information, and deployment bundle details for tracking specific versions of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model code deployments.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-application-revision \
  --application-name {application-name} \
  --revision revisionType={revision-type},s3Location={bucket-info} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --application-name (string, required): Name of the CodeDeploy application containing the revision
    • --revision (structure, required): Revision specification including type and location information
    Return Value
    • applicationName: Name of the Django application
    • revision: Detailed revision information and metadata
    • revisionInfo: Additional revision details including creation time and description

    get-deployment-target

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific deployment target within a CodeDeploy deployment. This command provides comprehensive target metadata including deployment status, lifecycle events, and instance details for monitoring Django jobpost application deployments on specific infrastructure components hosting Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model services.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-deployment-target \
  --deployment-id {deployment-id} \
  --target-id {target-id} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --deployment-id (string, required): Unique identifier of the deployment containing the target
    • --target-id (string, required): Unique identifier of the specific deployment target
    Return Value
    • deploymentTarget: Complete deployment target information
    • targetId: Unique identifier of the deployment target
    • targetType: Type of deployment target (EC2, Lambda, ECS)
    • deploymentStatus: Current deployment status
    • lifecycleEvents: Array of lifecycle event details

    get-on-premises-instance

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific on-premises instance registered with CodeDeploy. This command provides comprehensive instance metadata including registration status, tags, and configuration details for monitoring on-premises servers hosting Django jobpost applications with Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model services.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-on-premises-instance \
  --instance-name {instance-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --instance-name (string, required): Name of the on-premises instance to retrieve information about
    Return Value
    • instanceInfo: Complete on-premises instance information
    • instanceName: Name of the on-premises instance
    • iamSessionArn: IAM session ARN if applicable
    • iamUserArn: IAM user ARN for the instance
    • instanceArn: ARN of the on-premises instance
    • registerTime: Instance registration timestamp
    • deregisterTime: Instance deregistration timestamp if applicable
    • tags: Array of tags applied to the instance

    get-deployment

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific deployment of your Django jobpost application, including deployment status, instance details, and execution timeline. This command provides comprehensive deployment monitoring data for tracking Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments across your infrastructure, including rollback information, failure details, and deployment progress metrics essential for troubleshooting and monitoring deployment health.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id {deployment-id}
    Parameters Description
    • --deployment-id (string, required): Unique identifier of the deployment to retrieve information about. This ID is returned when creating deployments and is used to track specific Django jobpost application deployment instances, including their status, progress, and execution details for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model updates.
    Attributes
    • deploymentId: Unique identifier of the deployment being retrieved
    • applicationName: CodeDeploy application name containing this deployment
    • deploymentGroupName: Target deployment group for the deployment
    • status: Current deployment status (Created, Queued, InProgress, Succeeded, Failed, Stopped)
    • createTime: Timestamp when deployment was created
    • deploymentOverview: Summary of deployment progress across target instances
    Methods
    list-deployments

    Syntax: aws deploy list-deployments --application-name {app-name}

    Parameters: application-name (string, required) - Application to list deployments for

    Return Value
    • deploymentInfo (object): Comprehensive deployment information including status, timeline, instance details, and error information for Django jobpost application deployment monitoring and troubleshooting
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy get-deployment Examples
    Example 1: Get Successful Django Development Deployment Information

    Retrieve detailed information about a successful Django jobpost application deployment in the development environment. This example shows how to inspect completed deployments for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model updates with successful validation and rollout completion.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get successful Django development deployment information
aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id "d-dev-success-12345-abcdef" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentInfo": { "deploymentId": "d-dev-success-12345-abcdef", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-dev", "deploymentGroupName": "dev-django-servers", "status": "Succeeded", "createTime": "2026-01-15T09:00:00Z", "deploymentOverview": { "Succeeded": 3, "Failed": 0 } } }
    Example 2: Get Failed Django Production Deployment with Error Analysis

    Retrieve detailed information about a failed Django jobpost application deployment in production, including error details, failure reasons, and affected instances. This example demonstrates how to analyze deployment failures for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model updates to identify root causes and plan remediation strategies.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get failed Django production deployment with error analysis
aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id "d-prod-failed-67890-xyz123" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentInfo": { "deploymentId": "d-prod-failed-67890-xyz123", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-production", "deploymentGroupName": "prod-django-asg-cluster", "status": "Failed", "errorInformation": { "code": "HEALTH_CONSTRAINTS_INVALID", "message": "The overall deployment failed because too many individual instances failed deployment" }, "deploymentOverview": { "Succeeded": 2, "Failed": 4 } } }
    Example 3: Get In-Progress Django Blue-Green Deployment Status

    Monitor an active blue-green deployment of Django jobpost application showing real-time progress and instance status. This example demonstrates tracking ongoing deployments for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model updates during blue-green rollout with traffic shifting and validation phases.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get in-progress Django blue-green deployment status
aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id "d-bg-progress-abc456-def789" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentInfo": { "deploymentId": "d-bg-progress-abc456-def789", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-bluegreen", "deploymentGroupName": "bg-django-validation-cluster", "status": "InProgress", "createTime": "2026-01-15T11:30:00Z", "deploymentOverview": { "Pending": 0, "InProgress": 2, "Succeeded": 1, "Failed": 0 } } }
    Example 4: Get Lambda Function Deployment Information

    Retrieve information about a Lambda function deployment for serverless Django jobpost components. This example shows deployment details for serverless Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost processing functions with version management and alias routing.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get Lambda function deployment information
aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id "d-lambda-deploy-def123-ghi456" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentInfo": { "deploymentId": "d-lambda-deploy-def123-ghi456", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-lambda", "deploymentGroupName": "lambda-functions-group", "status": "Succeeded", "createTime": "2026-01-15T12:00:00Z", "deploymentOverview": { "Succeeded": 1, "Failed": 0 } } }
    Example 5: Get Multi-Region Deployment Information

    Monitor a multi-region deployment of Django jobpost application across primary and secondary regions. This example demonstrates tracking cross-region deployments for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models with coordinated rollout and disaster recovery validation.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get multi-region deployment information
aws deploy get-deployment \
  --deployment-id "d-multiregion-789-uvw456" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentInfo": { "deploymentId": "d-multiregion-789-uvw456", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-global", "deploymentGroupName": "multiregion-django-cluster", "status": "Succeeded", "createTime": "2026-01-15T10:45:00Z", "deploymentOverview": { "Succeeded": 6, "Failed": 0 } } }

    get-deployment-group

    Retrieves comprehensive information about a specific deployment group configured for Django jobpost application deployments. This command provides detailed deployment group configuration including target instance settings, deployment strategies, load balancer configurations, and alarm settings. Essential for managing Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployment targets and validating deployment group configurations.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name {application-name} \
  --deployment-group-name {deployment-group-name}
    Parameters Description
    • --application-name (string, required): Name of the CodeDeploy application containing the deployment group. This application manages Django jobpost deployment lifecycle for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models across your infrastructure environments.
    • --deployment-group-name (string, required): Name of the deployment group to retrieve configuration information about. The deployment group defines target infrastructure and deployment behavior for Django application updates.
    Return Value
    • deploymentGroupInfo (object): Comprehensive deployment group configuration including target settings, deployment strategy, and infrastructure details for Django application deployment management
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy get-deployment-group Examples
    Example 1: Get Development Deployment Group Configuration

    Retrieve configuration details for a development environment deployment group managing Django jobpost application deployments. This example shows target instance configuration, deployment settings, and infrastructure details for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get development deployment group configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-dev" \
  --deployment-group-name "dev-django-servers" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupId": "dev-group-123-abc-def456", "deploymentGroupName": "dev-django-servers", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-dev", "serviceRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CodeDeployServiceRole", "ec2TagFilters": [ {"Key": "Environment", "Value": "development", "Type": "KEY_AND_VALUE"} ], "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce" } }
    Example 2: Get Production Auto Scaling Group Configuration

    Retrieve configuration for production deployment group with Auto Scaling Groups and load balancer integration for Django jobpost applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get production Auto Scaling Group configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-production" \
  --deployment-group-name "prod-django-asg-cluster" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupId": "prod-group-789-xyz-abc123", "deploymentGroupName": "prod-django-asg-cluster", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-production", "autoScalingGroups": [ {"name": "django-jobpost-asg-prod"} ], "loadBalancerInfo": { "targetGroupInfoList": [ {"name": "django-jobpost-tg-prod"} ] }, "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenAllAtOnce" } }
    Example 3: Get Blue-Green Deployment Group with Lambda Hooks

    Retrieve configuration for blue-green deployment group with Lambda validation hooks for Django jobpost applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get blue-green deployment group with Lambda hooks
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-bluegreen" \
  --deployment-group-name "bg-django-validation-cluster" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupId": "bg-group-456-def-ghi789", "deploymentGroupName": "bg-django-validation-cluster", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-bluegreen", "triggerConfigurations": [ { "triggerName": "PreDeploymentHook", "triggerTargetArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:django-pre-deployment-validation" } ] } }
    Example 4: Get Lambda Deployment Group Configuration

    Retrieve configuration for serverless Lambda deployment group managing Django jobpost functions.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get Lambda deployment group configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-lambda" \
  --deployment-group-name "lambda-functions-group" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupId": "lambda-group-789-jkl-mno012", "deploymentGroupName": "lambda-functions-group", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-lambda", "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes" } }
    Example 5: Get Multi-Region Deployment Group Configuration

    Retrieve configuration for multi-region deployment group coordinating Django deployments across regions.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get multi-region deployment group configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-group \
  --application-name "anihpj-jobpost-global" \
  --deployment-group-name "multiregion-django-cluster" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroupInfo": { "deploymentGroupId": "global-group-999-pqr-stu345", "deploymentGroupName": "multiregion-django-cluster", "applicationName": "anihpj-jobpost-global", "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATimeMultiRegion" } }

    get-deployment-config

    Retrieves detailed information about a specific deployment configuration used by Django jobpost application deployments. This command provides comprehensive configuration details including rollout strategy, minimum healthy hosts requirements, and traffic routing settings. Essential for understanding deployment behavior, validating configuration settings, and troubleshooting deployment strategy issues for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost model deployments.

    Syntax
    aws deploy get-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name {deployment-config-name}
    Parameters Description
    • --deployment-config-name (string, required): Name of the deployment configuration to retrieve information about. This configuration defines the deployment strategy and rollout behavior for Django jobpost application updates, including traffic shifting patterns, failure thresholds, and platform-specific deployment settings.
    Return Value
    • deploymentConfigInfo (object): Comprehensive deployment configuration details including rollout strategy, platform settings, and traffic routing configuration for Django application deployment management
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy get-deployment-config Examples
    Example 1: Get Built-in All-At-Once Deployment Configuration

    Retrieve information about the built-in all-at-once deployment configuration for Django applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get built-in all-at-once deployment configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "builtin-allat-once-123", "deploymentConfigName": "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce", "computePlatform": "Server", "minimumHealthyHosts": { "type": "FLEET_PERCENT", "value": 0 } } }
    Example 2: Get Custom Canary Deployment Configuration

    Retrieve information about a custom canary deployment configuration for Django jobpost applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Get custom canary deployment configuration
aws deploy get-deployment-config \
  --deployment-config-name "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min" \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentConfigInfo": { "deploymentConfigId": "django-canary-config-456", "deploymentConfigName": "DjangoCanary10Percent5Min", "computePlatform": "Server", "trafficRoutingConfig": { "type": "TimeBasedCanary", "timeBasedCanary": { "canaryPercentage": 10, "canaryInterval": 5 } } } }

    list-applications

    Lists all CodeDeploy applications in your AWS account that manage Django jobpost deployments. This command provides a comprehensive overview of all applications configured for deploying Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models across different environments. Essential for application discovery, account auditing, and managing multiple Django application deployments across development, staging, and production environments.

    Syntax
    aws deploy list-applications
    Return Value
    • applications (array): List of all CodeDeploy application names configured in your AWS account for Django jobpost deployment management
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy list-applications Examples
    Example 1: List All Django Applications

    Retrieve a complete list of all CodeDeploy applications managing Django jobpost deployments in your AWS account.

    #!/bin/bash
# List all Django applications
aws deploy list-applications \
  --region us-east-1
    { "applications": [ "anihpj-jobpost-dev", "anihpj-jobpost-staging", "anihpj-jobpost-production", "anihpj-jobpost-lambda", "anihpj-jobpost-ecs", "anihpj-jobpost-global" ] }
    Example 2: List Applications with Filtering

    List applications and filter results for Django-specific deployments using command-line tools.

    #!/bin/bash
# List applications with filtering for Django deployments
aws deploy list-applications --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '.applications[] | select(. | contains("jobpost"))'
    "anihpj-jobpost-dev" "anihpj-jobpost-staging" "anihpj-jobpost-production" "anihpj-jobpost-lambda" "anihpj-jobpost-ecs" "anihpj-jobpost-global"
    Example 3: List Multi-Region Django Applications

    List CodeDeploy applications across multiple regions managing Django jobpost deployments.

    #!/bin/bash
# List applications across multiple regions
for region in us-east-1 us-west-2 eu-west-1; do
  echo "Applications in $region:"
  aws deploy list-applications --region $region
  echo "---"
done
    Applications in us-east-1: { "applications": [ "anihpj-jobpost-production-east", "anihpj-jobpost-lambda-east" ] } --- Applications in us-west-2: { "applications": [ "anihpj-jobpost-production-west", "anihpj-jobpost-staging-west" ] } --- Applications in eu-west-1: { "applications": [ "anihpj-jobpost-production-eu", "anihpj-jobpost-global-eu" ] } ---
    Example 4: List Applications with Deployment Group Count

    List applications and retrieve deployment group counts for Django jobpost management oversight.

    #!/bin/bash
# List applications with deployment group details
applications=$(aws deploy list-applications --region us-east-1 --query 'applications[]' --output text)
for app in $applications; do
  groups=$(aws deploy list-deployment-groups --application-name $app --region us-east-1 --query 'deploymentGroups | length(@)')
  echo "Application: $app, Deployment Groups: $groups"
done
    Application: anihpj-jobpost-dev, Deployment Groups: 3 Application: anihpj-jobpost-staging, Deployment Groups: 2 Application: anihpj-jobpost-production, Deployment Groups: 5 Application: anihpj-jobpost-lambda, Deployment Groups: 1 Application: anihpj-jobpost-ecs, Deployment Groups: 4 Application: anihpj-jobpost-global, Deployment Groups: 8
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django queryset for counting deployment configurations:

    # Count deployment groups per application
from collections import defaultdict

deployment_counts = defaultdict(int)
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-dev'] = 3
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-staging'] = 2
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-production'] = 5
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-lambda'] = 1
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-ecs'] = 4
deployment_counts['anihpj-jobpost-global'] = 8

# Display application deployment summary
for app_name, group_count in deployment_counts.items():
    print(f"Application: {app_name}, Deployment Groups: {group_count}")
    Example 5: List Applications with JSON Output Processing

    List applications and process JSON output for Django jobpost deployment management and monitoring.

    #!/bin/bash
# Process application list with JSON formatting
aws deploy list-applications --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '{
    total_applications: (.applications | length),
    django_apps: [.applications[] | select(. | contains("jobpost"))],
    lambda_apps: [.applications[] | select(. | contains("lambda"))],
    production_apps: [.applications[] | select(. | contains("production"))]
  }'
    { "total_applications": 6, "django_apps": [ "anihpj-jobpost-dev", "anihpj-jobpost-staging", "anihpj-jobpost-production", "anihpj-jobpost-lambda", "anihpj-jobpost-ecs", "anihpj-jobpost-global" ], "lambda_apps": [ "anihpj-jobpost-lambda" ], "production_apps": [ "anihpj-jobpost-production" ] }
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django queryset for categorizing applications:

    # Categorize Django applications by type
all_apps = [
    "anihpj-jobpost-dev",
    "anihpj-jobpost-staging", 
    "anihpj-jobpost-production",
    "anihpj-jobpost-lambda",
    "anihpj-jobpost-ecs",
    "anihpj-jobpost-global"
]

# Filter applications by category
django_apps = [app for app in all_apps if 'jobpost' in app]
lambda_apps = [app for app in all_apps if 'lambda' in app]
production_apps = [app for app in all_apps if 'production' in app]

# Create categorized summary
app_summary = {
    'total_applications': len(all_apps),
    'django_apps': django_apps,
    'lambda_apps': lambda_apps,
    'production_apps': production_apps
}

print(f"Total applications: {app_summary['total_applications']}")
print(f"Django apps: {len(app_summary['django_apps'])}")
print(f"Lambda apps: {len(app_summary['lambda_apps'])}")
print(f"Production apps: {len(app_summary['production_apps'])}")

# Display JobPost models that would be managed
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location

# Get statistics for production deployments
production_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(
    status='published',
    created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=30)
)

print(f"Jobs requiring production deployment: {production_jobs.count()}")
print(f"Active skills in production: {Skills.objects.filter(is_active=True).count()}")
print(f"Authors with deployable content: {Author.objects.filter(jobpost__isnull=False).distinct().count()}")

    list-deployment-configs

    Lists all CodeDeploy deployment configurations available for managing Django jobpost application deployments. This command shows both AWS managed and custom deployment configurations that define how Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models are deployed across EC2 instances, Auto Scaling groups, Lambda functions, and ECS services. Essential for understanding available deployment strategies, selecting appropriate configurations, and managing deployment speeds and safety measures across different Django application environments.

    Syntax
    aws deploy list-deployment-configs
    Return Value
    • deploymentConfigsList (array): List of all available deployment configuration names including AWS managed and custom configurations for Django jobpost deployment strategies
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy list-deployment-configs Examples
    Example 1: List All Deployment Configurations

    Retrieve a complete list of all deployment configurations available for Django jobpost deployments.

    #!/bin/bash
# List all deployment configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentConfigsList": [ "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce", "CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime", "CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime", "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceBlueGreen", "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenCanary10Percent15Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenCanary10Percent30Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenLinear10PercentEvery1Minute", "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenLinear10PercentEvery10Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery1Minute", "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery2Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent10Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce", "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes", "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minute", "anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min", "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min", "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min" ] }
    Example 2: Filter EC2 Deployment Configurations

    List deployment configurations specifically for EC2 instances hosting Django jobpost applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Filter for EC2-specific deployment configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '.deploymentConfigsList[] | select(. | contains("BlueGreen") or contains("AllAtOnce") or contains("HalfAtATime") or contains("OneAtATime") or contains("anihpj"))'
    "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce" "CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime" "CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime" "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceBlueGreen" "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenCanary10Percent15Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenCanary10Percent30Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenLinear10PercentEvery1Minute" "CodeDeployDefault.BlueGreenLinear10PercentEvery10Minutes" "anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min" "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min" "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min"
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django queryset for EC2 deployment strategy selection:

    # EC2 deployment configurations for Django jobpost applications
ec2_configs = [
    "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce",
    "CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime", 
    "CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime",
    "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceBlueGreen",
    "anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min",
    "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min",
    "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min"
]

# Map configurations to Django model deployment strategies
deployment_strategies = {
    'fast_deployment': ["CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce"],
    'safe_deployment': ["CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime", "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min"],
    'balanced_deployment': ["CodeDeployDefault.HalfAtATime"],
    'blue_green_deployment': [
        "CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnceBlueGreen",
        "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min"
    ],
    'canary_deployment': ["anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min"]
}

# Select deployment strategy based on Django model criticality
from jobpost.models import JobPost

# High-traffic production JobPost deployments
if JobPost.objects.filter(status='published').count() > 10000:
    recommended_config = deployment_strategies['blue_green_deployment'][1]
    print(f"Recommended for high-traffic: {recommended_config}")
else:
    recommended_config = deployment_strategies['safe_deployment'][0] 
    print(f"Recommended for standard traffic: {recommended_config}")
    Example 3: Filter Lambda Deployment Configurations

    List deployment configurations specifically for AWS Lambda functions handling Django jobpost serverless operations.

    #!/bin/bash
# Filter for Lambda-specific deployment configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '.deploymentConfigsList[] | select(. | contains("Lambda"))'
    "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery1Minute" "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery2Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent10Minutes"
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django implementation for Lambda deployment configuration selection:

    # Lambda deployment configurations for Django serverless functions
lambda_configs = [
    "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery1Minute",
    "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery2Minutes", 
    "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes",
    "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent10Minutes"
]

# Map Lambda configs to Django serverless function types
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location

serverless_functions = {
    'job_search_api': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes",
        'model': JobPost,
        'criticality': 'high'
    },
    'skills_matcher': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery1Minute", 
        'model': Skills,
        'criticality': 'medium'
    },
    'user_notifications': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.Lambda10PercentEvery2Minutes",
        'model': User,
        'criticality': 'low'
    },
    'location_geocoder': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent10Minutes",
        'model': Location,
        'criticality': 'medium'
    }
}

# Select deployment config based on function criticality
for func_name, func_info in serverless_functions.items():
    print(f"Function: {func_name}")
    print(f"Model: {func_info['model'].__name__}")
    print(f"Config: {func_info['config']}")
    print(f"Criticality: {func_info['criticality']}")
    print("---")
    Example 4: Filter ECS Deployment Configurations

    List deployment configurations for Amazon ECS services running Django jobpost containerized applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# Filter for ECS-specific deployment configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '.deploymentConfigsList[] | select(. | contains("ECS"))'
    "CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce" "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes" "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minute"
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django implementation for ECS deployment configuration management:

    # ECS deployment configurations for Django containerized applications
ecs_configs = [
    "CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce",
    "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes",
    "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minute"
]

# Map ECS services to Django application components
ecs_services = {
    'anihpj-web-service': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.ECSCanary10Percent5Minutes",
        'models': [JobPost, User, Author],
        'environment': 'production',
        'traffic_level': 'high'
    },
    'anihpj-api-service': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.ECSLinear10PercentEvery1Minute",
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Location],
        'environment': 'production', 
        'traffic_level': 'medium'
    },
    'anihpj-worker-service': {
        'config': "CodeDeployDefault.ECSAllAtOnce",
        'models': [JobPost],
        'environment': 'staging',
        'traffic_level': 'low'
    }
}

# Select deployment strategy for ECS services
from django.utils import timezone
from datetime import timedelta

for service_name, service_info in ecs_services.items():
    print(f"ECS Service: {service_name}")
    print(f"Deployment Config: {service_info['config']}")
    print(f"Models: {[model.__name__ for model in service_info['models']]}")
    print(f"Environment: {service_info['environment']}")
    
    # Check recent JobPost activity for deployment timing
    recent_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(
        created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=1)
    ).count()
    
    if recent_jobs > 50 and service_info['traffic_level'] == 'high':
        print(f"High activity detected ({recent_jobs} jobs), using cautious deployment")
    else:
        print(f"Normal activity ({recent_jobs} jobs), standard deployment")
    print("---")
    Example 5: List Custom Deployment Configurations

    List custom deployment configurations specifically created for Django jobpost application requirements.

    #!/bin/bash
# Filter for custom (non-AWS default) deployment configurations
aws deploy list-deployment-configs --region us-east-1 | \
  jq '.deploymentConfigsList[] | select(. | startswith("CodeDeployDefault") | not)'
    "anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min" "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min" "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min"
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django implementation for custom deployment configuration management:

    # Custom deployment configurations for Django jobpost applications
custom_configs = [
    "anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min",
    "anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min",
    "anihpj-production-blue-green-30min"
]

# Map custom configs to Django deployment scenarios
deployment_scenarios = {
    'anihpj-django-canary-20percent-5min': {
        'type': 'canary',
        'percentage': 20,
        'interval': '5 minutes',
        'use_case': 'Feature rollouts with quick feedback',
        'models': [JobPost, Skills],
        'risk_level': 'medium'
    },
    'anihpj-django-linear-5percent-2min': {
        'type': 'linear',
        'percentage': 5,
        'interval': '2 minutes', 
        'use_case': 'Gradual production deployments',
        'models': [User, Author, Location],
        'risk_level': 'low'
    },
    'anihpj-production-blue-green-30min': {
        'type': 'blue_green',
        'percentage': 100,
        'interval': '30 minutes',
        'use_case': 'Zero-downtime production releases',
        'models': [JobPost, User, Author, Skills, Location],
        'risk_level': 'very_low'
    }
}

# Display custom configuration analysis
for config_name, config_info in deployment_scenarios.items():
    print(f"Configuration: {config_name}")
    print(f"Type: {config_info['type']}")
    print(f"Deployment Speed: {config_info['percentage']}% every {config_info['interval']}")
    print(f"Use Case: {config_info['use_case']}")
    print(f"Risk Level: {config_info['risk_level']}")
    print(f"Models: {[model.__name__ for model in config_info['models']]}")
    
    # Calculate estimated deployment time for JobPost models
    from jobpost.models import JobPost
    active_instances = 10  # Example instance count
    
    if config_info['type'] == 'linear':
        total_time = (100 / config_info['percentage']) * 2  # 2 minutes per interval
        print(f"Estimated deployment time: {total_time} minutes for {active_instances} instances")
    elif config_info['type'] == 'canary':
        canary_time = 5  # 5 minutes canary
        full_time = 10   # 10 minutes full deployment
        print(f"Estimated deployment time: {canary_time + full_time} minutes total")
    elif config_info['type'] == 'blue_green':
        warmup_time = 30  # 30 minutes warmup
        print(f"Estimated deployment time: {warmup_time} minutes (instant switch)")
    
    print("=" * 50)

    list-deployment-groups

    Lists all deployment groups for a specified CodeDeploy application managing Django jobpost deployments. This command shows deployment groups that define target environments for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost models across EC2 instances, Auto Scaling groups, Lambda functions, and ECS services. Essential for understanding deployment targets, managing environment-specific configurations, and organizing deployments across development, staging, and production environments for Django applications.

    Syntax
    aws deploy list-deployment-groups --application-name <application-name>
    Parameters
    • --application-name (string): [REQUIRED] The name of the CodeDeploy application for which to list deployment groups. Must be an existing application managing Django jobpost deployments.
    Return Value
    • deploymentGroups (array): List of deployment group names associated with the specified Django jobpost application for managing model deployments across different target environments
    5 Detailed CodeDeploy list-deployment-groups Examples
    Example 1: List Development Environment Deployment Groups

    List all deployment groups for a Django jobpost development application.

    #!/bin/bash
# List deployment groups for development application
aws deploy list-deployment-groups \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-dev \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroups": [ "anihpj-dev-web-servers", "anihpj-dev-api-servers", "anihpj-dev-worker-nodes" ] }
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django queryset for development deployment group management:

    # Development deployment groups for Django jobpost models
dev_deployment_groups = [
    "anihpj-dev-web-servers",
    "anihpj-dev-api-servers", 
    "anihpj-dev-worker-nodes"
]

# Map deployment groups to Django model responsibilities
from jobpost.models import JobPost, Skills, User, Author, Location

deployment_group_models = {
    'anihpj-dev-web-servers': {
        'primary_models': [JobPost, User, Author],
        'responsibilities': ['User interface', 'Job display', 'Author profiles'],
        'instance_type': 't3.small',
        'capacity': 2
    },
    'anihpj-dev-api-servers': {
        'primary_models': [JobPost, Skills, Location],
        'responsibilities': ['API endpoints', 'Search functionality', 'Data processing'],
        'instance_type': 't3.medium', 
        'capacity': 1
    },
    'anihpj-dev-worker-nodes': {
        'primary_models': [JobPost],
        'responsibilities': ['Background jobs', 'Data migration', 'Cleanup tasks'],
        'instance_type': 't3.small',
        'capacity': 1
    }
}

# Display development deployment configuration
for group_name, group_info in deployment_group_models.items():
    print(f"Deployment Group: {group_name}")
    print(f"Models: {[model.__name__ for model in group_info['primary_models']]}")
    print(f"Responsibilities: {', '.join(group_info['responsibilities'])}")
    print(f"Instance Type: {group_info['instance_type']}")
    print(f"Capacity: {group_info['capacity']} instances")
    
    # Calculate development data load
    if JobPost in group_info['primary_models']:
        test_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(status='draft').count()
        print(f"Test JobPost records: {test_jobs}")
    print("---")
    Example 2: List Production Environment Deployment Groups

    List deployment groups for a Django jobpost production application with multiple target environments.

    #!/bin/bash
# List deployment groups for production application
aws deploy list-deployment-groups \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-production \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroups": [ "anihpj-prod-web-asg", "anihpj-prod-api-asg", "anihpj-prod-worker-asg", "anihpj-prod-cache-nodes", "anihpj-prod-admin-servers" ] }
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django queryset for production deployment group management:

    # Production deployment groups for Django jobpost applications
prod_deployment_groups = [
    "anihpj-prod-web-asg",
    "anihpj-prod-api-asg",
    "anihpj-prod-worker-asg", 
    "anihpj-prod-cache-nodes",
    "anihpj-prod-admin-servers"
]

# Map production deployment groups to Django model scaling
production_scaling = {
    'anihpj-prod-web-asg': {
        'models': [JobPost, User, Author],
        'auto_scaling': True,
        'min_capacity': 3,
        'max_capacity': 10,
        'target_cpu': 70,
        'load_balancer': True
    },
    'anihpj-prod-api-asg': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Location],
        'auto_scaling': True,
        'min_capacity': 2,
        'max_capacity': 8,
        'target_cpu': 60,
        'load_balancer': True
    },
    'anihpj-prod-worker-asg': {
        'models': [JobPost],
        'auto_scaling': True,
        'min_capacity': 1,
        'max_capacity': 5,
        'target_cpu': 80,
        'load_balancer': False
    },
    'anihpj-prod-cache-nodes': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills],
        'auto_scaling': False,
        'min_capacity': 2,
        'max_capacity': 2,
        'target_cpu': None,
        'load_balancer': False
    },
    'anihpj-prod-admin-servers': {
        'models': [User, Author],
        'auto_scaling': False,
        'min_capacity': 1,
        'max_capacity': 2,
        'target_cpu': None,
        'load_balancer': True
    }
}

# Analyze production deployment requirements
from django.utils import timezone
from datetime import timedelta

for group_name, scaling_info in production_scaling.items():
    print(f"Production Group: {group_name}")
    print(f"Models: {[model.__name__ for model in scaling_info['models']]}")
    print(f"Auto Scaling: {scaling_info['auto_scaling']}")
    print(f"Capacity: {scaling_info['min_capacity']}-{scaling_info['max_capacity']} instances")
    
    # Calculate current production load
    if JobPost in scaling_info['models']:
        active_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(
            status='published',
            created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=1)
        ).count()
        print(f"Active JobPost records (24h): {active_jobs}")
        
        # Recommend scaling based on load
        if active_jobs > 1000:
            recommended_instances = min(scaling_info['max_capacity'], 
                                      max(scaling_info['min_capacity'], active_jobs // 200))
            print(f"Recommended instances: {recommended_instances}")
    print("---")
    Example 3: List Lambda Deployment Groups

    List deployment groups for AWS Lambda functions handling Django jobpost serverless operations.

    #!/bin/bash
# List deployment groups for Lambda application
aws deploy list-deployment-groups \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-lambda \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroups": [ "anihpj-lambda-search-api", "anihpj-lambda-notifications", "anihpj-lambda-data-processor" ] }
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django implementation for Lambda deployment group management:

    # Lambda deployment groups for Django serverless functions
lambda_deployment_groups = [
    "anihpj-lambda-search-api",
    "anihpj-lambda-notifications",
    "anihpj-lambda-data-processor"
]

# Map Lambda groups to Django model operations
lambda_functions = {
    'anihpj-lambda-search-api': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Location],
        'operations': ['search', 'filter', 'recommendations'],
        'memory': 512,
        'timeout': 30,
        'concurrent_executions': 100
    },
    'anihpj-lambda-notifications': {
        'models': [User, JobPost],
        'operations': ['email_alerts', 'push_notifications', 'sms'],
        'memory': 256,
        'timeout': 15,
        'concurrent_executions': 50
    },
    'anihpj-lambda-data-processor': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Author],
        'operations': ['data_cleanup', 'analytics', 'reporting'],
        'memory': 1024,
        'timeout': 300,
        'concurrent_executions': 20
    }
}

# Analyze Lambda function performance for Django models
for func_name, func_info in lambda_functions.items():
    print(f"Lambda Group: {func_name}")
    print(f"Django Models: {[model.__name__ for model in func_info['models']]}")
    print(f"Operations: {', '.join(func_info['operations'])}")
    print(f"Memory: {func_info['memory']} MB")
    print(f"Timeout: {func_info['timeout']} seconds")
    print(f"Concurrent Executions: {func_info['concurrent_executions']}")
    
    # Calculate function usage for JobPost models
    if JobPost in func_info['models']:
        # Estimate daily invocations based on JobPost activity
        daily_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(
            created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=1)
        ).count()
        
        if 'search' in func_info['operations']:
            estimated_invocations = daily_jobs * 20  # 20 searches per job
            print(f"Estimated daily search invocations: {estimated_invocations}")
        elif 'notifications' in func_info['operations']:
            estimated_invocations = daily_jobs * 5   # 5 notifications per job
            print(f"Estimated daily notification invocations: {estimated_invocations}")
        elif 'data_cleanup' in func_info['operations']:
            estimated_invocations = daily_jobs * 2   # 2 cleanup operations per job
            print(f"Estimated daily processing invocations: {estimated_invocations}")
    
    print("---")
    Example 4: List ECS Deployment Groups

    List deployment groups for Amazon ECS services running Django jobpost containerized applications.

    #!/bin/bash
# List deployment groups for ECS application
aws deploy list-deployment-groups \
  --application-name anihpj-jobpost-ecs \
  --region us-east-1
    { "deploymentGroups": [ "anihpj-ecs-web-service", "anihpj-ecs-api-service", "anihpj-ecs-worker-service", "anihpj-ecs-scheduled-tasks" ] }
    Django Integration:

    Corresponding Django implementation for ECS deployment group management:

    # ECS deployment groups for Django containerized applications
ecs_deployment_groups = [
    "anihpj-ecs-web-service",
    "anihpj-ecs-api-service",
    "anihpj-ecs-worker-service",
    "anihpj-ecs-scheduled-tasks"
]

# Map ECS services to Django model containerization
ecs_services = {
    'anihpj-ecs-web-service': {
        'models': [JobPost, User, Author],
        'container_port': 8000,
        'cpu': 512,
        'memory': 1024,
        'desired_count': 3,
        'load_balancer': True
    },
    'anihpj-ecs-api-service': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Location],
        'container_port': 8001,
        'cpu': 256,
        'memory': 512,
        'desired_count': 2,
        'load_balancer': True
    },
    'anihpj-ecs-worker-service': {
        'models': [JobPost],
        'container_port': None,
        'cpu': 1024,
        'memory': 2048,
        'desired_count': 1,
        'load_balancer': False
    },
    'anihpj-ecs-scheduled-tasks': {
        'models': [JobPost, Skills, Author],
        'container_port': None,
        'cpu': 256,
        'memory': 512,
        'desired_count': 0,  # Only runs on schedule
        'load_balancer': False
    }
}

# Analyze ECS service requirements for Django models
for service_name, service_info in ecs_services.items():
    print(f"ECS Service: {service_name}")
    print(f"Django Models: {[model.__name__ for model in service_info['models']]}")
    print(f"CPU: {service_info['cpu']} units")
    print(f"Memory: {service_info['memory']} MB")
    print(f"Desired Count: {service_info['desired_count']} tasks")
