Django-Archive-2025Q3-JobPostStats - Purpose: Q3 2025 reporting - Created: 2025-07-01 - Size: 14,987 bytes 6. Django-Temp-SkillsOptimization - Purpose: Query optimization testing - Created: 2026-01-06 - Size: 5,643 bytes 7. Django-Archive-2025Q2-AllModels - Purpose: Q2 2025 reporting - Created: 2025-04-01 - Size: 18,765 bytes ✓ Batch deleted successfully Deletion Summary: - Total dashboards deleted: 7 - Temporary dashboards: 4 - Archived dashboards: 3 - Total size freed: 65,028 bytes ✓ All matching dashboards deleted HTTP Status: 200 OK
    # Django QuerySet - Metrics from deleted temporary/archived dashboards from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q, Avg from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta print("Bulk Dashboard Deletion - Affected Metrics") print("="*60) deleted_dashboards = { 'temporary': [ 'Django-Temp-Skills-Debug-20260110', 'Django-Temp-UserAuth-Test-20260112', 'Django-Temp-LocationGeo-20260108', 'Django-Temp-SkillsOptimization' ], 'archived': [ 'Django-Archive-2025Q4-AuthorMetrics', 'Django-Archive-2025Q3-JobPostStats', 'Django-Archive-2025Q2-AllModels' ] } print(f"\nDeleted Dashboard Summary:") print(f" Temporary: {len(deleted_dashboards['temporary'])}") print(f" Archived: {len(deleted_dashboards['archived'])}") print(f" Total: {len(deleted_dashboards['temporary']) + len(deleted_dashboards['archived'])}") # Skills metrics (from temp dashboards) print("\n1. Skills Model (Temp Dashboards):") skills_total = Skills.objects.count() skills_popular = Skills.objects.annotate( usage_count=Count('jobpost') ).filter(usage_count__gte=10).count() print(f" Total skills: {skills_total}") print(f" Popular skills (10+ jobs): {skills_popular}") # Output: # Total skills: 0 # Popular skills (10+ jobs): 0 # User authentication (from temp dashboards) print("\n2. User Authentication (Temp Dashboards):") users_active = User.objects.filter( is_active=True, last_login__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=30) ).count() print(f" Active users (30d): {users_active}") print(f" Total users: {User.objects.count()}") # Output: # Active users (30d): 0 # Total users: 0 # Location geocoding (from temp dashboards) print("\n3. Location Geocoding (Temp Dashboards):") locations_geocoded = Location.objects.exclude( Q(latitude__isnull=True) | Q(longitude__isnull=True) ).count() locations_total = Location.objects.count() print(f" Geocoded locations: {locations_geocoded}") print(f" Total locations: {locations_total}") # Output: # Geocoded locations: 0 # Total locations: 0 # Author metrics (from archived dashboards Q2-Q4 2025) print("\n4. Author Metrics (Archived Q2-Q4 2025):") q2_start = timezone.datetime(2025, 4, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc) q4_end = timezone.datetime(2025, 12, 31, tzinfo=timezone.utc) historical_posts = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=q2_start, created_at__lte=q4_end ).count() if hasattr(JobPost, 'created_at') else 0 print(f" Posts (Q2-Q4 2025): {historical_posts}") print(f" Total historical authors: {Author.objects.count()}") # Output: # Posts (Q2-Q4 2025): 0 # Total historical authors: 0 # JobPost stats (from archived dashboards) print("\n5. JobPost Statistics (Archived Dashboards):") posts_by_status = JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate( count=Count('id') ).order_by('-count') print(f" JobPost by Status:") for stat in posts_by_status: print(f" {stat['status']}: {stat['count']}") if not posts_by_status.exists(): print(" (No data)") # Output: (No data) # Cross-model relationships print("\n6. Cross-Model Relationships:") posts_complete = JobPost.objects.filter( skills__isnull=False, location__isnull=False, author__isnull=False ).distinct().count() posts_total = JobPost.objects.count() print(f" Complete posts (skills+location+author): {posts_complete}") print(f" Total posts: {posts_total}") if posts_total > 0: completion_rate = (posts_complete / posts_total) * 100 print(f" Completion rate: {completion_rate:.1f}%") else: print(f" Completion rate: N/A") # Output: # Complete posts (skills+location+author): 0 # Total posts: 0 # Completion rate: N/A print(f"\n⚠ Dashboard Cleanup Impact:") print(f" 7 dashboards removed") print(f" Temporary debugging dashboards: Cleaned up") print(f" Historical Q2-Q4 2025 dashboards: Archived metrics") print(f" → Metrics still available via CloudWatch API") print(f" → Create new dashboards as needed for current monitoring") print(f" → Consider dashboard naming convention for future cleanup")
    Example 5: Delete Dashboard and Export Configuration Backup

    Remove JobPost metrics dashboard while preserving its configuration for potential restoration. Exports dashboard JSON definition before deletion to maintain infrastructure-as-code history for Django application monitoring setup.

    #!/bin/bash
# Backup dashboard configuration before deletion

DASHBOARD_NAME="Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics"
BACKUP_DIR="dashboard-backups"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
BACKUP_FILE="$BACKUP_DIR/${DASHBOARD_NAME}-${TIMESTAMP}.json"

# Create backup directory
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

echo "Step 1: Backing up dashboard configuration..."

# Get dashboard configuration
aws cloudwatch get-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name "$DASHBOARD_NAME" \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --output json > "$BACKUP_FILE"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "  ✓ Configuration backed up to $BACKUP_FILE"
  
  # Extract metadata
  WIDGETS=$(jq '.DashboardBody | fromjson | .widgets | length' "$BACKUP_FILE")
  echo "  Dashboard contains $WIDGETS widgets"
else
  echo "  ✗ Backup failed"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Step 2: Deleting dashboard..."

# Delete dashboard
aws cloudwatch delete-dashboards \
  --dashboard-names "$DASHBOARD_NAME" \
  --region us-east-1

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "  ✓ Dashboard deleted successfully"
  
  # Create restoration script
  cat > "$BACKUP_DIR/${DASHBOARD_NAME}-restore.sh" << 'RESTORE'
#!/bin/bash
# Restore dashboard from backup
aws cloudwatch put-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name "Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics" \
  --dashboard-body file://$(dirname $0)/$(ls -t $(dirname $0)/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-*.json | head -1) \
  --region us-east-1
RESTORE
  
  chmod +x "$BACKUP_DIR/${DASHBOARD_NAME}-restore.sh"
  echo "  ✓ Restoration script created"
else
  echo "  ✗ Deletion failed"
  exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Backup and deletion complete"
echo "  Backup: $BACKUP_FILE"
echo "  Restore: $BACKUP_DIR/${DASHBOARD_NAME}-restore.sh"
    Step 1: Backing up dashboard configuration... Dashboard Configuration: { "DashboardName": "Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics", "DashboardArn": "arn:aws:cloudwatch:us-east-1:123456789012:dashboard/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics", "DashboardBody": "{\"widgets\":[...]}", "Size": 23456 } Parsing dashboard body... Dashboard contains 15 widgets Widget Details: 1. JobPost Creation Rate (Metric) 2. JobPost Status Distribution (Pie Chart) 3. JobPost by Author (Bar Chart) 4. JobPost by Location (Map) 5. JobPost Skills Tagging (Histogram) 6. Database Query Performance (Line Chart) 7. JobPost Search Latency (Heatmap) 8. User Engagement (Number widgets) 9. Author Activity Timeline (Line Chart) 10. Skills Popularity (Bar Chart) 11. Location Coverage (Gauge) 12. JobPost Lifecycle (Funnel) 13. Error Rate Monitoring (Alarm Widget) 14. Custom Annotations (Text) 15. System Health Composite (Numbers) ✓ Configuration backed up to dashboard-backups/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-20260115-142345.json Step 2: Deleting dashboard... ✓ Dashboard deleted successfully Creating restoration script... Restoration Script Content: #!/bin/bash # Restore dashboard from backup aws cloudwatch put-dashboard \ --dashboard-name "Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics" \ --dashboard-body file://$(dirname $0)/$(ls -t $(dirname $0)/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-*.json | head -1) \ --region us-east-1 ✓ Restoration script created chmod +x applied ✓ Backup and deletion complete Backup: dashboard-backups/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-20260115-142345.json Restore: dashboard-backups/Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-restore.sh Files Created: 1. Dashboard backup (JSON): 23.4 KB 2. Restoration script (bash): 312 bytes HTTP Status: 200 OK ✓ Dashboard safely archived before deletion
    # Django QuerySet - JobPost metrics from backed-up dashboard from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost from django.db.models import Count, Q, Avg, Max, Min from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta import json print("JobPost Detailed Metrics (Dashboard Backed Up & Deleted)") print("="*60) # Dashboard backup info backup_info = { "dashboard_name": "Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics", "backup_timestamp": "20260115-142345", "widgets": 15, "backup_size_kb": 23.4, "status": "Backed up and deleted" } print(f"\nDashboard Backup Status:") print(f" Name: {backup_info['dashboard_name']}") print(f" Backup time: {backup_info['backup_timestamp']}") print(f" Widgets: {backup_info['widgets']}") print(f" Status: {backup_info['status']}") # 1. JobPost Creation Rate (Widget 1) print("\n1. JobPost Creation Metrics:") last_24h = timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=24) last_7d = timezone.now() - timedelta(days=7) posts_24h = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=last_24h ).count() if hasattr(JobPost, 'created_at') else 0 posts_7d = JobPost.objects.filter( created_at__gte=last_7d ).count() if hasattr(JobPost, 'created_at') else 0 print(f" Posts (24h): {posts_24h}") print(f" Posts (7d): {posts_7d}") print(f" Avg per day: {posts_7d / 7:.1f}") # Output: # Posts (24h): 0 # Posts (7d): 0 # Avg per day: 0.0 # 2. JobPost Status Distribution (Widget 2) print("\n2. Status Distribution:") status_dist = JobPost.objects.values('status').annotate( count=Count('id') ).order_by('-count') for stat in status_dist: print(f" {stat['status']}: {stat['count']}") if not status_dist.exists(): print(" (No posts)") # Output: (No posts) # 3. JobPost by Author (Widget 3) print("\n3. Posts by Author:") top_authors = Author.objects.annotate( post_count=Count('jobpost') ).filter(post_count__gt=0).order_by('-post_count')[:5] for idx, author in enumerate(top_authors, 1): username = author.user.username if author.user else 'No account' print(f" {idx}. {username}: {author.post_count}") if not top_authors.exists(): print(" (No authors)") # Output: (No authors) # 4. JobPost by Location (Widget 4) print("\n4. Geographic Distribution:") posts_by_location = JobPost.objects.filter( location__isnull=False ).values( 'location__city', 'location__country' ).annotate(count=Count('id')).order_by('-count')[:5] for loc in posts_by_location: city = loc['location__city'] or 'Unknown' country = loc['location__country'] or 'Unknown' print(f" {city}, {country}: {loc['count']}") if not posts_by_location.exists(): print(" (No location data)") # Output: (No location data) # 5. JobPost Skills Tagging (Widget 5) print("\n5. Skills Tagging:") posts_with_skills = JobPost.objects.filter( skills__isnull=False ).distinct() if posts_with_skills.exists(): skill_stats = posts_with_skills.annotate( skill_count=Count('skills') ).aggregate( avg=Avg('skill_count'), max=Max('skill_count'), min=Min('skill_count') ) print(f" Posts with skills: {posts_with_skills.count()}") print(f" Avg skills/post: {skill_stats['avg']:.1f}") print(f" Max skills/post: {skill_stats['max']}") print(f" Min skills/post: {skill_stats['min']}") else: print(" Posts with skills: 0") print(" Avg skills/post: N/A") # Output: # Posts with skills: 0 # Avg skills/post: N/A # 6. User Engagement (Widget 8) print("\n6. User Engagement:") users_who_post = User.objects.filter( author__jobpost__isnull=False ).distinct().count() total_active_users = User.objects.filter(is_active=True).count() print(f" Users creating content: {users_who_post}") print(f" Total active users: {total_active_users}") if total_active_users > 0: engagement_rate = (users_who_post / total_active_users) * 100 print(f" Engagement rate: {engagement_rate:.1f}%") else: print(f" Engagement rate: N/A") # Output: # Users creating content: 0 # Total active users: 0 # Engagement rate: N/A # 7. Skills Popularity (Widget 10) print("\n7. Most Popular Skills:") popular_skills = Skills.objects.annotate( usage_count=Count('jobpost') ).filter(usage_count__gt=0).order_by('-usage_count')[:5] for skill in popular_skills: print(f" {skill.name}: {skill.usage_count} jobs") if not popular_skills.exists(): print(" (No skills data)") # Output: (No skills data) print(f"\n⚠ Dashboard Status:") print(f" Dashboard: Deleted") print(f" Backup: Available at dashboard-backups/") print(f" Restoration: Run Django-JobPost-Detailed-Metrics-restore.sh") print(f" Metrics: Still queryable via CloudWatch API") print(f" → Dashboard can be restored if needed") print(f" → Configuration preserved in version control")

    delete-insight-rules

    Deletes CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules. This command removes rules that analyze log data to identify top contributors to application behavior in your Django jobpost system, such as which Skills, Users, or Authors generate the most traffic or errors.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch delete-insight-rules \
  --rule-names {rule-name} [{rule-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --rule-names (list, required): Names of Contributor Insights rules to delete
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns a list of failures if any rules couldn't be deleted.

    delete-metric-stream

    Deletes a CloudWatch metric stream. This command stops streaming Django jobpost application metrics (Skills performance, User activity, JobPost trends) to destinations like Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, S3, or third-party monitoring services.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch delete-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream to delete
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful deletion.

    describe-alarm-contributors

    Retrieves information about the top contributors to a CloudWatch alarm. Use this to identify which metric dimensions (such as specific Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, or JobPost attributes) are contributing most to alarm state changes in your Django jobpost application, helping pinpoint root causes of performance issues.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-alarm-contributors \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --start-time {timestamp} \
  --end-time {timestamp} \
  [--max-contributors {integer}] \
  [--sort-by {sort-field}] \
  [--sort-order {order}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the alarm to retrieve contributors for
    • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start time for contributor analysis (ISO 8601 format)
    • --end-time (timestamp, required): End time for contributor analysis (ISO 8601 format)
    • --max-contributors (integer, optional): Maximum number of contributors to return (1-100, default 10)
    • --sort-by (string, optional): Field to sort by (Contribution, Name)
    • --sort-order (string, optional): Sort order (Ascending, Descending)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Contributors array with dimension values, contribution scores, and data points for each top contributor to the alarm.

    describe-alarm-history

    Retrieves the history of the specified CloudWatch alarm, including state changes, configuration updates, and actions taken. Use this to track alarm behavior for Django jobpost application monitoring, reviewing when alarms for Skills, Users, or JobPost metrics triggered and what actions were performed.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-alarm-history \
  [--alarm-name {alarm-name}] \
  [--alarm-types {alarm-type} [{alarm-type}...]] \
  [--history-item-type {item-type}] \
  [--start-date {timestamp}] \
  [--end-date {timestamp}] \
  [--max-records {integer}] \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--scan-by {scan-direction}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-name (string, optional): Name of the alarm to retrieve history for
    • --alarm-types (list, optional): Types of alarms (CompositeAlarm, MetricAlarm)
    • --history-item-type (string, optional): Type of history item (ConfigurationUpdate, StateUpdate, Action)
    • --start-date (timestamp, optional): Start date for history retrieval (ISO 8601 format)
    • --end-date (timestamp, optional): End date for history retrieval (ISO 8601 format)
    • --max-records (integer, optional): Maximum number of records to return (1-100, default 50)
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --scan-by (string, optional): Sorting order (TimestampDescending, TimestampAscending)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing AlarmHistoryItems array with timestamp, alarm name, history item type, summary, and detailed data for each history event.

    describe-alarms

    Retrieves information about CloudWatch alarms configured in your account. Use this to list and review alarms monitoring your Django jobpost application metrics, including alarms tracking Skills performance, User activity, Author contributions, Location-based metrics, and JobPost trends.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-alarms \
  [--alarm-names {alarm-name} [{alarm-name}...]] \
  [--alarm-name-prefix {prefix}] \
  [--alarm-types {alarm-type} [{alarm-type}...]] \
  [--children-of-alarm-name {parent-alarm-name}] \
  [--parents-of-alarm-name {child-alarm-name}] \
  [--state-value {state}] \
  [--action-prefix {prefix}] \
  [--max-records {integer}] \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-names (list, optional): Names of specific alarms to retrieve
    • --alarm-name-prefix (string, optional): Prefix to filter alarm names
    • --alarm-types (list, optional): Types of alarms (CompositeAlarm, MetricAlarm)
    • --children-of-alarm-name (string, optional): Name of composite alarm to retrieve child alarms for
    • --parents-of-alarm-name (string, optional): Name of alarm to retrieve parent composite alarms for
    • --state-value (string, optional): Filter by alarm state (OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA)
    • --action-prefix (string, optional): Prefix of alarm action ARN to filter by
    • --max-records (integer, optional): Maximum number of records to return (1-100)
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON with MetricAlarms and CompositeAlarms arrays containing alarm configurations, states, metrics, thresholds, actions, and other settings.

    describe-alarms-for-metric

    Retrieves all alarms configured for a specific CloudWatch metric. Use this to find which alarms monitor particular Django jobpost metrics, such as all alarms watching JobPost creation rates, Skills query counts, or User authentication failures.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-alarms-for-metric \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  [--statistic {statistic}] \
  [--extended-statistic {percentile}] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--period {seconds}] \
  [--unit {unit}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric to find alarms for
    • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric (e.g., AWS/EC2, Custom/Django)
    • --statistic (string, optional): Statistic type (Average, Sum, Maximum, Minimum, SampleCount)
    • --extended-statistic (string, optional): Percentile statistic (e.g., p99, p95)
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions to filter the metric by
    • --period (integer, optional): Period in seconds for the metric evaluation
    • --unit (string, optional): Unit of the metric (Seconds, Bytes, Count, etc.)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing MetricAlarms array with alarm configurations for the specified metric.

    describe-anomaly-detectors

    Lists anomaly detectors configured in CloudWatch. Use this to review machine learning-based anomaly detection configurations for Django jobpost application metrics, identifying which Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost metrics have automatic anomaly detection enabled.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-anomaly-detectors \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--max-results {integer}] \
  [--namespace {namespace}] \
  [--metric-name {metric-name}] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--anomaly-detector-types {type} [{type}...]] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of results to return (1-100)
    • --namespace (string, optional): Namespace to filter detectors by
    • --metric-name (string, optional): Metric name to filter detectors by
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions to filter detectors by
    • --anomaly-detector-types (list, optional): Types of detectors (SINGLE_METRIC, METRIC_MATH)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing AnomalyDetectors array with detector configurations, metrics, namespaces, dimensions, and state information.

    describe-insight-rules

    Retrieves information about CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules. Use this to review rules analyzing log data from your Django jobpost application, identifying top contributors like most active Skills, Users generating the most errors, or Authors with highest JobPost activity.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch describe-insight-rules \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--max-results {integer}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of results to return (1-500)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing InsightRules array with rule names, states, schemas, and definitions.

    disable-alarm-actions

    Disables actions for specified CloudWatch alarms. Use this to temporarily stop alarm notifications and automated responses for Django jobpost application alarms without deleting the alarm configurations, useful during maintenance or known issue windows.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch disable-alarm-actions \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name} [{alarm-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-names (list, required): Names of alarms to disable actions for
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on success.

    disable-insight-rules

    Disables CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules. Use this to temporarily stop analyzing log data for top contributors in your Django jobpost application without deleting the rule configurations, useful when reducing costs or during troubleshooting.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch disable-insight-rules \
  --rule-names {rule-name} [{rule-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --rule-names (list, required): Names of Contributor Insights rules to disable
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Failures array listing any rules that couldn't be disabled.

    enable-alarm-actions

    Enables actions for specified CloudWatch alarms. Use this to resume alarm notifications and automated responses for Django jobpost application monitoring after maintenance windows or troubleshooting periods, restoring monitoring for Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPost metrics.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch enable-alarm-actions \
  --alarm-names {alarm-name} [{alarm-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-names (list, required): Names of alarms to enable actions for
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on success.

    enable-insight-rules

    Enables CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules. Use this to resume analyzing log data for top contributors in your Django jobpost application, restarting identification of most active Skills, Users, Authors, or Locations after maintenance or cost optimization periods.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch enable-insight-rules \
  --rule-names {rule-name} [{rule-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --rule-names (list, required): Names of Contributor Insights rules to enable
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Failures array listing any rules that couldn't be enabled.

    get-dashboard

    Retrieves the details and body of a CloudWatch dashboard. Use this to export or review dashboard configurations displaying Django jobpost application metrics, including visualizations for Skills performance, User activity, Author contributions, Location analytics, and JobPost trends.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name {dashboard-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --dashboard-name (string, required): Name of the dashboard to retrieve
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing DashboardArn, DashboardBody (JSON defining widgets and layout), and DashboardName.

    get-insight-rule-report

    Retrieves the results of a Contributor Insights rule for a specified time period. Use this to analyze top contributors in your Django jobpost application, such as which Skills generate the most errors, which Users create the most traffic, or which Authors publish the most JobPosts.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-insight-rule-report \
  --rule-name {rule-name} \
  --start-time {timestamp} \
  --end-time {timestamp} \
  --period {seconds} \
  [--max-contributor-count {integer}] \
  [--metrics {metric-name} [{metric-name}...]] \
  [--order-by {order-field}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --rule-name (string, required): Name of the Contributor Insights rule
    • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start time for the report (ISO 8601 format)
    • --end-time (timestamp, required): End time for the report (ISO 8601 format)
    • --period (integer, required): Aggregation period in seconds
    • --max-contributor-count (integer, optional): Maximum number of contributors to return (1-100, default 10)
    • --metrics (list, optional): Metrics to include in the report
    • --order-by (string, optional): Field to sort contributors by
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing KeyLabels, Contributors array with keys and data points, AggregateValue, and ApproximateUniqueCount.

    get-metric-data

    Retrieves metric data for multiple metrics using a single request. Use this to efficiently query Django jobpost application metrics across Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPost models, supporting both single metrics and metric math expressions for complex calculations.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-metric-data \
  --metric-data-queries {queries-json} \
  --start-time {timestamp} \
  --end-time {timestamp} \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--scan-by {scan-direction}] \
  [--max-datapoints {integer}] \
  [--label-options {timezone-options}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --metric-data-queries (list, required): Queries specifying metrics or math expressions to retrieve
    • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start time for metric data (ISO 8601 format)
    • --end-time (timestamp, required): End time for metric data (ISO 8601 format)
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --scan-by (string, optional): Order to scan data (TimestampDescending, TimestampAscending)
    • --max-datapoints (integer, optional): Maximum data points to return (1-100800)
    • --label-options (structure, optional): Timezone options for data point labels
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing MetricDataResults array with Id, Label, Timestamps, Values, StatusCode, and Messages for each queried metric.

    get-metric-statistics

    Retrieves statistics for a specified CloudWatch metric. Use this to analyze Django jobpost application performance by getting aggregated statistics (average, sum, max, min) for metrics tracking Skills queries, User logins, Author activities, Location searches, or JobPost creations.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-name {metric-name} \
  --start-time {timestamp} \
  --end-time {timestamp} \
  --period {seconds} \
  --statistics {statistic} [{statistic}...] \
  [--extended-statistics {percentile} [{percentile}...]] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--unit {unit}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --namespace (string, required): Namespace of the metric (e.g., AWS/EC2, Custom/Django)
    • --metric-name (string, required): Name of the metric to retrieve statistics for
    • --start-time (timestamp, required): Start time for statistics (ISO 8601 format)
    • --end-time (timestamp, required): End time for statistics (ISO 8601 format)
    • --period (integer, required): Granularity in seconds (multiple of 60)
    • --statistics (list, required): Statistics to retrieve (Average, Sum, Maximum, Minimum, SampleCount)
    • --extended-statistics (list, optional): Percentile statistics (e.g., p99, p95, p50)
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions to filter the metric by
    • --unit (string, optional): Unit of the metric (Seconds, Bytes, Count, etc.)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Label, Datapoints array with Timestamp, SampleCount, Average, Sum, Minimum, Maximum, Unit, and ExtendedStatistics.

    get-metric-stream

    Retrieves information about a CloudWatch metric stream. Use this to review stream configurations sending Django jobpost application metrics (Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, JobPosts) to destinations like Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose, S3, or third-party monitoring platforms.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream to retrieve
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Arn, Name, IncludeFilters, ExcludeFilters, FirehoseArn, RoleArn, State, CreationDate, LastUpdateDate, OutputFormat, and StatisticsConfigurations.

    get-metric-widget-image

    Retrieves a snapshot graph image of one or more CloudWatch metrics. Use this to generate programmatic visualizations of Django jobpost application metrics for reports, notifications, or automated documentation showing Skills performance, User activity trends, or JobPost creation rates.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch get-metric-widget-image \
  --metric-widget {widget-json} \
  [--output-format {format}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --metric-widget (string, required): JSON string defining the widget configuration (metrics, time range, visualization type)
    • --output-format (string, optional): Format of the image (png, default is png)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing MetricWidgetImage (base64-encoded PNG image data).

    list-dashboards

    Lists all CloudWatch dashboards in your account. Use this to discover and manage monitoring dashboards for your Django jobpost application, finding dashboards displaying Skills metrics, User activity, Author contributions, Location analytics, and JobPost performance.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch list-dashboards \
  [--dashboard-name-prefix {prefix}] \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --dashboard-name-prefix (string, optional): Prefix to filter dashboard names
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing DashboardEntries array with DashboardName, DashboardArn, LastModified, and Size for each dashboard.

    list-managed-insight-rules

    Lists available managed Contributor Insights rules provided by AWS. Use this to discover pre-configured rules that can be quickly enabled for your Django jobpost application to analyze common patterns in Skills usage, User behavior, or JobPost activity without creating custom rules.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch list-managed-insight-rules \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--max-results {integer}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the AWS resource to list managed rules for
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of results to return (1-500)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing ManagedRules array with TemplateName, ResourceARN, and RuleState for each managed rule.

    list-metric-streams

    Lists all metric streams in your account. Use this to discover and manage streams continuously sending Django jobpost application metrics to external destinations for real-time monitoring, long-term storage, or integration with third-party analytics platforms.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch list-metric-streams \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--max-results {integer}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --max-results (integer, optional): Maximum number of results to return (1-500)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Entries array with Arn, Name, FirehoseArn, State, CreationDate, and LastUpdateDate for each stream.

    list-metrics

    Lists available CloudWatch metrics in your account. Use this to discover which metrics exist for your Django jobpost application, finding custom metrics tracking Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPosts, as well as AWS service metrics for infrastructure monitoring.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch list-metrics \
  [--namespace {namespace}] \
  [--metric-name {metric-name}] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--next-token {token}] \
  [--recently-active {time-period}] \
  [--include-linked-accounts] \
  [--owning-account {account-id}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --namespace (string, optional): Namespace to filter metrics by (e.g., AWS/EC2, Custom/Django)
    • --metric-name (string, optional): Metric name to filter by
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions to filter metrics by
    • --next-token (string, optional): Token for pagination
    • --recently-active (string, optional): Filter to metrics with data in last 3 hours (PT3H)
    • --include-linked-accounts (boolean, optional): Include metrics from linked monitoring accounts
    • --owning-account (string, optional): Filter by account ID that owns the metrics
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Metrics array with Namespace, MetricName, and Dimensions for each available metric.

    list-tags-for-resource

    Lists tags for a CloudWatch resource such as alarms or Contributor Insights rules. Use this to manage and organize monitoring resources for your Django jobpost application, discovering tags identifying environment, team, or model type (Skills, Users, JobPosts) for each resource.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch list-tags-for-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the CloudWatch resource to list tags for
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Tags array with Key and Value for each tag attached to the resource.

    put-anomaly-detector

    Creates or updates an anomaly detector for CloudWatch metrics. Use this to enable machine learning-based anomaly detection for Django jobpost application metrics, automatically identifying unusual patterns in Skills queries, User logins, Author activities, or JobPost creation rates without manual threshold configuration.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-anomaly-detector \
  [--namespace {namespace}] \
  [--metric-name {metric-name}] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--stat {statistic}] \
  [--configuration {config-json}] \
  [--single-metric-anomaly-detector {detector-config}] \
  [--metric-math-anomaly-detector {math-detector-config}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --namespace (string, optional): Namespace of the metric (e.g., AWS/EC2, Custom/Django)
    • --metric-name (string, optional): Name of the metric to monitor for anomalies
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions of the metric
    • --stat (string, optional): Statistic to monitor (Average, Sum, Maximum, Minimum, SampleCount)
    • --configuration (structure, optional): Configuration for the anomaly detector
    • --single-metric-anomaly-detector (structure, optional): Configuration for single metric detector
    • --metric-math-anomaly-detector (structure, optional): Configuration for metric math detector
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful creation or update.

    put-composite-alarm

    Creates or updates a composite alarm that combines multiple metric alarms using Boolean logic. Use this to create sophisticated monitoring for Django jobpost application by combining alarms across Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPosts with AND, OR, and NOT logic for complex alert conditions.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-composite-alarm \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --alarm-rule {boolean-expression} \
  [--actions-enabled | --no-actions-enabled] \
  [--alarm-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--alarm-description {description}] \
  [--insufficient-data-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--ok-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--tags {tags-json}] \
  [--actions-suppressor {alarm-arn}] \
  [--actions-suppressor-wait-period {seconds}] \
  [--actions-suppressor-extension-period {seconds}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the composite alarm
    • --alarm-rule (string, required): Boolean expression combining child alarms (e.g., "ALARM(alarm1) AND ALARM(alarm2)")
    • --actions-enabled (boolean, optional): Whether actions should be executed (default true)
    • --alarm-actions (list, optional): ARNs of actions to execute when alarm state is ALARM
    • --alarm-description (string, optional): Description of the composite alarm
    • --insufficient-data-actions (list, optional): Actions when alarm state is INSUFFICIENT_DATA
    • --ok-actions (list, optional): Actions when alarm state transitions to OK
    • --tags (list, optional): Tags to apply to the alarm
    • --actions-suppressor (string, optional): ARN of alarm that suppresses actions
    • --actions-suppressor-wait-period (integer, optional): Wait period in seconds before actions are suppressed
    • --actions-suppressor-extension-period (integer, optional): Extension period in seconds for action suppression
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful creation or update.

    put-dashboard

    Creates or updates a CloudWatch dashboard. Use this to create custom visualization dashboards for your Django jobpost application, displaying metrics and graphs for Skills performance, User activity trends, Author contributions, Location analytics, and JobPost creation rates in a unified view.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-dashboard \
  --dashboard-name {dashboard-name} \
  --dashboard-body {dashboard-json} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --dashboard-name (string, required): Name of the dashboard (up to 255 characters)
    • --dashboard-body (string, required): JSON string defining dashboard widgets, layout, and metrics
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing DashboardValidationMessages array listing any warnings or errors in dashboard configuration.

    put-insight-rule

    Creates or updates a Contributor Insights rule. Use this to analyze log data from your Django jobpost application, identifying top contributors such as most frequently queried Skills, Users generating the most errors, Authors with highest JobPost volumes, or Locations with peak activity.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-insight-rule \
  --rule-name {rule-name} \
  --rule-definition {rule-json} \
  [--rule-state {state}] \
  [--tags {tags-json}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --rule-name (string, required): Name of the Contributor Insights rule
    • --rule-definition (string, required): JSON string defining the rule schema and log group
    • --rule-state (string, optional): Initial state of the rule (ENABLED, DISABLED)
    • --tags (list, optional): Tags to apply to the rule
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful creation or update.

    put-managed-insight-rules

    Creates or updates managed Contributor Insights rules provided by AWS. Use this to quickly enable pre-configured rules for your Django jobpost application without creating custom rule definitions, automatically analyzing common patterns in application logs and metrics.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-managed-insight-rules \
  --managed-rules {rules-json} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --managed-rules (list, required): List of managed rule configurations to create or update
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Failures array listing any rules that couldn't be created or updated.

    put-metric-alarm

    Creates or updates a CloudWatch metric alarm. This is one of the most important commands for monitoring your Django jobpost application, allowing you to set thresholds and receive notifications when Skills queries exceed limits, User errors spike, Author activity drops, or JobPost creation rates become abnormal.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --comparison-operator {operator} \
  --evaluation-periods {integer} \
  [--metric-name {metric-name}] \
  [--namespace {namespace}] \
  [--statistic {statistic}] \
  [--extended-statistic {percentile}] \
  [--dimensions {dimensions-json}] \
  [--period {seconds}] \
  [--unit {unit}] \
  [--datapoints-to-alarm {integer}] \
  [--threshold {double}] \
  [--threshold-metric-id {metric-id}] \
  [--treat-missing-data {behavior}] \
  [--evaluate-low-sample-count-percentile {mode}] \
  [--metrics {metrics-json}] \
  [--tags {tags-json}] \
  [--alarm-description {description}] \
  [--actions-enabled | --no-actions-enabled] \
  [--ok-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--alarm-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--insufficient-data-actions {arn} [{arn}...]] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the alarm (up to 255 characters)
    • --comparison-operator (string, required): Operator for comparison (GreaterThanThreshold, LessThanThreshold, etc.)
    • --evaluation-periods (integer, required): Number of periods to evaluate the alarm
    • --metric-name (string, optional): Name of the metric to monitor
    • --namespace (string, optional): Namespace of the metric (e.g., AWS/EC2, Custom/Django)
    • --statistic (string, optional): Statistic type (Average, Sum, Maximum, Minimum, SampleCount)
    • --extended-statistic (string, optional): Percentile statistic (e.g., p99, p95)
    • --dimensions (list, optional): Dimensions of the metric
    • --period (integer, optional): Period in seconds for metric evaluation (multiple of 60)
    • --unit (string, optional): Unit of the metric (Seconds, Bytes, Count, etc.)
    • --datapoints-to-alarm (integer, optional): Number of datapoints that must breach threshold
    • --threshold (double, optional): Threshold value to compare against
    • --threshold-metric-id (string, optional): ID of metric to use as threshold for anomaly detection
    • --treat-missing-data (string, optional): How to treat missing data (notBreaching, breaching, ignore, missing)
    • --evaluate-low-sample-count-percentile (string, optional): How to evaluate percentiles with low sample count
    • --metrics (list, optional): Metrics for metric math expressions
    • --tags (list, optional): Tags to apply to the alarm
    • --alarm-description (string, optional): Description of the alarm
    • --actions-enabled (boolean, optional): Whether actions should be executed (default true)
    • --ok-actions (list, optional): Actions when alarm transitions to OK state
    • --alarm-actions (list, optional): Actions when alarm state is ALARM
    • --insufficient-data-actions (list, optional): Actions when alarm state is INSUFFICIENT_DATA
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful creation or update.

    put-metric-data

    Publishes custom metric data to CloudWatch. This is essential for sending Django jobpost application-specific metrics such as Skills search counts, User registration rates, Author activity scores, Location popularity, and JobPost creation metrics that aren't automatically collected by AWS services.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace {namespace} \
  --metric-data {metric-data-json} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --namespace (string, required): Namespace for the metric data (e.g., Custom/Django, JobPost/Application)
    • --metric-data (list, required): Array of metric data points including MetricName, Value, Timestamp, Dimensions, Unit, StatisticValues, and StorageResolution
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful data publication.

    put-metric-stream

    Creates or updates a CloudWatch metric stream. Use this to continuously stream Django jobpost application metrics to destinations like Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose for real-time analytics, long-term storage in S3, or integration with third-party monitoring platforms for comprehensive observability.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch put-metric-stream \
  --name {stream-name} \
  --firehose-arn {firehose-arn} \
  --role-arn {role-arn} \
  --output-format {format} \
  [--include-filters {filters-json}] \
  [--exclude-filters {filters-json}] \
  [--tags {tags-json}] \
  [--statistics-configurations {stats-config-json}] \
  [--include-linked-accounts-metrics] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --name (string, required): Name of the metric stream
    • --firehose-arn (string, required): ARN of Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream
    • --role-arn (string, required): ARN of IAM role for CloudWatch to assume
    • --output-format (string, required): Format of streamed data (json, opentelemetry0.7)
    • --include-filters (list, optional): Namespaces to include in the stream
    • --exclude-filters (list, optional): Namespaces to exclude from the stream
    • --tags (list, optional): Tags to apply to the metric stream
    • --statistics-configurations (list, optional): Additional statistics to stream
    • --include-linked-accounts-metrics (boolean, optional): Include metrics from linked monitoring accounts
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    Returns JSON containing Arn of the created or updated metric stream.

    set-alarm-state

    Temporarily sets the state of a CloudWatch alarm. Use this for testing alarm notifications and actions for your Django jobpost application monitoring without waiting for actual threshold breaches, verifying that alerts for Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPost metrics trigger correctly.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state \
  --alarm-name {alarm-name} \
  --state-value {state} \
  --state-reason {reason} \
  [--state-reason-data {json-data}] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --alarm-name (string, required): Name of the alarm to set state for
    • --state-value (string, required): State to set (OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA)
    • --state-reason (string, required): Reason for the state change
    • --state-reason-data (string, optional): JSON data providing additional context
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful state change.

    start-metric-streams

    Starts streaming metrics for one or more metric streams. Use this to resume continuous metric streaming for your Django jobpost application after stopping streams for maintenance, cost optimization, or troubleshooting, re-enabling real-time metric delivery to external destinations.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch start-metric-streams \
  --names {stream-name} [{stream-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --names (list, required): Names of metric streams to start
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful start operation.

    stop-metric-streams

    Stops streaming metrics for one or more metric streams. Use this to temporarily pause continuous metric streaming for your Django jobpost application during maintenance windows, cost optimization periods, or when troubleshooting downstream processing issues without deleting stream configurations.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch stop-metric-streams \
  --names {stream-name} [{stream-name}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --names (list, required): Names of metric streams to stop
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful stop operation.

    tag-resource

    Adds tags to a CloudWatch resource such as alarms or Contributor Insights rules. Use this to organize and categorize monitoring resources for your Django jobpost application, adding metadata identifying environment (prod, dev), team ownership, or model type (Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, JobPosts).

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch tag-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  --tags {tags-json} \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the CloudWatch resource to tag
    • --tags (list, required): Tags to add to the resource (Key and Value pairs)
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful tagging.

    untag-resource

    Removes tags from a CloudWatch resource. Use this to clean up or update metadata on monitoring resources for your Django jobpost application, removing outdated or incorrect tags from alarms monitoring Skills, Users, Authors, Locations, and JobPost metrics.

    Syntax
    aws cloudwatch untag-resource \
  --resource-arn {resource-arn} \
  --tag-keys {key} [{key}...] \
  [--cli-input-json {json-file}] \
  [--generate-cli-skeleton {skeleton-format}]
    Parameters Description
    • --resource-arn (string, required): ARN of the CloudWatch resource to remove tags from
    • --tag-keys (list, required): Keys of tags to remove
    • --cli-input-json (string, optional): Reads arguments from JSON file
    • --generate-cli-skeleton (string, optional): Generates JSON skeleton for input
    Return Value

    No output returned on successful tag removal.
